Description:
The White House Leadership Development Program (WHLDP) is a career development fellowship program that serves to develop the next generation of career senior executives with the skills necessary for successful enterprise leadership that benefits the interest of the government as a whole. Sponsored by the Executive Office of the President, the program offers participants: a broad federal perspective on high-priority challenges, access to senior decision-makers and skills development. 

The White House Leadership Development Program (WHLDP) aims to strengthen enterprise leadership across the government by providing senior-level federal employees with expanded perspectives and skill sets to address challenges facing the country. These increasingly complex challenges span agencies and jurisdictions, requiring senior leaders to work across agencies and organizational boundaries while leveraging networks on a greater scale than in the past to ensure the government continues to effectively deliver services to the American people.

The program is primarily focused on:
Developing Talent: Building the next generation of career senior executives with an enterprise perspective through a placement focused on the complex, cross-agency challenges that increasingly confront the Federal government. Also, strengthening enterprise leadership skills through targeted development sessions.

The White House Leadership Development Program (WHLDP) engages a diverse cohort of talented GS-15 career employees for a year of executive leadership development. The Fellows spend their year working on the Federal Government’s highest priority and impact challenges in the Executive Office of the President (EOP) and learning from senior executives and each other in development sessions.
Novelty: The WHLDP is unique in its ability to provide an opportunity for a diverse cohort of high-potential career employees to develop executive leadership skills as they seek to become future senior executives. The selected fellows spend approximately 80 percent of their time in a placement assignment gaining on-the-job experience. The remaining 20 percent of their time is spent in leadership development, which consists of navigating change, building community, and evolving as leaders. The cross-government performance allows fellows to develop a deeper understanding of the importance of organizational performance, measuring impact and delivering results. Also, by identifying new ways of doing business, embracing innovation, focusing on outcomes, and effective service delivery, they become change agents across government.
Impact & Results:
The WHLDP has had a number of successful outcomes and benefits to both the Fellows and Agencies. Some benefits to come out of the program include:
- Increased exposure to a variety of different agencies – cultures, missions, functional areas, senior leaders and, new ways of delivering results in government.
- Improved ability to address complex problems that cut across organizational and jurisdictional boundaries.
- To build and leverage relationships that have an impact beyond their formal authority.
- Heightened government-wide perspective on problem-solving, best practices, and real solutions that can be applied to related challenges within the home agency.
- Experience relying on collaborative leadership skills, rather than technical expertise, to meet mission goals.
- Firsthand experience integrating into a new culture and fast-paced, ambiguous environment with an emphasis on flexibility and entrepreneurial skills.
- Increased ability to leverage newly expanded professional network

Description:
The Cross-Agency Priority Goals (CAP) are a tool used by leadership to accelerate progress on a limited number of Presidential priority areas. These goals require active collaboration among multiple agencies for implementation and focus on a limited number of cross cutting policy areas, ones that are critical for a government to deliver. CAP Goals are long-term in nature but help to drive cross-government collaboration to tackle government-wide management challenges affecting most agencies.

The Cross-Agency Priority Goals (CAP) are a tool used by leadership to accelerate progress on a limited number of Presidential priority areas. These goals require active collaboration among multiple agencies for implementation and focus on a limited number of cross cutting policy areas, ones that are critical for a government to deliver. CAP Goals are long-term in nature but help to drive cross-government collaboration to tackle government-wide management challenges affecting most agencies.

To drive these priorities, the Administration leverages Cross-Agency Priority (CAP) Goals to coordinate and publicly track implementation across Federal agencies. CAP Goals are updated or revised every four years with each Presidential Administration’s term. CAP Goals are a tool used by leadership to accelerate progress on a limited number of Presidential priority areas where implementation requires active collaboration among multiple agencies. 

The criteria for selection includes: 1) whether a goal has clear cross-agency implementation actions that have been identified, 2) whether setting a CAP Goal would likely accelerate progress, and 3) whether it aligns with Administration priorities. To drive these priorities, the Administration leverages CAP Goals to coordinate and publicly track implementation across Federal agencies. CAP Goals are updated or revised every four years with each Presidential Administration’s term.
Novelty:
CAP goals are a powerful vehicle for defining major priorities across the U.S. federal government as well as tools to ensure that government managers put their energy behind the effort. 

This system-wide focus can include, for example: opportunity and issue spotting, including resolution of conflicts across discrete lines of effort; training and guidance for practitioners within agencies; data-management and evidence-building strategies that advance multiple existing activities; and other capacity-building strategies that build lasting change and, further out, identify opportunities to leverage these management systems.
Impact & Results:
CAP goals produced truly significant improvements in federal management, in areas including:
- Infrastructure permitting process. Few things frustrated persons both inside and outside government more than the long delays involved in permitting infrastructure projects. An OMB-led effort reduced the average time needed for obtaining a permit from 4.5 years to 2.5 years, a 45 percent reduction that saved billions of dollars.
- Category management. Federal agencies often conducted their own purchasing instead of taking advantage of the vast economies of scale that could result from bundling multiple agencies’ purchases of key items ranging from security services to information technology.
- Employee engagement. An effort launched in 2014 by OMB, the Office of Personnel Management and the White House attempted to improve employee morale. Employee engagement scores rose from 64 to 72 percent.

Description:
An equivalent of 12% of total canopy in Freetown was lost per year between 2011 and 2018. Loss of tree canopy directly affects catchment areas for water reserves, exacerbating the risks of landslides, flooding, and coastal erosion. Loss of tree and vegetation cover also threatens biodiversity. Against the backdrop of population expansion and rapid urbanization, the Freetown City Council came up with a plan to plant and grow 1 million trees by 2022—an effort that would increase the city's vegetation cover by 50%.

Freetown City Council, working closely with neighboring Western Area Rural District Council, partnered with Greenstand, a nonprofit that develops open-source technology to manage environmental goods and services. Community-based growers use its TreeTracker app on locally available smartphones to create a unique geotagged record, or ID, for each new tree planted—including a photo. Growers revisit each seedling periodically, to water and maintain, verify and document the plant's survival—and receive mobile money micropayments for their efforts.

To ensure sustained financing for tree growth, tree IDs can be turned into “impact tokens,” which can be bought, sold, and traded by businesses and individuals. This approach will generate a new revenue stream to fund more tree-planting and growing support.
Novelty: The campaign pioneers an innovative, sustainable tree-growing model.
Impact & Results:
In the initial, 2020-21 phase, planters successfully planted and tracked 250,000 trees during the rainy season, focusing on higher slope areas where communities are susceptible to landslide risks. Together with 10 commercial tree nurseries and 10 community-based organizations, the project has created more than 550 short-term jobs, especially for marginalized, vulnerable, and underemployed women and youth. An additional 50,000 mangrove trees will be planted to restore damaged coastal wetlands and to cap off an incredibility successful inaugural phase. The project has also sold its first 5,000 tree “impact tokens,” and the revenue will provide financing to plant and grow an additional 5,000 trees during the next phase. 

The second phase of this effort to build a climate-ready city will continue to endorse community engagement, participation, and impact. Another 700,000 trees will be planted during the 2021 and 2022 rainy seasons. These trees will be cultivated along major and secondary roads, around key water sources and other critical infrastructure, and within communities, neighborhoods, and public spaces.

Transform Freetown – Freetown City Council (fcc.gov.sl)

Treetracker by Greenstand

Greenstand | History

Project/Initiative Name: GovChat to improve citizens services and promote active citizenship
Organisation: GovChat / Health Department and Social Development Departments of South Africa (other private, public and multilateral too)
Description:
Govchat helps the South African government in provide quicker citizen services while holding themselves accountable. 
It is a chatbot available on web messengers like Whatsapp or Facebook so citizens can use it to access government services including their municipality services or grant applications or to contact their public representative. During COVID-19 GovChat allowed processing grant and check of the application status to >8 million citizens without a waiting line, which facilitated the delivery of >60 billion Rands. The chatbot also provides information about COVID-19 such as symptoms, testing centres and obtain testing results. It processed >365,000 COVID-19 test results throughout the pandemic. 

Nowadays it provides a wide range of services, from finding a public representative base on a constituent's postal code or rate government services such as schools, healthcare or finding voting stations. The data is shared with the owners of the service (service providers) in real time so they can rectify the issue as quickly as possible.
Novelty:
'- co-created with the South African Government assures there is uptake and that they could properly address citizen concerns. 
- it is accesible: GovChat focus on messaging platforms that citizens already have on their phones (WhatsApp, Facebook Messenger) to expose government inefficiencies
- Anonymised and aggregated data
Impact & Results:
'- 9,3 million active users
- >13,5 million south african social security agency (SASSA) applications received
- >644,1 million messages processes
- 117,4 million access to the Progressive Web Application (PWA)

Project/Initiative Name: ShelterApp - The city of St. Paul's App to Help Unsheltered Residents Get Help
Description:
ShelterApp is a tool - a centralized platform - for unsheltered residents to search and learn more about the essential services that they need, whenever they need them, in the city of St. Paul. 

Based in public data on services for unsheltered residents, ShelterApp collects evidence of service delivery from 30 public service providers and unsheltered residents. It seeks to overcome the barriers and opportunities within the service delivery system for this target group. 

Providing photos of the city’s shelter spaces and adding step-by-step directions on how to access a bed upon arriving at a shelter, beneficiaries can search for local resources including food, shelter, health and work through the mobile and web platforms.
Novelty:
There’s no user data on ShelterApp yet, but Saint Paul is seeing the impact of its more holistic approach to serving unsheltered residents, which includes intensive outreach and expanding the supportive services available. Furthermore, data relating to homelessness (often inaccessible) across partners and real-time data on service availability become available. 

ShelterApp has the potential to serve as the initial infrastructure for an open data system that improves service delivery and access in Ramsey County.

Project/Initiative Name: Chillicothe's adoption of cutting edge and responsive public transport
Description:
The city maintained a bus service that served most parts of the town, but it suffered from low ridership and a common perception that public transportation was underfunded and undesirable. Chillicothe zeroed in on two potential transit solutions: a dual fixed-route/on-demand public transportation system and a highly popular trolley. After prototyping these ideas with 400+ residents, the city is now advancing both ideas as pilots.

About the on-demand public transportation system:
Through agile methods the city found out that users were not particularly interested in having the entire system switch away from fixed routes and to on-demand. Existing users liked the fixed routes, and while many were intrigued by the idea of an on-demand option, there wasn’t an appetite for a complete overhaul. Instead, a hybrid system was proposed - keeping the fixed-route public transit system, and expanding an on-demand option that would provide a more flexible and responsive capacity-expanding service.

About the trolley:
The town will be experimenting with different trolley routes at different times, trying out four different routes over four months, including a dedicated history tour route and a downtown loop.
Novelty: Incorporates innovation training "the power of bringing community engagement to residents" as part of the public transit planning.

Project/Initiative Name: Democracy Club to support everyone's participation in democratic life
Description:
It collects information on the candidates running for elections across the different levels of governance in UK. Since 2017 they are a data supplier of the Electoral Commission.

Their work focuses on collecting data from the UK's approximately 400 local authorities, either via communicating with them directly or taking information from their websites using a infrastructure of web 'scrapers'. The collected data is then processed and published as open source. 

As a civic-tech, they provide non-partisan platforms which enable citizens to scrutinise, evaluate, and engage directly with their politicians and candidates. They have delivered personalised election information to well over ten million people since 2018.
Novelty:
Through algorithms and coding they have created what they call "an infrastructure of web scrapers" to monitor electoral processes ex ante: https://github.com/DemocracyClub
A good example is the TwitterBot: this checks Twitter handles added to candidate profiles, and updates them if they change.
Moreover, they have managed to develop an apparently well established community of practice and volunteer around: https://candidates.democracyclub.org.uk/volunteer/
Impact & Results:
During every election round since 2019 they have sent >250 million messages and communications in partnership with all major social media firms. Are host of >1000 active members through an online community. 
In 2022 elections: 1,500 volunteers made 64,000 edits to our database, collecting details on over 21,000 individual candidates.

Project/Initiative Name: HCS EdConnect to bridge the digital divide and provide home WiFi until 2030
Organisation: City of Chattanooga - Hamilton County schools
Description:
The iniviative seeks that all students can access the internet for online learning as the COVID-19 crisis continues. It enables a community-wide fiber optic network in place and seeks to provide internet services to >28,000 economically challenged students in Hamilton County Schools at no charge. They address the digital divide for education by providing a high-quality broadband solution. 

Each family fills out an online/offline application. If elegible, they will receive a router and at least 100 Mbps internet service at no charge. The service is up to four times faster than typical educational access offerings from private providers and the only one that deliver symmetrical speeds (same speed for uploads and downloads). Inclusiveness is a critical factor here so if a family lives in the outskirts or in an area where the service is not available, they still can receive a mobile hotspot with unlimited data free of charge. 

The service infrastructure is provided through a partnership between the City of Chattanooga, several foundations, EBP and Tech Goes Home.
Novelty:
Two fronts of novelty:
1) Fundraising strategy to make it happen: upfront investment of $8.2 million in additional infrastructure and equipment needed to outset the project. This allowed to quick fundraise $6 million.
2) Community and PPP partnerships to enable the project along the pipeline
3) Ensure internet access at almost 4 times the speed provided in the market
Impact & Results:
- 100 Mbps+ fiber internet access to >30,000 students
- >$15 million of direct ivnestment in infrastructure and equipment

Project/Initiative Name: Plymouth Alliance Contract to support people with complex needs
Description:
The alliance is a non-legal entity that uses a systems-based approach to supporting people with complex needs through the use of an Alliance Contract, which is based on integrative and non-siloed service provision with the focus on creating systemic change to support a new relational way of working with people in homeless condition and with complex needs. Partners share responsibility for achieving outcomes, making decisions based on the best outcome for the service user.

This model of contracting opened the way for the prospect of a whole system of service, designed around the needs of people, rather than a fragmented market built around the needs of commissioners and services, which was usually top-down.

The Plymouth Alliance has eight member organisations including the Council and seven other social enterprises and charities. The Alliance can be viewed as a virtual organisation, where partners work collectively to create a collaborative environment without the need for a new organisational form. The Alliance Contract had a first year budget of £7.7m. The initial contract was for five years (from 2019) with a potential for extension to ten.

The contract covers:
- all substance misuse services,
- all services supporting homeless people contracted by the council,
- defined offers and asks with mental health services
- and a wider group of 20 other services either subcontracted by the alliance or part of the system group.

There are four levels of governance in the Alliance: Commissioner as owner, Alliance leadership team, Alliance management team, and Alliance manager.
Novelty: Innovative contracting and cross-silo working. Contracts had been commissioned in separate silos, often resulting in duplication, inefficiencies and poor outcomes for the person using multiple services. Twenty five contracts spanning substance misuse and homelessness were aligned under the co-produced Alliance model.
Impact & Results:
- Alcohol outreach prototype has delivered 44% reduction in admissions and 33% reduction in bed days
- Many hostel residents are now in their own accommodation, which has reduced bed and breakfast spending by around £1/2 million.
- To respond with urgency to the Covid-19 Crisis the Alliance has made decisions according to two key principles – Is it legal? and Is it safe?
- Prevention of Covid-19 outbreaks among people supported by the Alliance
- Less than 10% no shows for drug addiction service

Project/Initiative Name: Go! Manila to access public services without the need to go outside
Novelty:
'- Update and condense available data in a single platform. 
- Initially a tax and payment platform, then evolved into a platform to connect and provide citizens with the most basic services (i.e. registration, cash transfers)
- Visibilized vulnerable and marginalized groups
- Bridges the digital divide
Impact & Results:
Created >350,000 unique digital identities 
More than 50,000 registered intermediaries to support service delivery 
Provide follow up on part of the 178,345 senior citizens, 27,172 persons with disabilities, 13,877 solo parents, 209,513 indigent youth.

Project/Initiative Name: Minas Atende to improve the quality of public services
Description:
The Minas Atende Program is an initiative of the Government of the State of Minas Gerais that aims to improve the quality of services offered to citizens and reduce the costs involved in providing such services.

The initiative is structured on three main fronts: (1) the simplification of procedures, (2) innovation in public services, through digitization, and (3) improvement of the relationship with the citizen, from the improvement of service channels and the availability of new interaction platforms. Thus, the Minas Atende Program is constituted as a government policy that encompasses different projects:
- MG App: an application for smartphones that centralizes several public services offered by the government in a single channel.
- UAI Share Program: program to expand the network of equipment for the issuance of documents.
- Alô Minas: offer of digital inclusion based on the expansion of access to mobile telephony and internet (3g).
- Estado Sabe Ouvir Award: annual award that promotes the recognition of public servants who develop innovation projects.
- Service simplification: process review, creation of defined steps and service mapping.
- Agents of Simplification Program: creation of a network of 300 public servants responsible for mobilizing the government's digital transformation agenda.
- “Simplify!”: channel for receiving improvement suggestions sent by users, for later sharing with the bodies responsible for offering and simplifying processes.
- Services Digital Transformation Index: indicator for calculating the percentage of fully digitalized services in the State of Minas Gerais.

The project is still in its early stages and has a delivery horizon of 2022
Impact & Results:
- 34 services were digitally transformed, totaling 52% of fully digital stages.
- substantial reduction in face-to-face service costs saving R$>21 millions
- digital service is around 30 times cheaper than face-to-face service and 10 times cheaper than telephone service

Organisation: Singapore's Digital Office - National Environment Agency
Description:
The Singapore Together Alliance for Action – Online Ordering for Hawkers is a workgroup, comprising delivery platforms, hawkers associations, community partners and government agencies. They work closely together to support hawkers by:
1. Helping them go online/digital
2. Develop a sustainable commercial model
3. Raise consumer awareness about delivery platforms and contactless payments.

To reach out to half of the 18,000 stallholders to adopt e-payments, there are a group of Digital Ambassadors available to guide them through the learning and adjustment process to share the benefits of food delivery platforms. Additionally, stallholders will be encouraged to adopt a contactless payment option by adopting an unique SGQR label at their stall. By using a SGQR, stallholders will be able to receive payments through 19 different payment schemes nationwide. 

The initiative includes too the Digital Resilience Bonus as an additional support of up to $10,000. The payout will offset the cost of adopting solutions from three pre-defined categories, namely business process solutions, digital presence and data-driven operations.
Novelty: #NAME?

Project/Initiative Name: Seniors Go Digital to address the digital gap
Organisation: Singapore's Digital Office - Ministry of Communication and Information
Description:
The program seeks to accelerate digital adoption in Singapore as the government embarks on a phased recovery post COVID-19 and the Digital for Life national initiative. 

SGD involved mobilising digital ambassadors to train over 100,000 senior citizens, through one-to-one coaching and small group learning sessions. They are provided with different options for learning, and used everyday language which these groups are comfortable with. 

Whether it is learning from one of the Digital Ambassadors at designated venues or taking guided lessons at home via a mobile phone, seniors are able to navigate through a learning journey that involves content, belonging to a community of practice and access to digital pods. 

The services are provided by multiple stakeholders like the National Library Board, or the Agency for Integrated Care, which all have various touch points with seniors.

For example, it involved teaching them about how to navigate a smartphone interface or go through step by step on how to go about learning how to use different apps.
Novelty: #NAME?

Project/Initiative Name: COMO CO2 for monitoring and cleaning the air inside of Berlin's buildings
Description:
The project is in a pilot phase and aims to monitor indoor air through CO2 concentration. COMo has developed a sensor system that is installed inside of closed spaces (private or business ones) and it's supported by LoRaWAN technology so the sensors can collect and transmit measurement data completely independently. People are allowed to call up their data at any time via the web portal and also make it directly visible.

From an energy point of view, window ventilation therefore seems unsuitable, especially in winter, regardless of the type of ventilation, as high heat energy losses can occur. But this doesn't have to be the case, because sensors enable the people to ventilate in a new way by displaying you the air quality on site. If the CO2 concentration in your rooms rises, you know that it is time to ventilate. 

LoRaWAN stands for Long Range Wide Area Network and means or enables energy-efficient transmission of data over long distances. This was specially developed for the Internet of Things (IoT) and Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT). With LoRaWAN it is possible to manage several hundred sensors within a network and to process sensor data. Sensors can be operated for up to 10 years without changing batteries. 

The pilot currently works in 6 different locations of Berlin (i.e. city lab berlin, Schwuz an LGTBQ+ club).
Novelty: CO2 indoor monitoring to self-manage ventilation and save energy (from heaters), specially during winter times.
Impact & Results: For the first time, COMo allows non-technicians to control their air quality based on measured values. This is relevant to facilities with many visitors (e.g. clubs, cinemas...). But also, it is evident that the number of installed sensors for single participants is still too small for general statements and forecasts on the development of indoor air quality.

Project/Initiative Name: Berlin's CityLab Public Design Handbook for innovative public administrations
Description:
The publication contains sound background knowledge and a diverse collection of templates and working documents from the city's and German public administration, as well as other tailored toolkits to the public administration. The aim is to enable a direct entry into new, effective ways of working and "save time" ideating over things that already exist. 

The handbook provides the basis for developing results-oriented, needs-based public innovations, regardless of whether these innovations are visible products and services or invisible structures and processes. It guides the public servant through the different phases of an innovation process that has been specifically designed with public administrations in mind. One major component of the book is its extensive collection of templates and working documents tailored to the needs of public administrations. 

It targets five areas of action: 
Impact-oriented
Understanding the status quo, revealing blind spots, generating target knowledge, developing for the common good
Human-centered
Qualitative and quantitative research, proceeding empathically, transforming affected groups into active participants
Evidence-based
Decision making based on data and findings from theory and practice, thinking and acting critically
Design-led
Visualization, prototyping, testing, error tolerance, iterative processes, experimental space
Multi-perspective
Interdisciplinary or interdepartmental cooperation, generating understanding between different fields of expertise, diversity through accessibility
Novelty: Clusters and summarizes good practices and experiences from toolkits and frameworks that work

Project/Initiative Name: GDS's guide to use AI in the public sector
Organisation: OAI, GDS, and The Alan Turing Institute (ATI)
Description:
The UK Government has published a guide on how to build and use artificial intelligence in the public sector. With its mission is to drive responsible and innovative uptake of AI technologies for the benefit of everyone, the document recognizes AI’s potential and its challenges for securing the people and the whole society. 

The document puts in perspective the UK’s Data Ethics Framework that sets out clear principles for how data should be used in the public sector. The Guideline is build on these ethical principles and apply them to the context of the public sector. Ultimately To help leaders across the civil service understand what AI is and how it can be used, ethical and safely, to solve problems within their departments.

The guidance covers how:
- to assess if using AI will help you meet user needs
- the public sector can best use AI
- to implement AI ethically, fairly and safely
Novelty:
Leaders across the public sector could benefit from better understanding the technology, the opportunities it presents and the limitations of its use.

Delivery teams needed more specific guidance on the different considerations for projects with AI components.

Project/Initiative Name: Kids Included - enabling meaningful child and youth participation within companies in the digital era
Description:
This co-creative document provides a practical tool intended for businesses that embark into meaningful child participation. It seeks to engage stakeholders inside of children's mindset to make them more aware of their digital rights.

Using and providing agile methods, the tool addresses the role of businesses in protecting children’s rights and fostering their well-being and represents an important step towards scenarios where children are systematically engaged and represented in how companies behave.

The tool spins around four main questions that seeks to help with: 1) what is meaningful child participation? 2) what are the key challenges to achieving this? 3) why is it important for children and businesses in relation to the digital environment? 4) how can businesses overcome these challenges?
Novelty: Visibilize Childs needs, participation and rights in the corporate environment (for product/service development and for awareness of risks). Addresses issues of children's digital rights.

Project/Initiative Name: White Collar Crime Risk Zones to predict likelihood of financial crimes
Organisation: White Collar Crime in The New Inquiry
Description:
The model and app provide a predictive policing application that targets white collar crime.

It uses machine learning to predict where financial crimes will happen across the U.S. The system was trained on incidents of financial malfeasance from 1964 to the present day, collected from open data from the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (FINRA), a non-governmental organization that regulates financial firms.

The system uses industry-standard predictive policing methodologies, including Risk Terrain Modeling and geospatial feature predictors, which enables the tool to predict financial crime at the city-block-level with an accuracy of 90.12%.

Predictive policing apps are designed and deployed to target so-called "street" crime, reinforcing and accelerating destructive policing practices that disproportionately target impoverished communities of color. Unlike typical predictive policing apps which criminalize poverty, White Collar Crime Risk Zones criminalizes wealth.
Novelty:
Unlike typical predictive policing apps which criminalize poverty, White Collar Crime Risk Zones criminalizes wealth. 

Use of open data from the financial regulatory authority (FINRA), the Federal Trade Commission and several related surveys.

Project/Initiative Name: Ukraine's Diia Wallet as a one-stop shop for public services
Description:
Diia is Ukraine's official one-stop-shop app for public services and a wallet for digital versions of official documents. The platform’s motto is “the state as a service, not a bogeyman”. 

It hosts more than seventy services: for example, Ukrainians can use the app to change their registered address without appearing in person at the old place of residence – a boon for the third of Ukrainians who live away from their formal address.

At the end of 2021, Diia was used by 13 million people (a third of the country’s 44 million inhabitants and a fivefold increase in one year). Adoption was partly driven by its use as a Covid certificate platform and the introduction of ePidtrimka (“eSupport”) – a one-off payment of 1,000 UAH (approx. 25EUR) for fully vaccinated Ukrainians linked to a digital bank card. In two months, the government >10 million cards.

During war period the eMaliatko service, for example, was available to residents of breakaway Donetsk and Luhansk regions so that they could register children born in the conflict zone as Ukrainian citizens. 

The app is linked with Ukraine's major social media Telegram, Viber and Messenger to inform to the citizens inlands or abroad.
Novelty:
One-stop-shop app for public services and a waller for digital versions of official documents.
Main point for accessing government services during wartime

Project/Initiative Name: WallBox Chargers to intelligently manage how your electric vehicle charges by connecting it to the grid
Description:
Wallbox develops charging stations for electric vehicles and homes. By using a software it enables electric vehicles, charging stations and charging operators to share data. So customers can regulate when a car is being charged to take advantage of variable energy loads and lower pricing during off-peak demand times of the day.

It offers a complete portfolio of charging and energy management solutions for residential, business and public use. It seeks to redefine users’ relationship to the grid. The Quasar, for example, enables users to charge their electric vehicles and use the energy from the vehicle’s battery to power their home, business or send it back to the grid.

They claim that are already innovating for the next wave of EV charging and energy management including vehicle-to-home and vehicle-to-grid, energy storage, peer-to-peer energy transactions, energy trading, and energy as payment. The creation process is ruled by three principles: 1) simple and easy to access/understand, 2) smart - always connected, 3) user centric and designed for real people.

Recently the Centre on Regulation of Europe issues a case study paper about energy data sharing.
Novelty:
Supports the development of a digital-driven “European energy data space” by allowing for data sharing and system integration between the energy sector and other sectors, e.g. mobility.

Creates a new relationship between the users of electric vehicles and its personal space.

Project/Initiative Name: HaydenAI & NYC Transit Authority for AI bus-mounted cameras
Description:
Hayden AI just recently contracted with the New York City Transit authority to implement the Automated Bus Lane Enforcement (ABLE) Camera Systems. The deployment consists of 300 interior mounted AI-based camera systems to increase safety and operational performance and improve customer experience. 

It seeks to utilize smart enforcement technology to reduce congestion, improve commuter safety, fleet reliability and on time performance. The information and data generated from the system would serve to identify locations that have the highest instances of vehicles blocking bus lanes. It is expected to help increasing compliance with dedicated bus lane stopping and parking restrictions. The pilot has already started. 

From bus lane and bus stop enforcement to digital twin modeling, the MTA will use a mobile perception system to speed up transit, make streets safer, and create more sustainable lanes. The technologies comply with security and privacy regulations and protect personal information. If there is no encroachment on a bus lane, the cameras do not record anything.
Novelty: metadata can be used to create a digital twin of the city and support public transport policy making

Project/Initiative Name: Culver City AR Urban Runoff and Stormwater Project
Description:
The Urban Runoff and Stormwater Project was added into the Culver Boulevard Realignment to incorporate structural stormwater best management practices that retain and reuse captured urban run-off. The aim of using AR is that government agencies have seen its value for improved training, sensitization and accessibility. 

The city has worked to improve mobility for pedestrians and bicyclists with the creation of a two-acre park in the median. A big portion of the action would happen out of sight, motivating the project team to include “interpretive signage” that explains the purpose of the project through an interactive, virtual experience. 

The AR experience will soon be available for visitors, who will be able to learn about the project by reading the information on the signs — printed in both Spanish and English — or by scanning the QR code to get deeper.

There are six different “experiences” in augmented reality that users can participate in. To design the experience, the city partnered Trigger XR team creating imagery to accompany it.

Project/Initiative Name: Cluny Abbey using AR and 3D recordings to visualise "what has today disappeared"
Description:
By providing additional imagery and historical or cultural details, AR has the ability to transform visitor experiences with city landmarks. The French town of Cluny has installed AR screens hroughout its historic abbey in order to illustrate what the community looked like in the Middle Ages, and other cities could pursue similar technologies to highlight their historic pasts. "Clunyvision" reveals the past of the town centre.

Many documents have been studied to include the latest archaeological knowledge in the virtual model. Most remains have been scanned for CAD restitution. The whole mock up was finally exported to dedicated software to run the rendering step.

The work consisted in creating a whole database of 3D models as well as 2D sources (plans, engravings, pictures) accessible by the scientific community. The scientific perspectives focus on a representation in virtual immersion of the grand church at scale 1 and an access to the digital mock-up through Augmented Reality.
Novelty: AR for displaying old architecture perspectives of historical places and areas of touristic interest

Project/Initiative Name: Corruption Risk Forecast to diagnose the present, forecast the future, and set policy targets against corruption
Description:
T-index is a unique assessment of government transparency based on different kinds of transparency services that countries offer to their citizens, encompassing both de jure and de facto aspects of transparency. This index can be used as an actionable tool for civil societies around the world and provides a guide through what needs to be advocated for. As such, the T-index offers information about the necessary changes in transparent services that should be offered by governments.

The new Computer Mediated Government Transparency Index (T-Index) produced by ERCAS in 2021 measures transparency as the existence of accessible (free of cost) public information required to deter corruption and enable public accountability in a society. The De Jure index reviews legal transparency (a country’s transparency legislation), and the De Facto Index rates 14 essential websites for the coverage and accessibility of data. Furthernore, it is specifically intended to evaluate the information available to the public during a particular phase of the budget year, not the past years.

The T-index seeks to discover what occurs in practice about transparency in the government, rather than what the law requires. Because governments can cheat the laws to avoid observing them.  That is why this project is very innovative because it seeks to analyze the practice with very specific concepts, which are feasibly verifiable and can be updated at any time.

The T-Index can also serve a “naming and shaming” purpose, identifying good and bad performers in the field of government transparency, giving visibility to best practices and (hopefully) producing incentives for laggards to improve transparency standards. As such, it offers information about the necessary changes in transparent services that should be offered by governments, which go further than the usual, but insufficient, adoption of freedom of information laws.
Novelty:
The T-Index may serve as a better and more valid measurement of government transparency, filling an important gap in the measure of this phenomenon.
One of the reasons we don't have the government fill out its own questionnaire is because we want the T-index to be independent, credible and easy to verify.
Something very innovative, because we are measuring transparency from the perspective of ordinary citizens, which would be a person who does not have much specific knowledge of technology but can surf the Internet and search from the government website, if we find this information on the Internet, because it is accessible, so the information must be.
Impact & Results:
This index showed that there is indeed a gap between real transparency and de jure transparency, which was initially the hypothesis of the research. So transparency efforts have to start being directed at de jure (legal) transparency. And not focus on the signing of treaties or mere regulations, without knowing if this transparency exists in practice. 

Because at the end of the day the way citizen information is made available affects their lives greatly and may violate their right to information. The T-index confirmed that high corruption and low transparency they drive the transparency-corruption association strongly

Project/Initiative Name: Toolkit DT4C - Digital Transformation for People-Oriented Cities and Communities
Description:
The Toolkit supports strategizing and planning the digital transformation of cities and communities to promote sustainable, inclusive, resilient and improved quality of life within cities and communities. 

This is a one-stop guide containing resources of international standards and guidance, as well as the latest research and projections, and cutting-edge reports on a variety of topics relevant to the cities and communities digital transformation. The resources cluster evidence and data from different levels of "smart implementation", so it can be used by everyone.

This is a comprehensive, yet non-exhaustive collation of information that seeks to accelerate progress toward the SDGs, in particular 11 at the local level.
Novelty: A one-stop guide with international resources from the different UN agencies and repositories.

Project/Initiative Name: Dynamic World - a near realtime land cover dataset for a constantly changing planet
Description:
Dynamic World is a near realtime 10m resolution global land use land cover dataset, produced using deep learning, freely available and openly licensed. It is the result of a partnership between Google and the World Resources Institute, to co-produce a dynamic dataset of the physical material on the surface of the Earth. 

Dynamic World is intends to be used as a data product for users to add custom rules with which to assign final class values, producing derivative land cover maps. It has the before/after option to evidence changes on land surface. 

They key aspects of Dynamic World are: 1) near realtime data with >5,000 images to build land cover data, 2) per-pixel probability across 9 land cover classes - an AI-powered system that for every pixel estimates covers, area patterns, etc., 3) 10-meter resolution.
Novelty: 1) near realtime data with >5,000 images to build land cover data, 2) per-pixel probability across 9 land cover classes - an AI-powered system that for every pixel estimates covers, area patterns, etc., 3) 10-meter resolution.

Description:
The web-based tool offers practical guidance on how to mature the societal readiness of research projects. The tool was developed in a three-step procedure (concept, design, validation) involving the consortium and participants in the Social Labs. Each step has been reported in a separate deliverable.

The SR Thinking Tool provides a generic methodology allowing researchers to reflect on the societal impact of their work at critical stages in the project life cycle. The primary goal is to help researchers align their project activities with societal needs and expectations. The SR Thinking Tool asks reflective questions to stimulate thinking about how to integrate ideas about RRI into research practice.

The tool is designed to complement not only shortfalls in technology readiness, data and innovation approaches, but also improve upon other efforts to integrate RRI, sustainability, and design thinking in research and innovation cycles.
Novelty: Incorporates reflexibility, anticipation, inclusion and responsiveness into the responsible data and innovation endeavors.

Description:
AISP develops data systems that integrate relevant information so that agencies working for good can serve policymaking and the community better, faster, and smarter. Through a network of agencies and cities, it has established cross-agency data agreements across multiple projects. 

It works in particular with state and local governments and collaborate in the responsible use of data to improve/impact lives under three components:
- Convene and advocate on behalf of communities that are sharing and using cross-sector data for good 
- Connect to innovations, best practices, and research and funding opportunities that support ethical data sharing
- Consult with data sharing collaborations to build the human and technical capacity to share data and improve lives 

It is possible do download datasets about multiple topics clustered on selected categories of interest: 
https://aisp.upenn.edu/data-sharing-landscape/

Project/Initiative Name: NHS Centre for Improving Data Collaboration
Description:
The Centre for Improving Data Collaboration is a new business unit within NHSX which has been created to support the health and social care sector to enter into data-sharing partnerships that benefit the NHS, patients and the public.

Additionally, it provides support to NHSX’s innovation functions, including the AI Lab and Innovation Lab, to support their commercialisation and Intellectual Property allocation. 

The Centre’s remit pertains to any data sharing partnership related to health and care data, providing tailored commercial and legal advice to NHS Trusts, medical charities and other health sector organisations. Moreover, the Centre for Improving Data Collaboration will facilitate data partnerships in a manner that brings standardisation
Novelty: it builds fairer and more beneficial data partnerships with industry through data and hands-on support and advice

Project/Initiative Name: DATA-POP Alliance to facilitate the exchange of knowledge and capacity-building about Big Data and SDG's
Description:
The goal of the alliance is to cultivate a global community of practice through an open and free interactive learning platform, to facilitate the exchange of knowledge and capacity-building about Big Data and Sustainable Development.

It's a non-profit "think-and-do-tank" that works through 3 pillars of work: 1) diagnosing local realities and human problems with data and AI, 2) mobilizing capacities, communities, and ideas towards more data literate and inclusive societies, 3) and transforming the systems and processes that underpin the datafied communities and countries.

It works around six thematic programs and projects: digital transformations, AI and statistics for SDGs, resilient livelihoods and ecosystems, data feminism, geographies of inequalities, technology and democracy. Furthermore, the platform identifies three main data-related challenges to overcome: 1) a scientific-technological bias, 2) the poor institutional connectivity, and 3) the limited political channels and technical capacities for the primary producers and users of data.

This is a project that involves adolescents who digitize and assess their daily home-to-school routes in different school neighborhoods in Flanders (Belgium). Through a web-based platform, called the “Bike Barometer” (“Fietsbarometer” in Dutch) the participants have the potential to offer detailed spatial insights about the local safety conditions in Flanders and in specific school neighborhoods. 

The potential for mobility policy is twofold: 1) the cycling friendliness and traffic flows in school environments can be monitored over time and 2) the platform has the potential to create local ecosystems of adolescents and teachers (both considered citizen scientists here) and policymakers.

The platform is explicitly education-oriented, providing formal curricular material for geography classes teacher development workshops. The participants can open a personal space and, first, digitize their trip by clicking at the intersection closest to their home on the map, and select the road segments they follow on their way from home to school. Second, they evaluate points and segments along their route in terms of cycling safety.
Novelty: Cycling and urban street policymaking by route tracking with students and schools participation.

Description:
CityLab is the first laboratory of public administration and urban society that work together to jointly design and implement projects for the digitization of Berlin. The Lab aims to test agile methods for public administration and develop needs-based smart city solutions for and with Berlin's citizens. 

Although the CityLab is currently running, they define themselves as a "not-yet-finished concept" and a experiment that is continuously under development. 

In the "Smart Cities Model Projects" program, Berlin is developing a new, ambitious smart city strategy. The strategy is therefore being developed in dialog with Berlin's diverse urban society - moving away from a purely technological focus towards co-design and inclusion. The CityLab is involved in the three-stage strategy process.
Novelty:
First City (public) laboratory in Berlin to experiment, prototype and escalate public solutions. 
Created to support the Berlin Smart Cities Model Project.

Project/Initiative Name: Jelbi - the mobility app for Berlin's public and sharing services
Description:
The Jelbi project links BVG (Berlin Transport Authority) services in Berlin with other mobility providers. At various points in Berlin, vehicles based on the sharing principle, such as scooters, bicycles, cars or scooters, are available to customers. These offers can be seen, rented and paid for via the free Jelbi app. In this way, an individually suitable combination of means of transport can be found for every route through the city - whether by train, bus, e-scooter, rental bike or car.

Uses the principle of registration once to use everything. Besides the online platform, the initiative incorporates an offline approach to allow people to easy recharge or buy services that without internet won't be possible.
Novelty:
Uses the principle of registration once to use everything (one-pass)
Integrates most transportation means available in the city

Organisation: Laboratorio de Gobierno - Inter American Development Bank
Description:
The Chilean Public Innovation Index serves for measuring and developing capacities to innovate and seeks to help the government adapt to the changing contexts, needs and expectations of citizens. This, to provide clear and simple evidence to guide decision making and promote the development of capabilities.

The Index has an annual cycle consisting of four phases: Preparation, measurement, delivery of results and capacity building. Currently >40 public entities from the different levels of government have participated. The third measurement process of the Public Innovation Index is in the Capability Measurement phase.

The Index is also sustained by the largest community of practice of public-private teams, institutions and people in the world (>22,000 people). They support the process of diagnosing, guiding and implementing and targeting capacity building actions.
Novelty:
First public sector index among Spanish speaking countries
Largest community of practice around public sector innovation in the world

Description:
The initiative offers a detailed state-of-play on upcoming initiatives, legislative proposals and procedures already finalized, as well as issues blocked or files withdrawn or continued. The initiatives are shown as a railway image with the aim to facilitate the follow up by all members, by the population of the legislative and non-legislative activity of the EU institutions. It is updated on a monthly basis and is available online

Starting 2020 a new format has been implemented and all the initiatives are organized in six trains corresponding to the Commission's six priorities. This information is possibly to be visualized by the 19 European Parliament committees responsible for the file. 

The Legislative Train Schedule aims at providing an interesting, content rich and easy to understand way to visualise the legislative elements that form the major part of the work programme. Using trains, carriages and railways as a metaphor, the application allows users to monitor the progress of legislative files during the current and past European Parliament term.
Novelty: Centralized visualization of the "paths and routes" of EU lawmaking towards the Parliament and the EU Commission.

Description:
This program includes strategically planting trees and urban vegetation to fight against the urban heat island effect. With the use of satellite sensors to monitor thermal variations of that effect, as well as satellite-based measurements, the city plans its response capacity and seeks to timely respond to public health threats. 

This is a $106 million program to mitigate UHI effect drivers by providing a comprehensive approach expanding the city's climate resilience agenda. It highlights the role that the physical environment plays in driving local temperatures, as well as technologies and local-based simple solutions (i.e. roof painting, increase canopy coverage) to help lower surface and indoor temperatures. 

The program consists too of updated LiDAR data - a mapping technology that determines distance to an object or surface using laser pulses to measure elevation, allowing the creation of three-dimensional topographic maps and highly accurate surveys of surface terrain, vegetation and manmade structures. Also helps with the better understanding of the geography of NYC microclimates and to create baselines of temperature information at neighborhood level.
Novelty: Deployment of sensors and monitoring systems across the city to create baseline microclimates and make sense of the city's urban and forestry potential.

Organisation: Walter Reed Biosystematics Unit, Smithsonian Institution
Description:
The Mosquito Occurrence Dataset is a citizen science system for investigating and managing disease-carrying mosquitos. The large database with >13,7 thousand datasets records in the Global Biodiversity Information Facility (GBIF) repository, all linked to photographs submitted by citizen volunteers and validated by entomological expert. 

The project records the occurrence of adult mosquitoes through photographs taken by citizens on an App (http://www.mosquitoalert.com/). The temporal coverage of the dataset is from 2014-2021, principally from Spain. It has validated as many as 28 mosquito species across Europe in two years with the following step-by-step:
- Perform data augmentation and calibration in mosquito distribution models.
- Perform risk assessments, like characterization of critical areas and seasonal variability for disease risk transmission.
- Mosquito images can be used to train machine-learning models for mosquito detection and classification.
- The Mosquito Alert dataset updates annually and could be used for early warning of invasive species from city to continental scales.
- The Mosquito Alert dataset and repository provides metadata and helps to optimise vector control.
Novelty: Large-scale citizens science project to surveillance and prevent the spread of the deadly mosquito Aegis
Impact & Results: Stats are found here: http://www.mosquitoalert.com/estadisticas-de-participacion/

Project/Initiative Name: The Łı́ı́dlı̨ı̨ Kų́ę́ First Nation scientific license to regain data sovereignty
Description:
LKFN introduced a new application process for researchers as a way to create a compendium of all research gathered on its land. They aim to shift the perspective in which research is conduced in their territories. So when a researcher gets a permit they are allowed to independently review it and make sure that certain topics are addressed in the application referred to the Scotty Creek and the whole territory. 

The ARI application form asks researchers to:
- share how they plan to release data, to ensure confidentiality;
- describe their methodology; and
- indicate which communities they expect to be affected by their work

The LKFN form asks researchers to:
- explicitly declare that all raw data will be co-owned by the Łı́ı́dlı̨ı̨ Kų́ę́ First Nation;
- disclose the specific equipment and infrastructure they plan to install on the land, lay out their demobilization plan, and note how often they will be travelling through the land for data collection; and
- explain the steps they’ve taken to educate themselves about Łı́ı́dlı̨ı̨ Kų́ę́ First Nation customs and codes of research practice that will apply to their work with the community.

The new protocol will require all researchers, not just research leaders, to obtain a licence to study on its land.
Novelty:
Ex ante screening and assessment of a research purpose, its outputs and outcomes, as well as the potential implications for the community. 
Local-based accountability mechanisms for research endeavors.

Project/Initiative Name: Health Map - an AI tool to scan the internet against COVID-19
Description:
The web platform uses AI to scan the internet to detect discussions about outbreaks from different types of viruses and diseases (i.e. avian influenza, COVID-19). Mostly using a classification of zoonotic diseases, vector borne, respiratory, and others, the algorithm locates the nexus of these information and verify their accuracy. 

In the case of COVID-19, for example, sent out alerts as early as 30.12.2019, pre-dating public knowledge of the virus. The map and the platform in general has access to real-time reporting of infectious disease events all over the world that are online and of publicly access. The map puts those alerts into context, displaying data where occurrences happen (i.e. with the COVID-19 map: https://www.healthmap.org/covid-19/), as well as putting those alerts into context and showing the readers the impact of infectious disease for policymaking and community actions.
Novelty: AI based tool to map and "make sense" of the data and information about infectious disease outbreaks

Description:
An app designed to rethink humanitarian interactions. It was designed using private sector principles to solve a humanitarian problem to empower refugees with engaging, simple information in plain formats and language. 

By using human-centered design principles to tailor the app according to the needs of the refugees and overall people on the move, the mobile site gather information journalistically and report it to the users strategically and connecting them with the information they need to solve their most basic problems (i.e. cash transfers, shelter). 

The entry point of this innovation is either the App or Facebook, where they can disclose initial information about themselves, as well as target locations so they can receive the right information. This is an effort of humanizing the first interaction process with the use of technology.
Novelty: Technology-based communication solution to provide first assistance to refugees and people on the move

Project/Initiative Name: Streamlining Data Entry Tasks to Increase Focus on People in Need
Description:
It consists of a streamlined data entry app using a Bot service to input client information into the multiple systems of healthcare facilities on demand. The solution allows staff to spend more time supporting clients doing healthcare work instead of administrative paperwork. 

The app is the umbrella for three integrated systems: 
1) After-hours data entry and administrative tasks Bot 
2) CareBot to streamline paperwork process
3) Smarter documentation managing system 

The whole system seeks to improve reporting processes through technology to provide a better working environment and stronger focus on clients. The solution streamlines the documentation of client/caregiver interactions and reduces the ongoing administrative load. The bot presents case planners with a personalized view of their case-load, then intuitively walks them through the steps necessary to keep those cases properly documented. The bot asks the case planner questions, then pulls information from a person’s file and The Foundling’s electronic health record system to populate forms. 

Using HIPAA-secure patient information stored on the cloud, the bot is able to automatically populate fields pertaining to the client, from care plans and physician contact information to names and birthdates.
Novelty:
improvement of reporting processes through technology
bot-based case planner

Project/Initiative Name: eBird - a tool to crowdsource the collection of bird data/information
Description:
eBird is among the world’s largest biodiversity-related science projects, with more than 100 million bird sightings contributed annually by eBirders around the world and an average participation growth rate of approximately 20% year over year.

This is a collaborative project that offers a free mobile app that allows offline data collection anywhere in the world, and the website provides many ways to explore and summarize your data and other observations from the global eBird community. eBird directly collaborates with hundreds of partner groups for regional data entry portals, outreach, engagement, and local impact. Most eBird review happens at the level of a single observation. 

The process involves the use of AI-based technology and community collaborations to review submissions and classification.

Project/Initiative Name: EJScreen: Environmental Justice Screening and Mapping Tool
Description:
To meet EPA's mandate related to the protection of public health and the environment, the institution has developed a new environmental justice (EJ) mapping and screening tool - EJScreen. As part of EPA's 2020 environmental justice strategic plan, It is based on nationally consistent data and using an approach that combines environmental and demographic indicators in maps and reports.

EJScree includes 12 environmental indicators, 7 demographic indicators and 12 EJ indexes which provides the tool with three (above all) capabilities: 1) color coded mapping, 2) generate standard reports for a selected area, 3) comparisons showing how a selected area compares to the state, EPA region or the nation. 

EJScreen users choose a geographic area; the tool then provides demographic and environmental information for that area. All of the EJScreen indicators are publicly-available data. The EJ system is of national application, and it's based in the concept of "fair treatment and meaningful involvement of all people regardless race, color, origin, among others".
Novelty: #NAME?
Other relevant URL(s) - eg reports, pres:
Map areas potentially subject to environmental justice: https://ejscreen.epa.gov/mapper/ 
https://www.epa.gov/ejscreen 
https://www.nj.gov/governor/news/news/562020/20200918a.shtml

Project/Initiative Name: State of Open Data Policy Repository
Description:
The Open Data Policy Repository is a resource hub supporting decision-makers at the local, state and national levels to responsible reuse and share open data. It seeks to assess recent policy developments surrounding open data, data reuse, and data collaboration around the world. Moreover, it surfaces >50 examples of recent legislative acts, proposals, directives, and other policy documents worldwide. 

The Data Policy Lab curated the repository. The main finding is that the majority of countries still lack open data policies or a way to implement the policies outlined. To further assess on the evidence, it will make use of the "Third Wave of Open Data framework" to enable more evidence and articulate cross-sector data collaborations.
Novelty: First repository of recent legislative acts, proposals, directives and other policy documents related to open data policies worldwide.

Project/Initiative Name: Filling data gaps on gender diversity in Canada
Description:
The 2021 Canadian Census of Population included for the first time a question on gender and "at birth" accuracy to the question on sex, allowing all people in Canada to describe their gender. This data was released in April 2022, making Canada the first country to collect and publish gender diversity data from a national census.

In recent years, there has been a significant increase in the recognition and sensitivity of the Canadian public and government to transgender and non-binary people. However, little data was available in Canada on transgender or non-binary people prior to the 2021 Census. This lack of data was seen as a major gap limiting the ability of governments, non-governmental organizations, health care and service providers, and researchers to study these populations, understand their lived experiences, listen to their needs, and create inclusive legislation, policies, and programs. The census is the only statistical tool that reliably counts the population at the local level, such as municipalities.

The two main objectives were to better reflect how people identify themselves and to obtain more inclusive data that reflects the gender diversity in Canada. These changes address important gaps in knowledge and information about gender diversity. This data can be used by different levels of government in many areas (e.g., social discrimination, violence, housing, social isolation, health, labor market, and welfare). This data can be used, for example, as part of the 2ELGBTQI+ (people who are Two-Spirited, Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual, Transgender, Queer, Intersex, or use other sexual or gender diversity terms) Action Plan.

In addition, other statistical agencies, such as the Institut de la statistique du Québec (in Canada) and the New Zealand Statistical Agency, have followed Statistics Canada's lead in introducing a new gender variable with a gender-inclusive option and using this default variable.
Novelty: The Government of Canada's approach to greater recognition of gender diversity in all government departments and agencies, including Statistics Canada's social statistics programs such as the Census, is an innovative project. Canada is the first country to adopt such an approach at the national level, seeking to modernize government practices with respect to sex and gender information and to make gender the default variable. As a result, Canada is the first country to collect and publish data on gender diversity, specifically on transgender and non-binary people, from a national census. This approach has inspired other regional and national statistical agencies, such as the Institut de la statistique du Québec and the New Zealand Statistics Agency, to follow Statistics Canada's lead.
Impact & Results:
It is still early to describe the impact of this innovation as the first census results on gender diversity were not released until April 2022. Statistics Canada has already published several documents related to this innovation including this article "Canada is the first country to produce data on transgender and non-binary people using the census" which summarizes the results by place of residence and age. 

This publication has generated a lot of public interest (for example, on the Reddit Canada platform). This innovation has been well received by experts and transgender/non-binary people in Canada who see it as evidence of increased social and governmental recognition for them.

Project/Initiative Name: Montevideo Decide - Uruguay's digital participatory budget
Description:
This is the first digital citizen's consultation platform in the country that allows them to express their opinions, propose and influence the actions carried out by the Municipality of Montevideo. This is intended to be a mechanism of participatory democracy and people can access online via an App or through a browser. Citizens above 16 years old can participate.

It has >15,5 thousands citizens registered in the platform, where they can access to the citizen's consultation, participatory budgeting and collaborative environments. The experience has been adapted from the software "Consul" that serves to more than 50 cities and offers reusable software componentes easy to adapt to every context. 

Differentiating from their different levels of registration (levels 1 or 2), citizens are expected to participate in debates, present project initiatives, as well as participate in surveys, interview the Mayor and his Government team and participate in co-creation of documents.
Novelty:
First digital citizen's consultation platform 
Provides a year-long consultative and accountability mechanism

Project/Initiative Name: EU Voice & Video to contribute to the European Union’s strategy for data and digital sovereignty
Description:
The EDPS has launched a public pilot phase of two social media platforms: EU Voice (microblogging) and EU Video (video channels). The two platforms are part of decentralised social media networks that connect users in a privacy-oriented environment, based on free and open-source Mastodon and PeerTube software. EU institutions, bodies and agencies can register and interact with users on EU Voice and upload videos on EU Video. Other public administrations can easily setup their own platforms.

EU Voice is the official ActivityPub microblogging platform of the EU institutions, bodies and agencies (EUIs). Together with EU Video, it is part of an alternative social media pilot program proposed, and provided by the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS). The pilot program contributes to the EU's strategy for data and digital sovereignty. EU institutions with privacy-friendly microblogging accounts that they typically use for the purposes of press and public relations activities. 

The website of EU Video and Voice stores Internet bandwidth and non-personal API credentials in the browser of unregistered and registered users until the browser deletes them for the purpose of ensuring a fast and secure interaction with the website. They also process feedback of users in the form of subscriptions, content appreciations (likes), promotions and comments for publication in the context of video pages upon instruction of the users’ respective (third-party) ActivityPub service.
Novelty: A social media platform for EU institutions to inform and connect with the citizens in a private-friendly environment of microblogging and clip accounts. Instead of relying on third-country services, the project foster digital sovereignty and keeps users’ data close to them.
Impact & Results:
During the first months of the pilot, EU Voice has already attracted 20k followers. Organisations such as the European Commission or the European Court of Justice have joined the platforms, together with other EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies (EUIs).
The first phase of assessment is based on continuous reporting from various stakeholders (EUIs, developers, community). The EDPS organises regular meetings with other EU institutions in order to coordinate and assess periodically the impact of the new two platforms. By the end of 2022, the EDPS is expected to issue a report with a preliminary assessment on the first phase of the pilot project. The report will be presented at the Inter-Organisational Communication Committee, which is the decision-making committee of all EUI.

Project/Initiative Name: The Periodic Table of Open Data's Impact Factors
Description:
The Periodic Table of Open Data Elements details the enabling conditions and disabling factors that often determine the impact of open data initiatives. The elements are included in five central issue categories - problem and demand, definition, capacity and culture, partnerships, risks, governance - are likely to either enable or disrupt the success of open data projects when replicated across countries. 

The tool has a particular focus in developing economies acknowledging a short supply of evidence to work or release data. By defining specific issues around data, the tool intends to help the development of metrics of success and a strategy for monitoring progress against a well-defined baseline. The periodic table is part of a larger Open Data's Impact repository to provide actionable insights and assess the evidence around the use of open data. 

It has had pilots of use across several public institutions and cities.
Novelty:
Frames the use of open data from a problem-demand perspective.
Strong focus on capacity and cultural assessments as a precondition to the use of open data.

Project/Initiative Name: NOMAD - use your favorite travel-app to travel anywhere in the Nordics
Description:
The Nordic Open Mobility and Digitalization (NOMAD) aims to enable seamless mobility using several modes of transportation across the Nordic countries. It will introduce a market enabler framework of technology and business practices for Mobility as a Service (MaaS) and other smart mobility services. 

MaaS is a new type of mobility service that combines several modes of transportation into a single package to facilitate intermodal travel. MasS roaming allows for multimodal travel via a single interface and can be used for booking, planning and administration of international trips whilst helping travel providers to reduce expenditures and adapt operational goals related to travel according to the SDGs. These entail a closer and significant collaboration between technology and travel companies. 

The pilot is organized in seven working packages: 
WP1: Market Enabler Platform (Lead: ITS Nordics)
WP2: Implementation Guidelines for Standards (Lead: ITS Nordics)
WP3: Recommendations for Nordic datasets and APIs (Lead: ITS Nordics)
WP4: Qualification and Planning (Leads: RISE & TØI)
WP5: Mobility Service Roaming Pilot (Leads: TBA)
WP6: Knowledge Sharing and Dissemination (Lead: Capital Region of Denmark)
WP7: Project Management (Lead: ITS Norway)
Novelty:
Aims to develop an open, pan Nordic mobility system for MaaS solutions
It explores the concept of roaming as a means to inspire to service agreements between MaaS operators that will benefit individual travelers and organizations, and that will support parallel developments in open data standards within the Nordic region

Project/Initiative Name: CurieuzenAir - to measure disparity in air pollution levels across Brussels
Description:
CurieuzenAir is a citizen science project that measures the air quality in Brussels. Through a concept of citizens science, it is the largest project on air quality in the Belgium capital. For over a month, citizen scientists mapped the concentration of nitrogen dioxide (NO2) on the streets, which resulted in a dataset showing the impact of road traffic on air quality within the city scope. 

The data is of publicly access and, for example, reveals patters of air inequality across the capital region's districts, as well as provides a high-resolution scan of the air quality. The data was collected at 3,000 measurement locations with a quality control criteria of >83%. The data is aimed to have direct impact to improve models and better inform future policies to better environmental policies.
Novelty: Large scale initiative of citizens science

Project/Initiative Name: MAIIA - Automated Mapping of Informal Settlements with AI
Organisation: Inter American Development Bank - National Planning Department of Colombia
Description:
MAIIA is a joint project between the Colombian Government and the Inter American Development Bank that aims to incorporate AI as part of the Colombian urban planning process. Moreover, to generate information about informal housing in urban areas.

The model established a set of processing algorithms that analyzed aerial or statellite images to recognize typical features of districts containing informal settlements. The data for the training was high-resolution satellite images of the City of Barranquilla, together with precise information on the location and outline of the informal areas of the city. 

To facilitate its implementation, it is distributed via a pre-configured operating system image (via docker), along with scripts that allow training a detection model and applying it to new images in just two steps.

The algorithm produced twin maps of informal settlements to be compared against the original ones with a coincidence ratio >85%. The system can be used in other urban areas as it is made of open-source components easy to program and operate. MAIIA leverages open source technologies such as Satproc, Unetseg and Tensorflow.
Novelty: First AI system applied to urban management in Colombia

Description:
OpenCovid19 is a JOGL program that develops open-source and low-cost tools and methodologies that are safe and easy to use in response to the COVID-19 pandemic. The OpenCovid19 program is powered by a global community of 4000+ volunteers and experts who create solutions to better prevent, detect, and treat COVID-19, and to help forecast the pandemic’s evolution.
