It is organized in a set of challenges which address specific topics related to the coronavirus pandemic: treatment, detection, prevention, data analysis, among others. There are currently 122 projects, 174 needs and 1584 members participating around them.

The innovation is a platform for public service delivery through video calling with citizens. It was developed to secure the continuity in public service provision amidst the pandemic. It is a user-centric service with the main purpose of keeping eye contact as a critical factor in the service provision. In that sense, the video calling and the channel are made to secure that provision. 

The Digital Counter makes public-service delivery easy as a full-digital service. It holds many features such as web-based device independent, one-stop-shop for payment and document exchange, GDPR proof, interactive feedback exchange civil servant-client and subject to WCAG audit. On the first point, citizens can use whatever device they want as no software is required or has to be installed. Registration for an account is also not required. Moreover, any button within the process pipeline will automatically lead to the required action in the digital counter. 

The platform acknowledges 2 groups of users: the citizens of Rotterdam and city employees. Both groups are allowed to give feedback of any kind and share ideas to improve the platform's performance.
Novelty:
The platform has been developed with a very high standard regarding human-centricity. 
It's a fully-based web application, device and software independent.

Description:
The service repository is a platform for local authorities to help them assess and compare their performance with their peers across multiple variables and similar initiatives related to delivering digital government. Moreover, it provides information for decision-making through the sharing of experiences and peer-consulting. 

Once completed, the project will: 
- reinforce local-EU collaboration through the operationalisation of the Tallinn Declaration to fit the needs and challenges of the local government and citizens and the preparation of high-level policy briefs and events bringing together EU and local decision-makers,
- provide an online benchmarking dashboard to compare the performance of cities on user-centricity,
- support local authorities in delivering user-centricity through the provision of a support toolkit and mutual learning service for bench learning from others, and
- reach out to a large number of local entities in order to achieve a durable impact over time beyond the duration of the project.
Novelty: Provide a centralized and peer-led guidance on what they digital user-centric initiatives look like and how to better implement them.

Description:
The WDQMS integrates the data of all World Meteorological Information platforms (WIGOS) and observing components to provide high-quality observation data based on near-real-time monitoring information about weather predictions. It links availability and quality of surface-based observational data with past/current metadata and provides information on network/station issues for follow up. In addition, a monitoring module of the global climate observing system, and upper-air information is collected on monthly statistics. 

The near-real-time NWP monitoring of data availability and data quality is presented in three different formats: 6-hourly temporal aggregation centred on the main synoptic hours (00, 06, 12 and 18UTC); daily aggregation; monthly aggregation (not available yet). An extra level of aggregation (Alert) based on 5-day moving average across the WIGOS Monitoring Centres is available to warn staff at WMO Regional WIGOS Centres (RWC) for potential observational issues. 

The variables included are: daily mean surface pressure, daily mean air temperature, daily maximum air temperature, daily minimum air temperature, daily mean water vapour, total daily precipitation and total daily sunshine duration. They are constantly revised and assessed by a large community of practice through the WIGOS learning portal. 

The development of WIGOS is requirements-driven with a clear orientation to public health, disaster risk reduction, water resource management and food security, renewable energy, tourism, travel, insurance, to mention just a few, as an enabler for sustainable development.
Novelty: WIGOS focuses on enhancing governance and management functions, mechanisms and activities to be accomplished by contributing observing systems to satisfy, in a sustained and cost-effective manner, the evolving observing requirements of Members in delivering their weather, climate, water and related environmental services.

Description:
The Data Maturity Assessment Tool aims to provide a neutral platform to build capacity and access to specific resources that allows to assess the data maturity/readiness of an organization or initiative via a shared taxonomy of topics and areas of interest. 

The platform curates and categorizes a manageable set of resources to provide social impact organizations with a jumpstart on their data journeys. Some of the topics displayed include data analysis, governance, leadership and strategy. The knowledge is clustered in a series of consumable guides to, for example, assess on how to build a data stack.
Novelty: An assessment repository to provide organizations with a pulse check, helping them measure and understand where they stand today on their data journey.

Project/Initiative Name: Bing Maps - The Open Source Maps Projects
Description:
Bing Maps is an open source, collaborative experience to enhance the overall experience of discovering new sights and experiences. Explore and contribute to the open source maps and source code for many of the Bing Maps platforms’ APIs and SDKs alongside other developers on GitHub.

Open source maps projects require transparency to give developers and volunteers dynamic tools for making a real-world impact. In an effort to increase the coverage available OpenStreetMap and humanitarian efforts, the Bing Maps team has released millions of building footprints as open data that anyone can download free of charge.

The platform applies Deep Neural Networks and the ResNet34 with RefineNet up-sampling layers to detect building footprints from Bing imagery. Then noise is removed and suspicious data, as well as false positives from the predictions. Then, the system applies a polygonization algorithm to detect building edges and angles to create a proper building footprint. With the goal to increase the coverage of building footprint data available as open data for OpenStreetMap and humanitarian efforts, the platform has released millions of building footprints as open data available to download free of charge.

The COVID-19 Sounds App enables a large scale, crowdsourced data collection from healthy and non-healthy participants. The app collects short recordings of cough and breathing and report symptoms to help researchers to detect if a person is suffering from COVID-19. 

The records take a few seconds and also collects some basic demographics, the medical history data and one location sample. It also asks about positive status of COVID-19. The results go through an machine learning-based predictive model for respiratory health. 

So far >53 thousand audio samples and >550 hours in total were crowdsourced from >36 thousand participants. The dataset generated is the largest multi-modal dataset of COVID-19 respiratory sounds, which consists of three modalities including breathing, cough, and voice recordings. All the data is available upon request in formats of: 1) metadata csv files, 2) all audio samples, 3) subsets used for benchmarks, and 4) an excel sheet for data format introduction.
Novelty:
Largest multimodal sound-detector app for public health
Use of machine learning to understand behavior of a public health hazard

Project/Initiative Name: City of Rimini's Chatbot to support citizens find the right public services
Description:
The Rimini's Chatbot is an AI-based interactive system embedded into the Municipality's website. It is available 24/7 and supports citizens looking for information related to all the administrative services offered by the municipality. 

The bot is a chat window from where it is possible to talk with the digital assistant to obtain information and get assistance and support. The bot learning process is done by utilizing machine learning and deep learning algorithms to train the application of natural language processing. It follows the principles of conversational design to ensure an intuitive and natural interaction experience. This is a complementary channel besides traditional ones thus also lightening the workload of the operators. 

The digital assistant was tested following specific standard guidelines for evaluating the usability of conversational interfaces, involving citizens in usability test sessions. Moreover, citizens also provided information about the propensity to use the chat in the future for information and support by managing specific requests.
Impact & Results: In the first five months of activity, the Digital Assistant responded to over 50,000 messages sent by citizens of the Municipality of Rimini. There were about 15,000 conversations: 93% were managed independently by the robotic assistant without the need of a human operator intervention.

Project/Initiative Name: Explore.Porto - discover and explore the city through a digital platform
Description:
Explore.Porto is an innovative web-based and App created to provide visitors with an integrated, accessible, intuitive and real-time user experience to go around Porto. The process to access explore.Porto differentiates between two types of users: 1) visitors and citizens access the web/app and select points of interest, 2) partners register in the city's platform and create a point of interest or mobility entry. 

The platform/App integrates different types of dispersed tourist and mobility information. It start with the identification of a point of interest within the city and suggests the best path and way of mobility and the places of interest around. The platform breaks with the type of services designed in the light of existing compartmentalized structures to focus on the user and foster the adoption of digitally-based local services. 

From the platform it is possible to access the ROPI (Official Repository of Points of Interest) which is an open source database. When used for official product/service improvements, several departments such as the mobility or infrastructure one, as well as private taxi companies benefit from accessing to this information. The creation of an integration, conversion and interoperability API allows data from different sources to be integrated into the Explore.Porto.
Novelty: Web-based and App created to integrate tourist and mobility information to provide visitors with an integrated, accessible, intuitive and real-time user experience to go around Porto. Further, by bringing together public and private partners providing services of public interest, Explore.Porto disrupts the service design process.
Impact & Results: Explore.Porto has been generating positive effects in two main areas: tourism development and environmental sustainability. The 2021 results reveal a strong usage rate, especially considering the fact that it started in a completely organic way and only started to be released after the launch of the App in June 2021. More than 56,000 users with a return rate of more than 30%. Further, the centralization of information about soft mobility, public transport and taxis, in one platform, contributes positively to their promotion.

Project/Initiative Name: Recidiviz - Public dashboards and open source to drive better criminal justice
Description:
The set of dashboards and data solutions seek to 1) bring data from fragmented agency databases into one place - providing a real-time and end-to-end picture of the system, and 2) build simple tools that get decision makers data to accelerate earned release, prevent avoidable prison admissions, and others. 

Recidiviz approach is based on a constant 5-step iterative loop that meet the needs of both agency staff and communities impacted by the criminal justice system: 
1. Identify pain points and areas of opportunity for building a safer, smaller, more effective system
2. Design tools based on these specific needs, wrapped around existing workflows
3. Validate designs with agency users and justice-impacted community members
4. Track outcomes against goals to ensure effectiveness
5. Iterate designs based on user feedback and impact metrics

One of the main impacts of these tools are the reduction in incarceration with a focus on racial inequities. Moreover, to engage the justice and police system to specifically look at racial disparities and bias.
Novelty: Integrate data about incarcerated populations across seven states and creates dashboards and online tools to help assessing the procedures and the decision-making.

Description:
The Tennessee Arts Education Data Project creates the state’s first-ever online searchable database on Pre Kinder-12 student access to and participation in arts education. The purpose of the project is to empower school districts, educators, policymakers and funders with arts education data for decision-making in support of a well-rounded education.

Measuring access to and participation in arts education helps create understanding and awareness and is a starting point for fostering arts education as well as the right provision of skills. 

The platform contains four different dashboards: 1) welcome and state overview, 2) discipline dashboard, 3) district/school dashboard, 4) year over year dashboard. They use aggregated and longitudinal data for the measurement and analysis.
Novelty: First online database on PK-12 student access to and participation in arts education. Provides assessment for Arts education policymaking.

Description:
IMMS (Intelligent Material Management System) is an intelligent material management system for the entire Helsinki's library collection of approximately 1.8 million items. 

An intelligent material management system was acquired as the library wanted to enable moving the material freely between different libraries (from the old towards the new one). 

The aim is to distribute the new material equally so that no library is in an unequal position based on its size. Thus the customers of different libraries can be served in an equal manner. The service enables tracking and tracing of the item movements. The material can be located in real-time using RFID tags attached to the material. The material to be borrowed from the Helsinki City Library was labelled with RFID tags before the system was introduced. RFID tags are also used in transport units, check-out machines sorting points, and in special shelf identification.

The service includes a mobile application used by library staff. With the help of the mobile application, the material can be transferred to the central sorter or to the library shelves, either box at a time or, if necessary, item at a time.
Novelty: First state library in Finland that implements IMMS to provide equal services to citizens/users across its different venues.

Project/Initiative Name: Obotti - Central Library Oodi’s recommendation chatbot
Description:
Obotti is Central Library Oodi’s recommendation chatbot. The service recommends books from Oodi’s selection according to the customer’s interest and feedback. The service can be downloaded as a mobile application. 

The service recommends books to the customer based on contextual analysis that the customer would not otherwise find. The purpose of the service is to present the material available in the library to customers interestingly and to make it easier for customers to find interesting and new material. 

The service is divided thematically into six chatbots, each recommending content according to their theme. Users can ask the service for recommendations using ready-made answer options, or in free search terms in the form of text or speech. The service utilises contextual analysis of natural language to determine the customer’s interests, as well as to produce corresponding book recommendations.
Novelty: AI-based chatbot that recommends books and content based on contextual analysis

Project/Initiative Name: 9Rs Framework: Establishing the Business Case for Data Collaboration and Re-Using Data in the Public Interest
Description:
The 9R Framework is a compiled method for describing and identifying the business case for data reuse for the public good. It consists of nine motivations identified through data collaboratives and categorized by different types of return on investment: license to operate, brand equity, or knowledge and insights. Considered together, these nine motivations add up to a model to help organizations understand the business value of making their data assets accessible. 

The nine motivations are clustered on three incentives: 
ROI Case: Knowledge and Insights: Ways in which data reuse improves an organization’s ability to pursue larger strategic goals.
ROI Case: Brand Equity: Ways in which data reuse promotes an organization’s image for internal and external stakeholders.
ROI Case: License to Operate:Ways in which data reuse strengthens the “social license” for an organization to operate.

This framework is useful not just for describing the rationales why organizations have made certain data assets re-usable but also for helping organizations articulate their motivations and build internal buy-in for data reuse.
Novelty: Guidance for companies (and overall institutions) on make their rationales why they have made certain data assets reusable.

Project/Initiative Name: Panelot - not-for-profit system for randomly and fairly selecting citizens’ panels
Description:
Panelot's goal is to facilitate the selection of citizens' panels, which bring together randomly selected people to discuss key questions and deliver policy recommendations. These panels should be representative of the population in terms of features like gender, age, ethnicity, and education. At the same time, Panelot aims to select a panel in a way that gives all volunteers a fair chance to participate.

Panelot receives as input the composition of the pool of volunteers, the desired panel size, and quotas meant to ensure a representative outcome (i.e: in a panel of 100 people the number of women might be required to be between 47 and 53). Panelot outputs a list of quota-compliant panels, each with an assigned probability. The list of panels and their probabilities are chosen to be leximin optimal: the output first maximizes the selection probability of any volunteer, then maximizes the second lowest selection probability, then maximizes the third lowest, and so on. 

Since the number of quota-compliant panels is astronomically large, this optimization problem is extremely difficult and requires finding the right panels to make selection probabilities fair. Panelot solves it through state-of-the-art optimization techniques. The algorithms choose representative panels and has already been used to select >40 citizens assemblies. It is adapted by settling the algorithm over a benchmark representing the previous state of the art which leads to substantially fairer selection probabilities.
Novelty: Fairer and data-driven way of selecting citizens' panels to deliver policy recommendations.

Project/Initiative Name: PrivaSeer - an evolving privacy policy search engine
Description:
PrivaSeer aims to make privacy policies transparent, discoverable and searchable. Various faceted search features aim to help users get novel insights into the nature of privacy policies. It can be used to search for privacy policy text or URLs.

PrivaSeer currently has over 1.4 million privacy policies indexed and its always looking to add more. The platform crawled privacy policies based on URLs obtained from Common Crawl and the Free Company Dataset. The PrivaSeer corpus is a collection of 1,005,380 privacy policies described in the following paper

Project/Initiative Name: Trade and Technology Council consultation platform
Description:
This is a web-based and virtual space for businesses, public authorities, innovators, researchers, civil society, and policymakers to shape the EU and US discussions in the Trade and Technology Council together.

The Trade and Technology Council was set up at the June 2021 EU-US summit and serves as a forum for the European Union and the United States to coordinate approaches to key global trade, economic, and technology issues and to deepen transatlantic trade and economic relations based on shared democratic values. 

Participants can browse through the Community website or voice their ideas by posting a position paper in one of the 10 online working groups.
Novelty: Consultative and crowdsourced platform to provide inputs and feedback to trade and technology related topics.

Description:
GOcité is a municipal infrastructure management software package designed by and for cities. Once collected, this data is used for numerous applications such as geospatial location of infrastructures, display of geographical information on different reference frames and creation of synthetic or thematic maps.

The GOcité technology is the result of collaboration between five Quebec municipalities - Sherbrooke, Longueuil, Victoriaville, Thetford Mines and Saint-Eustache - and the City of Montreal's Saint-Laurent borough. By pooling their expertise in municipal geomatics, they developed, more than 25 years ago, the first instance of a geographic information system (GIS) that met their needs and allowed them to achieve significant savings. Today, GOcité has 25 municipal partners and uses the latest Esri technologies

GOcité offers a standardised, proven and scalable data model that integrates seamlessly. It supports industry-standard database management systems, including ORACLE and SQL Server, Windows operating systems, and Windows and Solaris server components.
Novelty: Co-designed software to serve cities in Quebec on numerous applications using geospatial location of infrastructures.

Description:
CANN Forecast utilizes AI for water to help preserve the resource in the Quebec Region. It develops decision-making tools that enable municipalities to protect public health, reduce operating costs and become more resilient. They are:

- InfoBaignade: InfoBaignade is an innovative predictive model based on an AI algorithm to reliably estimate the concentration of E. coli in rivers.
- InfoBris: InfoBris is an AI-based solution capable of identifying the segments of aqueducts most vulnerable to breakage.

The information is provided through SMS alerts when established criteria of measurements in water are exceeded and when normalcy is restored. Additionally the cities can visualize the results on a tailored dashboard with hourly predictions and forecasts for the next 3 days.
Novelty: AI-based models applied to water management in cities.

Project/Initiative Name: Add-In ILOSTAT to support research on labor and COVID-19
Description:
The newly updated ILOSTAT add-in provides free access to data series covering a wide range of labour topics (e.g., employment, unemployment, industrial relations, informality) directly via Microsoft Excel. The version includes COVID-19 data from all over the world. 

By installing this add-in to significantly reduce the amount of time researchers spend downloading ILOSTAT data.
Novelty: Incorporation of COVID-19 data and statistics into labor evidence to support country members.

Project/Initiative Name: Stall Catchers to advance Alzheimer’s solutions
Description:
Stall Catchers is an online game that anyone can play - no experience necessary. The users look at movies from the brains of mice and try to identify vessels as flowing or stalled. This helps to speed up Alzheimer's disease research at Cornell University. It was built on one of the oldest volunteer thinking projects: stardust@home, and is using the Virtual Microscope developed by their team.

Large part of our community includes middle school students, seniors, caregivers and Alzheimer’s patients themselves. Nowadays not even the most powerful computer technologies can’t do this accurately enough yet, but the users eyes can help process decades worth of data. The idea is to help the medical community fully understand how stalls are contributing to Alzheimer’s and discover potential treatment targets.
Novelty: Web and App-based crowdsourced innovation to detect "and catch" stall inside of the brain to help advance in the fight against Alzheimer.
Impact & Results: Since its inception in 2016, 45,000 global volunteers have contributed 13.5 million annotations accomplishing two decades of data analysis in 4 years while producing expert-like data analysis and supporting several discoveries reported in top tier journals, including the identification of a therapeutic agent that restores memories in transgenic Alzheimer's mice.

Description:
CS Track seeks to broaden the knowledge about Citizen Science and the impact their activities can have. The initiative investigates a large diverse set of CS activities, disseminating good practices and formulating knowledge-based policy recommendations. It partners 9 organizations from the academia and cities across Europe. 

CS Track combines methods of social sciences, the humanities and computer sciences. It (1) reviews literature on citizen science – by scientists, policy makers, funders and interest groups, among others – and conducts expert interviews on open issues, (2) compiles a database of citizen science projects and conducts semi-structured interviews to explore projects that have been selected by drawing lot, (3) analyzes digital manifestations and traces of citizen science projects, and (4) conducts online surveys.
Novelty: The work done in this project so far should be seen as pioneering in its efforts to use mixed analytic and social science methods along with the triangulation of results from different sources, to achieve a broad picture of social phenomena related to the case of citizen science. Future work is expected to concentrate on refining the measurement and evaluation scheme for CS and summarize results of our analysis to provide further recommendations for best practices and policies for the CS community.
Impact & Results: Ultimately, using mixed methods of analytics and social science provides methodological richness, allowing for the triangulation of data. That is a systematic comparison of data obtained from different sources and research perspectives which provides a coherent, validated, and holistic perspective of the CS landscape. This approach allows us to deepen our understanding about the main topics and concepts which are communicated over the web and characterize CS and the different ways it is perceived and approached by people who lead or take part in CS activities.

Project/Initiative Name: AFYANET to leverage crowdsourced health data for disease surveillance and forecasting
Description:
Afyanet is a voluntary, non-profit network of National Health Institutes and Research Centers across Africa seeking to leverage crowdsourced health data for disease surveillance and forecasting. The aim is to use technology and digital solutions to radically enhance how traditional disease surveillance systems function and the ways we can model epidemics.

Its vision is to create a common framework to collect standardized real-time data from the general population, allowing countries to leapfrog existing hurdles in disease surveillance and information sharing.  

DPS is a proven methodology for gathering health-related information directly from citizens using widespread mobile technology. It relies on citizens regularly self-reporting their health status and symptoms through online surveys, which can be calibrated to a range of illnesses. With the information it generates an integrated early warning system that turns the crowdsourced data into valuable disease spread models.
Novelty: Non-profit network of African National Health Institutes to leverage crowdsources health data.

Description:
WanaData is a Pan-African network of female journalists, data scientists and techies working on changing the digital media landscape by producing and promoting data-driven news while applying digital technologies in their storytelling. 

It is an initiative of the pan-African federation Code for Africa which is an impact accelerator, and a collaborative pan-African federation of civic data and civic technology organizations. The platform uses technology and data to support the building of digital democracies, and to empower citizens - principally women - with actionable information. It has a strong journalism focus, believing that media is a powerful information and mobilization agent.
Novelty: Network of female African journalists and technologists that produce and promote data-driven news.

Project/Initiative Name: Takwimu - a resource for African CSOs and their development partners
Description:
Takwimu.africa supports and enables African human development policy. It is an Africa Practice initiative, originally launched in 2018 in partnership with Code for Africa and iHub. With this 2021 relaunch, Takwimu is focussing on the critical capacity gap facing African civil society organizations and their development partners in making use of available data throughout the policy life cycle. The site offers a practical data visualisation and engagement toolkit including:
 
The Policy space: This resource catalogues the institutions that are directly involved in or influential on decision making in each country - and the policies, laws and active spending programmes that serve as the formal expression of these choices.  
Process indicators: a simple means to visualise and compare national political and institutional indicators that shed light on capacities and opportunities for effective policy engagement. 
Data explorer: a means to chart and compare comprehensive African SDG indicators overlaid on sector by sector development budget data.
Partner Programme: all charts and infographics are freely downloadable in a range of formats. All the data utilised is freely available via the Takwimu API and the website itself has been built for modular re-use to support the visualisation and publication of data-driven content across multiple different policy contexts and data sources.
Novelty: Platform focussing on the critical capacity gap facing African civil society organizations and their development partners in making use of available data throughout the policy life cycle.

Description:
PesaCheck is a pioneering verification initiative that is kickstarting fact-checking across Africa. Initially focused on verifying the financial and other statistical numbers quoted by public figures in Kenya, Tanzania and Uganda, PesaCheck is now Africa’s largest fact-checking platform in Africa. 

PesaCheck fact-checks in two international languages (English and French), as well as major African languages such as Kiswahili and Amharic. Its network helps track political promises by politicians (through the Wajibisha/PromiseTracker toolkit), helps unpack budget and census data (through our PesaYetu and TaxClock platforms), and builds machine learning/artificial intelligence tools (such as DebunkBot) to help automate verification. 

PesaCheck also helps watchdog media and NGOs establish their own standalone fact-check teams, and works with universities across the continent to train a new generation of civic watchdogs. PesaCheck is an initiative of Code for Africa (CfA) and was established in 2016 with an innovateAFRICA fund award.
Novelty: SP: interesting. Note that there is also PesaYetu entry (resulting from the submission on OPSI of Code for Africa)

Project/Initiative Name: Bonga na Mzalendo & Dokesa - tools to monitor Parliamentary law making process
Description:
Both innovations aim to facilitate public participation in Kenya's Parliamentary processes through information. Dokesa (Share your Idea), specifically, aims to facilitate and enhance public participation in lawmaking by reviewing laws and providing feedback/ideas, while Bonga na Mzalendo is a digital platform that asks a Parliamentary/law-related question every week and collects citizens SMS as responses. 

Both projects believe that following and tracking Parliamentary proceedings are essential for development, so citizens should be able to participate and respond (live) to new bills or laws. In that sense, the innovation's main aim is to bridge the public participation gap by using online platforms.
Novelty:
Digital mechanism for citizens involvement in law reviewing and law making. 
Crowdsourcing of opinions through SMS on specific Parliamentary proceedings and laws.

Description:
SEMA is an initiative which collects citizens’ feedback on public institutions and services, and translates their responses into recommendations. SEMA provides citizens a platform where they can share their latest experience with a public institution anonymously, easily and free of charge. 

Its feedback tools include an IoT feedback device, a USSD code, a toll-free line, telephone- and WhatsApp surveys and in-person surveys. They seek to translate citizens needs into recommendations for improved service delivery. They analyse citizen feedback and provide insightful, actionable and office-specific data reports to public partners to facilitate improvement.
Novelty: IoT feedback platform and USSD code (in addition to multi channel digital and offline options) to collect citizens feedback for law and policy making in Uganda.

Description:
U-Report is UNICEF’s  free mobile empowerment platform. It gives over 10 million young people in 68 countries opinion on certain challenges that affect them (i.e., climate crisis at the local level, youth employment). It also shares vital information in emergencies and across all areas that impact on young people’s lives. Furthermore, through communication channels such as SMS, Viber, Facebook Messenger, and WhatsApp users can ask U-Report questions about crisis management such as the Corona Virus and receive preprogrammed answers from experts on the matter. 

The U-Report COVID-19 newly incorporated AI-based chatbot aims to assess needs, tackle misinformation, and in partnership with governments share reliable information on where communities can seek assistance. It was initially intended to be a support tool for the U-Report programme and its 68 active countries.
Novelty: Incorporation of chatbot and the use of multiple social media and communication tools to receive feedback from youth populations in >69 countries.

Organisation: Stanford University - Center for AI in medicine and imaging
Description:
The beta platform is conceived as an open science medical imaging data community. By facilitating a collection of de-identified annotated medical imaging data, it seeks to foster transparent and reproducible collaborative research based on AI and machine learning systems to benefit patients. The dataset clusters five main categories: bone, chest and extremity, body and head/brain/neck. 

The innovation harmonizes de-identified annotated medical imaging data across organizations to foster transparent and reproducible collaborative research. The ultimate goal is to complete a petabyte-scale searchable repository of annotated de-identified medical images (radiology, echocardiography, ophthalmology, dermatology, pathology, and more) that are annotated and, eventually, linked to genomic data and electronic medical record information, for use in rapid creation of computer vision systems.
Novelty: The innovation harmonizes de-identified annotated medical imaging data across organizations by using AI and machine learning systems to complete a petabyte-scale searchable repository of annotated de-identified medical images.

Project/Initiative Name: Newronika - Restoring Brain and Body functions with Neural Device
Description:
Newronika's recent innovation to tackle Parkinson is based on implanting an AlphaDBS adaptive closed-loop deep brain stimulation (aDBS) system. Closed-loop stimulation is the most important advancement in the DBS field to date. Adaptive stimulation and closing the loop is a patient-centered approach to treating Parkinson's disease. 

The stimulation system continuously records and interprets bioelectrical neuronal activity in the brain where stimulation is delivered. The proprietary sensing technology records noise-free local field potentials while electrical stimulation is delivered and adapts the amount of electrical current delivered to meet the individual needs of each patient at any given moment.

Real time sensing and recording will enable Newronika to rapidly build a large dataset of bioelectrical signals, the Newronika WebBioBank. Data will be uploaded through an App connected to the implanted device via Bluetooth. The unique web platform is a bridge between patients and neurologists. The technology opens new avenues of research to develop new indications and explore new opportunities.

The benefits of remote DBS programming are clear: practical advantages for patients such as reductions in travel and cost to clinic visits, improved access for patients in rural locations. Additional studies have focused on the advantages of remote DBS programming during the COVID-19 pandemic, and have found that patients were satisfied with the telemedicine approach and there were no significant adverse events, such as loss of network connections or other software malfunctions.
Novelty: Newronika has initiated a multi-center clinical trial to evaluate the AlphaDBS adaptive closed-loop system in patients with Parkinson's disease. The first implant was performed at Department of Neurosurgery, Maria Sklodowska-Curie National Research Institute of Oncology, Warsaw, Poland by Professor Tomasz Mandat.

Project/Initiative Name: Find Food Support - search for food banks and food pantries in the USA
Description:
Google and Food for Good Team have just launched a new platform and App that aggregates >90 thousand locations offering free food support. This includes a food locator tool powered by Google Maps which people can use to search for their nearest food bank, food pantry, or school lunch program pickup site in their community. 

The innovation's mission is to “create a smarter food system,” which includes standardizing data to improve communication between food distributors to curb food waste. Additionally, Google is publishing five new videos on YouTube aimed at de-stigmatizing food insecurity. The site will also include links to food support hotlines, state-by-state benefit guides, and information for specific communities, such as seniors, families and children, and military families. Visitors can also find information about how you can donate food, time and money to support those in need.
Novelty: Address food waste and enable food security by standardizing data to improve communication between food distributors to curb food waste
Impact & Results: The platform/App aggregates >90 thousand locations offering free food support. It also aggregates data from food banks & pantries, SNAP, EBT, and private and small groceries.

Organisation: Amsterdam City, Utrecht City, Eindhoven City, Rotterdam City and The Hague City
Description:
The CDS-M aims to set out how data interchange between mobility providers and public authorities should take place, and based on what standards. It will be used in partnerships between mobility providers and municipalities (the Netherlands Big 5). The G5 cities have developed an initial version to mature CDS-M.

A single CDS-M will give public authorities more information and ways of planning the use of public spaces, and additional information for developing and improving policy. The most important element in the CDS-M is the Application Programming Interface (API), an application written in a coding language that any database can understand. The API thus acts as a ‘plug’ between two systems, enabling standardized data interchange at all times.
Novelty: Integrative data that will provide a better understanding of the use of shared mobility and the possible effects on public spaces, thus to develop and adjust and improve current policies and regulations.
Impact & Results: A single CDS-M will increase the effectiveness of collaboration between mobility providers and public authorities: 1) a uniform standard will make it clear how the data should be processed so as to be GDPR-proof and what type of information should be supplied to which public authorities. 2) it will make it easier for mobility providers to roll out their services in different areas. 3) the public authorities and mobility providers can work together on the CDS-M design and agreement.

Description:
Sinking Tax Base quantifies the impact of sea level rise on county tax bases as property lines move inland. The platform has created >250 individual county-level reports providing data on the acreage, improvements, and number of parcels potentially affected by rising seas.

The analysis takes into account the state-specific boundary definitions, allowing for a clearer assessment of the potential loss of private (taxable) property in the coastal U.S. Climate Central generated this report also at the national-level report. 

These reports are based, principally, on three main data products: 1) sea level rise and coastal flood layers map - a Global GIS illustrating the extent of sea level rise and coastal flooding, 2) portfolio analysis tool - a web service to quantify coastal flood risk to analyze >10 thousand locations at once, 3) CoastalDEM - proprietary digital evaluation model for coastal areas to reveal assets at risk from flooding.
Impact & Results:
More than 250 county-specific reports are available.
The analysis indicates as many as 649,000 parcels with a hydrological pathway to the ocean may have some elevation below the tidal boundary level by 2050, relative to 2000. More than 48,000 parcels may be entirely below the tidal boundary level by 2050, with greatest exposure for properties in Florida, Louisiana, and Texas.

Description:
Metroverse is an urban economy navigator. It is based on over a decade of research on how economies grow and diversify and offers brand new insights on a city's growth prospects by placing its existing technological capabilities and knowhow at the heart of how diversification unfolds. Metroverse was built using a user-centered design process to help city leaders, policymakers and researchers grapple with 21st-century urbanization challenges.

Metroverse delivers new insights on these questions by placing a city’s technological capabilities and knowhow at the heart of its growth prospects, where the range and nature of existing capabilities strongly influences how future diversification unfolds. Metroverse makes visible what a city is good at today to help understand what it can become tomorrow. It is a prototype tool that gathers data visualizations, Harvard's scientific research, and high-resolution datasets for over 1000 cities worldwide.

Under four core concepts (Metroverse Concepts), the tool assesses not only the capabilities and potential of cities, but also the readiness of a city to provide economic growth and associated wellbeing. These concepts are: 1) relative presence - a measure of whether an industry accounts for a larger or a smaller share of overall employment, 2) knowledge cluster - a set of industries grouped by their technological relatedness, 3) Industry space - a data visualization that depicts the technological relatedness between industries and in which of these industries a selected city has an outsized presence, 4) city similarity - a set of cities that are similar to the selected city.
Novelty: Metroverse presents a pioneering advance in urban analysis. For the first time, cities can visualize their capabilities and growth challenges and prospects alongside cutting-edge research on how to drive prosperity. Metroverse provides city-specific data visualizations, powerful comparators and actionable insights, in a user-friendly narrative format, to unleash new understandings on urban prosperity.
Impact & Results:
Since its launch in 2021, the Metroverse prototype has seen over 35,000 users across 160 countries. Between December 2021 to March 2022, the number of new users increased by 60% compared to the previous 3-month period. For example, a group of policy researchers has applied Metroverse to their project in South Africa, South Africa, where they worked to improve the prosperity of cities.

We continue engaging with end-users through presentations, demo sessions, and interviews. This enables us to collect feedback and use cases to evaluate our processes and learn for future development.

Description:
The DigiProfileTest helps people understand the fundamentals of the data economy and the opportunities to protect their data. The test also helps people recognise and assess their behaviour on the internet and offers practical tips for digital everyday life. For example, according to the digiprofile test, those under the age of 20 have the least understanding of data economy mechanisms and the weakest data protection ability among all age groups.

The questions will focus on three dimensions of digital life: your knowledge, attitude and actions. The analysis of these dimensions aim to restore confidence in digital services. A fair data economy means consumers’ increasing control over who collects their personal data, and when and how the data is used and for what purposes. In practice, this is shown by new, transparent services and the way in which digital services and products enhance our everyday lives.
Novelty: Self-assessment tool to evaluate the personal value of data privacy and one-self data.
Impact & Results: The test has been taken >30 thousand times

Description:
The Deterioration Index is poised to upend a key cultural practice in medicine: triage. The core assumption of the DI is that traditional triage can be augmented by machine learning and big data. 

The system will alert clinicians when a patient's main condition begins to deteriorate. The tool will enable staff to identify deterioration sooner and has the potential to prevent adverse outcomes and even death. Patients who experience certain medical situations, such as a rapid response or a code blue, have a significantly increased risk of mortality. Often, these patients exhibit subtle signs of clinical deterioration up to 24 hours prior to the need for intensive intervention. Early detection of clinical deterioration using predictive analytics, coupled with active clinical decision support, may increase early clinical evaluation and treatment, improving quality and patient safety. 

The model evaluates 125 data points, including demographic information, vital signs, lab results, and nursing assessments to calculate the score. When a patient’s deterioration index score reaches 60, the primary nurse will receive a Rover notification and a Best Practice Advisory in Epic Hyperspace. At a score of 60, a patient has an 80% chance of experiencing an adverse event and may need early intervention.
Novelty: The Deterioration Index is an AI-based and machine learning model using big data to support/improve triage and the likelihood that patients increased risk would lead to serious deterioration or even dead.
Impact & Results: More than 50 health systems already use the Deterioration Index predictive model. Epic released an update to help them measure the model’s performance for patients with COVID-19, and now 21 organizations including Parkview Health, ProMedica Health System, Confluence Health, and North Oaks Health System are using the model for more than 16,000 of these patients.

Description:
The Department of Neighborhood Empowerment has considered data as an asset for community efforts and therefore developed useful resources and tools to help community leaders in their advocacy strategy and their decision-making process. 

EmpowerLA is a one-stop-shop platform that gathers all the maps, applications, dashboard, and other data-related links that will help authorities of the different counties and the citizens to better understand the different faces of the LA community. 

For example, LA GeoHub was incorporated and improved under this process (https://geohub.lacity.org/), which now allows for not only exploring, but also for visualizing and downloading location-based Open Data in layers. On the EmpowerLA data platform it is also possible to get direct access to the Neighborhood Council elections comparable tool, or the community impact statements dashboard.
Novelty: LA's first one-stop-shop for data and all the visualizations that are publicly available and open source.

Description:
The New York City Street Tree Map is the world’s most accurate and detailed map of a city’s street trees. Through this platform, citizens can explore NY city’s urban forest, mark trees as favorites and be informed about the impact of trees in their communities. 

The platform uses data from the Tree Census and it allows to explore the city's urban forest, learn about the species, since and ecological benefits of individual trees, as well as record a tree care activity and stewardship actions. The TreesCount mapping methodology accurately positions trees along each block edge so that tree spacing, size, and other variables including road width can be analyzed to measure how impactful trees are on the block.

Trees are categorized and each have a tag or ID that ponders its impact over the ecosystem as "ecological benefits". This information is clustered in five indicators: 1) stormwater intercepted, 2) energy conservation, 3) air pollutants removed, 4) carbon dioxide reduced, 5) total value of annual benefits. These data is then aggregated up to borough and NYC level.
Novelty: For the first time, you have access to information about every street tree in New York City. Learn about the trees that make up our city’s urban forest, mark trees as favorites and share them with your friends, and record and share all of your caretaking and tree stewardship activities.
Impact & Results:
- Number of Trees. Surveyors mapped 666,134 street trees on 131,488 blocks in New York City, walking a total of 11,093 miles.
- Number of Volunteers. The 2,241 volunteers is double the number that participated in 2006. Volunteers completed 34 percent of the census.
- Innovative Mapping Technology. The use of innovative geospatial technology and a strong quality review process has yielded an exceptionally accurate inventory of street trees.
- Data-driven Urban Tree Management. This map and associated data is the backbone of Parks’ internal forestry management database, and supports short- and long-term agency operations.

Also some calculations about their impact: 
- Stormwater intercepted each year: 1,053,675,413 gallons at a value of: $10,431,381.32
- energy conserved each year: 649,263,466 kWh at a value of: $81,966,190.34
- Air pollutants removed each year: 1,231,548 pounds with a value of: $6,439,369.77
- CO2 reduced each year: 1,177,000,498 tons at a value of: $3,931,153.41
- Total value of annual benefits: $102,768,094.84

Project/Initiative Name: Odour Collect Map to address odor pollution
Description:
D-NOSES map provides a collection of maps to enable citizens to share anything related to odor pollution, from mapping affected communities to plotting regulations specifically related to odor pollution. The innovation addresses the fact that odor pollution has been repeatedly ignored in policy agendas, therefore resulting in harmful socio-environmental conflicts within the impacted communities. 

The Citizen Science approach to monitoring odor brings together crowdsourced information from the citizens through recording the frequency, intensity and type of oros they experience with individual observations using technology on site when available. This approach also support the co-creation of local improvements together with the local authorities and the industrial sector. The platform uses real-time data and a multi-level governance model to map and manage the information.
Novelty: First odor mapping platform incorporating crowdsourced citizens science and technology.
Impact & Results:
Identified that environmental complaints linked to odor are ~30% with limited information about it 
Determined that only 28 countries have some regulation to address the issue. Europe has 2 standardized measurement tools
From the mapping tool it is possible to see >50 cities around the world already using the App/platform to report odor pollution.

Project/Initiative Name: Guiding Principles for Post-Pandemic Court Technology
Description:
The Guiding Principles was drafted and enacted by the Post-Pandemic Planning Technology Working Group of the Conference of Chief Justices/Conference of State Court Administrators. There they recommend key principles as to deliver justice proceedings by using technology: 

1. Ensure principles of due process, procedural fairness, transparency, and equal access are satisfied when adopting new technologies. 
2. Focus on the user experience. 
3. Prioritize court-user driven technology. 
4. Embrace flexibility and willingness to adapt. 
5. Adopt remote-first (or at least remote-friendly) planning, where practicable, to move court processes forward.
6. Take an open, data-driven, and transparent approach to implementing and maintaining court processes and supporting technologies. 

At the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic, the NCSC allowed courts from across the country to participate in a real-time national learning community. State courts are emerging from the pandemic facing huge opportunities to innovate in the delivery “Virtual justice” (the preferred term) from an emergency response to a day-to-day situation
Novelty: The principles aim to build common ground for the implementation of digital and virtual technologies in the provision of justice and court services across the USA.
Impact & Results:
Different impacts across USA:
- 15 minute divorce procedures by doing them online
- >60 users of Michigan's virtual court channels
- Texas handling >1.1 million procedures virtually

Description:
OpenLandMap (2020) is an independent not-for-profit research initiative promoting open source and open data collaborative solutions for monitoring essential biodiversity variables at scale through open geospatial data. Furthermore, it provides training and receive collaborations as training data and map layers to enrich its platform. 

The innovation aims at accelerating the uptake of key environmental data by a diverse community of users, including landholders and citizens. The OpenLandMap is a system for global soil and vegetation mapping using legacy field observations and state-of-the-art Machine learning. Old legacy data is used to produce information in almost real time and without new significative investments. The system enables the transfer of technology, software & algorithms from data-rich countries to data-poor ones.
Impact & Results:
>20,000 citations 
>75,000 stacked geospatial layers 
0.5 petabytes of data

Description:
The Healthy City Dashboard is built over 12 goals clustered in 23 indicators that show inequities in the social and economic factors that influence Vancouver's wellbeing. These social determinants of health are considered to affect all the citizens individually, in the community and the environment. 

These are: 1) a good start, 2) a home for everyone, 3) feeding ourselves well, 4) healthy human services, 5) making ends meet and working well, 6) being and feeling safe and included, 7) cultivating connections, 8) active living and getting out, 9) lifelong learning, 10) expressing ourselves, 11) getting around, 12) environments to thrive in.

The platform is displayed as part of the one-stop-shop dashboard system of the city of Vancouver. On each goal it is possible to visualize the indicator with a specific metric, as well as the city's desired target and trend for the given year. By clicking on the indicator, the citizen will access to the specific data visualization and raw data, as well as the owner of such indicator. All data sources and datasets are publicly available.
Novelty: Data dashboard that incorporates a strategic approach of the city towards wellbeing with publicly available information.
Impact & Results: Difficult to tell directly from the dashboard, however since it is possible to monitor the progress of every indicator and to make the owner institution accountable, evidence of improvement or downgrading conditions are the main evidence of impact and results.

Description:
The MCC platform applies state-of-the-art data science methods to create an algorithm that recommends job transitions and retraining to workers, with a focus on supporting those at high risk of automation. The algorithm works by measuring the similarity between over 1,600 jobs, displayed in an interactive map based on the skills and tasks that make up each role. The closer two occupations are to each other, the greater the overlap in their skills and work activities.

In the map, similar occupations are placed close to each other, showing how a worker's skills and experience can be transferred to a range of nearby jobs. These jobs can then be compared along different dimensions, such as their average earnings, risk of automation and potential exposure to COVID-19.

Using an open source code and algorithm, the platform overlaps on job similarities on the map, which reflects potential exposure to automation risk by similar work activities. These estimates of risk are derived from the results of a study by Brynjolfsson, Mitchell and Rock (2018), in which the authors used crowdsourcing to rate thousands of tasks on their suitability for machine learning. NESTA translated these estimates to a European set of occupations (by developing a crosswalk between the O*NET and ESCO frameworks).
Novelty: An algorithm that recommends job transitions and retraining to workers, with a focus on supporting those at high risk of automation by measuring the similarity between over 1,600 jobs.
Impact & Results:
- Providers of careers information, advice and guidance: Enriching the advice given to workers. The insights provide a standardised source of data that incorporates automation risk, and can be used to recommend career transitions and upskilling opportunities for workers in high-risk roles.
- Policy makers: Identifying and supporting at-risk workers. The insights can be used to identify the groups of workers who are most at risk of automation, and recommend specific skills that would help these groups to move into lower-risk roles.
- Employers: Redeploying and upskilling workers. The insights can support employers with identifying suitable job transitions and skills development opportunities that would lower the automation risk for their workers.

Description:
Grow Observatory is a Citizens' Observatory that uses crowdsourced data to collect and monitor data from the soil across urban and rural areas. GROW has used crowdsourced ground observations from low-cost sensors to validate soil moisture information from satellites, including the new generation of high-resolution satellites, Sentinel-1.

The project aims to gather data about the quality of the soil, and to inform and empower people (and their communities) to take action on soil degradation and potential, and about the effect of the climate crisis in Europe. GROW have placed >6 thousand sensors to feed soil moisture and quality data. So far the innovation has created >516 million data rows clustered in 11 datasets, which supported the creation and validation of 5 satellite-monitoring products.
Novelty:
GROW is the first continental-scale Citizens’ Observatory to monitor a key parameter for science, continuously over an extended period, and at an unmatched spatial density. GROW has used crowdsourced ground observations from low-cost sensors to validate soil moisture information from satellites, including the new generation of high-resolution satellites, Sentinel-1.

24 GROW communities in 13 European countries create an unprecedented network of 6,502 ground-based soil sensors and a dataset of 516M rows of soil data.
Impact & Results:
Our evaluation has revealed many insights, GROW has helped thousands of people to learn about and test regenerative food growing techniques. Farmers in the Canary Islands have reduced their use of water for irrigation by 30%. Our communities and data services are continuing after the current funding period is ended.

- 24 communities and sensor clusters in 13 EU countries
- >20 thousand people engaged in citizen science or data collection
- >7 million people reached
- 165 countries reached (including the 13 EU countries initially targeted)

Project/Initiative Name: BILL C-11 - Consumer Privacy Protection Act and the Personal Information and Data Protection Tribunal Act
Description:
An Act to enact the Consumer Privacy Protection Act and the Personal Information and Data Protection Tribunal Act and to make consequential and related amendments to other Acts.

Part 1 enacts the Consumer Privacy Protection Act to protect the personal information of individuals while recognizing the need of organizations to collect, use or disclose personal information in the course of commercial activities. In consequence, it repeals Part 1 of the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act and changes the short title of that Act to the Electronic Documents Act. It also makes consequential and related amendments to other Acts.

Part 2 enacts the Personal Information and Data Protection Tribunal Act, which establishes an administrative tribunal to hear appeals of certain decisions made by the Privacy Commissioner under the Consumer Privacy Protection Act and to impose penalties for the contravention of certain provisions of that Act. It also makes a related amendment to the Administrative Tribunals Support Service of Canada Act.
Novelty:
The Bill updates the Canadian data protection law. 
It lays the groundwork for the possibility of creating data trusts for private organizations to disclose de-identified data to specific public institutions for “socially beneficial purposes.”
Another instrument for privacy protective innovation is referred to as the “regulatory sandbox.”

Project/Initiative Name: Enslaved - Peoples of the Historical Slave Trade
Organisation: Mellon Foundation, Michigan State University, University of Maryland
Description:
Enslaved is a robust, open-source and big data-based architecture to discover, connect, and visualize >600,000 people records and 5 million data points regarding enslaved people. From archival fragments and spreadsheet entries, and using AI and machine learning algorithms it is possible to see the lives of the enslaved in richer detail. 

The primary focus of Enslaved.org is making visible the live of people who were enslaved, owned slaves, were connected to the slave trade, and worked to emancipate slaves. work with Linked Open Data and specifically Wikibase – the free database software from Wikimedia Germany. The database features 613,458 entries, and seeks to streamline the research process by linking dozens of complex datasets. If, for instance, a user searches for a woman whose transport to the Americas is documented in one database but whose later life is recorded in another, the portal can connect these details and synthesize them. 

As a big number, the database craft narratives about some 12,5 million enslaved Africans transported between the 16th and 19th centuries. The innovation showcases four main aspects: 
1. Stories: Present the variety and scope of the lives of the enslaved through interactive, narrative biographies, written for student and general audiences.
2. Linked Open Data (LOD): To accomplish the focus on people, it uses linked open data (LOD), i.e. structured data available under an open license (Wikibase) to interconnect individual projects and databases. A LOD-based approach facilitates federated searching and browsing across all linked project data on Enslaved.org.
3. Preservation and sustainability: it provides a mechanism for the preservation of datasets in the Harvard Dataverse or other appropriate repositories. All facets of the project are open source. 
4. Inclusion: it is building an inclusive community of researchers and scholarly communications informed and guided by the principles of an ethical, anti-racist approach to the peoples of the historical slave trade and their descendants.
Novelty: Algorithm that explore slavery records, datasets, pictures, and linked open data platform.
Impact & Results:
1,110,970 Total Records
667,624 People
425,469 Events 
15,047 Places 
2,830 Sources
https://enslaved.org/explore/visualizations/

Description:
Carnye is a GovTech startup that provides digital and data-driven solutions for all types of emergencies response. Their approach to technology is built on the cloud and integrating mission-critical centers to use an advance call management platform that unifies the flow of information.

Specifically on the case of APEX, it is a response integration and cloud-based solution that provides a one-screen interface to amplify contact centers' response capacity. APEX technology is fully i3 compliant (an open standard IP-based signaling and media for delivery of emergency calls). The innovation unified the flow of audio, video, instant messages, third party applications, IoT devices, and mobile devices with its system to disseminate their information in real-time from callers to call-takers to field-responders for decision-making. It reduces call-waiting time and allows for continue 24/7 operations from remote backup stations using VPN, with zero-dependency on the Centers' communication infrastructure. 

The platform is customized on demand-base and is resilient to infrastructure failures and most internet outages due to a robust could infrastructure. Both APEX and a similar system Native are available as open source API. They work with the private sector as well.
Novelty: APEX cloud-based platform was used to modernized 911 system by adding location data, chat and video to emergency calls.
Impact & Results: Ongoing modernization processes of several emergency response systems across the USA (911 and state/city level ones).

Description:
Orion is an open-source tool that uses machine learning and interactive data visualizations to spot gaps and opportunities in scientific research. It enables users to monitor progress in science, visually explore the scientific landscape, and search for relevant publications. The algorithm collects, enriches and analyzes scientific publications using a Microsoft-based Academic Graph. 

Users can leverage Orion’s views to interact with the data. The Exploration view shows all of the academic publications in a 3D visualization. Every particle is a paper and the distance between them signifies their semantic similarity; the closer two particles are, the more semantically similar. The Metrics view visualizes indicators of scientific progress and how they have changed over time for countries and thematic topics. The Search view enables the users to search for publications by submitting either a keyword or a longer query, for example, a sentence or a paragraph of a blog they read online.

Orion's views of results are interconnected. In that sense, users can identify an interesting country profile in the Metrics view, visualize its publications and filter them in the Exploration view and retrieve those papers and their metadata in the search view. 

- Measure progress in science: it creates indicators showing dimensions of scientific progress, like research diversity.
- ML-powered search: its semantic search engine enables queries with anything from a keyword or phrase to entire paragraphs of text.
- Flexibility: the algorithm indexes bioRxiv papers but it can be deployed for any field of study, journal or conference.
- Information-rich, domain-specific explorations: it combines data from a variety of sources and was designed for visual exploration and discovery.
Novelty: A machine learning algorithm and search engine that accelerates knowledge interlinkage to accelerate knowledge discovery and production.
Impact & Results: Potential to increase scientific production and to depth the co-production of "similar" knowledge.

Description:
Civy is a cloud-based mobile platform for civic engagement, including cyber security and cloud hosting. The platform crowdsourced data provides evidence for self-diagnosis which is designed to address cities' challenges in public engagement. Among its features features, the platform helps improve Opacity & Transparency, Ease & Efficiency, Inclusivity, and Data Privacy (i.e., by reducing legacy systems). 

Civy's vision is to not only increase engagement, but to also diversify the demographics that participates in civic discussion (young, middle, older, all income levels, & major US languages). The tool was created to bridge the communication gap between the cities and their residents. It is done in four different ways:  
- Mobile platform: Civy is a convenient mobile platform easily accessible from anywhere.
- Feedback from resident is directly sourced back to the project creator; for example, resident feedback for project created by Public Works is directed back to public works.
- Social media linkages: Civy links with social media platforms to increase participation rates and demographic depth.

The platform/App displays an interactive county or ZIP-based where citizens are able to visualize new and existing projects, as well as repairing events, disasters and more with a single click.
Novelty: The platform/App centralized information from cities activities related to the provision of certain services (i.e. repairing efforts, disaster management, project execution) and eases direct online interaction with the citizens.
Impact & Results: Several cities across the USA are using it.

Project/Initiative Name: Making Sense of the Future Toolbox
Organisation: Alexander von Humboldt Institute for Internet and Society gGmbH
Description:
Future(s) of the digital world. This toolbox aims to promote transdisciplinary, multi-perspective and creative (future) thinking. The six different exercises combine future studies methods with topics and materials around the issues of digitalisation and its impact on our society.

This toolbox provides lecturers and teachers in the public sector with an innovative and hands-on Open Educational Resource (OER) to train Futures Literacy in the field of digitalisation. It also enables a broad public to take part in discourses on the social impact of technology. The freely available teaching and learning materials aim to promote transdisciplinary, multi-perspective and creative (futures) thinking combining future studies methods with topics around digitalisation. 

"Making sense of the future” is an Open Educational Resource (OER) and toolbox that aims to promote transdisciplinary, multi-perspective and creative (futures) thinking. It is designed based on the idea of Futures Literacy. Borrowing from the concept of literacy, Futures Literacy is a skill that everyone can and should acquire. Through becoming “futures literate” we can learn to recognise patterns of past developments, change perspectives and uncover new options for action.

“Making sense of the Future” can be used in knowledge-based engagement with possible futures in schools, universities and organisations. It can also be used privately for education, inspiration or as intellectual training. In the educational context, the toolbox is suitable as creative content for seminars and teaching units. A combination of several exercises can also be used to create workshop formats. The toolbox is written and designed in an easy-to-understand language and complemented with illustrations.

The toolbox consists of 6 different exercises:
1) Answer by moving: show your stance and impact towards digital futures
2) Criticize your utopia: develop a realistic future
3) Transform future language: deconstruct and reconstruct metaphors
4) Act up for change: discover different dimensions for change
5) From weak signals to mega trends: explore signals of change
6) Newspaper from 2040: Think ahead and write an article from 2040
Novelty:
Using methods from future studies, the exercises encourage and promote future thinking and the development of future thinking skills. Combining the concept of Futures Literacy in the field of digitalisation with the format of Open Education Resources (OER) is a unique and innovative approach in the research and education sector. Especially the combination of complex research based topics and findings with easily accessible educational material in a new approach within the Humboldt Institute for Internet and Society (HIIG) besides its long tradition in knowledge transfer activities.

First impressions show that the approach meets established quality standards of civic education in Germany (especially “Beutelsbacher Konsens”) but also encourages new modes of learning. Therefore the collaboration of both institutions enabled this new and innovative project approach.
Impact & Results:
Toolbox to think of future(s) of the digital world.
Since its launch in late August, the toolbox has received positive feedback and requests from diverse multipliers and educators including public schools, universities and NGO’s. Further, several organisations developing course formats and workshops have reached out for an exchange and consultation.

Description:
Earth Species is an open-source collaborative and nonprofit dedicated to decoding non-human language. They believe that an understanding of non-human languages will change our ecological impact on this planet.

Based on a 2013 unsupervised algorithm (T.Mikolov, 2013) that represents words as points in a high-dimension space, where the distance and direction between points encodes the relationship between words. They are building data sets, benchmarks, and community for unsupervised translation of non-human language. For example, instead of using embedding alignment to translate between species, the algorithm use the same technique to translate between individuals in the same species. This allows to discover all the different ways of saying the same thing. This in turn informs about which part of the sound matters: that is, discover which part of the sound is true signal and which part is background carrier.

The roadmap of the project follows five steps: 1) extending unsupervised translation of audio to increasingly difficult human languages, 2) better unsupervised multilingual dictionary alignment, 3) abstracted meaning, 4) modality inference, 5) cross-modality translation.
Novelty: Set of machine learning algorithms to decode non-human language and improve the awareness on ecological topics related to the climate crisis and human - non-human beings interactions.
Impact & Results: To decode non-human communication using a form of artificial intelligence (AI) called machine learning, and make all the knowhow publicly available, thereby deepening our connection with other living species and helping to protect them.

Description:
Public bodies that serve the people of Wales are required to provide services in Welsh and English. Public bodies cannot, currently, provide digital mapping services in Welsh and official sources of Welsh names for places and features are far from comprehensive. 

Mapio Cymru is a project hosted by Data Orchard CIC that aims to highlight and solve the problem of the empty maps. The project provides a full set of mapping tiles covering Wales using only Welsh-language data (from OpenStreetMap and Wikidata) and works with public bodies to help them solve the problem of empty maps. The initiative worked with Transport for Wales to investigate mapping, geo-location and journey planning in Welsh.

Officially, Google Maps does not support the Welsh language. Because of this, a developer cannot ask for the data to be provided in Welsh. It makes it difficult to measure how good a map's coverage is. There is simply no "correct" data set to compare. In many ways this is one of the strengths of the Welsh language, which really is a living language, so things are called according to the usage of all the people who use the language.

The Welsh language map is based on OpenStreetMap and Wikidata databases edited by the community. The innovators suggest that these datasets are likely to remain part of the place-naming mix and feature, at least, commercial competitors.
Novelty: The initiative incorporates concepts of crowdsourced data (in Welsh language), algorithmic supervision and mapping data from Google to develop a more comprehensive map of the places and streets across Wales.
Impact & Results: it aims to "complete" the street and places map of Wales. This will not only allow for better mobility, but also thrive development of digital applications and technologies based on Welsh language.

Novelty:
The Global Registry of Fossil Fuels integrates an emissions model that converts fossil fuel production into their embedded CO2e, differentiated by country.

The emissions methodology is based on the range of constants provided by the IPCC process for combustion of fossil fuels, and for supply chain emissions on datasets from theInternational Energy Agency and Opgee for crude oil and gas, and the database of coal mines built by Global Energy Monitor.
Impact & Results:
The Global Registry of Fossil Fuels is designed to be a tool to support policymakers and investors in making sensible, 1.5°C-aligned decisions around future fossil fuel production. The Registry therefore focuses on tracking fossil fuel production by country and by project.

Contains data for over 50,000 fields in 89 countries representing projects covering 75% of global production.

The Registry is also expected to be a useful tool for researchers and non-profit organisations in providing the foundation for rigorous analysis on how the world is tracking towards the goal of 1.5°C, and where production is occurring.

Description:
The map of Mega Warehouses gathers publicly available data and creates visualization about the impact of online retailing (and the transport system behind it, in particular warehouses and trucks) on public health in communities located in nearby mega warehouses facilities. They flag a little known and explored public health issue. Moreover, they also make evident the lack of regulation in zoning policies around these areas, which is almost nonexistent. 

Today in the United States, there are 39,116 warehouses and distribution centers larger than 100,000 square feet. For nearby residents, mostly in suburban and deprived areas the arrival of the mega warehouses means that traffic noise and air pollution bombard increases substantially. While trucks make up only a small percentage of all vehicles, they are one of the largest sources of smog and particulate pollution.
