The platform analyzes nationwide data categorized as warehouse, distribution, light distribution, refrigeration, among others, as well as queried ambient concentration of PM 2.5 air pollution, National Air Toxics Assessment diesel particulate matter exposure.
Novelty: Open data platform to investigate the impact of online retailing and its logistics chain on public health in communities located in nearby mega warehouses facilities.
Impact & Results:
- 79 percent of the population within a half mile of the Houston metro area’s 1,186 warehouses are people of color.
- Officials estimate that evidence don’t capture the full brunt of toxic emissions in their communities. 
- esidents who live within a half mile of a warehouse have elevated rates of asthma and other respiratory diseases >88%

Description:
Sen̓áḵw is the largest partnership with any First Nation in Canadian history. Having the Squamish Nation as main shareholders, the project aims that they act not just as passive landlords but active investors and partners in developing the land. 87% of Squamish Nation membership voted in favor of progressively developing their lands. 

This a real estate, architectural/design and environmental project that aims to be 100% GHG-free, all of Senákw's heating and cooling will be produced by a new 10MW district energy system fed by waste heat from Metro Vancouver's adjacent sewer infrastructure. Under a new associative form (Nch’kaỷ) proposed as the economic development arm of the Squamish Nation. Its mandate is to develop, manage and own the active businesses of the Nation, and was created to allow for the separation of business and politics within the Squamish Nation.
Novelty: Community - private sector partnership to develop real estate in a sustainable and culturally respectful way on indigenous lands.
Impact & Results:
- 10.5 acres
- 4,000,000 sf
- 6,000+ rental homes
- 1200+ affordable homes
- Largest net zero carbon residential project in Canada
- Largest First Nations economic development project in Canadian history
- Historic architectural, cultural, artistic, construction, and entrepreneurial opportunities for the Squamish Nation

Description:
The Black Wealth Data Center is building a comprehensive repository for Black wealth data. The Racial Wealth Equity Database aims to empower decision-makers with reliable data and raise the national standard for data collection and accessibility. It also aims at increasing generational wealth and addressing systemic underinvestment in Black communities. Furthermore, it seeks to help address the problem of insufficient and inaccessible data on the topic of Black wealth.

The data methodology is a hybrid of best practices from the fields of open data, civic tech, and data science, in addition to emerging frameworks around responsible and transparent use of big data. For example, every data sheet they collect goes through the Large Datasets Analysis Framework. They also make publicly available all the data and heavily rely on external feedback.
Novelty: This is a comprehensive repository for exploring, evidencing and delving into data about Black wealth, making it visible and accessible for decision-making and engagement.
Impact & Results:
Several results are displayed on the explore the data section (https://www.blackwealthdata.org/explore/assets): 
- African American communities have the lowest average of total assets in the USA
- These communities also carry the most monetary burden (debt) in comparison to their net incomes. 
- Asset distribution is low/poor in southern states in comparison to the ones in the north and midlands.

Project/Initiative Name: Masakhane - a grassroots NLP community for Africa, by Africans
Description:
Masakhane is a grassroots organisation whose mission is to strengthen and spur NLP research in African languages made by Africans. Despite the fact that 2000 of the world’s languages are African, African languages are barely represented in technology. 

The initiative's main goal is to shape the way NLP reproduces Africa to advance in an inclusive community building, open participatory research and multidisciplinarity. Moreover, they consider that it is imperitive that NLP models are developed to address some of the main issues in relation to technology adoption, data creation and benefiting from the digital advance: 
- Low resourced: The lack of resources for African languages hinders the ability for researchers to do NLP
- Lack of publicly-available benchmarks: Due to the low discoverability and the lack of research in the field, there are no publicly available benchmarks or leaderboards to new compare NLP techniques to
- Reproducibility: The data and code of existing research are rarely shared, which means researchers cannot reproduce the results properly.

They have built a community that consists of >1000 participants from 30 African countries, and >3 countries outside Africa. As of February 2020, over 49 translation results for over 38 African languages have been published by over 35 contributors on GitHub. They run several related projects - 6 in total - about language processing, text enrichment and NLP based on African languages.
Novelty: Masakhane is the largest platform in Africa that promotes NLP, text and speech technology development in African languages.
Impact & Results:
From the Makerere Language NLP project: 
- Parallel text Corpora for Luganda (900,000 sentences), Swahilli (900,000 sentences), Runyankore-Rukiga (200,000 sentences), Acholi (200,000 sentences) and Lumasaaba (200,000 sentences) obtained from various sources.
- Speech data set for Luganda (1000 hours) and Swahilli (1000 hours) on the Common Voice platform. The voice data will be collected using the Common voice platform based on the text corpora in (a) and through established voice communities in the three countries.

Description:
Nagrika is a non-partisan organization creating knowledge about Indian cities with <500,000 inhabitants. They co-build accessible and free knowledge resources for citizens and city governments. They create knowledge about Indian smaller cities that was nonexistent to understand these cities better. They aim that smaller cities become part of policy agendas of urban policy making as 70% of India's urban population live in cities with <1 million. In that sense, their work focuses on creating evidence that is context specific to enable localized solutions. 

The main output of such evidence is Nagarpedia: a platform that centralizes evidence about smaller cities to encourage and enable citizens and public authorities to bridge the knowledge gap of information available online on small cities, starting with Wikipedia. They mobilize people to enrich Wikipedia's information about India's smaller cities https://www.nagrika.org/nagarpedia 

Using a three-step process they:
- Create knowledge: converting data into meaningful information by leveraging on the networks of local knowledge in small cities to inform policy.
- Decision aiding: creating tools and platforms that aid decision making related to community and economic development for small cities. They also provide applied policy cycle support for citizens and city level agencies.
- Capacity building: enhancing the capacity of agencies and individuals to use information, knowledge and decision making capabilities to shape cities.
Novelty: Data and evidence development that is context specific of India's smaller cities below 1 million inhabitants.
Impact & Results:
Nagarpedia creates and updates evidence about small cities: https://www.nagrika.org/nagarpedia 

Several impacts can be visualized here: https://www.nagrika.org/initiatives

Description:
The Africa Agriculture Watch developed by Akademiya is a web-based platform that combines remote sensing data and machine learning techniques for informed decision-making in African food production systems. The aim is to create, across Africa and led from Rwanda, state-of-the art technical capacities to support the efforts by the Member States of the African Union to achieve the key goals of the agenda 2063 in relation to the agriculture sector.

The main goal is to help meet the needs of African countries, and those of the African Union, in terms of data, analytics and mutual learning for the effective implementation of Agenda 2063 and the realization of its outcomes. Moreover, overcome the agricultural statistics data gap in Africa by the use of remote sensing data through satellite images.

The Africa Crop Production (AfCP) model provides forecasts on food crop production for several crops and countries across the African continent. Its spatial resolution is at the community level with a grid cell size of 10 by 10 kilometers. The estimations are based on a combination of three components: i. remotely sensed biophysical parameters retrieved from satellite images, ii. historical production maps, and iii. machine learning techniques. Each pixel contains the crop’s production quantity for the following agricultural season based on the learned relationship between previously observed explanatory variables and production quantities.
Novelty: It facilitates the access and use of remote sensing products and forecast maps by embedding the ready-to-use AAgWa outputs in a web-based tool.
Impact & Results: The AfCP model provides a map of production values for a selected crop at a 10 x 10-kilometer grid cell size. Each pixel contains the crop’s production quantity for the following agricultural season based on the learned relationship between previously observed explanatory variables and production quantities.

Description:
The newly launched platform researches and analyses employment dynamics across groups, industries, occupations and regions. Small Area Labour Markets (SALM) presents estimates based on the Structure Preserving Estimation (SPREE) methodology.

The purpose of SPREE is to produce small area unemployment, unemployment rate and labour force estimates that reflect the regional disparities of the Centrelink data, while being consistent with ABS Labour Force Survey estimates.

Given the level of disaggregation involved, the small area figures produced by SPREE are smoothed (i.e., averaged) over four quarters, to dampen the variability inherent in the small area estimates.
Novelty: Platform to discover new trends in the Australian jobs market and evidence the latest insights from the National Skills Commission towards the 2026.
Impact & Results:
Information can be found here: https://labourmarketinsights.gov.au/our-research/employment-projections/ 
- 16,666,800 Working Age Population (15-64)
- 77.7% Employment Rate (15-64)
- 66.6% Participation Rate (15+)
- 3.5% Unemployment Rate
- 8.4% Youth Unemployment Rate (15-24)

It is likely that 91.7% of new jobs will require post-school education, and more than three-fifths of the total projected employment growth will occur in high skill level jobs.

Employment is projected to grow in 291 out of 358 occupations over the five years to November 2026:
- Employment is projected to increase across all 19 broad industries
- Employment is projected to increase across all 8 broad occupational groups
- 9 in 10 new jobs are projected to require post-school education

Description:
The Reflection Prototype facilitates interdisciplinary encounters to reveal and form connections. In attempting to provide proof of concepts for ideas, prototypes create opportunities for reflection and collaboration. The idea aims to build an online tool that would develop the data ecosystem network through personal and collective reflection. The functioning technical prototype allows people to see how they situate themselves in the field of data and AI ethics. 

Incorporating bibliometric analysis, the prototype created static maps of what individuals previously published. The Reflection Prototype continuously captures current conversations that might make a difference in the future. Given that issues in data and AI ethics are present and pressing, it is important to be able to expand the map of an emerging field in real-time. Furthermore, the Prototype draws on a reflective survey that asks participants to think about what motivated them to start working in data and AI ethics, how they define their own work, their relationships with others, and what ideas are significant to them. 
​
The online survey produces a network diagram graphic inspired by biological metaphors of network connection that occur in non-hierarchical ways, through growth, branching, and reproduction. The survey generates a visual prop to use when talking to people who have made different life choices but might share certain interests - or who may be able to develop those interests in the future.
Novelty: Online self-reflective survey that asks participants to think about what motivated them to start working in data and AI ethics, how they define their own work, their relationships with others, and what ideas are significant to them.
Impact & Results:
Two visualizations: 
- the individuals "fingerprint" in data and AI ethics using a branching schema and color density that depict their autobiography
- second diagram that consolidates answers across respondents so individuals can see themselves in relation to others.

Project/Initiative Name: Emerald Cloud Lab for remotely accelerated R&D
Description:
Emerald Cloud Lab is the only remotely operated research facility that handles all aspects of daily lab work — method design, materials logistics, sample preparation, instrument operation, data acquisition and analysis, troubleshooting, waste disposal, and everything in between — without the user ever setting foot in the lab. Recently ECL has incorporated Amazon Web Services (AWS) as part of its operations to allow full automation of routine lab scripts, such as inventory checks, instrument qualifications, and experiment scheduling. Whereas previously it ran ~1,000 scripts/week with regular compute resources, AWS has allowed to seamlessly scale to ~5,000 scripts/week in the cloud.

ECL Command Center provides a full platform for data analysis and visualization with a suite of over 4,500 functions, including advanced machine learning, image processing, and multi-dimensional analysis, and seamlessly integrates with our ECL knowledge base. ECL Constellation automatically saves and annotates all experimental methods and data with complete metadata to ALCOA+ standards and verifies data integrity to ensure that everything is model quality. This eliminates the need for individual data visualization, analysis, ELN, LIMS, LES/MES, QMS and even ERP systems and streamlines them into a single coherent package.
Novelty: Incorporation of AWS to allow the improvement of remote Lab compute services, as well as to repeat past work at the push of a button by saving the scripts and its process and running them.
Impact & Results: Since the focus is on intellectual contribution instead of manual labor, ECL innovations have incorporated machine-learning tools and cloud-base technologies to translate biology into an information technology.

Description:
AlphaFold is a machine learning and AI-based algorithm that can accurately predict 3D models of protein structures and is accelerating research in nearly every field of biology. The system can now predict the shape of a protein, at scale and in minutes, down to atomic accuracy. This is a significant breakthrough and highlights the impact AI can have on science.

Two releases where showcased: 
- The first one on 22.07.2021 covers over 350,000 structures, including the human proteome – all of the ~20,000 known proteins expressed in the human body – along with the proteomes of 20 additional organisms important for biological research, including yeast, the fruit fly, and the mouse.
- The second one released in 2022 that expands the database from nearly 1 million structures to over 200 million structures – including nearly all catalogued proteins known to science.

Finding ways to close this rapidly expanding gap and predict the structure of millions of unknown proteins can not only help us tackle disease, and more quickly find new medicines, but perhaps, also unlock the mysteries of how life itself works. The dataset can be found here: https://alphafold.ebi.ac.uk/
Novelty: AI model that predicts 3D models of protein structures to accelerate research in the biology, biomedicine and medicine fields.
Impact & Results: To date, more than 500,000 researchers from 190 countries have accessed the AlphaFold DB to view over 2 million structures. The publicly available structures have also been integrated into other public datasets, such as Ensembl, UniProt, and OpenTargets, where millions of users access them as part of their everyday workflows.

Organisation: Max Planck Institute for Human Development
Description:
The initiative re-analyzes the Google Flu platform and trends to demonstrate that a simple recency heuristic forecast could better predict such trends instead of using big data analytics or a black-box algorithm. The findings document states that "in an unstable world, reducing the amount of data and complexity can lead to more accurate predictions. In some cases, it might be advisable to ignore everything that happened in the past and instead rely on the most recent data point alone."

It also shows that psychological AI can match complex machine learning algorithms in prediction. In general, it aimed to demonstrate that “fast-and-frugal” heuristics that need little data are a good candidate for implementing psychological AI. This heuristic predicts that “this week’s proportion of flu-related doctor visits equals the proportion from the most recent week.” It is based on psychological theory of how people deal with rapidly changing situations.
Novelty: Less can be more. Using a single data point, the platform can predict the spread of the flu better than Google Flu Trends, a big data algorithm.
Impact & Results: The mean absolute error (from Figure 1) in predicting the proportion of flu-related doctor visits is 0.38 for Google Flu Trends, but only 0.20 when using one single data point, that is, recency.

Description:
The Future City Playgrounds is a gamification platform that is still in its prototype phase. It is designed with academic researchers, urban planners and city dwellers in mind. Players are walked by a robot through their tasks and helps users understand the building site and the goals of the game. The game is a sort of Minecraft for urban design

Users will be able to model project heritage building and experiment with different technologies while learning about their effects on the building energy demand, and ultimately its lifetime CO2 emissions. Design modifications include: adding rooftop vegetation and seeing its effects on heating and cooling demand, adding and modifying windows, experimenting with wall and roof building materials, choosing different photovoltaic modules based on cost and efficiency, and modeling heat pumps given different sources (air, water or geothermal). 

The goal of the game is to create the most sustainable building with a budget of $1 million. Players have the freedom to roam around the site, seeing the consequences of having the most expensive and heat-transfer resistant windows, or different types of solar panels arranged to optimise their direction to the sun. Within the given budget, they will learn how materials, vegetation, and windows work together to affect energy consumption, how local solar energy can offset grid energy, the basics of heat pumps, and gain an understanding on lifetime carbon emission sources.

In the game, users can play with a digital version of Montreal: they can shape a single building or cluster of buildings to simulate a neighbourhood retrofit project, click on surfaces or streets to modify them, or design buildings in empty lots to see how changing materials or adding clean-energy systems can affect the neighbourhood’s character, energy use and emissions.
Novelty: A game that creates a digital version of the city of Montreal so that players are able to model project heritage building and experiment with different technologies while learning about their effects on the building energy demand, and ultimately its lifetime CO2 emissions
Impact & Results: Impacts or results not yet displayed

Description:
This project was created by a group of social scientists, citizen scientists, and designers. The aim is to open up conversations about emotion recognition systems: from the science behind the technology to their social impacts--and everything else in between. Moreover, to promote public understanding of these technologies and citizen involvement in their development and use.

The platform gathers information about people’s experiences of emotion recognition systems and their thoughts on their practical applications in order to further people-centered learning on the topic. This is a participatory knowledge-building exercise and the information is collected in a two-step process: 

- Contact data: personally identifiable information about the end user, such as end user's email address or name or any other related information. They do not request and solicit this type of data from users, we would collect such information only if it has been provided by a user.
- Traffic data:  information that browsers automatically make available, including: IP address; domain servers; date and time of your visit; details of transactions you carry out through this Website, browser type, computer type, operation system type, etc. Traffic data is anonymous information which does not personally identify the user.
Novelty: An online survey-based scientific project, which aims to bring public understanding and awareness in the development of the emotion recognition systems and their social impact.
Impact & Results:
Example of a scientific article: https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/1754073913490045 
Example of a report:
https://www.article19.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/01/ER-Tech-China-Report.pdf 
Example of an art/activism derived project: https://stealingurfeelin.gs/

Project/Initiative Name: Tech tools for human rights documentation
Description:
Tech Tools explored the use of tech and digital tools as part of its contribution to conducting human rights documentation. The initiative focus on tools that are: 
- Intentionally designed to address the needs of documenters working in a human rights or social justice context.
- Flexible: they accommodate a range of types of rights violations 
- Currently in use by documenters in the field. 
- Non-exploitative (without fees or locking strategies)

The platform explores technology tools currently being developed and used by civil society for human rights documentation. This involved combining existing knowledge with desk research, including sources such as recent, relevant blog posts, articles and online discussions, tool websites and documentation, and GitHub repositories and issues to create demos of the different tools. 

The tools are to support documentation in human rights, with a high focus on preserve the "chain of custody" of the digital evidence/information, which refers to the "journey" that a piece of evidence takes from the time it is collected through its presentation in court. In that sense, the relevance of digital tools to maintain and ensure those pieces of information are critical.
Novelty: Set of digital tools to contribute to human rights documentation and preserve the "chain of custody" of the digital evidence/information and its journey until it is presented in court.

Project/Initiative Name: Heat.Gov - tools for communities facing extreme heat
Organisation: National Integrated Heat Health Information System
Description:
Heat.gov is the webportal for the National Integrated Heat Health Information System (NIHHIS). It serves as the premier source of information regarding heat and health for the United States. The portal seeks to improve federal, state, and local information and capacity to reduce the health, economic, and infrastructural impacts of extreme heat. It aims to build societal understanding of heat risks and create heat resilience across the United States to effectively address extreme heat and its impacts.

In collaboration with several federal partners, the initiative is an  integrated information system to develop and provide actionable, science-based information to help protect people from heat. To achieve its goals, Heat.gov centralizes different tools, maps and information from the different federal agencies to provide consistent and one-stop-shop information to policymakers and the citizens.
Novelty: An  integrated digital and web-based information system to develop and provide actionable, science-based information to help protect people from heat.

Project/Initiative Name: Maēs da Favela App (Mothers of the Favela)
Organisation: Central Única das Favelas - UNESCO Brazil
Description:
A coalition of activists in the favelas and corporate partners developed an app that is facilitating the distribution of food and emergency income to thousands of women spearheading families in the Favelas. The app has a facial recognition feature that helps volunteers identify and register recipients of aid and prevents fraud.

That aid serves a double purpose: it provides temporary relief for the families and helps flatten the curve by ensuring people have the means to stay at home during the pandemic.
Novelty: App with facial recognition technology to make food supply distribution more transparent and organized amid the COVID-19 pandemic.
Impact & Results:
US$ 26 million in food parcels and cash allowances to more than 1.5 million families in 5,000 neighborhoods. This is through the delivery of >20 tones of food supplies. 
https://www.maesdafavela.com.br/

Description:
Project Patient Voice is an online platform for patients and caregivers along with their healthcare providers to look at patient-reported symptom data collected from cancer clinical trials. 

The aim is to show patient-reported symptom data consistently from select cancer clinical trials of approved products. This data is usually not in the US Prescribing Information (drug label) but can give healthcare providers extra information to discuss with patients and caregivers. This represents a significant step in advancing a patient-centered approach to oncology drug development, where patient-reported symptom information is collected rigorously. 

Project Patient Voice creates a consistent source of publicly available information describing patient-reported symptoms from cancer trials for marketed treatments. While this patient-reported data has historically been analyzed by the FDA during the drug approval process, it is rarely included in product labeling and, therefore, is largely inaccessible to the public. Patient-reported outcome (PRO) data is collected using questionnaires that patients complete during clinical trials. These questionnaires are designed to capture important information about disease- or treatment-related symptoms. This includes how severe or how often a symptom or side effect occurs.

Project Patient Voice serves as a complement to FDA patient labeling and patient information, not a sole source of information on which to make decisions about medical care.
Novelty: An online platform to advance a patient-centered approach to oncology drug development where patient-reported symptom information is collected rigorously.

Description:
Google Health AI is researching robust new AI-enabled tools focused on diagnostics to assist clinicians. Drawing from diverse datasets, high-quality labels, and state-of-the-art deep learning techniques, they are making models to support medical specialists in diagnosing diseases such as cancer or eye-related conditions. The aim is to demonstrate that AI has the ability to enable novel, transformative diagnostics.

Through computer vision AI and image search capabilities, they are developing different tools to support better scientific research and to help people pre-screen their own conditions and have a rapid initial self-assessment. For example, they are using AI to help doctors address eye disease, as well as non-eye related conditions. Furthermore, they have launched research initiatives AI-based to improve lung and breast cancer detection through imaging and diagnostics. Similar initiatives are conducted for skin disease information. For example, on the latter their data include more than 80% of the conditions seen in clinics and more than 90% of the most commonly searched conditions. 

The initiative seeks to work too in promoting health equity and impact the drivers of structural and social determinants of health. So they accelerate  innovation by leveraging Google's technological capabilities and resources to collaborate with new and longstanding medical leaders and decision makers across many fields.
Novelty: Initiative to run several research and pilot programs making use of AI and image search capabilities to develop tools to support better scientific research and to address the early detection of common types of diseases, with a focus on cancer disease.
Impact & Results: Detailed results can be found here: https://health.google/health-research/publications/

Description:
Coronavirus Resilience Innovation Map and Rankings is a project by the Health Innovation Exchange of the UNAIDS and StartupBlink. It is a global directory with over 2,400 innovations and solutions that help people cope and adapt to life amid the COVID-19 pandemic. Rankings featuring 60 countries and 160 cities were also produced. Countries and cities were ranked based on an algorithm to measure innovation across cities and countries globally. 

The aim of this platform is to help teams of innovators to cooperate and build solutions faster related to the post COVID-19 recovery. The map will also help the public and private sector identify innovative projects relevant to them by location (from vaccines and prevention to diagnostics and treatment, to life and business adaptation, etc.). With the emergence and clear effect of the Coronavirus (COVID-19), people around the world are dramatically changing all aspects of life. In that sense, the platform contributes on how to become resilient, adapt faster, and innovate in order to navigate these challenging times.

A project will be eligible to join the directory if it directly offers a scalable innovative solution to one of the below-mentioned categories linked to the pandemic. The innovations were scouted by the StartupBlink research team and contributors creating one of the world’s most comprehensive databases on COVID-19 innovations. The innovation map is also crowdsourced, allowing everyone to submit their projects to the map. Those projects are reviewed by the StartupBlink team to make sure they are relevant and innovative. 

The ranking algorithm considers the number and type of innovations in each city and country with extra points given to outstanding initiatives, identified as the Champions. In June 2020, we published our first Coronavirus Innovation Report with updated rankings of 32 countries and 80 cities across 1,000 projects. The second version of this ranking was published in 2021 featuring 40 countries, and 100 cities. This current Coronavirus Resilience Index has an expanded scope and also features innovations that help us adapt to the new normal. The most updated ranking (released in 2022) features 60 countries and 160 cities.
Novelty:
The Coronavirus Resilience Map is innovative because: 
1. It is the most comprehensive resource on innovation born out of the COVID-19 pandemic. This free resource helps innovators identify other projects who are working on a similar view and facilitates open innovation. 
2. The rankings celebrate the countries and cities that went above and beyond during this crisis, producing inspiring projects to the benefit of their local community and developing solutions that have had an impact around the world.  
3. This current iteration of the Coronavirus Resilience Innovation Map serves as an ongoing resource for innovators to cite in case a similar epidemic or pandemic hits. Keeping these innovations in one place and constantly updating them with help us prepare for other pandemics that may arise.
Impact & Results: Mapped more than 500 medical and social-oriented innovations to address COVID-19 and that support "normal life" and with potential for the post-recovery process.

Description:
The TransCap Initiative is a collaborative space for developing, demonstrating, and scaling systemic investing to transform the economy systems and deploy capital strategically to fight the climate crisis. The model applies systems thinking, human-centred design, and the principles of mission-driven innovation to explore how financial capital can catalyse the transformation of systems in service of a low-carbon, climate-resilient future scenarios. 

The innovation aims to develop knowledge and innovation base, testing model concepts and approaches, and building a community of practice around the goal of re-adapting human patterns to the fundamentals of the natural systems. In that sense, the innovation emphasizes the need for collaboration, experimentation and learning.

Specifically, the transformation capital approach is a new systemic investment logic aiming to transform real economy systems by deploying capital with a broader intent, using systemic methodologies, structures, and decision-making frameworks, and moving from a project-by-project mentality to a strategic blending paradigm.
Novelty: This is an approach to rethink and redirect financial flows to the most critical aspects to fight the climate crisis by using systems thinking and decision-making and future frameworks. It follows an investing logic and incorporates socio-technical aspects, as well as concepts of climate-resilience and inclusiveness.
Impact & Results: #NAME?

Project/Initiative Name: Indice de Capacidades para la Innovación Pública - Public Sector Innovation's Capacity Index
Description:
The Public Sector Innovation's Capacity Index its first of its kind in the Colombian Government. It aims to strengthen the institutional conditions to foster public innovation and remove barriers to innovation. The tool is an Index to provide national and territorial entities with inputs to define and guide strategies to promote public innovation.

As such, the Index is a commitment by the National Planning Department (DNP) to measure the capacities of national and territorial entities to innovate and will serve national level institutions to make evidence-based decisions to improve the service delivery with a public officer and citizens-centered approach. 

The Index measures 719 public entities, of which 147 are national entities and 572 are territorial ones. The unit of measurement of the index is the Colombian public entities of the different administrative levels of government, i.e. it includes national departments, ministries, agencies, governors' offices, mayors' offices and other institutions that perform an administrative and governmental functions in the country. It is composed of four dimensions: 1) Innovative Talent, 2) Knowledge Management and Use, 3) Collaborative, and 4) Innovation, 3) Collaborative and 4) Regulatory and Processes.
Novelty: First public sector innovation index in Colombia.
Impact & Results:
Here it is the report of the Index: https://colaboracion.dnp.gov.co/CDT/ModernizacionEstado/EiP/Indice_de_capacidades_para_la_innovacion_publica_ICIP_2021.pdf 
- The innovation capacity nationwide is of 53,73%, where around 60% of the evaluated public entities fall below the median threshold.

Description:
The project is designed to convene 22 change makers together with African futurists and next-generation thinkings to launch a set of conversations on data-driven technology as imagines, developed, and used by communities across the continent. 

During the process, members of the Next Generation Foresight Practitioners (NGFP) network worked in teams centred in Kenyan, Nigerian, and pan-African communities. Together the teams used a group foresight process to identify emerging signals and insights, explore alternative future scenarios, and generate visions for the worlds we want to create through stories, new media and artefacts from the future. The programme supports them by driving their integral role in agenda-setting and connecting them with today’s decision-makers. 

With over 500 members from more than 80 countries across six continents, the NGFP network is one of the largest global networks of future-alert activists and practitioners in the world. The Next Generation Foresight Practitioners (NGFP) programme exists to accelerate the transformative potential of the next generation of changemakers envisioning inclusive and equitable futures globally.
Novelty: First large "storytelling" platform that convene African futurists to deliver insights on 22 cases about data-driven technology, imaginaries and development.
Impact & Results:
The initiative identified six key priorities from the storytelling processes: 
1. Data needs to be seen as a collective resource for public good. A change of focus from the rights on the individual and the interest of companies that are enshrined in many data protection regulations (e.g. GDPR) to an approach that respects and respond to local culture and cooperative ownership.
2. Data to support good governance. A shift from data being used for citizen surveillance to support government accountability transparency and direct democracy.
3. Bio-internet and connectivity. Connecting data to the natural world. Technology becomes additive not extractive to nature and the environment.
4. Technology as way back not just a way forward. Progress and globalization with not against African languages, practices, cultures and tradition.
5. Sincere, humane, and co-designed technology. A focus on the digital divide. Technology is used to meet people’s real needs, not to exclude and divide society.
6. African voice. Creators, innovators, scientists, technologists, researchers are able to shape the global conversation.

Description:
The initiative is developing a series of lexicons that support a new knowledge infrastructure of health justice:
- health lexicon: There is a need to introduce a more ecological and systemic lexicon to public health. This is growing lexicon of definitions and framings that support a movement to Health Justice.
- environment lexicon: We expect this to be used by communities who are facing environmental injustice to have access to a lexicon that can support their knowledge and lived experience.
- justice lexicon: To develop a new approach to health we must have one where our terminologies and approaches are justice based. 

This is a research, collaboration and advocacy programme to support those working to improve public health and to establish the appropriate knowledge infrastructure at the community, campaign, policy and practice levels that improves urban health.

They expect the lexicon to be used by communities who are facing environmental injustice so that it can support their knowledge and lived experience. This is especially important as when they face industry polluters they are purposefully gaslit through the use of obscure terminology and industry jargon.
Novelty: The initiative develops a series of climate-crisis and urban health-related lexicons to expand the knowledge infrastructure of health justice.
Impact & Results:
Health lexicon: https://www.urbanhealthcouncil.com/health-lexicon 
Justice lexicon: https://www.urbanhealthcouncil.com/justice-lexicon 
Environmental-data lexicon: https://www.urbanhealthcouncil.com/environmental-data-lexicon

Description:
Urban Sound Lab is Soundtrackcity's itinerant listening workshop. Together with residents, the city sounds of Amsterdam South are mapped out. Residents of Zuid in Amsterdam have been collecting sounds that they find meaningful for the city for three years now with the purpose of developing a sound map collection of public sounds. 

Amsterdam is normally packed with sound. Due to the corona crisis, everything is different now. Few or no people on the street, shops closed, much less traffic, hardly any planes flying over. The soundscape of the city has completely changed.

Following two questions: How do you experience this? What do you hear? Soundtrackcity is making a city- wide call to submit sound impressions in times of corona and in the aftermath to collect all responses in the Urban Sound Lab. Beyond the artistic and awareness use, it will help the City of Amsterdam to "make sense" of the city from a sound point of view and to incorporate the learnings and experiences of its citizens as part of its various programs (i.e., artistic, tourist, environmental)
Novelty: An itinerant listening workshop where residents of Amsterdam map the city sounds and provide meaningful information about them to support the city in creating better policies.
Impact & Results: The urban sound map: http://archive.urbansoundlab.nl/

Description:
Kochi Open Mobility Network, an initiative of the Kochi Metropolitan Transport Authority to support the local community in Kochi. KOMN, drives the integration of urban transport-across modes using technology, which is a key objective of Kochi MTA. KOMN, is one of the novel initiatives towards digitising mobility, where all mobility apps in the network are built in such a way as to enable them to communicate with each other in the same language. This offers an open interoperability led integration approach that is akin to the design of the Internet itself and to the UPI payments open infrastructure in India that has witnessed population scale adoption in a noticeably short period of time across the country.

Kochi MTA is the first authority of its kind in India to integrate, plan and regulate different commuting modes. KMTA is proud to power KOMN – the world’s first inclusive, multimodal mobility network enabled by open specifications of beckn protocol. This is an open mobility network runs on fully open lightweight and interoperable specifications

Kochi Open Mobility Network (KOMN) is the world’s first open protocol enabled mobility network. This, first of its kind open mobility marketplace is enabled to create many possibilities and equal opportunities for all the participants – customers, drivers and service provider applications. In short, it is an open network where Kochi passengers, Kochi drivers and the service providers in Kochi can come together and access each other seamlessly and transparently. KOMN is enabled to empower the local mobility community in Kochi from taxis to buses to auto-rickshaws to ferries to metros to even bicycles.

By using the app, customers can access a choice of taxi services in the city. And for the local driver, they can use Yatri Partner app to get enrolled through an aggregator or an agency of your choice.
Novelty: Kochi MTA is the first authority of its kind in India to integrate, plan and regulate different commuting modes.
Impact & Results:
The world’s first open mobility network using open protocols: 
1200+ taxi drivers
50+ pioneers
5+ government agencies

Description:
AbenaAI is an app for people who don’t speak English or don't use other common voice assistant languages in Ghana. The goal of the innovation is adding more African languages and Africa-centered features that aren’t central to voice assistants like Alexa and Google Assistant. It is the first voice assistant fluent in Twi (also called Akan Twi), the most widely spoken language in Ghana. 

The app can handle much of what English-speaking voice assistants do, including checking news and weather, setting alarms, operating smart home devices, and even reading out tweets. It also comes with more Ghana-centered features like allowing users to check, transfer, and recharge their mobile phone accounts and transfer money through connected payment apps. Abena recently extended its features to enable voice commands for speed-dialing and phone call control. To compensate for potentially patchy internet limiting Abena’s usefulness, the app has an offline mode that sustains most of its functions.

Users can check trending news on Twitter; follow weather reports; check, transfer, and recharge their airtime balances; and transfer money with their preferred mobile payment app, all using voice commands on Abena AI. The offline mode also supports an in-app community feature that allows users to send text messages, images, and videos with each other.
Novelty: It is the first AI-based voice assistant fluent in Twi (also called Akan Twi), the most widely spoken language in Ghana.
Impact & Results: Africa’s incredible linguistic diversity and the relatively sparse digital representation of those languages suggest a huge and underserved market. African demand fueled the $3,4 million investment by Mozilla Common Voice to create linguistic databases as a precursor for a voice assistant that can speak Kiswahili, known elsewhere as Swahili.

Organisation: Oxfam, the University of Edinburgh, Bayes Centre
Description:
Ox-Chain is a major research project between the Universities of Edinburgh. It brings together experts in digital design, cryptography, business and international development. Through collaborative research, it implements Blockchain for Oxfam to better support the circulation and re-circulation of valuable items within its business model – hence ‘Ox-Chain’.

Ox-chain will explore the circulation and re-circulation of valuable items within its business model, how blockchain might support different ways of engaging donors and recipients of development, and how it might promote transparency in the supply of ethical second-hand goods. More broadly, drawing on the expertise and practices of our research partners, we will explore the reconfiguration of economic, social and cultural life in the context of international development which may be made possible by digital, peer-to-peer value exchange.

The project is highly interdisciplinary. It combines a collaborative design-led approach to practical problems of designing with data, with a critical approach which draws on multi-site ethnographies, social and political theory and human geography, along with business studies and entrepreneurship.
Novelty: A blockchain for Oxfam to better support the circulation and re-circulation of valuable items within its business model
Impact & Results:
Smart donations app: https://smart-donations.oxchain.uk/ 
Pizza blocks: https://oxchain.uk/the-project/pizzablock/ 
Smart seesaws: https://oxchain.uk/the-project/seismic-see-saw/

Description:
Sphere Transgression Watch is a digital tool that tracks the growing presence of 9 Big Tech companies (Microsoft, Apple, IBM, Meta Alphabet, Amazon, Huawei, 23anMe, Palantir) over time and how they move into ever more spheres of society, including health, education, new provision and urban planning, thus reshaping these spheres in dangerous ways.

This is an initiative by the Digital Good Research Team. The project studies the effects of the growing influence of tech companies in the sphere of health and medicine and seeks to develop a normative framework for mitigating risks that can account for conflicting societal discourses about justice and the common good.

The initiative focuses on the series of sphere transgressions by tech companies, whereby the legitimate advantages they have accrued in the sphere of digital goods are translated into aparent illegitimate advantages in other spheres. Visualizing these transgressions in space and time is a means of grasping the breadth of this phenomenon and recording its many instances.

The platform displays several spheres of transgression and once a user clicks on them they will display news/events related to such sphere where any of the Big Tech were involved at. Moreover, it will display the specific areas within a given sector (i.e., medicine or public health) where they have the most presence or influence (i.e., cloud services or biomedical research): https://sphere-transgression-watch.org/company/?sphere=health&company=
Novelty: Sphere Transgression Watch is a digital tool that tracks the growing presence of 9 Big Tech companies (Microsoft, Apple, IBM, Meta Alphabet, Amazon, Huawei, 23anMe, Palantir) over time and how they move into ever more spheres of society, including health, education, new provision and urban planning, thus reshaping these spheres in dangerous ways.
Impact & Results: Every sphere will display news/events related to such sector where any of the Big Tech were involved at. Moreover, it will display the specific areas within a given sector (i.e., medicine or public health) where they have the most presence or influence (i.e., cloud services or biomedical research).

Description:
#MeToo Anti-Network is a project with textual and conceptual analysis. From a random selection of one million #MeToo tweets, the code read through all examples with more than 100 retweets in the timespan from 2017 - 2020. The collection currently contains >32 million tweets referencing #metoo and dozens of related hashtags 

The vast majority are news media posts and political (trolling) discussions, most of them neglecting the specific issues and survivor voices at the heart of the #MeToo movement. Some of the results display, for example, that a vast number of tweets have been withheld based on local laws, even though they violate twitter rules -- over a fifth possess doubtful veracity content and there is also a high bot-boosted activity. Moreover, the results display that the measurements of "success" in networked social media do not render visible the actual importance of #MeToo or the broader phenomenon of structural sexual violence.
Novelty: Network analysis of over 1 million tweets related to #metoo to visibility the robustness of the movement on the digital sphere, and to double check the depth of "success" metrics about any gender or social injustice-related movement.

Organisation: Goldsmiths, University of London (The Museums +AI Network)
Description:
This toolkit facilitates the way to influencing museum practice in the area of artificial intelligence for years to come. The initiative will create an ethically robust professional framework, to support museums who are keen to engage with the possibilities of these technologies, develop new audiences and increase access to their collections.

In 2019 the Museums + AI network engaged with 50 senior museum professionals, and leading academics across the UK and US. AI is becoming an increasingly pressing concern for many large museums who are beginning to experiment with its potential to provide new ways to engage with audiences, visitors, art and objects.

The framework bring about several topics around AI technologies including machine learning, predictive analytics and others, bring exciting possibilities of knowing more about visitors and collections. It also focuses on the implicances of these technologies in terms of the ethical questions for museums. With an increasing awareness and regulations about data usage in wider society, museums, must approach AI with both caution and fervour. As such exploring, critiquing and understanding the ethical implications of AI within a museum context is increasingly becoming a pressing need for museums.
Novelty: Framework that influences the museum practice in the area of AI.
Impact & Results: The English language version of the toolkit has been downloaded 3,000+ times, with print copies distributed to policy makers in the UK and USA. The Library of Congress stated that they sought to ‘mirror’ the strategic model outlined in the toolkit, in their Machine Learning strategy. The Toolkit was presented to the European Parliament's Committee on Culture and Education. Evidence drawn from this project was submitted to the Inquiry on the Future of the Creative Industries, which is being held by the House of Lords Communications and Digital Committee (UK).

Project/Initiative Name: PALOP-TL Civil Society Online Budget Platform
Description:
The PALOP-TL online budget platform is a budget simplification and analysis tool/method to explore data from official budgetary reports published by governments in 6 Portuguese-speaking countries (Angola, Cape Verde, Guinea Bissau, Mozambique, São Tomé e Principe, and Timor Leste). 

It aims to promote visibility and encourage to advance in the agenda of open budgets, improved public expenditure and fiscal transparency towards the citizens. In that sense, the tool has developed the first budget transparency index with a tailored method that applies to these countries only. Moreover, It intends to be a tool to guide civil society in demanding more public data. 

The platform gathers information collected from different official reports in .pdf format. The information is identified, extracted, structured, and made available for consultation in open-format databases and interactive visualizations that broaden access and consumption. The analysis is sustained under three dimensions: 1) budget reports, 2) information quality, 3) open data.
Novelty: First open budget tool and index tailored and applied to the contexts of 6 Portuguese-speaking countries.
Impact & Results: Visualized here: https://www.paloptl-ebudgets.org/static/reports/detailed_results_2021_en.bd940a822227.pdf

Organisation: iNtaka Centre for Law, Technology at the University of Cape Town
Description:
LawTechLab.Africa is an initiative by the iNtaka Centre for Law & Technology at the University of Cape Town that aims to provide a space for exploration, experimentation and the development of legal tools at the intersection of law and technology. They seek to shape the next generation of African lawyers and non-legal scholars and practitioners and prepare them to navigate the modern law and technology landscape.

It provides an opportunity to gain hands-on experience with technologies and tools that are poised to revolutionize the legal landscape, including practical exposure to Artificial Intelligence, open public blockchains, and smart-contracts. The lab is a space where legal practitioners and students can learn about technology to future proof the legal career.

The web platform, in particular, showcases two initiatives: 1) the legal word association generator - Words appearing in most semantically similar context from AfricanLII collection, and 2) African LII's Citator - the beta version of a first-of-it’s-kind Pan-African automated legal citator and summarizer for African caselaw – the Citator.
Novelty: The innovation provides a mapping of thousands of links that connect Africa’s jurisprudence, making availability of legal source materials, towards effective access to legal information. This is an inter and intra jurisdictional citator for Africa that promises to provide deeper insights into the development of the common law on the African continent.
Impact & Results: LTL provides for the first time a means to track how courts have analysed, discussed, and imported principles of law from the continent and more broadly.

Organisation: USC Michelson Center for Convergent Bioscience
Description:
The initiative has developed a new way to present molecules that are recognizable, relatable, and widely accessible, through the invention and development of a radically new structural language. Each constituent of a Cell is represented as a new fluid, but modular, architecture. These molecules and organelles are translated into universally recognizable constituents through a next-gen [new generation] visual language co-developed by biologists, chemists, crystallographers, programmers, engineers, animators, architects, artists and designers.

To prototype, develop, and apply this new representation, the starting point is the glucose regulated insulin release by the Pancreatic Beta Cell. For the first time, the modular representation of the constituents of the Pancreatic Beta Cell allows the user/viewer to see the constituents of the cell in direct relationship to one another. Within mixed-reality, immersive and interactive environments, the viewer can observe relative scale, relative geo-location, relative activity, and relative function of each constituent, through time and fully immersive space.

Using narrative systems, storytelling, and world building the project presents cellular biology in an experiential world based on the structure and function of a single cell. This rich biochemical world engages the user in concepts, pathways, and implications through design narrative, all backed by scientific rigor.
Novelty: For the first time, the modular representation of the constituents of the Pancreatic Beta Cell allows the user/viewer to see the constituents of the cell in direct relationship to one another.

Project/Initiative Name: Mapa de los Espacios de Innovación Pública (The map of public sector innovation outfits)
Description:
The initiative aims to give visibility to the spaces where innovation projects are promoted, whether in the form of laboratories or other formulas across Spain. Similarly, whether they are within public administrations or in outlets that provide support to them. They have made an open call to collect the work in this regard of other private and social non-profit sector entities that also focus their work towards the creation of public and social value. 

The platform displays an interactive map where these spaces are located, and indicate the entity and the information to which they belong and how a brief description of how they focus their current/future work. This is a crowdsourced project from the entire public innovation community in Spain. 

There is a growing relevance of public innovation in Spain, with the proliferation of spaces, laboratories, projects and initiatives at different levels of public administration. On the other hand, there was no open inventory of this type of innovation spaces. The visibility of these spaces, through a collaborative mapping carried out mainly with the support of the NovaGob community (with nearly 17,000 members, professionals from the public sector), offers the possibility to promote connection, common learning and collaboration.
Novelty: In the Spanish case, there was a lack of resources to identify and make visible the projects and organizations of the public innovation ecosystem. The collaborative mapping and the elaboration of the web map has allowed us to fill this gap, and we hope that it will boost the interconnection and collaboration between the different actors.
Impact & Results: - In the participation process, the platform received 24 outlet proposals that were included. So far there have been 2,750 visits to the map's website https://novagob.org/mapa-espacios-de-innovacion-publica.

Description:
This is an environmental surveillance platform that aims to promote systematic wastewater surveillance and other environmental surveillance tools for early detection and prevention of disease outbreaks by creating an early warning system in India as a public health tool. It aims to bring environmental surveillance for public health in the state and national level public health system through research, advocacy and scale-up initiatives, data modelling and communication. 

The surveillance platform has been set up in the city of Bangalore as an Early Warning System for COVID outbreaks. This initiative helps understand the trends of SARS-CoV-2 infections within the city and to support the local municipal administration by informing them about the trend of infection within the population. The surveillance platform works on wastewater based epidemiology that has also initiated pilot studies to monitor other pathogens of pandemic proportions, antimicrobial resistance, Influenza, Monkeypox. 

The platform provides technical support, digital backbone, learning and sharing platform and communication support for believers, enthusiasts and implementers of environment surveillance. It is a partnership driven program, which explores, tests and utilizes learnings to promote Wastewater Based Epidemiology (WBE) as a significant complementary public health surveillance tool. 

The novel innovation in the platform stems from three critical principles: 
1. Public access of the dashboard: The platform is the only ES initiative in India and possibly in Asia, which hosts a publicly accessible dashboard for data insights from ES. 
2. Openness of sense-making platform: The platform hosts an open forum for sense-making of data trends for non-profits, academicians of several sectors such as social, epidemiology, molecular biology, data science, GIS, and drain systems and local govt. 
3. Trust, support and communication: The local govt use their COVID-19 Twitter handle to post and share ES insights either by posting tweets or retweeting platform's posts.
Novelty:
Precision Health platform was hosted Asia's first city wide Environmental Surveillance platform in Bengaluru city to support city's local govt in COVID control and management. The novel innovation in the platform stems from three critical principles: 
1. Public access of the dashboard: The platform is the only ES initiative in India and possibly in Asia, which hosts a publicly accessible dashboard for data insights from ES. 
2. Openness of sense-making platform: The platform hosts an open forum for sense-making of data trends for non-profits, academicians of several sectors such as social, epidemiology, molecular biology, data science, GIS, and drain systems and local govt. 
3. Trust, support and communication: The local govt use their COVID-19 Twitter handle to post and share ES insights either by posting tweets or retweeting platform's posts.
Impact & Results:
The early warning signals for the third wave gave rise to escalating clinical testing rates, mandating masking and restrictive movement in public and crowded places which resulted in lesser positivity rates in the city. 
1. Covered more than 80% population of Bengaluru(approximately 7 million citizens) 
2. Reported 4 early warning signals for COVID outbreaks 
3. More than 90% correlation between the ES data to clinical cases. 
4. Traced 40 emerging variants. 
5. Scaling up to 4 cities.

Description:
COVID-NMA is an international research initiative to support decisions on COVID-19 treatments and preventive interventions through a living Evidence Ecosystem. The initiative congregates several (>10) medical, scientific and research institutions and aims to conduct research planning by producing an up-to-date mapping of COVID-related trialed evidence. Furthermore, to inform public health and healthcare decisions and to improve the research value by producing accessible and trustworthy synthesis of high-quality evidence about such interventions for the prevention and treatment of COVID-19. 

The initiative is based on three main pillars: 1) performing a living mapping of all registered trial evidence to help funders and researchers planning future trials, 2) producing a living systematic review of trial evidence to support healthcare decision making, 3) assessing trials methodology and transparency to improve future research. So far the network has conducted >4000 randomized trials of which 3433 were RCTs on treatments, 381 focused on prevention and >600 on vaccines. They are still trialing above >1000 RCTs despite the pandemic is now under control.
Novelty: A broad transdisciplinary consortium to support decisions on COVID-19 treatments and preventive interventions through a living Evidence Ecosystem.
Impact & Results: - 4478 Randomized Trials: 3433 RCTs on treatments, 381 RCTs on prevention, 664 RCTs on vaccines.

Description:
The Civic Design Library is a crowdsourced data set of civic design resources and case studies created by global practitioners working at the intersection of design and the public sector.

It aims to showcase the breadth and depth of civic design and offer data and visual formats for peer-to-peer learning and inspiration.
Novelty: A crowdsourced data set (library) of civic design resources and case studies for peer-to-peer learning and inspiration.
Impact & Results: Not possible to visualize impacts.
Other relevant URL(s) - eg reports, pres:
https://www.civicdesignlibrary.com/about
Case study data: https://www.civicdesignlibrary.com/case-study-data 
Resource data: https://www.civicdesignlibrary.com/resource-data 
Civic design library taxonomy: https://www.civicdesignlibrary.com/taxonomy 
https://twitter.com/arielmai/status/1469074069291208704

Project/Initiative Name: Stewardship Navigator - The data economy Lab
Description:
The Stewardship Navigator is an interactive tool that guides users through various foundational decisions and structural considerations for building a steward - including accountability and governance processes, how to structure consent and facilitate participation etc. It is based in an interactive questionnaire built for individuals, researchers, policymakers and practitioners interested in building stewardship entities or data intermediaries. Based on choices made, it also surfaces emerging and unique case studies or pilots (e.g data cooperatives) across domains and use-cases. 

Part of the solution may lie in Data Stewardship which envisions a diverse ecosystem of data stewards that can thrive in addition to, or often as part of, democratic public digital infrastructures. Data stewards are intermediary organisations or institutions that seek to empower individuals and communities, providing them with the ability to control or make decisions that relate to their data and allowing them more directly benefit from its value.

The Stewardship Navigator tool has been designed to help organisations better think through a range of aspects related to organisational structure, business model, services provided and how tethered stewards can be to their end users, beneficiaries and society at large. It draws from a rich and emerging domain of experimentation where across the world, public sector agencies, non-profits and businesses and are testing methods to give users a tangible way to exercise their preferences on how their data is used and shared.  

Through case studies, the tool demonstrates how Stewards are uniquely striving to ensure individual and community interests are represented and upheld, navigating relationships around trust and consent and balancing incentives around data's value and its consequent delivery. Data for Policy platform hosted by the UNDP Regional Innovation Centre in partnership with Rockefeller Foundation of which Aapti acts as a key knowledge/technical partner.
Novelty:
The Data Stewardship landscape within which Aapti functions as a global thought leader has expanded in terms of attention from policy makers and new research interests globally. The Stewardship Navigator tool is a novel approach that is a key contribution to this research. It is innovative as: 
- It adopts a clear community-driven, bottom-up view that is focused on discovering and enabling models of data stewardship that enable community participation in data governance - an especially nuanced view in an often technocratic, siloed tech policy ecosystem.
- The step-by-step guide to making decisions on stewardship is grounded in experience of over 100 global use-cases, which breaks down this complex concept into a more actionable format that is codified and designed for ease of access for practitioners.
- The innovation aims to gamify the experience of the tool, encouraging users to make a set of choices and be guided to various stewardship models
Impact & Results: The tool contributes to growing knowledge around stewardship and has been largely evaluated during live use/demo or through 1:1 discussions with partners and qualitatively oriented feedback forms. In the vast majority of cases, users have marked their appreciation at the depth of questions provided and interaction possibilities but have highlighted areas for further improvement in length and usability - greater investigation on this is required and can be done in the future by analysing usage/telemetry data.

Project/Initiative Name: DEEP - a collaborative analysis platform for effective aid responses
Description:
The Data Entry and Exploration Platform (DEEP) is a collaborative analysis platform for effective aid responses. It offers a suite of tools and collaborative workflows that meet the needs for compiling, storing and structuring data and qualitative information. 

The intelligent web-based platform is open-source and aims at simplifying the workflow of design, data acquisition, analysis, communication and documentation of humanitarian-related events. The platform provides a customized use of analysis frameworks to structure desk reviews, analysis or monitoring where users can create or collaborate on projects as well as import and tag documents and export the results.

AI-Powered assisted tagging – the advanced Natural Language Processing enables the user to efficiently annotate and categorize content in the documents saving time and resources significantly. An active learning system is installed to train the platform further to achieve higher accuracy. DEEP uses data standards and common taxonomies, e.g., CODs, to ensure compatibility with other tools, datasets and processes.
Novelty: An intelligent and open source web-based platform that uses NLP to enable the annotation and categorization of humanitarian-related documents and events.
Impact & Results:
- +2,4 thousand projects
- +5,053 users 
- +70 countries 
- +66 thousand resources mapped 
- +555 thousand resources annotated

Description:
Gavel is a not for profit/non-governmental organization with the aim of increasing the pace of justice delivery through access to justice, digital technology and citizens’ engagement. The initiative has tracked court cases, promoted transparency and accountability in the justice sector and also provided free legal support and advocate for the rights of indigent Nigerians.

It monitors and tracks anti-corruption cases in court and provided necessary data to the public on these cases with the aim at galvanizing the conversation around these cases and to advocate for their early resolution. In that sense, the initiative uses technology to accelerate justice delivery within the legal framework of Nigeria, as well as technology to digitize the activities of the court for public use to achieve a transparent judicial system in Nigeria.

On of their initiatives "Measuring the pace of justice through data: A ranking of states high courts of Nigeria" was launched in the absence of data to benchmark the performance of High Courts of States. The research paper gives the estimated number of legal problems per state, how long a case stays in Court's docket from filing to judgement, the rank of each state high courts vis a vis other states in Nigeria. The data samples randomly chosen per state are 50 cases from filing date to judgment date. In total, 1,388 cases were analysed from 33 states.
Novelty: Initiative that measures the pace of justice through data in Nigeria. It is a first attempt at estimate the burden of the judicial system through data.

Description:
The Oxford Supertracker keeps an eye on existing tracking sources of research developed during the COVID-19 pandemic and in the aftermath. The platform compiles a collection of currently over 100 policy trackers and datasets on the topic. 

This is a global directory of over several hundred policy trackers and surveys related to COVID-19. This novel collection is designed to assist researchers and policy-makers in keeping track of a rapidly growing number of data sources. Users can search and identify relevant information resources, such as datasets, surveys, and systematic collections, across different areas, countries and data types.

The Oxford Supertracker builds on the tracker of trackers - compiled since March 2020 from a collection of over 100 novel tracking sources. It covers cross-country research in the areas of non-pharmaceutical interventions, economic and social policy responses, public attitudes, politics and media coverage. So far, the policy tracker displays 232 results about other COVID-19 trackers, and 63 surveys.
Novelty: A tracker of trackers - a global directory - of over several hundred policy trackers and surveys to assist researchers and policy-makers in keeping an eye on the rapidly growing number of data sources during the COVID-19 pandemic.
Impact & Results: The project has received funding by the Economic, Social, Cultural & Environmental Impacts of COVID-19: Urgent Response Fund funded by the Higher Education Innovation Fund (HEIF), the Oxford ESRC Impact Acceleration Account (IAA), and the Higher Studies Trust Fund. The collection includes policy trackers and surveys covering the first two years of the pandemic.

Description:
Roofpedia is an open registry of sustainable roofscapes around the world. It uses deep convolutional neural network to detect sustainable roof typologies from satellite images. Footprints of buildings identified with solar panels or rooftop greenery are tagged automatically, and the results are visualized above for a score of cities. It is on an experimental research prototype phase. 

While most studies so far have been focused on estimating the potential of rooftops, Roofpedia enables researchers and decision-makers to understand the current and actual status of the rooftops (i.e., how many of them are currently occupied with solar panels and/or extensive greenery). Such information advances the state of the art, complements existing studies (including those estimating the potential), and it is useful for several purposes, (i.e., gauging the efficiency of government policies, tracking and monitoring pledges by businesses, verifying the use of subsidies, among others). 

This is an open initiative — data and code are released openly, and the project is modular, enabling the addition of new geographies and roof typologies. Furthermore, a paper that summarizes both the initiative and its main output - the Roofpedia Index - is of open access. 

The Index introduces a measure of the penetration of sustainable roof typologies in major cities around the world. Solar roofs and Green roofs mapped by Roofpedia are compared against the total number of buildings and areas of the buildings in a city. Aggregate scores are calculated for both solar and green coverage and cities are ranked with an combined score.
Novelty: An open registry of sustainable roofscapes around the world using deep convolutional neural network to detect sustainable roof typologies from satellite images.
Impact & Results:
- The Roofpedia Index 
- https://ual.sg/publication/2021-land-roofpedia/

Description:
Foundation for Public Code is an initiative to help public organizations (collectively) develop and maintain public codes, so the updates and benefits are more cost effective and involving more local stewardship and control along the process. 

The initiative enables public purpose software and policy that is open and collaborative by building a unique association or network of ambitious public organizations that share codebases for the benefit of citizens, and understand the importance of codebase stewardship and co-development for scaling open source policy projects. In that sense, the initiative's goals are: 1) to enable a public code software ecosystem, 2) to build a collaborative community of cities and developers to maintain code products, 3) to provide a certification system for software, as well as meeting the standard of the industry.

They deliver two main solutions to the public sector. First, the data stewardship mechanism - to guarantee a trustworthy, usable, maintained, and sustainable public code.  Second, the standard - a guidance document that gives a step-by-step model for building open source solutions, thus bringing private sector best practices.
Novelty: Initiative that support public sector's open and crowdsourced code development, maintenance, and improvements.
Impact & Results:
In numbers:
- 20 municipalities / 4 national governments working with co-created open codes 
- Developing 4 national frameworks for municipal code collaboration

One example: 
A coalition of +45 municipalities in the Netherlands co-created OpenZaak (an open procurement platform) based on a market consultation to run a shared codebase governance and community dynamics.

Description:
TechDetector is a platform that intends to bridge the gap between sustainable development challenges and accelerating technologies. The tool was created to simplify the understanding and assessment of emerging technologies regarding sustainable development. First developed in 2019-2020 through a partnership between GIZ and Envisioning, the study gathered hundreds of technology applications, assessed according to their technological maturation (TRL assessment) and sustainability evaluation.

In 2021, there is a new chapter of the techDetector. The main mission of the techDetector remains the same: to assist people in bridging the gap between emerging challenges and accelerating technologies. However, the new version includes many upgrades such as continuous technology assessment and update, workshop modules and new narrative formats (e.g. case studies, articles, interviews, future scenarios, and much more). Its goal is not only to present and analyze future technologies but also to engage tech-based sustainability mindsets through various stimuli.

The radar showcases stories as narratives that describe how emerging technologies behave when applied to specific circumstances and industries. From future scenarios to case studies, we illustrate the impact of emerging technologies through stories. Whereas the sectors are areas of expertise within GIZ's sectoral divisions. There are currently seven different sectors comprised of various topics related to sustainable development. Furthermore, information is classified too in technology domains and technology applications groups.
Novelty: A platform that intends to bridge the gap between sustainable development challenges and accelerating technologies
Impact & Results: More than 160 initiatives mapped.

Description:
REInA (Rural European Innovation Area) is a pan-European open platform which aims to gather innovative initiatives to support the creation of a new Rural European Innovation Area. The goal is to become the loudspeaker for European rural area voices, pushing their development, growth, business and natural resources by a fair, green and sustainable management. The platform will also present relevant challenges, calls, contests and other actions, all of them related to rural and territorial verticals.

Innovation ecosystems can provide highly skilled jobs in technology areas and a new future for the rural communities. The development of rural innovation ecosystems will allow rural communities to benefit from the third wave (IT and biotech startups) and fourth wave (deep-tech startups) of innovation creating high quality jobs in rural areas.
Novelty: A platform that creates an exhibition hall, a matchmaking platform and crowdsourcing initiatives with a focus on rural areas of Europe.
Impact & Results: The project is still ongoing, no results possible to visualize yet.

Description:
