Novel models of community mobilisation and participation in cities are needed to identify dynamic hot spots for two global mosquito species that can cause dengue, Zika and chikungunya outbreaks. We optimised a simple, colorimetric-based (LAMP) diagnostic kit (LDK) to provide a versatile diagnostic tool to rapidly identify these species. Our point-of-care kit can empower community-based mosquito surveillance to help mitigate disease outbreaks by improving autonomy in localised decision-making.

Innovation: We developed a simple, inexpensive diagnostic kit based on widely used point-of-care diagnostic technology (LAMP) to increase the regional capacity for urban mosquito surveillance to be serviced by decentralised hubs. T
Novelty: The LDK is a novel molecular tool to enhance the spatial and temporal granularity of urban Aedes mosquito monitoring programs. Current ‘early-warning’ of invasions into South-east Queensland rely on a small partnership program between Queensland Health and councils that links ovitraps to a PCR test from the centralised laboratory. Our LDK is a low-cost, simple and rapid alternative to increase capacity to identify target Aedes species without expensive equipment and consumables or specialist training.
Impact & Results: Blinded testing of target species created confidence in the proficiency of end-users and highlighted areas for further refinement to standardize interpretation of colorimetric results. Hubs will be able to deliver those programs over the next 12 months.

Project/Initiative Name: Portal Único de Becas “Beca tu Futuro” (Unique Scholarship Portal)
Organisation: Government Offices of Information Technology and Communications
Description: "Beca tu Futuro" is a web platform that aims to concentrate all scholarship opportunities in a single portal that documents and manages the whole process from the publication to the award of all scholarships. The portal provides benefits to all the citizens of the Dominican Republic who wish to apply for a scholarship. Moreover, the portal serves as a management tool for the scholarships evaluators and the stakeholders from the Minister of Higher Education, Science and Technology (MESCyT), with a front-end user interface and a back office for management.
Novelty: The Unique Scholarship Portal is innovative because it gathers all the scholarships opportunities from a national level in one digital platform. In doing so, it provides a fair opportunity for every applicant, improving the workflow and processes for both the final user and the administrators of the scholarships.
Impact & Results: As to the latest count the Portal has registered more than 130,000 applicants. More than 30 educational universities have been registered in the platform and more than 100 academic scholarship offers are with the universities. On the portal, 41,059 applications for scolarships were conducted which resulted in 5,516 scholarships granted. Now the portal has been stablished as the sole channel of publication for scholarships.

Project/Initiative Name: MiLAB – Govtech and Public Impact Laboratory
Description:
MiLAB - Govtech and Public Impact Laboratory, aims to contribute on the digital transformation acceleration of the public sector, by connecting it, through collaboration and open innovation strategies, with start-ups and SMEs that uses emergent technologies and innovative methodologies. MiLAB successfully specialized its activity within the public impact ecosystem, attending the high demand and global tendency of rely on digital innovator among the Government.

MiLAB designed a methodology, based on the Public Procurement of Innovation tool, with 5 phases, i) Call for Challenges, ii) Identifying the challenge, iii) Connecting Govtech solutions, iv) Strengthening Govtech Solutions, v) Implementation. This process enables the public entity to make an innovative public purchase.
Novelty: MiLAB  facilitates the understanding and implementation of emergent technologies, in order to improve public administrations and their relationship with citizens.
Impact & Results:
Results:
- 27 identified public challenges and 24 connected with the govtech solvers ecosystem
- 35 govtech solutions strengthened
- Strategic management and execution of the technical secretary for the Regulatory Sandbox Committee
- 185 Govtech and public impact start-ups and SMEs mapped
- Led the Govtech Leaders Alliance, with 20 member countries such as: Lithuania, Brazil, Spain and Poland.

Project/Initiative Name: A handbook through the care pathway for people with neurocognitive disorders (dementia)
Description:
The handbook makes it possible to support the safe and functional life of people with neurocognitive disorders (NCDs, such a dimentia), to develop the overall care pathway and treatment practices, and to ensure the memory care skills of professionals. The handbook is the first overall description of care pathway development. It describes the entire development process from an idea to the introduction of results, monitoring of outcomes and continuous development.

It is based on the development of the NCDs pathway project carried out by the City of Espoo between 2018 and 2022. The goal of the project was to execute the national memory programme launched by the Finnish Ministry of Social Affairs and Health and to develop good treatment practices that support the well-being of people with NCDs and their loved ones.

The goals are: 
1. People with NCDs get the service they need at the right time in the right place. 
2. People with NCDs live safely and functionally at home. 
3. People with NCDs and their loved ones get the support and help they need smoothly. 
4. Cooperation between organisations and service areas improves and the division of work becomes clearer.
Novelty:
Unlike our inspiration, the dementia pathway in England, our path starts long before people have any symptoms of NCDs. We have implemented the FINGER model (a multi-domain lifestyle intervention for the prevention of cognitive decline, which has been studied worldwide). We are trying to identify those who are at risk and advise them on how to promote healthy lifestyles that support healthy ageing.

Unlike others, we also defined that after-diagnosis follow-up is done by care coordinators who work in public social and health services. In our model, care coordinators work in the unit of Guidance and Service Counselling for Senior Citizens (known as Nestori). In the handbook, we describe a care coordinator’s way of working, responsibilities and cross-sector powers. We also looked closer at special issues, such as support for informal care families and for people with NCDs who are without loved ones.
Impact & Results:
Based on the feedback from managers, the development work has increased employees’ time with clients, reduced additional work resulting from ambiguous tasks, and improved well-being at work.

In order to compare the models of NCDs care pathways developed in different regions in Finland, we need nationally defined monitoring metrics. In the handbook, we propose that we should start using the Clinical Dementia Rating Scale nationally and collect data at the stage of diagnosis and at the stage when people with NCDs need support services for the first time.

Project/Initiative Name: Breaking Barriers in the Public Sector: The Chris Hani Baragwanath Academic Hospital Cochlear Implant Program
Description:
The Chris Hani Baragwanath Academic Hospital (CHBAH) Cochlear Implant Program, situated in a large public sector hospital in South Africa, works to restore hearing to adults and children with severe-profound hearing loss. The team has implemented international protocols and engaged with patients to reduce cultural, financial and geographical barriers to access. The team is constantly working towards providing a sustainable, contextually relevant service to the communities we serve.

This innovation started in 2020, when the team engaged in strategic planning sessions to contextualize services and embark on the inclusion of patients in service planning. The team identified the need for peer support and started running monthly support groups to this end. Then the team obtained external funding to employ one of its cochlear implant recipients to work within the team to provide peer support and well as to assist the team in the codesign and coproduction of services with patients.

Further, during the COVID-10 lockdown in March 2020, the team were prohibited from bringing patients into the hospital. Within one month it was able to move all speech therapy sessions for children with hearing loss onto virtual platforms, becoming among the first in the South African public sector to obtain permission to engage in telehealth services and resulting in a 300% increase in patient attendances. The hybrid model of telehealth was maintained after the pandemic, reducing geographical and financial access barriers for patients who live many hours' drive away.
Novelty: The team acknowledges that international models cannot simply be replicated within their context. Since 2020, it has focused on pioneering a model for cochlear implantation, working to increase financial, cultural, and geographical access to patients. The team engages in extensive multidisciplinary teamwork with clustering of appointments and host patient-led support groups, providing culturally appropriate, peer-based support.
Impact & Results: The innovation has scaled from two implants a year in 2006 to 50 implants in 2022. Funding has been secured for cochlear implants, device maintenance and upgrades, with a total spend of over $4 million. Careful data capturing and descriptive statistics have allowed for outcome monitoring and thematic analysis has been done through focus groups and questionnaires. The team has achieved Key Performance indicator, with waiting times decreasing from 96 weeks in 2019 to 30 weeks in 2022. 96% of recipients can hear soft sounds. 80% of patients report little or no participation restrictions after receiving intervention with increased involvement in family and community life (vs. 98% indicating severe participation restrictions prior). 100% of the caregivers of pediatric recipients report satisfaction with services. The use of hybrid telehealth and face-to-face intervention has resulted in a 300% increase in weekly attendances compared to 2019 and an attendance rate of 84%.

Organisation: City of Greater Sudbury and CANN Forecast
Description:
Water networks are confronted with aging infrastructure, increased urban population and climate change. The City of Greater Sudbury has collaborated with CANN Forecast to implement InteliPipes, an AI-based decision-support system that leverages various data sources to improve the overall reliability of its water network. The City can (a) understand better the network’s degradation over time, (b) tailor inspection plans and replacement programs and (c) optimize watermains replacement.

The likelihood of failure of a given watermain depends not only on its physical and structural characteristics, but also on environmental and operational factors. Given the very high number of parameters that can reduce the useful life of a watermain, a computational, AI-based approach can leverage existing water infrastructure and break history data to identify groups of pipes that are most at risk of failure. Further, cost of failure estimation has traditionally been a highly subjective endeavor, which can be attributed to the fact that - direct and indirect - economic, social, and environmental costs are usually difficult to quantify and compare. However, by combining historical work order data and domain knowledge from municipal staff, it is possible to build a data-driven model that provides the most up-to-date insights regarding the potential socio- economic impacts of a future break, for all linear assets.

The City of Greater Sudbury has made investments in the past to collect data on its water infrastructure, watermain breaks and work order history. As such, these datasets can now be used in conjunction with Machine Learning algorithms to identify the most critical pipes in terms of overall risk, combining their likelihood of failure and their cost of failure. The use of innovative, data-driven decision-making tools will help ensure that future investments have the greatest positive impact while limiting adverse consequences related to watermain breaks.
Novelty: The innovation is novel because it makes use of machine learning algorithms applied in the field of water infrastructure management. Further, it does so with attention to explainability of AI and a human-centric approach.
Impact & Results: The data-driven decision support tool is now being used by several City of Greater Sudbury departments to better understand their water network degradation over time, and optimize inspection plans and replacement programs.  InteliPipes was able to identify 25Km (2.8 % of the total network) with a break rate of more than 70 breaks/100km/year which is above 10 times the normal rate of failure for the City of Greater Sudbury.

Description:
InfoNile is a cross-border initiative led by a group of geojournalists with a mission to uncover critical stories on water issues in the Nile River Basin through data-based multimedia storytelling. The platform provides story grants, training and mentorship to journalists across the Nile Basin to help them conduct in-depth stories on issues of water, environment, biodiversity and climate change. They highlight stories of solutions that are working to solve issues such as climate change and wildlife trafficking in local communities by approaching to innovation and collective actions.
 
In partnership with Code for Africa, InfoNile trains and mentor journalists in data journalism, visualization and geojournalism to produce original cross-border investigations, including on foreign land deals, wildlife trafficking and conservation, climate change, and the impact of large-scale dams. In such a way they promote collaboration across countries and new geo-storytelling techniques such as data-based maps, drone videos and satellite imagery.

The InfoNile platform displays also interactive and actionable maps on issues related to water and environment, and “map” their stories onto these maps to provide a human layer to the data.
Novelty: Initiative that promotes geojournalism through data-based multimedia and storytelling by applying techniques of geolocation, drone videos, satellite imaginary, code development and qualitative approaches.
Impact & Results:
152 journalists trained
253 stories produced
6 cross-border investigations
NileWell platform
Youth SciComm Competition
InfoNile Whatsapp Chat

Description:
The UK Government Policy Profession has piloted a new model called Shared Policy Capability Project to help government departments go beyond traditional capability building activities by better understand their wider policy making environment and to identify opportunities for improvements. Through this the government aims to build capability at a department level and enable policy makers to take an active role in improving the environment in which they make policy.

The model was created based on research conducted with over 100 policy makers of different grades and from different departments across UK Government. So far, the model has been co-designed and tested with a single department. However, there are plans to scale this across government. In this model, the department puts together a diverse working group representing different policy relevant areas and of different grades, that would undertake the self-assessment using the policy making environment framework.

The self-assessment is done with a series of 10 sessions facilitated by the Shared Policy Capability team. Through the 10 sessions the group collectively makes a diagnosis of their policy making environment by: (i) defining key and unique components of their policy making environment, (ii) mapping the strengths and weaknesses of their department’s policy making capability, (iii) selecting capability areas from the framework most important to them, (iv) conducting interviews with colleagues to gather more evidence, (v) framing self-assessment statements on the selected capability areas.
Novelty:
The Shared Policy Capability Project is an innovation in capability building because:
1) While efforts have been made to assess departments’ general capability, or the capability of its policymakers. This is the first model, in the UK, that seeks to improve policymaking capability by looking into policy making environments and applying that at department level.
2) It is agile, collaborative, and bottom up, giving policy makers at working level the opportunity to reflect and to drive the diagnosis process. The agility and flexibility of the model allows policy makers to consider unique constraints of their policy making environment in the diagnosis and to adjust it towards their needs.
3) It is people-centred by using experienced-based qualitative data and providing safe space for people to share their experiences.

Project/Initiative Name: Photodentro LS: Photodentro Learning Scenario Repository & Learning Scenario Designer tool
Description:
Photodentro LS is a new Greek national open digital repository and e-service for teachers and researchers, to design, author, classify, publish and share online structured learning scenarios with didactive activities as well as to search, adapt and reuse learning scenarios shared by others. It fosters the role of teachers as content creators, inspiring them to take an active role in the creation of instructional materials, and can be used to support teachers’ professional development programs.

The Computer Technology Institute and Press developed with open source tools the Photodentro Learning Scenario platform and e-service, which includes the following three modules:
1. The Photodentro LS Repository, an open digital educational repository, for hosting, organizing, classifying, publishing, and openly sharing structured Learning Scenarios and LS Templates
2. The Photodentro LS Designer, an online learning design environment, where teachers are guided step-by-step to create structured learning scenarios with didactic activities, starting from online, pre-defined LS Templates and following specialized pedagogical guidelines and suggestions
3. The Photodentro LS Template Designer, an online tool, which allows scientific experts to define and share online custom pedagogical LS Templates.
Novelty: Photodentro LS provides an organized, secure, easy to use, and dynamic environment that both assists and trains teachers, pedagogues, and researchers in the process of designing structured learning scenarios. It constitutes the only open public digital instructional design tool addressed to Greek educator. Further, it is the only public and open too that supports custom Templates for learning scenarios with adaptable structure and pedagogy.
Impact & Results: In one year of operation, the Photodentro LS Repository & LS Designer tool has had approximately 24.700 visits mainly from Greece but also from the U.S.A., Cyprus, Germany, the U.K., etc. Presently, it has 2.200 registered users, while approximately 2.800 learning scenarios and templates have been downloaded, in order to be used by the educational community. The e-service has been utilized by university faculties from all over Greece as part of student training, while it has also been adopted by teacher training programs.

Organisation: BHTrans - Belo Horizonte Transport and Transit Company
Description:
Most Brazilian metropolises have a transport network operated by a private company which makes it difficult for transport authorities to have a bright view of the public transport functioning. TRANCITY is a public transport monitoring dashboard which integrates different sources of data, such as bus location, ticketing and cameras, providing real time and historical information that supports management, planning and the operation of public transport networks, with data driven evidence.

As the volume of data grows, analyses become more complex and demand greater computing power, storage space, and more advanced processing systems. Many types of data are generated whose complexity and unstructured nature makes decision-making difficult. Trancity comes to revolutionise traditional transport planning methodologies assisting public servants on their daily transit management and control tasks, and finally contributing to the creation of more human and sustainable cities and providing better service to their citizens.

Trancity is a product of Scipopulis, a private company focused on making cities become smart. BHTrans officials, a PPP responsible for urban mobility in Belo Horizonte, was partner in the pilot.
Novelty:
Trancity combine the use of cutting-edge technologies in the computing area, such as cloud infrastructure, platforms and techniques for processing huge amount of data (big data of real time location and ticketing system from thousands of buses, alerts such as flooding, traffic accidents, and video monitoring), the use of machine learning algorithms for prediction and automation of data analysis and recent techniques to protect privacy in the processing of personal data.

Trancity also has a module that estimates the volume of greenhouse gas and air pollutant emissions, from each bus circulating through the city, based its average speed and details about the vehicle (such as its engine technology, and type of fuel). The platform is offered as a Software as a Service, with unlimited number of users for a given organisation, and every new feature implemented is available for all users and clients.
Impact & Results: Trancity has already been used by 15 different cities in Latin America and Europe. To date, the number of buses monitored on the platform, which today stands at 28,000, corresponds to 16 million passengers and a total of more than 56 million citizens who can benefit from improved services, planned from the use of the Trancity.

Organisation: Ministry of Foreign Affairs, Ministry of Digital Transformation
Description:
Diia. (Action) Digital State is the largest-scale digital project in Ukraine. Its goal is to make it easy and transparent for individuals and business to communicate with their government. The platform is the access point to all public services that the government provides to individuals and businesses. 

The purpose of this site is that "everything is clear, quick and easy-to-understand". The services have been completely streamlined, the language is simple, and the interface is based on UX/UI design. The Ministry of Digital Transformation plans for 100% of government services to be accessible online by 2024. 

The main goal is to make 100% public services available online. As of the 1 of April 2021, almost 4.5 million Ukrainians have updated the Diia 2.0 application. There are more than 3.6 million users of the Diia portal by now — over 50 government services are available online. Mobile application Diia 2.0 allows Ukrainians to access 9 digital documents (ID card, foreign biometric passport, student card, driver’s license, vehicle registration certificate, vehicle insurance policy, tax number, birth certificate, IDP certificate) and 12 services in total.

The concept of the digital state was preceded by the development of Diia as a national brand, which was also done with the support of the EGAP Program. The Diia brand stands for “the State and I” and is based on the contemporary idea that interactions between individuals and their government should be easy, understandable and quick.
Novelty: A platform that enables the concept of a Digital State and makes possible that Ukrainians have access to 9 key digital documents and 12 services in total.
Impact & Results:
Diia portal is a single state portal with over 70 digital services
The service has already been used by 370,000 private entrepreneurs and more than 4,500 companies.

Description:
Glass is a Silicon Valley e-Commerce startup with a focus on how governments buy. Glass Commerce platform and software powers government marketplaces for convenient and compliant small purchases. They build a marketplace enabling compliant and paperless transactions, thus helping Local, State and Federal agencies make inclusive, sustainable, and compliant purchases through government-exclusive market environments. 

Once on the platform, government authorities can supervise every cardholder using an integrated software, and to obtain more accurate budget insights through a specific Budget Breakdown system. The platform is cloud-based and enables compliant transactions between government buyers and pre-verified vendors without bids or paperwork.

The mission of the innovation is to streamline small government purchases. Specifically, by maximizing value for taxpayers' money, increase the quality and variety of products and services acquired by governments, diversify and expand the pool of available government vendors, and simplify decision-making for public employees.

Through the platform, officers can: 
- Have access Curated Products: Search the marketplace's daily updated inventory of products and services, from safety and industrial equipment.
- Shop Verified Vendors: All vendors are verified in advance and count with business certifications and clearances from U.S. blacklists.
- Choose How to Pay: Select the most convenient payment method for your agency, from P-Card and PO to Wire Transfer and Check.
- Track Orders & Spending: Small purchases dashboards that enable easy tracking, flagging, and returning of orders from different vendors in one single place.
Novelty: A cloud-based platform enabling compliant transactions between government buyers and pre-verified vendors without bids or paperwork.
Impact & Results:
$4 MILLION+ Procured using our platforms and tools
5,826,451 Products Delivered
20+ Government partners and clients
15,000+ Verified products and services
500+ Verified government vendors

Project/Initiative Name: GTAP - Electronic Citizen's Charter in Government
Description:
Guide to Transaction and Processes (GTAP) is one of the newest innovations in GSIS that supports the government’s thrust to simplify processes and make services convenient to stakeholders.

GTAP enables GSIS members and pensioners to electronically access the complete and latest information they need to transact with GSIS, covering a checklist of documentary requirements, procedures including those for filing of complaints, and a turnaround time to conclude each process. It is a handy guide for filing claims and loans, GTAP empowers members and pensioners.

GTAP is an award-winning self-service facility where members can quickly obtain on-demand information on GSIS services that will guide them in their transactions. GTAP bagged the 2020 Silver Stevie Award for Innovative Use of Technology in Customer Service and a trailblazer in service delivery.
Novelty: Guide to Transaction and Processes (GTAP) is one of the newest innovations in GSIS that supports the government’s thrust to simplify processes and make services convenient to stakeholders.
Impact & Results: Impacts and results information not available

Project/Initiative Name: IRIS Core - Centralizes all Government's Data and Tools
Description:
IRIS is a software that centralises all data and tools in one single place allowing Incident Commanders to obtain real-time situational awareness and coordinate the teams in a simpler and safer way. 

This is the first intelligent assistant designed to bring real-time situational awareness on every stage of an incident. It helps Incident Commanders to cope seamlessly with saturation & coordination at the scene of the incident. It was created with the aim of helping emergency services work in a safer way, by developing simple end-user focused technology. We believe technology can unlock operative response for emergency services worldwide.
Novelty: A software that centralizes all the data and tools required for an adequate emergency response bridging tactical and operational commanders.
Impact & Results:
Rating is made by the IE PublicTech Lab, a center from the Instituto de Empresa expert in public digital transformation and generation of gov tech ecosystems, and SILO, an expert consulting firm in driving innovation through new models of public-private collaboration.
Unblur is mentioned in a Public Management and integrity section.
https://lnkd.in/eEsnQkp
Other relevant URL(s) - eg reports, pres:
https://www.unblur.co/en/iris/#iris-mobile-tablet 
https://www.unblur.co/en/top-100-govtech-startups-in-latam-spain/ 
The Latin America's Bank for Development (CAF) invested in Unblur: https://www.caf.com/es/actualidad/noticias/2022/07/caf-invierte-en-unblur-para-impulsar-el-sector-govtech-en-europa/

Description:
CHAISE is a 4-year transnational initiative funded by the European Commission under the Erasmus+, Sector Skill Alliance call for proposals, to set forward a sectoral approach to Blockchain Skills Development.

CHAISE will formulate and deliver a European strategy to address skill mismatches and shortages in the Blockchain Sector and deliver appropriate and future focused training, qualifications and mobility solutions, geared towards sectoral realities and needs.

The Blockchain sector is challenged by talent shortages, global competitive pressures, limited connection between education and the market, and low responsiveness from formal education to new workplace requirements.
Objectives
- Improve Blockchain skills intelligence and document prevailing skills mismatches at the EU level.
- Set up a collaborative approach to monitoring the evolution of workplace requirements and anticipating future Blockchain skill needs, to act as an early warning information mechanism for imbalances between demand and supply.
- Design a European learning outcome-oriented modular VET programme and educational resources on Blockchain, to address technical, non-technical and cross-disciplinary (horizontal) skills requirements.
- Define EU-wide occupational requirements for the Blockchain workforce, to address fragmentation in the labour market.
- Establish a sectoral qualification linked to the new Blockchain specific occupational profile, to set common educational requirements for Blockchain Skills across the EU.
- Connect jobseekers and Blockchain companies to support professional transnational mobility and increase the attractiveness of the Blockchain sector.
- Set up a post project permanent cooperation network to systematically monitor labour market and skill developments, and keep the European Blockchain Skills Strategy up-to-date and relevant.
Novelty: Based on a thorough analysis on skills needs and shortages to define the current state-of-play and demarcate the Blockchain sector, the CHAISE consortium will develop a European Blockchain Skills Strategy that will set the framework for sectoral cooperation and outline the actions to address the evolving skills needs in the blockchain sector.
Impact & Results: CHAISE will develop a 5-semester VET programme in 11 EU languages with an innovative approach

Description:
The Ecosystem Digital Product Passport (CIRPASS) prepares the ground for gradual piloting and deployment of the Digital Product Passports (DPPs).

CIRPASS wants to open up possibilities for innovative workflows, especially to further the circularity of the flow of tangible goods, but also for consumer information. The initial focus is on three priority areas: the electric & electronics, battery, and textile sectors while laying the groundwork for a cross-sectoral DPP based on common rules, principles, taxonomy, and standards. More specifically, the design and introduction of DPPs relate to technological feasibility and technical support for circular economy strategies, conformity to the needs of various stakeholders especially companies and consumers, economic viability, and a contribution to monitoring and guiding the introduction of the circular economy by political and administrative actors.

Objectives
- Present an unambiguous cross-sectoral definition and description of the DPP
- Define a cross-sectoral product data model for the DPP with demonstrated usefulness for the Circular Economy
- Propose an open DPP data exchange protocol adapted to the needs of CE stakeholders and propose such a protocol based on up-to-date digital technologies
- Build stakeholder consensus on key data for circularity and related open European and global vocabulary standards to be included in the DPP for the batteries, electronics, and textiles value chains.
- Develop use cases and roadmaps for piloting, deployment, and circular business value generation of cross-sectoral DPPs
Novelty: Project to prepare the ground for gradual piloting and deployment of the Digital Product Passports (DPPs).
Impact & Results:
The project aims to deliver at least three DPP prototypes for the three sectors as well as reaching agreements on key data for circularity and sustainability. 
Besides the collaboration of project partners, stakeholder engagement is a crucial part of CIRPASS’s action plan. An ecosystem for stakeholder engagement will be set up.

Project/Initiative Name: PolicyKeys: Where Can We Agree (AI Assisted Digital Engagement Game)
Description:
PolicyKeys is firstly a robust one-page narrative tool built around a ground truth so an AI can assist in predicting support for any public policy solution. PolicyKeys is secondarily a role-playing game to help player's better understand their own internal conflicts, as well as better understand their fellow citizen's differing beliefs - the result is a leaderboard of ideas that have deep and wide acceptance approaching consensus. 

The one-page narrative tool helps the puzzle creator organize 32 Yes and No reasons, not cherry-picked, themed around the four-sides of a politically balanced table of 128 societal roles. The building of the puzzle gives many ahah moments for the organizers. The method could be used to score and rank every possible public policy solution. 

Citizens can play the sixteen games in a weekly puzzle to get a 360º view of the topic, puzzle, proposed solution, the best reasons to say Yes or No, which the roles might say and why, and at the end of the game be markedly better informed. Most players will come to realize we probably agree more than disagree, and the disagreements are more focused and more easily understood and discussed. Consensus ideas will naturally rise up the leaderboard, and perhaps become foregone conclusions.

PolicyKeys is designed to be a smoothie of crosswords (short clues), trivia (overall knowledge), role playing (empathy), mystery (solve the role’s motivation), poker (find each role’s tell), chess puzzles (best answers are so cool), jigsaw puzzles (how the roles fit together), and word games (sometimes there's no choice remaining but to guess). The initial AI is 256 distinct, seemingly deadlocked “supreme courts” of 9 roles, 128 with an implied 5-4 bias to change, and 128 with a 5-4 bias for status quo. After a puzzle is scored the AI identifies which remained deadlocked 5-4 and/or 4-5 which is an internal reliability error margin for the policy’s score.

Governments can use the tool themselves, partner with the private sector, and/or set-up a nonpartisan commission to narrate, rate, create and curate the best solutions. The private sector can do the same in the form of media outlets looking to compete to be the most trusted source for public policy leadership news.
Novelty: What's different about PolicyKeys is that it is role-based. People self-identify with some number of the 128 roles on the game-board. It becomes clear very quickly that these roles will have differing views of the proposed solution. The player comes to understand that it is natural to be conflicted about proposed solutions. This also presents as empathy for the roles in society that are not well known by the player. The ahah moment is that we agree on lots more than we are lead to believe
Impact & Results: We have a working alpha test and expect to launch a weekly public policy puzzle in the US in November. The book that the game is based on Politics 4.0: How Gamification, AI, and Idea Leaderboards Can Help You Depolarize the World is scheduled to be released Spring 2023 probably from Real Clear Politics publishing. There are 50+ weekly puzzles awaiting final quality checks before being published.

Project/Initiative Name: Resolving Community Problems by Helping Rather than Punishing
Description:
Community Protection was a way to use penalties for people who would not comply with community rules. But after undertaking an analysis we discovered that this makes people's lives worse. We now help those people who are anti-social, to overcome a the cause of the problem in their lives, and the results for both lower cost and better lives.

We decided to create a prototype of a new approach using systems thinking methodology. The prototype was designed around firstly understanding the problem that anti-social people had. We did this by approaching people in their homes, without our official behaviour. We went there to listen.
Once we had an understanding, and how that was linked to the anti-social behaviour, we then helped that person to get their part of their life back together. After doing this for many people, we realised that this caused people to stop offending, and for us to use less resources than we did before.
Novelty:
We see this as innovative because it is focused on an approach that was in opposition to what we believed. And it created front line teams that had to work using their initiative rather than procedures.

1. Our innovative approach consisted of using systems thinking to understand the problem form the citzens perspective.

2. Then we used an agile prototyping method to discover solutions that the staff would never have believed would work.

3. Lastly helped manager and staff to develop delegated teams, and new learning management approaches.
Impact & Results:
The outcomes were a drop of repeat demands by 50%. There was a drop in time required to deal with problems of up to 30%. In some cases the staff spent longer with the public, but the end result meant that the time was saved in the long term.

In addition, the impact on staff has been a significant rise in their motivation as they now have to think for themselves rather than follow rules. They see real results of their actions.

Project/Initiative Name: Smart policy development for circular economy and extended producer responsibility
Description:
In the year 2019 the EU adopted a new directive to reduce the amount of plastics in the oceans while boosting progress towards a circular economy.  

Estimates show that 80 to 85% of marine litter in the EU consist of plastics. Of this, 27% are fishing-related items, and now a number of operators and agencies are coming together to implement a producer responsibility scheme designed to deal with the plastic instead of it ending up in the sea.

The producer responsibility scheme is set to be fully up and running on 31 December 2024; it means that the operators who manufacture and sell products will also be responsible for their collection and recycling. From 1 November 2022, producers of fishing gear – i.e. operators selling fishing gear on the Swedish market – must register with the Swedish Environmental Protection Agency.

In the year 2021 two Swedish Government agencies teamed up with Sotenas municipality, running Sweden’s only marine recycling center in order to test and create new policy that both fulfills the upcoming EU directive about extended producer responsibility and the transformation into a circular economy. 

The innovation work targets the challenge of plastic in the oceans consisting of fishing gear. The work is about how enterprise, local government, Government agencies and organisations within the EU can create polices and practices that works on all levels via 'practice-policy-feedback-loops', system innovation and work outside the comfort zone for agencies. The approach and way of working can benefit the oceans and other public initiatives aimed at transformation of praxis and policy.
Novelty:
Our work is innovative in: How we foresaw an upcoming challenge and acted early, and how we identified a problem and challenge that wasn’t addressed and was in between our organisations operational space.

We framed the innovation work and the collaboration with stakeholders such as an local authority that we as Government agencies doesn’t do. We experimented with different methods and tools such as the municipality doing test with collection of fishing lines in shops, participating in hackathon with the challenge of 'free riders' of the extended producer responsibility, developing and using new tools and methods for our sector such as praxis-policy feedback loops, organisational/business model canvas, and service design. We have done something new, created insight and new value.
Impact & Results:
- We identified a grand challenge of creating sustainable and profitable business models for the labor intensive work of taking care of used fishing gear. 
- For the participating agencies the results are measured in how well the new extended producer responsibility is executed with in the agencies and how well the measure impact as intended. 
- Tangible results are officially measured in weight and percentage collected and producers customer satisfaction.

Project/Initiative Name: Using Drone Technology for Effective Service Delivery
Description:
The province uses drones to do oversight of infrastructure projects within the province. It has addressed a challenge of lack of transparency and improved the quality of service delivery.

Drone technology has been able to expose the practical performance of the infrastructure funded projects from the provincial administration as well as to show inadequacies where infrastructure was delivered, being substandard or costs not aligned to the performance.
Novelty: It starts with transparency: A picture says it all, oversight structures don’t rely only on what is written on the report. The picture/video if free from any attempt to hide something. With transparency, we are now starting to see the quality of work improving. People are being held to account, which improves professionalization. We then see more credible and improved value for money on the projects that are being carried out.
Impact & Results:
The National Treasury has recommended the use of drones to all other provinces in South Africa on their infrastructure site visits, In one meeting i was invited to, they encouraged all to come to the province to benchmark on the technology.

It was after First National Bank (FNB) saw the pictures and video of the disaster area that they sponsored a relief effort in Deelpan, a village struck by floods.

Project/Initiative Name: Agora.brussels- engaging citizens drawn by lot in participatory, inclusive, deliberative democracy
Description:
Agora.brussels was set up by dozens of volunteers keen to reinvigorate democracy. The movement has piloted deliberative assemblies of citizens since 2020 and aims at institutionalising them. Gathering people drawn by lottery from all strata of the Brussels population, four citizens’ assemblies have deliberated and made proposals on a number of regional priorities. Agora’s elected MP promotes them inside the Brussels Parliament while Agora members push for their implementation outside Parliament.

The resulting assembly mirrors Brussels population in terms of age, gender and level of education.
The newly established assembly then chooses a regional priority to focus on. Themes chosen so far include housing, employment, energy and climate.

Deliberations consist of online exchanges and from 4 to 6 full-day sessions over a period of 4 to 5 months. Key steps are: 
-             Exploration of the chosen theme with a panel of experts and identification of sub-topics
-             Discussion in smaller groups and generation of ideas and draft proposals
-             Fact checking, examination of existing legislation and programmes and production of detailed proposals 
-             Validation of proposals 
-             Presentation of proposals to the government and the press
Novelty:
Given that none of the existing political parties were genuinely open to citizens’ direct involvement in policymaking, Agora brussels ran in the regional elections in 2019 and won one seat in the regional Parliament. The mandate of Agora’s MP is shaped and constrained by citizens’ assemblies. The MP voices proposals emanating from them inside Parliament. This is a new way of combining participatory and representative democracy. 

This unique feature comes in addition to an accumulation of novel methods and tools to reach out to residents in Brussels; randomly select participants in citizens assemblies; provide expertise from different angles, nurture deliberation; facilitate discussions in a neutral way; relay citizens’ proposals in and outside Parliament, evaluate, learn and improve.
Impact & Results:
Quantitative results. as measured by internal and independent evaluators till Sept. 2022, are: 
-9508 randomly selected people invited to participate in 4 Brussels citizens’ assemblies; 
-973 positive replies 
-219 finally selected
-3 resolutions (on housing, employment and climate/energy) with some 40 proposals each to improve life in Brussels 
-all resolutions and positions presented by Agora’s MP in Parliament
-104 comments formulated by Agora’s MP on draft legislation put forward by other MPs in areas tackled by the assemblies
-97 parliamentary questions asked to the government in line with the assemblies’ proposals 

Maximising the impact of the assemblies’ proposals is our priority for 2023.

Project/Initiative Name: X-Road Message Rooms for Proactive Life Events
Description:
Public authorities need a secure and unified way to notify other authorities about events happening in a citizen’s life. Based on the notifications, various processes are then triggered to provide the citizen with services related to the event. The X-Road Message Room feature provides a secure and unified way to deliver the notifications between public authorities and therefore, it’s a key enabler for implementing proactive life event based public services.

Estonia and Finland had already developed and implemented their national data exchange layers to enable interoperability across government agencies of the same country, through X-Road. Therefore, it was logical to expand the capabilities of X-Road, instead of designing and building a new separate tool. In this way, also the existing X-Road users could benefit from the new capabilities.
Novelty: The Message Room feature in X-Road is fully decentralised, and the implementation doesn’t have any centralised components that have access to the data that is exchanged. Instead, all the messages are exchanged directly between the publisher and subscribers. Therefore, both publishers and subscribers are in full control of their own data.
Impact & Results:
Since the innovation is still in a PoC phase, no results and impacts have been measured yet. However, the innovation has a potential for huge impacts since it enables a new and proactive way of delivering public services.

Making the Message Room feature available through the national data exchange platforms means that all the existing user organisations will get access to it without any additional actions. 

The Message Room feature will bring savings to public sector IT costs by utilising existing technology instead deploying new one. Providing proactive life-event based services to citizens speeds up and automates service processes and provides citizens with better services.

Project/Initiative Name: Make Strategy Work -  Planning Process & Platform
Description:
The City of Ashdod has pioneered a new strategic planning model, in collaboration with Insights.Us. The new methodology enables city leadership to define key measurable outcomes, harnessing cross-silos action plans. The digital platform was designed to celebrate success stories, and connect the community, allowing an inclusive whole-of-city approach. 

The solution is both methodological (“how do we do government processes better?”) and practical (“what tools and mechanism should be in place to ripe technological benefits of the change?”).
Novelty:
The strategic planning process & platform is innovative in the strategy planning landscape because: 

1.           We start with outcomes. Too often, cities build their strategy based on what they do - and not where they want to get. Here, the platform resides on a strict method of "outcomes first." 
2.           We cut performance bureaucracy. Our two layers approach (goals - plans, or outcomes - outputs) makes a clear difference between strategy and project management. We enabled leaders to ask what works and not write hundreds of technical tasks. 
3.           We connect people, and build trust. Strategic Plans and Action Plans rarely speak the language of ordinary employees and citizens. We changed the language so that every can understand what the city focus is, and what happened in the field. 
4.           We leverage technology. Powered by AI to support better decisions to create a transparent, inclusive, responsive digital interface that makes strategy fun and accessible to all.
Impact & Results:
'-We moved from ~1,500 goals to only 13 strategic outcomes for the entire municipality 
-All strategic goals have been formulated in terms of measurable outcomes (what is the shift in real world we want to see), and the plan are stipulated in outputs terms (what will the organization produce)
-Most city executives have entered the platform, added their plans for next year and expressed satisfaction as for the simplicity of our design and user experience.

Project/Initiative Name: Police and Public Protection Technology - Discovery as a Service (DaaS) - Home Office
Description:
Discovery as a Service (DaaS) enables successful delivery of UK Policing Technology through standardised and outcome based discoveries to provide evidence and overcome inconsistency in project initiation. By employing user centric thinking, agile delivery, innovation, and multi-disciplinary teams, DaaS ensures new national technology initiatives provide beneficial capabilities to UK Law Enforcement so they safeguard the UK public. This service is the first of its kind in the Home Office.

Discovery as a Service (DaaS) enables successful delivery of UK Policing Technology by standardising and embedding best practise into Project Initiation. Historically, Discoveries were fragmented across portfolios, each utilising different methods and approaches often delivering limited to no value.

The DaaS mission is: To accelerate and de-risk digital delivery to realise early benefits. We strive to fully understand the problem and explore ideas through multiple lenses. We apply expertise and diverse thinking, in collaboration with partners, to explore and develop actionable, evidence-based insights and solutions.
Novelty:
Historically, PPPT, Discoveries were fragmented across PPPT Portfolios, each utilising different approaches and tools leading to varying outputs and usefulness.

DaaS is innovative because:

1. Our methodology embeds best practise (processes and artefacts) over a portfolio delivery function of 500 FTE that has previously used inconsistent approaches. The service also provides quality assurance, uniformity, and performance metrics to track success.

2. The approach ensures users’ needs are at the heart of future system development and a “corporate knowledgebase” is created.

3. The methodology applies Mission Model Thinking, considering value created for specific customers, key activities, resources, and partners required to deliver it, as well as the cost-benefit balance.

4. Evidence based recommendations are at the core of the DaaS Service.

5. Our methodology uses Agile approaches to deliver in time-boxed sprints enabling us to pivot quickly and build value incrementally.
Impact & Results:
DaaS to date has delivered 9 Discoveries, interviewing over 336 stakeholders in 168 interviews:

1. Digitising of the UK Extradition / Mutual Legal Aid processes by designing a replacement Case Working System used by over 600 FTE. This enabled more effective handling of oversea arrest requests reducing months to weeks.

2. Designing new capability to enable Policing to access EU Vehicle Registration Data (Prum). This removes the need to follow a slow and manual process (5+ weeks to 10 seconds).

3. Designing an integrated audit capability for all UK policing systems enabling up to £8m cost saving per annum.

Organisation: Government of the Principality of Asturias - General Directorate of Public Governance, Transparency, Citizen Participation and Agenda 2030
Description:
"Plaza Mayor" has been an innovative multi-channel citizen participation project. Its ambitious conception as a wide-ranging participatory process stood out. In which both face-to-face and digital media were used simultaneously to give a voice to the entire Asturian population. The data analysis methodology used allows for an immediate implementation of the results obtained, as well as the reuse of the information in the future.

The "Plaza Mayor" project has ended. The administrative units have the proposals made corresponding to their field of action. Currently, the proposals have been considered and integrated into the services and public policies, in order to improve them.
Novelty:
The innovation lies in the simultaneous use of different face-to-face and digital channels that give the entire population the opportunity to participate while carrying out outreach and training work on citizen participation: web page,
presence of agents located in different towns in Asturias (Oviedo, Gijón, Avilés, Pola Siero, Mieres, Llanes, Navia, Ribadesella and Luarca), social and social media plan.

Furthermore, the analysis method used is great opportunity to this increase administrative transparency, having a reinforcing effect on democratic values and enabling citizen participation in public policies.
Impact & Results:
This Consultative process can be considered valid and effective, because the participation was inclusive and representative.
Nearly two thousand proposals were obtained from the entire regional territory and with a good representation of the different population groups according to gender, age or place of residence. This fact confirms that the use of different simultaneous channels has been a success.
In addition, the capacity for systematic analysis, categorized by thematic areas, has allowed the effective implementation of the results obtained.

Project/Initiative Name: Theory of change process for public services for and with youth
Description:
The UK government launched a new “youth hub” offer of employment services to young people affected by the disruption of the covid-19 pandemic. Through four days of workshops with key stakeholders, plus engaging young people, Impetus ran a theory of change process to enable one of the flagship hubs to co-design their new service to best serve their young people. This is the first time the Impetus has applied it’s theory of change tools, and it’s impact focus, to a government policy initiative.

England’s West Midlands Combined Authority (regional government) and the regional office of the government’s Department of Work approached Impetus to see if their theory of change tools could be adapted to apply to the new “youth hub” service. The government had plans for around 150 youth hubs across England, with a site in Birmingham in the West Midlands region identified as a “demonstrator” site for others to follow. 

Through a series of workshops held from May to July 2021, we aimed to bring together strategic partners. To align on:
-             A high-level mission
-             Which young people the youth hub was trying to help
-             What outcome it wanted to achieve with them
-             How best to use the resources at its proposal to achieve that
-             How to track the impact of it’s work and use that to manage performance 

The output of this work is a blueprint for what a youth hub should look like. This blueprint offers a framework for the specific Birmingham library, as well best practice which can be rolled out at other youth hubs across the country. This theory of change approach enables government policy to be translated into successful delivery by helping the frontline fill in the details behind a headline announcement.
Novelty: There is often a disconnect between the policy community of big ideas and those who ultimately have to deliver on decisions. This approach tries to bridge that gap and is the only attempt we are aware of to bring theory of change work from the third sector to government initiatives and public services
Impact & Results:
The blueprint is being implemented in Birmingham and has influenced the government’s thinking across the wider youth hubs network.

The government has not yet published outcomes data or evaluations of youth hubs, so there is no official data on the affects of the policy or the theory of change work.

Project/Initiative Name: FOODBACK: Centre d'Aprofitament Alimentari de Mercabarna.
Organisation: Mercados de Abastecimientos de Barcelona, S.A. (MERCABARNA)
Description:
Foodback is a circular bioeconomy project, located in Mercabarna, which aims to recover fruit and vegetables that have been removed from the commercial channel but are suitable for consumption, avoiding food waste. The project generates an environmental and social impact, reducing greenhouse gas emissions from the management of these surpluses as waste, contributing to access to quality food for vulnerable people and providing employment opportunities.

Mercabarna is responsible for managing the commercial waste of the Food Unit operators. The new management of organic vegetable matter contemplates a new waste management system, launched in February 2022, aimed at maximum reduction of food waste in the Food Unit and maximising the use of products that have already left the market but are still fit for consumption. 

The environmental management system has the following infrastructures for the management of organic vegetable matter:
- Containers with access control: Designed for small daily quantities. They count the quantities deposited by each operator in the different market halls.
- Foodback: Installation that receives surplus fruit and vegetables that can be used for human consumption. There, a qualitative selection of the product is made from a food safety perspective. It is then distributed to social organisations. The product that is considered unfit for human consumption is sent to the user to Punt Verd so that the product can be managed as waste.
- Green Point: Commercial waste transfer plant. It receives organic matter unfit for consumption.

Foodback is a pioneering project in the management of surplus produce in wholesale markets in Spain, aimed at giving a second life to surplus fruit and vegetables, with the priority aim of contributing to guaranteeing access to fresh fruit and vegetables of high nutritional value to people in vulnerable situations, in direct collaboration with social organisations.
Novelty:
The Foodback is part of a comprehensive strategy to reduce food waste and promote the circular economy. It is being developed with two infrastructures, in addition to the Foodback, which are linked to each other:
- Punt Verd: The nerve centre for commercial waste management at Mercabarna, since 2002. With this system the recycling rate is over 70%. 
- Smart containers at the Fruit and Vegetable Market: In 2022, new containers for vegetable waste were installed, with access control, monitoring the quantities contributed by each user.

FOODBACK brings together several pioneering principles in a single project: Waste reduction; circular bioeconomy, Fight against food waste, Promotion of social employment for groups at risk, Direct distribution to vulnerable groups, Co-management system, based on a public-private-social alliances.
Impact & Results:
The first data are (from 1/02/2022 to 31/08/2022):
- Foodback entries: 731 tonnes.
- Product utilised: 530 tonnes.
- Internal foodback utilisation rate: 76.4%.
- Total utilisation rate (product utilised / total plant organic matter managed): 8.22%. 
- 149,050 beneficiaries.
- Job placement of 7 people.
- 44 companies contributing on average per month.

Project/Initiative Name: Carbon Neutral Pathway - Camino Hacía Carbono Neutral (CHCN)
Description:
The project seeks to accelerate the process of decarbonisation of the energy and transport sectors in Colombia, working on the training and technification of processes for multiple sectors of society involved. 

The project is committed to support 115 municipalities in 5 prioritised departments, and has focused its efforts on accelerating decarbonisation initiatives in the transport and energy sectors, through rigorous support to civil servants, where they have been provided with a series of tools, training and links, which will allow them to implement decarbonisation projects in the territories.

This process includes: a toolkit as a guide for the initial construction of the projects, inclusive climate action workshops to include the differential and gender approach in the projects, funding fairs to contact the projects with relevant funding agents in the country, virtual and face-to-face training for young people in the territories, among others.
Novelty: Using digital tools, but also understanding the processes specific to each territory, CHCN has built training and support mechanisms for public officials in the different municipalities, which have enabled the development of more than 150 fundable projects in just a few months, and intersecting different areas of the public sector, academia, funders and the private sector.
Impact & Results:
Construction of 5 decarbonisation visions for the departments with the highest GHG impact.
115 prioritised municipalities, divided into 5 departments of Colombia. 
167 bankable climate action projects at national level, divided into; 42 projects in Atlántico, 52 projects in Antioquia, 15 projects in Valle del Cauca, 37 projects in Cundinamarca, and 21 projects in Boyacá. 
222 participants in virtual course on decarbonisation for mayors. 
646 participants virtual course on climate action for youth. 
407 participants in the virtual course on inclusive climate action. 
519 participants in the Inclusive Climate Action workshop. 
More than 30 collaborations with national media.
Future-compromised projects of EUR >98 million.

Project/Initiative Name: Improving prenatal, postpartum, and pediatric care in Cusco, Peru
Description:
The innovation consisted in implementing a policy delivery methodology (Deliverology) in 14 public primary healthcare establishments of Cusco’s Northern health network to transform the service culture and improve prenatal, postpartum, family planning, and pediatric care indicators in only 5 months, benefiting 22,900 low-income patients.

The innovation wanted to fix a “broken connection” problem between low-income patients and inadequate health staff, in the Northern health network. 

The innovation consisted in implementing a policy delivery methodology
(Deliverolog’) to help health establishments in Cusco’s Northern health network to visualize medical service mismatch and, therefore, to better deploy health workers to meet patient demand. This methodology consisted of empowering health authorities and healthcare staff in the Northern network to design, adopt, monitor, improve, disseminate, and use a digital record-keeping database.
Novelty:
The Cusco Project is an innovation in public health service because:
1) It is a low-cost, yet effective intervention: the implementation of
a new methodology to improve policy delivery completely
changes the way that a public primary healthcare establishment
deploys healthcare workers to attend patients’ needs, improving
health service culture and patients’ trust vis-a -vis public
services.

2) It focuses on both patients’ and providers’ needs: to fix a
“broken connection” between both types of actors, the
intervention incorporates both patients’ and providers’ fears,
doubts, challenges, but also needs and aspirations.

3) It empowers public healthcare establishments and workers to
continue doing a great work after the intervention: the main goal
is to permanently change the culture of doing things.

4) It includes the use of social media (Tik Tok) to engage with patients in a novel way and the implementation of digital applications instead of paper to manage personnel shifts.
Impact & Results:
The Cusco’s Project achieved the following results, in only 5 months
since the beginning of its implementation, and during the Covid-19
pandemic:
-          14% increase in baby consultations
-         5% increase in pediatric vaccinations
-         14% increase in nutritional supplementation for infants
-         5% increase in prenatal and postpartum consultations for Women
-         12% increase in family planning visits

Project/Initiative Name: Data Leadership: A data-driven problem solution journey toward Govpreneurship
Description:
The Aspire to Innovate (a2i) has piloted an innovative approach called "Data Leadership" to develop data-driven decision-making culture within the Bangladesh Civil Service in order to create readiness for addressing multidimensional future development problems. It does not view technology and data as silver bullets, rather the innovative approach is to use pedagogical approaches that are constructivist, collaborative, integrative, reflective, and inquiry-based learning.

The roadmap of this approach involves three steps: 
1. Problem Identification, which includes sensitization and problem identification with mid and junior level officials of Bangladesh Agricultural Research Institute (BARI) with the expected outcome being awareness on data and development of skills in data driven decision making
2. Match-making, which includes sensitization of senior officials and policy makers, problem selection and team formation with the objective of creating a bridge between the Problem Identification Phase to Problem Solving through Data.
3. Problem Solving through Data, which includes analysis, studying the availability of data analysis, data governance, analytics and visualization to develop architecture and leadership

Through the data leadership journey, the interaction between the officials of BARI has improved. Their skills and empathy have also developed due to the team formation for problem identification and problem selection and they have generated the solutions to their problems through data. Moreover, the networking between the researchers and the government officials has also improved which indicates that this approach also works as a bridge between academic sector and ministries.
Novelty:
The data leadership is an innovative initiative because:
1. It is agile and rapid, focused on the creation of database
2. By sensitizing the officials on data, it helps to build up their capacities, identify the problems, enrich networking skills and increases the support for data-focused policy solution
3. It is a prototype approach and it emphasizes leadership attributes of the government officials by developing skills and enhances their abilities to measure the depth of a problem and they reach to a decision through a database.
Impact & Results: The innovation has improved the interaction between official of different levels and policymakers. Further, it increased the support for data focused policies.

Description:
The innovation is developed because of the need to integrate the project financial reporting process with Start-Up Chile's acceleration programme, benefiting the programme's entrepreneurs and the Chilean government itself. The process aims to educate on the relevance of project financial planning, while preparing them to perform efficient and high standard financial reporting to the Chilean government. It sought to integrate the financial reporting process to the acceleration programme in an effective way, mainly benefiting the entrepreneurs.

Financial reporting in Start-Up Chile was too slow, it took more than 40 days, entrepreneurs were not interested in the process, executives were demotivated and the platforms used were not adapted to the general needs of the programme. Given this, a new financial process was implemented that considers a standard calendar of virtual meetings with entrepreneurs, free office hours, online educational platforms and internal information gathering, as well as periodic workshops on topics raised from the entrepreneurs' own needs. 

Its main objectives are: 1) to reduce review times per project (while maintaining a high standard), 2) to improve the entrepreneurs' perception of the financial process and to integrate the executive team in the improvements to be implemented. In the long term, the objective is to 3) maintain the review metrics (currently 15 days), 4) to improve the follow-up and education platforms for entrepreneurs and 5) to generate connections with public and private institutions to continue with the initiative's development process.
Novelty: The model implemented in Start-Up Chile is proactive, as opposed to the traditional reactive model. The accelerated process is based on the premise of preparing the entrepreneur during the project execution period to reach the end of the process with the approved financial return successfully and in a short period of time. The traditional monitoring process is based on assumptions that are not 100% confirmed, such as the full understanding of the financial monitoring process and its relevance. At the same time, the traditional model is triggered at the end of the implementation period, so there is no time or possibility for corrections or improvements. There is no room for diagnostics, which is possible in the accelerated model.
Impact & Results:
- Review times per project have been reduced by more than 50%, measured through data collection and centralisation of information in office and Google platforms. 
- The perception of the financial process has improved, based on surveys conducted by the acceleration area and focus groups of the internal communications area with various entrepreneurs. 
- Entrepreneurs have valued the online material we have made available 24-7 as fundamental.

Project/Initiative Name: Detecting irregular tenders in Spain using Big Data and AI
Description:
A software application has been developed that detects fraud in public tenders. The programme, using Big Data and Artificial Intelligence, massively reads millions of tender documents in Spain and detects those documents that contain metadata of the company that was finally the winner. 

The IT system (Big Data + Artificial Intelligence) massively analyses the tenders on the Spanish procurement platform, around one million documents. For each tender, it automatically reads the metadata of the technical specifications (the document properties) and checks whether the name of the successful bidder appears. This indicates that the technical specifications have been linked in some way to the successful bidder and that an irregularity may therefore have been committed.

This tool detected more than 15 tenders that resulted in several news items published in newspapers all over Spain (El País, El Periódico de Cataluña, El Periódico de España, La Nueva España, El Día de Tenerife, La Provincia de Las Palmas, etc.).
Novelty: The tool for detecting irregular tenders in Spain is a pioneer in the technological field for detecting corruption in public procurement by a citizen. It is an illustrative example of how digital technologies help public administration.
Impact & Results:
More than 15 fraudulent tenders were detected throughout Spain. It led to several news stories in major national newspapers and some are still under investigation by the media. Finally, legal action is being considered against the contracting agencies that committed the irregularities. 

The following are some of the news items:
1)https://elpais.com/economia/2021-12-10/los-pliegos-de-tres-concursos-publicos-incluian-al-ganador-en-los-metadatos-antes-de-adjudicarse.html
2)https://www.epe.es/es/espana/20220703/concesion-millones-contratos-publicos-investigacion-metadatos-ineco-13981672
3)https://www.epe.es/es/espana/20220704/navantia-pliegos-concursos-publicos-investigacion-metadatos-13952523

Project/Initiative Name: Cultural and technological transformation of the criminal justice service in the province of Buenos Aires
Organisation: Public Prosecutor's Office of the Province of Buenos Aires
Description:
The innovation was developed to help ensure access to justice for all citizens. The cultural and technological transformation of this service increases the quality of the experience of the citizen and the judicial agent involved. In this way, by applying emerging technologies and innovative management techniques, the aim is to achieve a more agile, productive and efficient criminal justice system.

The project consists of the integral digitalisation of the work processes, the interoperability of the computerised management system with those used by the bodies involved in the criminal process (Police and Justice Administration), the incorporation of a CRM to manage the relationship with the citizen by applying Design Thinking, the adoption of agile methodologies in the judicial offices, the incorporation of control panels, the adoption of qualitative research techniques to ascertain the needs of the judicial offices, and the adoption of a new system to manage the relationship with the citizen by applying Design Thinking.

The objectives are to contribute to guaranteeing access to justice, speed up criminal proceedings, further increase the levels of institutional transparency, optimise the human and technical resources of the organisations involved, expand the information available for evidence-based decision-making and improve the experience of the citizen and the judicial agent.
Novelty: The uniqueness of the case lies in the strategic and planned application of a set of emerging technologies and modern management techniques in the criminal justice service. This has led to the emergence of an emerging functionality, which is more than the sum of the parts involved in the system, and which has improved both the Customer Experience and the Employee Experience. It should be noted that the project has a potential reach of 17 million people. In Argentina and Latin America, we have not found any precedents for similar initiatives. Internationally, some judicial bodies in the State of California have leveraged digitalisation to improve the Customer Experience in the judicial sphere. There is also a people-oriented service design lab at Harvard and Stanford Universities.
Impact & Results:
- 3,483,969 procedures signed digitally or electronically in the Electronic File in which the criminal investigation is being processed. 
- 1,395,680 messages exchanged through the agency's Virtual Assistant.
- 865,217 notes sent digitally in administrative processes. Before, for example, an employee's promotion had to be sent on paper and had to travel 627 km. Today, it is completely digital. 
- 647,599 victims contacted through the CRM of the Public Prosecutor's Office which keeps them informed about relevant procedures.
- 217,418 citizen complaints filed digitally. In Pandemic, its use grew by 569.29%. 
- 23,351 judicial working days saved in the uploading of criminal complaints. 
- 344 judicial offices adopted agile methodologies.

Description:
"Bogota Productive" 24 Hours is a strategy that promotes productive and cultural activity at non-conventional times as part of the actions aimed at reactivating the economy in Bogota and the international positioning of the city.
The aim is to establish a 24-hour circuit that guarantees minimum conditions for public and private transport, as well as a security scheme and public and private services.
Novelty: The strategy promotes productive and cultural activity at non-conventional times as part of the actions aimed at reactivating the economy in Bogota and the international positioning of the city.
Impact & Results:
In 2022, the strategy involved 5,009 businesses in 11 locations across the city in 13 zones, with an estimated 24,000 visitors in different parts of the city.

- More than 88 artistic, musical and civic interventions were carried out. 
- Nine 24h trade fairs, with 503 entrepreneurs.
- 1st Night of City Museums: 43 museums; 29,000 estimated visitors.
- 59% of participating businesses saw an increase in sales.
- Articulation with more than 35 public and private entities, local, national and international.
- Zero criminal acts during the 2 months of intervention.

Description:
As part of the national plan for the digital transformation of education in Greece, CTI has designed and developed “e-me”, a social, collaborative, and extendable cloud-based digital educational platform for pupils and teachers. Being the third pillar of the digital infrastructure developed to support K-12 learning, “e-me” supports formal, non-formal, or informal learning experiences, encouraging equal participation of members, socially-mediated construction of knowledge, co-creation of educational content and self-reflection on tasks.

The initial version of e-me (1.0) was launched in 2015, whereas the fully functional innovative version 2.0 of e-me was released in late 2019. Just a few months later (in early 2020) e-me was widely and successfully used to support emergency remote teaching in primary and secondary education, in response to the Covid-19 pandemic crisis and the subsequent global shutdown. School teachers nationwide were offered the option to use the brand-new e-me for asynchronous distance learning, as part of the Greek MoE’s plan to guarantee continuity in primary and secondary education during school lockdown due to pandemic.

According to e-me’s Personal Learning Environment framework and its fundamental design principles, e-me views innovation as the opportunity given to its members: (a) to take responsibility and self-regulate their own learning; (b) to adopt active participation roles within their own open educational digital communities; (c) to co-work and communicate in self-paced, social spaces to construct individual and socially-mediated learning; and (d) to become prosumers (a combination of being a consumer and producer) of ICT tools and technology-based methodologies, adjusted to their own educational needs so as to reach learning outcomes and multiple literacies.
Novelty: e-me is a social, collaborative and extendable cloud-based educational platform, which grounds its pedagogical innovation on the Personal Learning Environment (PLE) philosophy and on the user-centred design principles to which it adheres. To the best of our knowledge, e-me is one of the first -if not the first- implementations of a PLE for school education, which has been designed to be used nationwide by all Κ-12 students and teachers and has been developed based exclusively on open source software.
