Impact & Results: Within just the first 2 months of school closure due to the pandemic, e-me reached 400,000 users (4,000% increase). More than 120,000 hives were created, representing either formal school classes or informal collaboration spaces. 10,000 hives were initiated by students, covering their need for interaction with peers. 15,000 public hives were set up to support open educational communities, mostly by teachers wishing to share good practices, guide each other and exchange learning resources. Regardless of their limited prior experience, teachers became content creators and developed over 230,000 interactive learning resources using the ‘e-me content’ app. Along with teachers, students developed digital literacy and participatory learning skills by creating their own hives and becoming co-creators of content, managing, thus, not only to cope with new educational practices but also to view digital tools as a means of informal learning fostering peer-to-peer communication and collaboration.

Project/Initiative Name: Rhode Island Evidence Scale for Budgeting
Organisation: The Policy Lab at Brown University
Description:
Governments must judge hundreds of new programmatic budget proposals each fiscal year with little objective information about whether they will achieve the results claimed. We leveraged existing public clearinghouses of peer reviewed evidence, simple evidence definitions, and updated data collection to provide that clarity. This evidence scale gives State of Rhode Island policy officials quick insight into the level of evidence each proposal contains so better budget decisions can be made.

This project begins with the insight that, when it comes to sharing information, the design of everyday paperwork and opportunities to discuss that paperwork matter. Templates can guide users on what information should be included, and templates can set guardrails for how to present that information. We redesigned the form (called a “Decision Package”) that Rhode Island agency staff use to propose new budget items. We trained staff on how to use the template, held daily office hours to help fill it out, and updated Budget Guidance. The design was meant to help users think and communicate more clearly about the evidence behind their proposal. 

Importantly, we introduced an Evidence Scale (0 to 5) which ranked a proposal in terms of its evidence base – developed from recommendations from the Pew Results First Initiative. Acknowledging that many programs have limited evidence, a series of questions were designed to help identify opportunities to invest in generating evidence. Pew’s Result’s First Clearinghouse and Arnold Ventures’ Social Programs that Work database, which catalog thousands of program evaluations, were used by agencies to help determine the level of evidence. The Rhode Island scale has four levels: Proven Effective, Promising, Theory-Based, and Evidence of Insufficient Impact.
Novelty: The Policy Lab at Brown University, the Rhode Island OMB, and the Governor’s Office collaborated to re-design the suite of budget guidance, trainings, and templates with user centered design and the latest thinking on evidence. The addition of the scale adds a dramatic element of clarity into the viability of the budget request.
Impact & Results: Agencies that took advantage of trainings and office hours, in particular, showed marked improvements; they were more likely, for example, to include citations in their evidence narratives and to provide details on projected cost-benefit ratios. The evidence scale has also motivated agencies to make budget requests for programs with a strong evidence basis rather than other programs. Most importantly the Governor felt she could make better budget decisions and staff felt like they could make better recommendations to her.

Project/Initiative Name: Semantic Analyser - Smart Text Search Engine
Organisation: Ministry of Public Administration of Republic Slovenia
Description:
Semantic Analyzer is an open-source smart engine using AI for text processing in Slovene language. It was developed together with the University of Ljubljana, Faculty of Informatics and Computer Science. The prototype enables easy and efficient algorithmic processing of large corpuses of documents and texts with finding content similarities using advanced grouping and visualization. A web tool supporting natural language (like legislation, public tenders) is planned to be developed.

Since reviewing many documents and selecting the most relevant ones is a time-consuming task, we have developed an AI-based approach for the content-based review of large collections of texts. The approach of semantic analysis of texts and the comparison of content relatedness between individual texts in a collection allows for timesaving and the comprehensive analysis of collections. The goal is to develop a general-purpose tool for analyzing sets of textual documents.
Novelty:
The Semantic Analyzer is using emergent AI technologies to find semantic relations among texts and could be used to search through among others large legal documents quickly and easily for different concepts, phrases, as a large collection of texts can be sorted or grouped according to their content. 

Semantic Analyzer is an open-source tool that combines interactive visualizations and machine learning to support users in fast prototyping the semantic analysis of a large collection of textual documents. The principal innovation of the Semantic Analyzer lies in the combination of interactive visualizations, visual programming approach, and advanced tools for text modelling. The target audience of the tool is data owners and problem domain experts from public administration.

Project/Initiative Name: Turning Anticipatory Regulation from Principle to Practice: The Journey of the Regulatory Horizons Council
Organisation: Department for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy (Regulatory Horizons Council)
Description:
Traditional ways of regulating are not able to keep pace with technological disruption, shifting sectoral boundaries, new types of problems and the opportunities innovation can bring. The Regulatory Horizons Council (RHC) is an independent expert committee set up the UK Government that identifies the implications of technological innovation, and provides government with impartial, expert advice on the regulatory reform required to support its rapid and safe introduction.

The Council works with a wide range of subject matter experts and non-experts (often around 50 per project). This will include other government departments, academics, SMEs, venture capitalists, civil society groups, regulators, and international stakeholders. Further, it does not only work on regulation on innovative technologies, but it also deploys innovative methods in its research, such as horizon scanning, backcasting, and hackatons.

In 2 years, the Council has delivered four reports on regulatory reform in its first year (fusion energy, medical devices, genetic technologies and drones). In 2022, it has produced its first crosscutting report on "closing the gap" between regulatory principles and practice on regulating new and emerging technology. By the end of the year, the Council will be producing reports on AI as a medical device, neurotechnology, and hydrogen in maritime.
Novelty: The Regulatory Horizons Council is an innovative venture in response to the lack of systematic overview and capacity of regulators and Government departments to consider the regulatory implications of emerging technologies in a timely manner to enable innovation and to capitalise on socio-economic and environmental opportunities. Furthermore, the Council is innovative not just in the 'what' it focuses on but also the how. It aims to embrace innovative tools and methods in engaging stakeholders, generating recommendations, undertaking monitoring and evaluation. This is outlined in more detail in 'innovation summary'.
Impact & Results:
While our reports encapsulate our evidence gathering and conclusions, the ultimate litmus test is the ability for the Council to influence and enact change. The Department for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy has accepted the Council's recommendations on fusion energy and the CEO of the Fusion Industry Association welcomed the report. He said: “The rest of the world should take note, this report shows how the U.K. intends to lead on commercializing fusion energy". The Council is working with other UK Government Departments to respond to the other reports. 

Evaluation of our first four reports have been published and this is based on interviews with stakeholders and a team retrospective. The feedback was that the RHC's independence added credibility to its reports and recommendations and enabled external stakeholders to be frank on where Government or other key actors could do better. The interviews highlighted that the RHC stakeholder engagement was incredibly effective. The RHC were generally seen to have spoken and engaged with the right groups and the right people, and critically, balanced these engagements well. Participants discussed how the reports have been able to stimulate a lot of thinking on the areas of emerging technology.

Description:
The Disciplinary Maturity Model (CRG-MM) is an operational and strategic tools aimed to evaluating and improving the management of disciplinary activity, through the definition of quality standards based on management and public governance procedures. This innovation seeks to provide greater stability and security to executors and managers of disciplinary activity, considering its role as a public integrity instance in the fight against corruption.

The Disciplinary Maturity Model (CRG-MM) is structured in five progressive levels of maturity, which have different levels of expected performance from the disciplinary units:
1. Initial level – the unit does not have a sustainable and consistent practice of the disciplinary macro processes.
2. Standard level – the unit has the ability of executing the main disciplinary macro processes in a standard level and has developed primary management structures.
3. Integration level – the disciplinary unit is formally structured as a part of the integrity system of the organization; it has more legal competences and it offers an internal training program.
4. Management level – the disciplinary unit has identified its weaknesses and threats; it has Project based team management with clear performance indicators; it has an adequate physical structure and enough financial, human and technological resources. The disciplinary unit works on an independent basis.
5. Optimized level – the disciplinary unit has an ongoing internal and external evaluation process geared to continuous innovation and self-development. Teams are encouraged to innovate. The disciplinary activity is a key component of the organization´s strategic planning. Regular surveys check internal corruption and impunity perceptions. The unit regularly checks progress in four crucial areas: service and disciplinary work, HRM, performance and transparency management, governance and institutional relations.

The Disciplinary Maturity Model (CRG-MM) reveals different development stages of disciplinary activity inside a given organization, besides working as a guideline tool to support units’ self-assessment of their current maturity level. It also guides each unit on moving to a higher level, without ready-made solutions, as the ideal structure of the disciplinary unit can change according to the organization size and its risks map.
Novelty: Originally, the National Disciplinary Board (CRG) monitored disciplinary activity focusing on individual procedures, in a strictly legal-procedural view. The Maturity Model brought a new approach based on units´ self-assessment process, empowering those units to identify their current maturity level and necessary changes to achieve a higher level.
Impact & Results: The first round of self-assessment took place in 2020, gathering 47% of SISCOR disciplinary units. In 2022, eighty percent of the units have joined the Disciplinary Maturity Model (CRM-MM), which represents a sixty percent increase in comparison to the previous round. The positive results are due to previous participants positive feedback regarding the benefits of adopting the Disciplinary Maturity Model in their units, as well as the mandatory engagement of some types of units.

Organisation: Aragon Open Government Laboratory (LAAAB) - Directorate General for Open Government and Social Innovation of the Government of Aragon
Description:
VisualGOB is an objective, visual and intuitive digital accountability tool that represents a breakthrough in the field of transparency. With it, it is possible to consult the government's progress in real time, following the evolution of the 156 strategic objectives of the Aragon Government Plan. In addition, VisualGOB has become a great balanced scorecard accessible to all.

The content of government portals and right of access resolutions are not easily understandable and, sometimes, the excess of information turns into disinformation. For this reason, the Government of Aragon developed VisualGOB, a tool that allows to know and evaluate the objectives of the Government Plan of the X legislature based on the triple agenda of progress (Social, Green and Digital), the seventeen SDGs of the United Nations Agenda 2030 and the ten Departments of the executive, all with a very intuitive design.

The information is published and disseminated in real time, accounting for each of the achievements and allowing detailed monitoring of all government action as a balanced scorecard, not only from an internal but also from an external point of view. More specifically, VisualGOB allows the checking of each commitment, its implementation status, the responsible body, the time estimate and the allocated budget.
Novelty: VisualGOB is a new digital visualisation system for the Government Plan, which combines the traditional vision of objectives and actions by Departments with the transversal visualisation based on the triple Government Agenda (Social, Green and Digital) and where each objective and action is aligned with the SDGs of the United Nations 2030 Agenda. The commitments thus achieve a quantifiable, traceable and inspiring dimension, being an accountability tool and a transparent balanced scorecard that is unparalleled in any other regional or state public administration. Moreover, the tool is very intuitive for the user, without sacrificing design.
Impact & Results: VisualGOB received 14,000 visits during its first three months of operation. The platform currently has more than 18,000 visits, although the current objective is to work to extend its use to other sectors of society, with the potential to double this impact.

Project/Initiative Name: Lego Serious Play in legal education processes
Description:
The development of pedagogical exercises based on LEGO Serious Play allowed for an approach to gamification exercises for teaching processes in law, specifically in human rights to communities without a permanent approach to legal concepts. This methodology allows an approach to legal concepts, from the game, which allows a permanent approximation of the citizen.

Lego® Serious Play® (LSP) consists of four phases based on prototypes: ask, design, build and reflect. This is based on 7 workshops duly consigned and developed in the methodology's facilitators' manuals, which allows it to be adapted to legal work, from citizen training to the creation or optimisation of products or services.

The objective is to apply the Lego® Serious Play® methodology to legal training as a pedagogical innovation tool for citizens. Also to analyse whether it allows to develop and strengthen the skills of citizens interested in learning basic concepts of law. Moreover, to strengthen legal competences through the development of legal activities, and to integrate the results of the workshops as factors that influence the formative process of legal activities.
Novelty:
The main characteristics of teaching law through gamification (building with your hands) are the following:
- The LSP methodology in legal training for citizens is an innovative tool that allows developing and strengthening the skills that citizens will need to claim and know their rights.
- With LSP the role of citizens is active and therefore encourages the development of new skills such as creativity and empathy, which are increasingly required by the labour market.
- The presentation of the models with LSP encourages active communication and legal argumentation on the part of citizens.
- It is a horizontal and not a vertical learning methodology, as it generates a reciprocal exchange of knowledge between the facilitator and the citizens.
- The role of the facilitator plays a fundamental role in the achievement of the proposed objectives, as he/she is in charge of the management of the LSP.
Impact & Results: #NAME?

Project/Initiative Name: “Carbon Net Zero 2030” Strategy of Canary Islands Health Service
Organisation: Servicio Canario de la Salud (Canary Islands Health Service)
Description:
The SCS has launched the strategy "Health Zero net emissions 2030" which aims to achieve net carbon neutrality in 2030, applying direct and indirect measures, especially on the supply chain of the public health system of the Canary Islands. 

The Canary Health Service will follow a BPM (Business Process Management) methodology that starts with the initial measurement of the carbon footprint in all its dependent centers, thereby defining actions, monitoring and control systems or any other tool that allows continuous improvement. This BMP methodology will allow the implementation of continuous improvement mechanisms for all processes, enabling the continuous optimization and adaptation of the lines of action, thus promoting efficiency in the decarbonization process.

The aim of the Strategy is for the Canary Islands public health system to be an example of sustainability in its activity in order to be consistent with its own functions of health promotion, prevention and protection. The Health Strategy Zer0 net emissions 2030 of the SCS introduces a new methodology of integral public management for the mitigation of the effects of climate change, centralized and led from its direction.
Novelty:
The "Salud Zer0 emisiones netas 2030" strategy innovates by generating a disruptive change in a regional public health organization, with a strategy led by the management and transmitted horizontally throughout the organization. This strategy presents a model of measurement and continuous improvement of results, providing a public administration with an agile tool in order to achieve an ambitious goal of decarbonization in a decade.
Finally, the strategy presents strategic driving actions, such as the commitment to offshore offshore wind power, or the integration of hydrogen and oxygen of renewable origin in the hospital environment.
Impact & Results:
The expected results are a reduction, by 2030, of the total net CO2 equivalent emissions. To this end, the carbon footprint of all hospital centers will be measured continuously, following the "Guide for calculating the carbon footprint and for drawing up an improvement plan for an organization" of the Ministry for Ecological Transition.
In 2030, 35% of energy savings are expected compared to 2022, and the remaining 65% will be produced by renewable energies.

Description:
Citizen Innovation Labs (LABIC) are an efficient methodology to generate innovative solutions between citizens and institutions for the common good.
These solutions focus on the most pressing issues of the 2030 agenda; they are replicable, scalable and generate concrete benefits to affected communities.

A Citizen Innovation Lab (LABIC) is a method to experiment, collaborate and accelerate innovative projects that emerge from the citizenry and have the potential to generate useful solutions to social, cultural, environmental and economic challenges. They are a proven and systematised instrument that allows to add collective intelligence and offer citizens possibilities to generate agile changes from their own contexts, which would otherwise be difficult to develop.

The Citizen Innovation Project of the Ibero-American General Secretariat (SEGIB) has developed 9 of these laboratories in different countries and cities, co-organised with national, sub-national and/or local public institutions in several Ibero-American countries (Argentina, Brazil, Colombia, Costa Rica, Mexico, Dominican Republic). In these 9 laboratories, 1000 citizens from 28 countries have participated, 83 replicable prototypes have been generated, 150 institutions (public, private and civil society) have been involved in the organisation and 120 collectives and communities have worked and received the benefits of the solutions created.
Novelty:
Four important factors of LABIC as an innovation in development cooperation can be identified:
1. Citizen protagonist: they are an innovation because they introduce citizens as the main creator of scalable solutions. 
2. Bridge between institutions and citizens: they form an ecosystem where citizens join national, subnational and/or local institutions, international agencies and organisations from other sectors with the aim of generating benefits for the common good.
3. Cross-border solutions: incorporate collaboration between people and institutions from different countries, generating replicable solutions in different contexts.
4. Scaling: they add a maturity and scaling programme for prototyped solutions, which is unusual in citizen labs that are generally limited to prototyping.
Impact & Results:
'- 1000 participating citizens from 28 countries.
- 83 innovative prototypes generated
- 150 partner institutions
- 120 groups and communities impacted 
In order to evaluate the projects generated in the LABICs, a matrix is used to quantify the level of maturity of the projects. This is the result of a qualitative systematisation for the evaluation carried out in the LABICs of Costa Rica (2019) and Mexico (2021), which has enabled the development of a framework for evaluating the progress of the projects in terms of their maturity and potential for scale. This framework is described in detail in a text to be published in 2022: Citizen solutions that work: the LABIC method.

Description:
Smart People Castro Urdiales was one of the 14 projects selected with the aim of redesigning the job profiles of the future in Castro Urdiales Town Council. The project's idea was based on the principle that "the most important thing in an organisation is its people". Therefore, innovative research on the new job profiles of the future, the skills and abilities needed in these jobs, would allow us to design training and adaptation plans for existing and new staff.

Taking these principles as the basis for the design of the project, we understood that we should approach an eminently practical project that would obtain products or deliverables that would provide tools to prepare or train people for the future jobs or skills required, for organisations to select the right people in view of the multiple retirements that are to come, and for society to stimulate public policies that take these objectives into account.

This project also proposes 26 new jobs that will be demanded by the public sector, while defining the responsibilities, functions, skills and knowledge that will be required for them. The project has been followed up with several initiatives such as the recruitment of four people with the profiles of the new jobs defined in one of the two documents produced, and is serving as an impetus for other organisations to innovate along this path.
Novelty:
The initiative has been able to define totally disruptive jobs that had not been defined before.

An expert from the beneficiary group has been named as a member of the 100 experts of the Cotec Foundation after the implementation of Smart People Castro Urdiales.

It puts on the table the need to change the bureaucratic model of the traditional bureaucracy and think about new models.
It gathers the expert knowledge of different professionals and university experts and uses it to design the future.
Impact & Results: The document 26 new jobs for the City Council of the future has had a great impact, as well as the new organisational model. Large organisations such as the Diputació de Barcelona have invited us to present the results, and the Cotec Foundation has been the driving force behind the dissemination of the results.

Organisation: Laboratorio Colaborativo de Innovación Pública (Innovaap UCR)
Description:
Promote social entrepreneurship in five cantons of Costa Rica: Cañas, Grecia, Orotina, Quepos and Turrialba, in order to develop co-creation processes and proposals for social impact solutions.

The main objective of this initiative has been to promote social entrepreneurship in five cantons of the country: Cañas, Grecia, Orotina, Quepos and Turrialba, in order to develop co-creation processes and proposals for solutions with social impact, through viable projects that translate into a good or service that generates welfare for the population of these localities.

In the process, the participants have managed to identify and delimit the problems present in their cantons, and intend to address them through the development of a social enterprise that is self-sustainable and translates into a solution taking into account the following Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).

The proposals generated in the different cantons participated in a competition to obtain funds for the prototyping of their initiatives. The competition included the participation of a panel of jurors from outside the laboratory, with outstanding profiles in entrepreneurship, social innovation, project management, among other topics. Three projects won: 1) laying hens, 2) cultivándome - collaborative urban gardens, 3) Bawo Chocolates - chocolates with cultural identity.
Novelty: The initiative sought to develop social enterprises, which were not only intended to generate income, but also to be proposals that would solve problems in their communities and generate productive linkages in the community, meaning that the people and the inputs required for the development of the project were part of that canton.
Impact & Results:
- The necessary materials were purchased and 5 homemade farms managed by women were built. 
- Cultivating me: Construction of the greenhouse and other structures or spaces required for planting crops and visits from clients.
- Help me to study: The website development process was carried out, with the following sections: donations, courses, student registration and a blog with information of interest. For the launch of the platform, a validation of an English course was carried out.
- Safe roads: 10 cameras were installed in trees on the road to check if animals are still crossing safely and to list the species that need flyovers to move from one place to another.
- Bawo chocolates: The product range was expanded by introducing a "chocolate spread" to the market and the image of the products was renewed by integrating new packaging designs and making them environmentally friendly.

Project/Initiative Name: Support for the implementation of Triple P in Chile Grows with You (Crece Contigo)
Description:
Chile Crece Contigo, a flagship Chilean programme for universal child protection, decided in 2019 to integrate Triple P into its programme offering, through the Parenting Support and Parental Skills Programme. The initial objective of Chile Crece Contigo was to complement the available offer of parenting programmes in the country.

This pilot programme has made it possible to reach 27 municipalities in the country and serve more than 14,000 families with Triple P's positive parenting programmes, using the implementation support model. 
Triple P's implementation support is a model to support organisations to implement their evidence-based positive parenting programmes. 

It consists of sessions guided by an accredited implementation consultant who, through the application of various tools, accompanies organisations to reflect and advance their knowledge and practice. By providing implementation support, capacities are transferred that can impact the practice of public institutions beyond the evidence-based programme. Therefore, this innovation allows strengthening the public apparatus in the provision of services to citizens, generating the theoretical and practical framework, conditions, skills and motivation for the successful implementation of programmes. 

In the context of Chile Crece Contigo, the objective of this innovation is to transfer and install capacities in the teams, so that they are able to carry out the successful and sustainable implementation of the initiative in the long term, without the need for external support. To make implementation support scalable, we designed a monitoring strategy that considers the different phases of implementation of the initiative across the country, from an initial phase, where the programme is installed, to a full phase, where the programme is sustainable and teams are able to maintain implementation autonomously.
Novelty: Triple P's implementation support is an innovative service, as it introduces implementation science to the public sphere in a concrete and effective way, increasing the effectiveness of public institutions in delivering quality services and evidence-based programmes to citizens. In the case of Chile Crece Contigo, implementation support is a new and little explored practice, as there are no other programmes within this system that have this support structure, making it a pioneering practice within a wide-reaching policy.
Impact & Results: We have trained more than 450 facilitators, who have served more than 14,000 families. In that sense, 27 municipalities in the country receive implementation support and we are on the verge of providing support to 4 more.

Description:
GenderDataLab.org is a digital commons that enables and encourages collective participation in the creation of gender-sensitive Open Data repositories. The platform collects best practice cases of administrations or organisations that have created OpenGenderDataSets to illustrate how to improve policy action through gender-sensitive data science.

- GDL is an online resource that promotes, through values of democratic transparency and collective participation, the opportunity that the new discipline of open data with a gender perspective offers to design public policies. 
- It opens windows of opportunity for activists, researchers, policy makers and organisations about the benefits of working with open data and a gender perspective. 
- In the face of the current gender data gap, it shows how simple, low-cost tools can be used to read reality, measure it and draw conclusions and act with greater precision.
- Promote the gender category in open, institutional data portals, which is invisible.
- GDL follows the framework of the Digital Social Innovation model, using open source and open knowledge tools to share real cases for inspiration.
Novelty: The innovation comes from merging two already innovative disciplines: OpenData and GenderData. In the era of data-driven societies, the focus on measuring gender gaps or monitoring the SDGs is a utility for governments and activists. GDL puts a new method within reach of the global digital community interested in the impact of measuring realities in a fairer and more equitable way with 50% of the global population.
Impact & Results:
+ 1000 registered users
+ 20 published datasets, millions of lines of data 
+ 15-20 online resource articles
+ 10 organisations involved
+ 20.000 visits
+ digital channels community almost 1000 people
+ 10 international conferences

Organisation: Ministry of Economy and Finance (MEF) and State General Accounting Department (RGS)
Description:
I-Lab Innovation Program is the answer to the growing demand for digital innovation and transformation, and to the multiplication, over time, of the IT platforms in use by the various Administrations (12 different) for accounting processes, with all the obvious consequences that this extreme fragmentation has entailed. I-Lab Innovation Program, adopted by Ministry of Economy and Finance (MEF) and State General Accounting Department (RGS), is a co-design program containing a complex set of activities organised to the prototyping and co-production of contents related to the system of Italian public accounting (InIt).

The new InIt system, which integrates financial accounting, economic-asset accounting and analytical accounting by cost centers in a single application, is replacing the systems currently in use at the Central Administrations for the management of public accounting (Legacy Systems). To implement this technological reform program, MEF and RGS have chosen to use the I-Lab program, thus orienting themselves towards the new techniques of co-design, co-production, requirements, and functional prototyping that derive from the hybrid methodology Waterfall/Agile/Lean. The Program was conceived according to the logic of a horizontal contribution, focused on experience, competence, innovation of the project proposal, and collective effort within a Design device based on a Digital-first approach.

With I-Lab it was possible to directly involve the staff of 14 Ministries and other Italian public bodies, transforming resistance to change into collaboration and creating a real "Public 2 Public" dimension, an almost absolute novelty in the world of Italian PA. From 2019 the I-Lab Program has been developed in several editions. In its 3.0 edition as a resident innovation program by RGS, the I-Lab co-design and requirements activity has produced a huge amount of materials: 107 offices involved; 160 digital blackboards for remote coworking; 169 interviews conducted; 4.000 MS Word folders, with 357 document attachments.
Novelty: The I-Lab Innovation Program is the result of a hybrid approach based on value principles and technical disciplines, Waterfall, Agile, and Lean at the base of modern designs of digital solutions. Hybridization has ensured the respect for (and resolution of) the complexity of the scenario in which the I-Lab program is inserted.
Impact & Results:
To have objective data on the effectiveness of I-Lab model and how it was perceived by the participants, both internal and external to the RGS, a survey was submitted. This activity, delivered in 20 days, involved 155 participants, with an average response rate of 70%. These are the most important outcomes stated by participants:
- through I-Lab it was possible to produce concrete requirements, capable of enhancing the opinions and expectations of the representatives of the users-administrations
- the perception that I-Lab allows you to "do more things", in less time and with a newfound order that allows one to "forget" the organisational problems related to participation in activities, optimising efforts to produce the best possible content
- the ability to focus on defining the solution proposal and identifying emerging models useful for synthesising the contributions produced and facilitating the subsequent phases of analysis, design and implementation by the relevant components

Description:
The innovation is a new and comprehensive awareness program to apprise the public of the existing policy and legal framework in which they operate in Lebanon, through the mapping of existing laws and decrees, by topics and sectors. The aim is to understand the existing framework as a first step in working toward a just one. This empowers public officials and citizens with the right information to establish rule of law and accountability and to flag out gaps, inconsistencies and required reforms.

The project provides a comprehensive, creative and simplified layout of existing legal and policy provisions in Lebanon, using people’s language, produced in visual and oral style through videos, displayed by series and per topic, in both Arabic and English. The objective is to create a participatory buy-in process, through which stakeholders understand, influence and share control over initiatives, decisions and resources that affect them.

The first series that was implemented, but yet to be completed, was entitled “Anti-Corruption Framework in Lebanon” and comprised 12 episodes. The methodology used was a comprehensive mapping of applicable laws and core documents, for each topic, including multiple sources of law (regulations, acts, bylaws, policy procedures, and laws and decrees etc.), followed by a short and simple summary of the basic provisions of each to produce the video series in an interactive and straightforward manner with English subtitles to reach local residents as well as Lebanese diaspora.
Novelty: The innovation is unique, personalized and targeted. It stands out from the rest and makes the life of public officials and citizens easier. It converts data into knowledge and benefits and brings together cross-cutting issues for efficiency and integrity.
Impact & Results: The innovation has reached 150'000 citizens. As it is just at its beginning, more reaches are expected increasing the ability to provide greater transparency, accountability, and, in this way, better policies.

Description:
The Porto Citizen Card is designed to identify residents but above all to offer them privileged use of their city, advantages and benefits in accessing experiences and services of the Municipality. One of the innovation principles of the Porto Citizen Card is to change the relationship between the Municipality and its citizens. Providing a better public service means communicating better and with greater proximity, reducing bureaucracy in contacts and anticipating responses to the needs and expectations of citizens. Thus, residents are involved from the very beginning in identifying the card's features. They were invited to participate, through a focus group, in defining the essential areas to be included in a city card.

The Porto Citizen Card is a card which is only available to individuals with tax domicile in the city of Porto and students who have proven to study and reside in the city, and is valid for the period of 5 years. The application for membership of the card is carried out by completing and sending a digital form available on the innovation website, which is later validated by the Porto City Council team. If needed, citizens can also benefit from the help of specialized staff in physical locations. This Card enables citizens to benefit from services that previously required one different card for each: municipal libraries, theatres, and swimming pools.

Another fundamental service that this card added was free use of public transportation by young people between 13 and 18 years old. Another important service, available to all users, consists of receiving relevant alerts in real time, free of charge on changes to traffic or parking, interruptions of water supply, and weather warning. Lastly, since the innovation launch on April 5, 2021, some initiatives have been included in the card to provide support to people, also helping to reduce some effects of the pandemic covering the price of trips to health structures.
Novelty: Porto Digital Association worked with co-creation tools, involving citizens and technicians of the Municipality in the design of the solution that best responded to it.
Impact & Results: The card has become an identity symbol for Porto citizens and counts with 46. 126 active users, from which 15.565 users are under 18 years old. The average number of daily registrations is 106. The main impact directly observed from the innovation was process simplification, which led to an increase in accessibility to services and infrastructures from the Municipality. Consequently, a sense of community and proximity begun to be felt among citizens, as identified in several co-creation sessions and through spontaneous contacts.

Project/Initiative Name: 'Con Vos' Network: Digitalization for Effective Governance. Community Markets to Improve Access to Online Public Services in Catamarca, Argentina
Organisation: Co_Lab Argentina (Accelerator Labs Network, United Nations Development Program)
Description:
As poverty increased rampantly due to pandemic, the digital gap hampered the urgent access of millions of citizens to online public services and state aid. Many could neither request their Universal Childcare Allowance (AUH), their new-borns' birth certificates, or even their own IDs. Communities have thought and offered a grassroot solution: store owners helping their peers to get online procedures done in their business, in exchange for a small fee. It is a simple innovation, yet effective when it comes to decentralize public services, foster digital inclusion, and strengthen local markets.

We had already implemented a three-month pilot program in Uruguay in 2020. Shop owners took part voluntarily, and we developed a narrative, a concept and a brand for the action “Red Con Vos” (With you Network) to engage all the relevant stakeholders. We distributed different materials with the identity of the project within the shops participating in the actions (bags, wallets, mousepads, pens), and each shop now has a distinctive logo and a poster with the services offered to the neighbors. 

Thanks to a grant from the United Nations Development Program, we are now escalating it in northern Argentina, in a province where 39.6% of its population is under the poverty line, alongside the municipal and the national government. The action has been coordinated in regular meetings with each local government along with the team of the UNDP and consultants in charge of the fieldwork. All the pilots rely on the collaboration of store owners which participated voluntarily in the project. As it was mentioned, we develop a narrative and a brand for the action with specific promotional materials that were delivered to the participants.
Novelty: "Con Vos" is a bottom-up smart solution. It is innovative because it takes advantage of the positive effect of peers and acquaintances at a local level to encourage people to do more things online. It relies on social capital networks at the local level to promote digital inclusion.
Impact & Results:
We used OLS regressions, descriptive statistics, continuous assessment via surveys to evaluate the three groups of the pilot program in Uruguay. The Network customers’ profile was people with high school diploma (54.2%), female (58.8%), 25-54 years old (65.7%). Most lacked means or knowledge to carry out these procedures online by themselves. Clients travelled a total of 293.7 km less and saved up to 637 hours. The closer a Network store wasto their home, the greater the savings. Online procedures increased significantly, as well as the demand of goods and services sold by the store owners.

From our current implementation in northern Argentina, we hope to have escalated to 25-40 nearby stores by December, with owners fully trained and ready to deploy the program. By July 2023, we expect to have reported better performance: increased ownership by the stakeholders; differential outcomes in terms of gender, socioeconomic status, and age; and a series of public policy recommendations.

Description:
The solution solves the lack of real time understanding on how tasks outside offices are being carried out. Georeferenced data can be logged while displaying the current state of tasks’ compliance, as well as improving planning possibilities. It also solves evasion and inadequate tax collection. GDW Government condenses in one platform the management of all procedures outside offices of the different administrations, units and governments. The government automates its management and is able to handle real time indicators provided by tasks’ compliance visibility. This makes the management’s times of execution and information more transparent for citizens.

The project is implemented through Agile (Scrum) method. Together with each involved area procedures were planned and designed, to follow up the fulfillment of major milestones that shape the project’s implementation, specific development requirements as well as integrations and general software tests, ensuring its compliance, satisfaction and quality. GDW Government is currently working in 6 areas and projects to be implemented shortly in other 9 additional ones.
Novelty:
This innovation makes it possible to manage activities carried out outside offices in a decentralized way and centered in real time. It ensures internal and external supplier’s service standards, allowing them to visualize the compliance of procedures. Simplifies administrative procedures of obtaining and managing field information.
It allows information to be tracked with precise location. It makes visible, more transparent and shares information to citizens through a free access portal, where it is possible to verify inspection’s pictures and records. This allows citizens to take part in performed operations.
Impact & Results: In general, this innovation improved government’s tax collection by recovering up to 15% of non-visible spaces and using planned finance mechanisms. This innovation improved the productivity of inspectors and field collaborators up to 20%. It decreased abnormal situations up to 90% and it lowered the use of paper up to 99%.

Project/Initiative Name: Data Governance Model for the National Power and Light Company (CNFL)
Description:
This project is about the collaborative development of a data governance model (DGM) for the CNFL that results in the delivery of quality public services through the use of data. The DGM is an innovative solution to a public challenge, which in turn is realised in the framework of a collaborative process.

Data management in public institutions is not only a challenge but also an opportunity that, if developed correctly, can represent great benefits for public institutions and therefore for citizens. In view of this, a proposal was made to develop a data governance model that manages data from its conception to its use, i.e., from the collection of information to its processing, and subsequently generating knowledge and wisdom for decision-making. 

In order to achieve this, it is initially necessary to have trained and sensitised human talent on the subject, and so, through a process of collaboration between INNOVAAP and CNFL, INNOVAAP began with the training of 30 civil servants from different areas, a task for which INNOVAAP was responsible. This training process was based on the Data Management Body of Knowledge of the Data Management Association (DAMA), which includes more than 15 sections on ethics, data modelling and architecture, data evaluation, roles and responsibilities. 

In this way, the project not only benefits the CNFL in data-based decision making, but after the correct management of data through the model, the public can access the data, now in open format, to carry out analyses and have clear and updated information on the work of the Company, parallel to an improvement in the quality of its service.
Novelty:
This project is a public innovation in that brings together public institutions and academia to respond to a need that has been identified and which will subsequently benefit the community chosen as the project's target population. 
It also seeks to respond, through a new methodology built on collaborative knowledge and an interdisciplinary group, to the necessary improvement in data management for effectiveness and efficiency as a result of the boom in the use of ICTs.
Impact & Results:
The results have been the training of 30 civil servants from different areas on data culture and governance, as well as the development of a methodological proposal for training in data analytics.
The methodology used to train human talent in data governance was the Data Management Body of Knowledge of the Data Management Association. The methodology used to assess the institution's level of data analytics maturity is based on the Gartner method, one of the most widely used and recognised currently.

Project/Initiative Name: Single digital platform for complaints: Fast and barrier-free service
Description:
Most public entities only had physical complaint books and no complaint handling processes to improve services to citizens.
The proposal was co-created between citizens and civil servants from different entities at the national level. It contemplates a standardised digital platform as a single channel for public entities and improves the management of citizen complaints through an agile and user-friendly process.

The proposed solution began in 2017 and was co-created in conjunction with citizens and civil servants from different entities at the national level, which includes a standardised digital platform as a single channel that integrates all public entities and improves internal management for handling citizen complaints, through an agile and user-friendly process. It also provides reports to take action and allows citizens to follow up on their complaints in real time. Its main function is to strengthen communication between the State and citizens in a timely and satisfactory manner.

The platform has been progressively implemented since August 2020 at national and international level in 161 countries, at the three levels of government. Its implementation has been achieved in more than 181 entities, which have dealt with more than 99,727 citizen complaints in a comprehensive and satisfactory manner, with an average response time of 10 working days (when the maximum period is 30 working days). Capacity building was also achieved through technical assistance, virtual training and MOOC courses (14,610 employees trained) so that they can carry out optimal management and internalise the importance of complaints in order to improve the quality of their services.

The experience was the winner of the Special Prize for Public Innovation of the Good Practices in Public Management 2022 Award, an annual recognition organised by Ciudadanos al Día since 2005. The information included in this form is part of the nomination dossier of the Secretariat of Public Management for the Award.
Novelty:
One of the most important aspects of the project is that it develops the "complaint route" from a citizen's point of view, and at the same time takes into account the management of complaints by the public administration.
Previous experiences, including the Complaints Book itself, considered normative aspects so that the citizen's complaint could be accepted and processed, without considering that the citizen does not necessarily know the regulations. The single platform guides the citizen who submits a complaint in an easy and friendly way, so that they can express their dissatisfaction with a service, even if they do not know the regulations on complaints and claims.
Impact & Results:
The platform has been progressively implemented since August 2020 at national and international level in 161 countries, at the three levels of government. Its implementation has been achieved in more than 181 entities, which have been attending to more than 99,727 citizen complaints in a comprehensive and satisfactory manner, with an average attention time of 10 working days (when the maximum period is 30 working days).
It is also possible to obtain information on the citizen's perception of the attention and response received through the digital platform report, where 38% of citizens are satisfied with the attention they have been receiving.

Project/Initiative Name: HazLab – Digital Lab for innovation in participation
Organisation: Directorate-General for Public Governance of the Ministry of Finance and the Civil Service
Description:
HazLab is a laboratory for the exploration and implementation of innovative solutions to improve participation in the design, implementation and evaluation phases of public policy. Although other public innovation labs exist, none provide a virtual space on the part of the central government.

Although there are public innovation laboratories at other levels of government, there was no laboratory in the General State Administration in which to experiment with improvements for the proper fit between citizens' demands and the initiatives promoted in public plans and programmes. HazLab is deployed in three initiatives:
- Digital tool
- Methodology of operation of a participatory laboratory
- Tested pilot tests

To this end, this digital tool has been designed, based on open source web solutions, together with the use of external tools (MS Teams, Sharepoint, Zoom, Mural), which is complemented by the development of a working methodology to implement a participatory laboratory in an agile, fast and efficient way, thanks to the lessons learned from the pilot experiences that will be carried out.
Novelty: HazLab was born with this purpose: to offer a digital space, a methodology and practical knowledge in which public entities can devise, design, experiment and learn about their policies and services to respond to social problems, listening to and actively collaborating with the different levels of the administration and civil society.
Impact & Results:
HazLab has generated these results:
- As a Meeting Point for the main actors involved in participation: +300 participants.
- As a collaborative work space, where knowledge is shared: +450 contents with +164,000 views.
- As a place for debate, where interaction with peers with common problems takes place through online sessions: +30 events with +500 attendees.
- As a project accelerator, using methodologies, guides and technological tools as support: 16 instruments.
- As an Incubator of initiatives, creating innovative solutions to citizens' needs: 3 working groups.
- As an Experimentation Centre, carrying out pilot experiences and testing them: 2 pilot tests (not finalised).
Many of the data are obtained from the reports generated by HazLab's own digital platform.

Project/Initiative Name: An anti-bullying tool for the Google Workspace
Organisation: Espoonlahti lower-secondary school / City of Espoo
Description:
The innovation addresses bullying in schools. It is a tool on how to report bullying quickly and react to it effectively. It also makes it possible to analyze data regarding bullying. The innovation lies in the use of existing tools within Google Workspace for Education and on how to use digital tools to identify and react to bullying. Many schools use Google Workspace for Education, so the innovation does not require any further investment.

The tool allows students and teachers to notify bullying by scanning a QR code. The reports are then stored in a safe place in the Google cloud. A group of teachers trained to address bullying can discuss incidents with students. The teachers can then store the reports of these discussions with a separate tool, also working within Google Workspace. All data on bullying is collected in the same place for the administration to be able to follow the proceedings on bullying. Follow-up is fast and easy for both the anti-bullying team and the administration. The tool allows the school to see trends (when, where, how often, who?) in bullying. 

The goal of this innovation is simply to make bullying more visible by empowering victims and witnesses of bullying to report bullying quickly and even anonymously if they want to.
Novelty: It is a fast way for students and pupils to report bullying. The data is stored in one place. The data collected allows for the administration to follow proceedings and for a deeper analysis of trends in bullying.
Impact & Results: This innovation was used in one school last academic year. The average number of reports on bullying went up from a previous two notifications a month to two notifications a week. This increased the effectiveness in dealing with bullying and also made it possible for the administration to follow the proceedings. It helped us teachers and administrators to identify pupils who were often the victims and also to identify pupils who bullied often. The tools within Google Workspace allowed for further data analysis on the reports as well, helping the team identify places at the school, where bullying occurs often. This allows the administration to enforce these locations at the school.

Project/Initiative Name: Appointments Platform of the Government of the State of Guanajuato
Description:
The project arose due to the low level of digitisation of the procedures offered by the government and the length of time spent at the counter. A state strategy of optimisation and digitisation was implemented, which streamlines services and saves time and resources for both the government and citizens. According to data collected by the Ministry of Transparency and Accountability in 2021, only 44.9% can be fully or partially carried out electronically.

Based on this problem, the state strategy for the optimisation and digitalisation of procedures was created, with the essential aim of improving the quality of service to citizens. This strategy was materialised through the development of an electronic appointments portal, which through the integration of various technologies, unifies the available means for the publication of information related to procedures, homogenises and anticipates the reception and validation of requirements, schedules appointments, generates appointments for attention at the counter, performs internal measurements of the efficiency of the process in search of its continuous improvement, reduces attention time and also raises the level of digitisation of procedures.

The process was designed in 4 main phases. In the first phase, the citizen selects the procedure and consults its requirements. In the second phase, the citizen registers the application by entering general data and attaching the required documents. In the third phase, the application is validated by public servants. If the application is correct, the citizen is notified so that an appointment can be made and a folio issued; otherwise, the citizen is given feedback so that the necessary adjustments can be made to the application and the appointment can be made. Finally, in the fourth phase, the citizen appears at the window on the date and time selected to finalise his or her application.
Novelty:
The new portal will allow citizens to have a single point of access to carry out any procedure with the State Government, with the creation of an account where they can update their general data and keep track of their procedure requests, with the certainty that the documentation will be correct at the time of presenting themselves to finalise the corresponding procedure at the counter.
The state strategy for the optimisation and digitalisation of procedures makes use of cutting-edge technology that ensures the identity of the citizen and the protection of personal data; in addition, the process includes the identification of apocryphal documents and reduces the materialisation of corruption risks.
Impact & Results:
The portal was published on 30 August 2022 and as of 28 September 20,780 requests for procedures have been received.
In the pilot area using the platform, the number of procedures in level 3 of digitisation has increased by 12% so far, leaving only 5% in level 2.
Before the portal, the duration of procedures was not recorded, however, on average a reduction of up to 40% of the time spent at the counter has been achieved.

Organisation: District Secretariat of Finance - Directorate of Statistics and Fiscal Studies - Subdirectorate of Sectorial Analysis
Description:
FiscalData is a tool created for the analysis of Bogotá's fiscal situation. It seeks to benefit the citizens of Bogotá by serving as a mechanism for participation on fiscal issues between civil society and the public and private sectors.

FiscalData is the District Fiscal Observatory, a tool to disseminate information on local public finance and public finances in Bogotá, aligned with objectives such as budget transparency and the quality of public spending. It includes data about the city's revenues and expenditures, associated with the collection and budget behaviour of the Colombian Capital District. 

In addition, it presents information in innovative and interactive formats on fiscal results and sources of financing for the analysis of the fiscal situation of the Colombian capital. It also contains indicators of the economic situation in Bogota, including the behaviour of economic activity, the labour market, consumer and business confidence and expectations, among others.

FiscalData has been developed at a time when Bogotá has joined transnational initiatives on open government and fiscal transparency such as the Open Government Partnership, the Open Data Charter, the Infrastructure Transparency Initiative (CoST) and the Global Initiative for Fiscal Transparency (GIFT) (Bogotá being the first local government to join the GIFT initiative). 

FiscalData is inspired not only by the experience and good practices promoted by the aforementioned transnational organisations and initiatives (on fiscal transparency and open government), but also by the functioning of other observatories with an emphasis on fiscal and economic issues that have operated not only in Bogotá and Colombia, but also in other regions and countries, and that focus on different key challenges of urban development (health, environment, mobility, economy, among others).
Novelty:
FiscalData provides a mechanism for transparency and inspection of tax expenditures and revenues, preventing corruption and tax evasion practices that have a direct impact on inequality.

FiscalData serves as a driver of sustainable development in the city through fiscal and social protection policies that progressively achieve greater equality at the district level: Bogotá accounts for a quarter of the national economy.
FiscalData is perceived as an institution that seeks to effectively and transparently assist with accountability on fiscal matters in the Capital District.
Impact & Results:
FiscalData has been the ideal scenario to publish and visualise key issues of interest to the Ministry of Finance. Quality spending strategies will have a space in the Observatory that will allow the involvement of new audiences and institutional allies. 
FiscalData has achieved more than 10,000 visits and has had more than 1,000 people answering questions to find out their expectations and bets about the Observatory. 
FiscalData has indicator dashboards, pieces and infographics on economic and public finance indicators, and sectoral information bulletins. It also contains recurrently updated budget, tax and financing dashboards.

Organisation: Secretariat for Innovation and Digital Transformation
Description:
Boti is the first government chatbot in the world to operate through WhatsApp, with a record 11 million conversations in January 2022 and a monthly average of 5 million. It has become the preferred channel for citizens to manage procedures, appointments, requests and access information provided by the City Government. We developed a new non-keyword based intelligence model that complements our AI and natural language processing.

Boti. The most widely used messaging application in the country allowed the City Government to be where the people are. 
Through the use of Artificial Intelligence systems, Boti processes users' messages and provides them with answers to their queries. A great advantage it has over other bots is its transactional feature, as it interacts with external systems to return a specific answer; in this case, about services available in government areas and external entities. 

Currently, the catalogue of contents available in Boti is very wide: first of all, the chatbot is informative. Its contents are aligned with the current situation of the City, constantly adapting to changes and new developments. In this line, it offers information on urban mobility, recycling, health, safety, culture, events, public space and tourism. It is also possible to learn more about social care programmes such as protection of rights, childhood and adolescence, family and disability, assistance for people in situations of socio-economic vulnerability or street situations, and care for addictions. 

The design of Boti's personality, friendly and approachable, allowed us to incorporate other content such as "Boticuentos", interactive stories to read and play with children, choosing the actions of the characters and the end of the stories, all through WhatsApp. In this line, the chatbot also has tongue twisters, jokes and the possibility of downloading stickers. These disruptive initiatives are an example of the constant opportunities for improvement and the diversity of content that Boti offers.
Novelty:
Boti pioneered the delivery of COVID-19 test results and certificates at a key epidemiological moment for the country and the city. It has also been a pioneer throughout the vaccination campaign, where more than 11,000,000 appointments have been offered through WhatsApp.
With more than 450 topics, 70 integrations and 15 human attention queues, Boti has revolutionised the way in which the City Government deals with the queries of its neighbours. The quality of the bot was ensured by analysing conversations and clustering messages that were not understood by means of NLP, which makes it possible to attack the most representative messages and distinguish them in different topics. With the collaboration of different providers (Amazon, Google, and Botmaker) we are working on a model that will allow the bot to perform SEO, indexing all the chatbot's content and learning automatically from the interaction of users with the bot.
Impact & Results:
Received 200,000 pre-pandemic conversations per month to one that today receives an average of 1,300,000 non-COVID-19 conversations. 
The exponential growth of conversations and neighbours who choose Boti as their main channel for communicating with the state can be explained by the more than 70 integrations that make it possible to take appointments, manage procedures and services that are key to their daily lives.
On the other hand, Boti has an average monthly satisfaction rate of 90%, a statistic obtained from satisfaction surveys carried out in different conversational flows.
More than 80% of the conversations are understood and in those that are not, Boti sends content suggestions or offers the possibility of talking to a person.

Project/Initiative Name: ELECTRONIC QUOTER: A PERU PURCHASING tool for efficient market research
Description:
It consists of the implementation of an information service called "Electronic Quoter", aimed at public entities that contract through the Electronic Catalogues of Framework Agreements, which allows them to estimate the price of the goods contained in their purchase request and has allowed them to reduce the time of the preparatory actions of the procurement process from 68.1 calendar days to only 1 day, for the benefit of 2,000 public entities and the citizens they serve.

PERU COMPRAS, as part of its continuous improvement process, conducts annual satisfaction surveys to identify opportunities for improvement in the procurement tools it administers, including the Electronic Catalogues of Framework Agreements. A recurring problem identified by the public entities surveyed was the lack of information on the prices of the goods included in the electronic catalogues, in order to process the budget certification, a document necessary to start any procurement process, of which we can highlight the following comment: "The application should display the prices of the goods offered in the Catalogues, to avoid setbacks with the Budget Credit Certification (CCP)". 

On 11 April 2018, a proposal was submitted to the Head of PERU COMPRAS for the development of a web application to estimate the price of the goods contracted through the electronic catalogues in a user-friendly way. This service started operations in pilot version (minimum viable). 

As a result, the new design also incorporated the information on the bids in a summarised form, i.e. using statistical algorithms to guarantee the confidentiality of the information indicated in the documents that regulate the Framework Agreements. Finally, and after incorporating these improvements in the design, on 12 February 2020 the "National Electronic Quotation System" was launched for the benefit of 1,969 public entities and as a consequence also for the benefit of the citizens who make use of their services.
Novelty:
It optimises the activities that make up the preparatory acts stage of the public procurement process (when improvement actions are generally focused on the selection and execution stages of the contract).

The Electronic Quoter makes use of statistical algorithms to estimate in less than a day the price of goods required by public entities. It uses a product search tool, with a user-friendly presentation and filters that optimise the search time, because it is based on artificial intelligence technology components. As well as blockchain, which allows us to preserve the immutability and transparency of the information.
Impact & Results:
The overall result is to have reduced the time for preparatory acts from 68.1 calendar days to 1 calendar day, thereby simplifying approximately 155,822 annual procurements at the national level, for an estimated amount of $ 400 million dollars.
According to a survey taken to measure the impact on users:
- 80.5% of users are satisfied with the service.
- 88.6% confirm that the service has allowed them to reduce the time it takes to carry out market research.
- 81.3% agree with the statement, "The Quotation Service has provided information to estimate the price of the requirements contained in the electronic catalogues and to manage the corresponding budget certification".
- NPS =13. The NPS (Net Promoter Score) recommendation indicator was positive, i.e. we have a higher number of users who would recommend our service.

Organisation: Centro Nacional en Sistemas de Información en Salud
Description:
Healthtech Builders Lab Chile (HBL Chile), a pioneering collaborative programme between the public, private and academic sectors, which promoted the design, development and implementation of a technological platform to improve the quality and timely care of oncology patients at the Instituto Nacional del Tórax, Chile's leading public hospital.

The main challenge at INT is the deficit of digital records that allow the construction and management of indicators to evaluate and improve care management that contribute to timely care and monitoring of cancer patients and also allow the dissemination of results for research and promotion purposes to society and decision-makers. 

This is a platform that allows the administrative management of performance, quality and clinical indicators for timely and quality care at different stages of the cancer patient's journey.
The platform has three modules; i) Patient notification and messaging tool via WhatsApp, patients have access to the best medical care, based on artificial intelligence algorithms remote triage is performed in the referral of a patient from PHC; ii) Workflow management tool for health staff throughout the cancer patient journey and iii) Oncology committee management tool that accompanies doctors and their teams during the patient journey integrating the necessary information to resolve a case.

The main beneficiaries are patients with suspected bronchopulmonary cancer who are referred from primary care to the National Thoracic Institute. On the other hand, INT will be able to improve the administrative management of its resources and clinical management for timely patient care.
Novelty:
The Health Tech Builders Lab Chile (HBL Chile) initiative is a pioneering collaborative co-creation initiative in Chile, between the public and private sectors and academia with the purpose of improving quality and timely care for oncology patients in Chile's public system. From the outset, it was designed to address the needs of INT, putting the patient at the centre of all decision-making processes. 
To date, it is the only collaborative platform that allows intelligent data management for clinical and administrative decision-making aimed at greater efficiency in the health system at the National Thoracic Institute, generating institutional strategies for catastrophic diseases such as bronchopulmonary cancer.
Impact & Results:
We are currently in a phase of training in the use of the platform. We have trained more than 30 technicians and professionals, both administrative and clinical, and the platform is in a white run process, and we expect in October to start using the platform at the operational level in the daily flow of INT.
The results will be displayed interactively in a module of the platform and correspond to multiple indicators that are relevant to measure to improve the administrative and clinical management of INT, the health service to which INT belongs and the Ministry of Health, as they will allow them to develop targeted strategies and national public policies.

Description:
The MINKACUNI programme aims to carry out minor works with neighbourhood associations through targeted tax collection campaigns. It is innovative because it is a participatory and collaborative alternative that promotes a tax culture.

To this end, a process was designed and implemented to assess the feasibility of carrying out a collection campaign based on the number of residents, the level of delinquency and the feasibility of carrying out the work required by the residents of the area. 

The coordination of the tax campaigns is carried out with the leaders of the district's neighbourhood associations, in order to promote community payment and to ensure that the revenue collected is invested in a project for that sector. In order to encourage even more participation, special tax benefits are granted for paying on that day and a raffle is held among all those who make payments in the campaign, a combo of electrical appliances. 

In 2021, 4,520 residents of the associations where the programme was implemented were assisted with the delivery of materials for the execution of minor works through Neighbourhood Development Programmes. Likewise, in the same year, delinquency has been reduced by an average of 6.40% in 34 associations where tax campaigns were carried out, reducing delinquency by 27.71% in one association in a single day of collection, being the highest indicator of all the campaigns in 2021. In terms of investments, in 2021, S/. 414,009.41 soles were invested in 13 neighbourhood development programmes, with funds generated by non-budgeted income from targeted tax campaigns on Sundays. The experience was the winner of the Public Advocacy category of the Good Practices in Public Management Award 2022.
Novelty:
The project is innovative because in the design phase it was very important to evaluate the delinquency of the neighbourhood associations of Manchay and the rural population centres, in order to prioritise the execution of the tax campaigns.
In addition, the proposal generates an alternative to the total remission of tax debts of delinquent taxpayers, establishing a viable alternative contribution, strengthening a tax culture among residents and community participation in the development of public works.
Impact & Results: Delinquency was reduced by an average of 6.40% in 34 associations where tax campaigns were carried out, reaching a 27.71% reduction in delinquency in one association on a single collection day (Sunday) as the highest rate in the year. Furthermore, In each collection campaign, evaluations and measurements are carried out through the itinerant modules and virtual means of payment.

Project/Initiative Name: Executive Secretariat of the National Anti-Corruption System
Organisation: Executive Secretariat of the National Anti-Corruption System
Description:
The Executive Secretariat of the National Anticorruption System of Mexico developed the National Digital Platform (PDN). This was an unprecedented effort to build the first ever public Platform based on standardised and open data from strategic sectors, to generate intelligence to fight corruption. The Platform promotes evidence-based decision making for authorities in charge of preventing and sanctioning corruption and fosters openness and accountability for citizens.

The PDN orders extensive volumes of strategic data, currently published in information silos by multiple government agencies, using data standards (rules to order the data). Institutions that generate the data will still control it, but the Platform is now able to consult it and make it interoperable in real time.

The data available in the PDN is integrated in six systems: S1) Evolution of assets, conflict of interest declarations, and certificate of tax declarations from all public officials, S2) Civil servants responsible for public contracting, S3) Debarred civil servants and companies, S4) Information and communication between the National Audit System and the National Anti-Corruption System, S5) Single window for public complaints for administrative offenses and corruption cases. S6) Public information about public contracting processes.

We have released four versions (Beta 0.6, 0.7, 1.0 and 1.1). The PDN currently has 22 entities connected (three levels of government and autonomous bodies); 4 out of 6 systems are now operational and have over 1 million data fields. We have also created the Digital Anticorruption Market (MDA), the first ever open-source software marketplace for government institutions in Mexico that facilitates connecting with the National Digital Platform.
Novelty:
The PDN is innovative because:
1.         The National Digital Platform is the first tool in the history of Mexico that allows processing millions of standardized data to generate anti-corruption intelligence.
2.         For the first time in history, the PDN allows processing data from asset declarations to analyze the evolution of assets and red flag any anomalies by generating algorithms that provide results with a click.
3.         It has the first Anticorruption Digital Market, the first ever free and open source space created by a public institution in the history of Mexico, with more than 22 tools that support interconnection with the PDN and generate savings of millions of pesos for public institutions.
4.         For the first time it provides open by default, timely, accessible, interoperable and inclusive strategic anticorruption open data at the national level from more than 5,000 institutions.
5.         Using Artificial Intelligence and machine learning the PDN helps to identify red flags
Impact & Results:
In 2019 there were only 3 entities connected, currently, there are 22 entities that have standardized their data and are now data providers of the PDN, resulting in more than 1 million data.
For the first time ever, authorities and public officials are now able to know with public officials have been sanctioned for committing an act of corruption at the federal level and in 12 other entities.
