Description:
This innovation provides the possibility of meeting public servants with digital video calls in real time allowing to identify and solve the citizen's questions more quickly and to assist in the realization of the service.

This innovation expands the service channels available to the citizen, making the service more inclusive and contributing to a greater digital empowerment of the citizen.

The existence of a prior scheduling with the indication of the service and information about the requirements of the same makes it possible for the operator's assistance to be more effective.
Novelty: Video calls with the public administrations.
Impact & Results: No evidence available.

Organisation: AMA - Agência para a Modernização Administrativa
Description:
The LabX language simplification methodology was developed to support public entities to adopt simple writing in their communication. The goal of the Language Simplification Workshops is to enable public servants to have collaborative sessions with their recipients and to tailor the communication according to the feedback received. This allows entities to effectively focus their communication on their readers, improving administrative efficiency and citizen participation itself.

During the Workshop, that had to be held online due to Covid-19 restrictions, public entities and citizens collaboratively went through these 6 steps:
1.  Survey and selection of the document
2. Diagnosis of the document, carried out with two specific exercises
3. Revision of the document, applying the 10 guidelines and good practices identified by LabX (AMA)
4. Pre-test of the collaborative session: design the planning and simulation of the session with the target audience of the communication
5. Test with the target audience, with 4 to 8 participants
6. Production of the final document, in which the entity analyzes the results of the session and draws up a new version of the document

The workshops are designed for 2 hours, with an explanatory part of the methodology and a more practical part for participants to read the communication and evaluate and rewrite it. LabX has already done these Workshops with 8 public entities.
Novelty: The Workshops, the "do-it-yourself" manual, and the 10 guidelines are innovative because they show how Public Administration entities can transform documents or communications into a more simplified language, that is more inclusive and accessible. The LabX methodology is innovative because it directly involves citizens.
Impact & Results:
The overall satisfaction of the 109 civil servants who have already participated in our workshops (average of 4.6 on a scale of 5) shows that this is a training project that should continue to be disseminated and replicated. On the positive aspects, the participants align their opinion that it is a theme with relevance to their professional activity and the 10 guidelines have an immediate useful effect right after the training. In the organization of the session they highlight the practical component and the dynamism that allows the active intervention of all participants and the good sharing of ideas.
We are currently evaluating the scope of the impact of the methodology's application in the field, through a questionnaire and interviews with the promoters of the initiative within the entities so we can review the materials and the organization of the workshop itself.

Description:
Historically, countries have been competing in the international trade space, approaching trade as a zero-sum game with winners and losers. The World Logistics Passport aims to shift the paradigm from competition to cooperation, by applying a Nash equilibrium in the form of a loyalty program that encourages countries to work with each other to grow trade, jobs, and prosperity.

The WLP network is composed of global hubs - countries distributed along the key global trade routes - and gateways - countries that constitute the backbone of regional trade corridors. In each WLP hub, key entities such as ports, airports, customs offices, chambers of commerce, logistics service providers, and financial institutions commit to providing incentives to all players who conduct international trade among any section of the global network. The incentives are offered in the form of financial and non-financial benefits, such as discounts on tariffs and fees, streamlined procedures for cargo clearance, and priority access to premium services, to name a few. Each member has access to a set of benefits based on its trade performance and is incentivized to grow its trade volume through the WLP network hubs to access higher-tier benefits.

Today, over 1,400 traders from 40+ countries across the Americas, Africa, Middle East, Asia, and Oceania benefit from being members of the WLP network and are able to claim benefits from the 120+ program partners. As the World Logistics Passport nears 3 years since its launch, the objective of the program is to reach more traders globally and continue to support international trade by further reducing its barriers.
Novelty: The WLP is the first freight-related loyalty programs encompassing more than one geography, more than one type of service provider and that is open for all entities evolving around the trade and logistics sector. The WLP’s partners range between airport and port operators, customs administrations, overarching ministries and relevant authorities, airlines, trade and logistics associations, credit banks, insurance companies, and more.
Impact & Results: The key metric to measure the success of the program, even at its current early stage, is the impact on trade growth. Several econometric methodologies have been used to estimate such impact, including the Trade Elasticity methodology, the Structural Gravity Model, the Difference-in-difference model, and the Synthetic Control Model. Overall, the joint assessment of these methodologies suggests that the program contributed to an incremental trade growth of around 1% in 2021 in the emirate of Dubai, the first and most advanced hub where the program pilot was implemented.

Description:
The City of Amman is working towards translating resident-driven data into usable knowledge. They aim to optimize service provision for a wide range of critical services, especially during times of crisis.

On its race to transform the delivery of services to citizens. It wants citizens to be able to self-serve a range of functions online or via mobile, from paying parking tickets to checking housing permits, to reporting abandoned waste, or to receive the city's support during a crisis outbreak. To date, 85% of citizen services have been converted to online. This is part of a city-wide program aimed at streamlining services and saving costs. It will also make GAM more responsive to citizen’s needs.
Novelty: First one-stop shop app for city's service delivery in Jordan.

Organisation: The State Agency for Public Service and Social Innovations under the President  of the Republic of Azerbaijan
Description:
Idea Bank was created by the State Agency. Idea Bank, as a single idea platform across the country, is a collecting place for ideas to improve the activities of public organizations, develop production and service areas, and take steps to further improve the social welfare of the society. The purpose of creating the Idea Bank was related to the improvement of the activities of state organizations, including the collection of ideas from citizens for the implementation of innovative solutions.

On the Idea Bank platform, every organization has the opportunity to collect ideas in any direction according to its requirements, and to involve people and experts in the management process. State organizations can register on the site and announce contests in any number and subject related to the improvement of their activities.

Allows for collecting ideas from civil servants and citiens alike.
Novelty: One of the main innovative advantages of the Idea Bank is the complete automation of all processes. All processes for announcing contests, sending ideas, receiving them, selecting people who will evaluate ideas, choosing criteria for evaluating ideas, conducting evaluation, announcing the results of the competition, preparing statistical data, and sending information about all these processes via email and monitoring them in a personal cabinet are realized in fully automated mode.
Impact & Results: Enhancement of digitalization in state processes, introduction of innovative ideas into management processes are the results of platform activities. Based on the ideas collected in the platform, various technical innovations were created, which were then installed in 24 ASAN Service centers around the country.

Organisation: Belgian Federal Public Service Policy and Support (BOSA)
Description:
The eBox Citizen is a digital mailbox in which Belgian citizens can receive official documents from Belgian public instances at every level. Given the complex nature of Belgium’s federal state structure, the deployment of such a centralized eBox has proven to be a crucial step in the continuous digitalization of public services in Belgium. It got an enormous boost throughout the Covid-19 pandemic, as the quickest way for citizens to receive an invitation for their vaccination was via their eBox.

3.964.677 Belgians (i.e. 30,13% of all Belgians over 18 years old) gave their consent to receive official documents in their eBox on this day. 74.036.492 documents were sent in 2021. 429 senders are currently using eBox for sending official documents. Given the increasing number of phishing attempts, additional innovative security measures are being integrated. For example, in case a user has given his/her consent and activated e-mail notifications, he or she can now choose a personal image (an icon in a specific colour) which will be attached to every notification this person receives from my eBox.
Novelty:
'- The eBox helps us moving towards a paperless administration, it is an important step with regards to using more sustainable ways for communication with citizens.
- It is an example of a succesful public-private partnership, as so called Human Interface Providers (HIPs) were actively involved from the beginning onwards.
- The eBox Citizen has integrated multiple features in order to defend citizens agains phishing: in case a user has given his/her consent and activated e-mail notifications, he or she can now choose a personal image (an icon in a specific colour) which will be attached to every notification this person receives from my eBox.
Impact & Results: 3.964.677 Belgians (i.e. 30,13% of all Belgians over 18 years old) gave their consent to receive official documents in their eBox on this day. 74.036.492 documents were sent in 2021. 429 senders are currently using eBox for sending official documents.

Project/Initiative Name: Intergovernmental Network and Competition for regulatory reform
Organisation: ministry of the interior and safety
Description:
This program was designed to provide a driving force for regulatory reform and for mutual learning between local governments. It is not the existing top-down regulatory innovation, but it motivates the bottom-up regulatory reform policy and enables mutual learning of best practices, allowing local governments to benefit. This is because regulatory innovation has been poorly shared between local governments and has been carried out in a top-down manner.

Local regulatory reform best practice competition (LLBPC) helps solve this problem. It provides a space to share the most urgent and important cases of regulatory reform implemented by region. Each local government has an incentive to innovate regulations because it the winning local government gains financial resources and its best practices are published. LLBPC is a kind of meta-regulatory innovation that innovates local government regulatory reform.

Our project has bottom-up and flexible approach. The competition begins by receiving several cases from 17 local governments through open recruitment. The first screening is jointly reviewed by the Ministry of the Interior and Safety and local governments to select twice the number of final selections. In the second examination, the cases selected in the first examination are reviewed by experts from academia and research institutes to determine excellent cases. The first screening procedure, which is carried out several times with the help of private experts, ensures the excellence of the case and the fairness of the competition. The spread of regulatory reform through competition between local governments creates new competition and leads to a virtuous cycle structure.
Novelty: Novel structure that supports best practice in the field of policy innovation with a competition among local governments. Bottom-up approach.
Impact & Results: The competition has been held every year since 2018, and a total of 55 best practices were selected by 2021. It plans to select 16 new cases this year as well. Excellent regulatory innovation cases implemented in each region. Contributes to the nationwide spread of best practices for regulatory innovation. Innovation tailored to consumers is possible through a bottom-up regulatory innovation method.

Organisation: National Infrastructures for Research and Technology (GRNET S.A)
Description:
Hellenic education system needs about 50,000 substitute teachers every year to staff Greek schools. These teachers have to travel to their district education headquarters, to sign their one-year contract with the state. Through the platform, contract is signed digitally, avoiding unnecessary travelling, while the necessary information systems of the public administration are updated interoperably, saving thousands of working hours for the employees of the 128 local education directorates.

The platform is developed as opensource software and as such, can be Retained, Reused, Revised, Remixed and Redistributed, offering any stakeholder (public or private) that contracts with citizens, the ability to reuse it and expand it, according to its needs. The benefits from the platform are many: substitute teachers avoid unnecessary travel, local primary and secondary education directorates save tens of thousands of work hours, school principals, by using the interoperability of the new platform, can inform the administration about the undertaking service of new teachers, the state's communication with citizens is modernized, the Ministry of Education adapts effectively, efficiently and responsibly to society's demands for immediate staffing of schools, bureaucracy is reduced effectively for all the stakeholders.
Novelty: The platform is opensource and able to interconnect information systems. In this way it leverages digital tools to reduce bureaucratic burden.
Impact & Results: Impact measurement is the quantitative assessment of impact based on measured observations from the log files of the application. Also the qualitative assessment is based on written and oral reports, of all involved stakeholders (teachers, school principals, local education directorates etc.) who mention job satisfaction, greater support from the central administration and simplification of procedures. During the school year 2021/22, 48.256 teachers digitally signed their contract, while during the first round of the school year 2022/23, 28.737 signed their contract.

Project/Initiative Name: Take Your Qualifications with You: Mobile National Technical Qualifications Service
Description:
Every year more than 3.5 million people apply for Korea’s National Technical Qualifications. Designed to represent an individual’s capacity as objectively as possible, they are integral to one’s career, academic degrees, pursuing a livelihood, and other important personal milestones. Annually, about 700,000 successful test-takers have to visit the district offices of the Human Resources Development Service of Korea (HRDKorea) to issue paper certificates or wait for it to arrive by post. Aware of the ongoing digital transformation and user needs, HRDKorea partnered with the private sector to leverage industry-leading technology for the Mobile National Technical Qualification Service. After a simple authentication process on the phone, you can now take your qualifications everywhere.

Breaking away completely from its previous paper-based format, the certificate layout was fully reconfigured to fit the smartphone environment. HRDKorea’s Q-Net - national qualifications data aggregation platform - synced its qualifications holder database with Naver and Kakao (private-sector firms with whom HRDKorea partnered) who used blockchain technology to develop a service that allowed users to immediately access their certificates after an authentication step on their apps. In January of 2021 the Mobile National Technical Qualifications Service came online. Within the year of launch alone more than 2.3 million users accessed the Service, quickly establishing itself as a widely used public convenience service.

We also implemented strategic collaboration projects to expand the use of Mobile National Technical Qualifications. When Naver develops integrated services with a job search website, HRDKorea also provides data to this website. Therefore, even when jobseekers are filling out CVs on that website, they can enter the HRDKorea qualifications data which has been saved to Naver. Further, for KakaoTalk users certificates come with digital badges for their profile images. This makes it easy to represent or recognize credentials on the messenger, which contributes to having a business enabling environment on the platform.

As such, the Mobile Technical Qualifications Service is not just an extension of the certification program, but is actually an augmented service that performs the added role of a bridge between individuals and the job market. Along with acting as a safer remote option of providing certificate issuing services during the pandemic, an annual cost reduction of KRW2.1 billion (approx. USD1.4 million) further helped operation efficiency. Cost and time burden upon the public has been almost completely removed while providing safer and more diverse application options.
Novelty: Access to public service is enhanced by leveraging high-penetration platforms for service delivery. HRDKorea selected Naver and Kakao as partners for the Mobile Technical Qualifications Service and provided them with access to its data. This is the first time a public agency tasked with operating national qualifications programs has developed a smartphone-based certificate for on-demand credentials checks. User convenience is significantly improved as no document scanning is needed, nor the downloading of a new app as the Service runs on either one of the two apps almost every Korean already has. Simply follow an authentication step and you have your certificate in hand. Perhaps most importantly, the Mobile National Technical Qualifications Service has a modular design that fits into a wide range of other services. More than just a guarantee of qualifications, it is an active communicator of the person’s capacity when doing business or during job searches.
Impact & Results: In 2021 – the launch year – more than 2.3 million people used the Service. About 700,000 people acquire National Technical Qualifications each year, so the initiative has established itself as a widely used public convenience service in a short period. It was selected as the 2021 Citizens’ Choice for Outstanding Government Innovation and was invited to showcase at the K-Innovation Expo. Today, the government even allows its use as ID for national qualifications tests and elections. This is the first time a public agency operating national certifications has developed a mobile-based certificate, thus leading digital innovation in public services, as evidenced by various peer organizations such as Korea Chamber of Commerce and Industry, Korea Coast Guard, and G-TELP Committee of Korea following suit. The initiative also reduced costs by about USD1.4 million, while also strengthening public trust by preventing misplacing of paper certificates, or forgery and other illegal practices.

Project/Initiative Name: MITOS - The National Registry of Administrative Procedures
Description:
MITOS is an informational platform, which provides all necessary information to citizens and end-users about administrative processes, governmental services, applicable regulations and rules (to some extent, as the full scope of applicable law and technical rules is beyond the scope of any given portal, at least at present), and competent authorities. At the moment, MITOS contains more than 2200 fully described services and procedures, another 2300 that are under processing. It is expected to collect about 5000 procedures and services in total, which are provisioned by the law for the public administration.

The project follows a vertical registration model, similar to hierarchical management, which comprises (a) several hundred editors, state employees from different ministries, who are responsible for registering and keeping track of a specific set of administrative procedures each; (b) supervisors at each ministry who guarantee the content quality and validity; and (c) a group of administrators from the Ministry of Digital Governance, who supervise the whole project. Editors and administrators are trained using an asynchronous training platform and a support forum.

MITOS core is based on a MediaWiki installation, the open source software used by Wikipedia. Building on MediaWiki and its extensions, MITOS stores its data in a relational database and employs the Semantic MediaWiki in order to allow queries on the well structured information of the Registry. Following the effort of the European Commission that developed standards and core vocabularies to facilitate the systematic description of public services, the MITOS API allows to share content with other applications and supports send and receive requests in the form of JSON or XML documents. The BPMN generation module automatically creates a simplified BPMN diagram of the procedure from the described execution steps.

MITOS is one of the three pillars of the National Policy for Administrative Procedures, along with the Observatory for the measurement of executional and administrative burdens and the National Framework for Process Simplification.
Novelty: The project is innovative because of its innovative mission and the crowdsourcing solution leveraging the power of MediaWiki.
Impact & Results: 1600 published procedures and another 660 are fully described and ready to be published. More than 400 editors and 100 administrators have enrolled to MITOS and have received the synchronous and asynchronous training. More than 242,000 pageviews and 34,000 visits on a monthly basis. For measuring the impact to the economy, the environment and society, we use a combination of tools and practices from popular methodologies including TIMM from PwC, Four pillars by NPC, Theory of Change, etc. The key impact objectives have been transformed to quantifiable targets and indicators, which are either visualized on MITOS dashboard, or depicted on the monthly reports we create for the Ministry of Digital Governance. The goal is to have all ~5000 administrative procedures by the end of the year and engage all editors and administrators, as well as to quadruple the daily views and visits by citizens.

Project/Initiative Name: ‘Performing and Visual Arts’ Ambedkar Schools of Specialized Excellence (ASoSE): Mainstreaming Arts  as education in the formal school set up
Organisation: Dialogue and Development Commission of Delhi
Description:
Performing and Visual Arts (ASoSE) represents India’s first bold endeavour to reimagine secondary education that currently offers students only 3 pathways. These schools offer learning in ‘Music’, ‘Visual Arts’ and ‘Filmmaking Acting and Media Studies’ for low-income students with creative aptitude/interests. They empower students with artistic interests to unlock aspirational higher-ed and career pathways in the creative field of their choice alongside pursuing academic studies.

The school nurtures young artists through an immersive four-year journey (Grade 9-12) of experiential learning in their chosen creative fields while also incorporating foundational academic subjects. The unique proposition of the school is not only that it pioneers industry-oriented and in-depth progressive learning of creative arts within schools, but that students’ chosen creative subjects are curricular, assessed, and hold at-par weightage in their exams as other academic subjects.

The launch of these schools took place in September 2021 with grade 9 admissions into 2 pilot schools and 2 more were added in 2022. For any selected creative field, 2 curricular subjects called ‘specialized subjects’ are taught to the students for 1-hr each every day while the rest of the school day incorporates ‘foundational subjects’ (grade-relevant academic subjects). For each creative-field, a top-tier Indian higher-ed institute has been on-boarded as the government’s ‘Knowledge Partner’ to develop industry-relevant curriculum and assessments.
Novelty: SoSEs represent India’s first bold endeavour to re-imagine traditional secondary education by introducing Performing and Visual Arts courses.  The uniqueness of this endeavour manifests in the school’s curriculum (‘inquiry-based learning’ where students’ curiosity leads teaching), assessments ( projects, tech exercises, research work etc.), and pedagogy (learning through independent projects and practical learning led by teachers who are industry professionals and bring real-world experiences into classrooms).
Impact & Results:
While early impact results in the pilot year are evolving, qualitative evidence of impact is consistently visible:
~2000 students applied for 300 seats offered in the admissions cycle 2022-2 (1:6 selection ratio) demonstrating high student traction
~30% of these applications were from private school students, reversing the typical student migration trend
Leading top-tier private higher-ed institutes such as WWI, SMI were interested to partner and become part of this revolutionary shift
98% students reported receiving greater exposure, improved learning, more career-awareness and higher engagement with schools
Students are already securing opportunities to do professional projects and performances.

Project/Initiative Name: Doorstep Delivery of Public Services - Delhi's Model of Transforming Public Service Delivery
Organisation: Dialogue and Development Commission of Delhi
Description:
‘Doorstep Delivery of Public Services’ allows citizens to access government services from the comfort of their homes without visiting any government offices. Any citizen in Delhi can book an appointment slot to receive one of these 100 services by dialing 1076 which redirects them to a centralized call center. A Mobile Sahayak then visits the citizen as per the appointment to collect and upload the requisite documents for the service and submit it to the corresponding government offices.

The project was launched in three phases of 40 services in September 2018 followed by 30 services in July 2019 and another 30 in December 2019. Doorstep Delivery currently hosts 100 services across 13 departments; these include revenue department services like caste, income, domicile and surviving member certificates - many of which are essentials for citizens to prove their eligibility for government welfare schemes, especially for those who belong to low-income backgrounds. It also offers other essential services related to transport, labor registration, pension, social welfare, food and civil supplies, property and land related services. The government is currently planning to onboard 200 more services to this project over the next financial year.

Any citizen can book an appointment slot to receive one of these 100 services by dialing a toll-free number 1076 which redirects them to a centralized call center, who will book an appointment slot for the citizen. A Mobile Sahayak is then allocated to visit the citizen as per the appointment schedule. These Mobile Sahayaks act as a face of the government and visit the citizens to collect and upload the requisite documents for the service and submit it to the corresponding government office, all at a modest fee of INR 50. The status of this service request can be tracked throughout the process through a unique application number.

Doorstep Delivery has thus far received More than 2 Million calls, processed about 4,30,000 service requests and successfully served 3,60,000 citizens since the inception of the project, including a major disruption to service delivery caused due to the pandemic. The project currently stands at an average serving 10,000 citizens per month. The Delhi Government ultimately envisages Doorstep Delivery to become the primary mode through which citizens in Delhi access government services. Delhi Chief Minister, The Delhi Government’s aim is to ensure that no citizen has to physically come to a government office for accessing the services one needs.
Novelty: The programme is a relatively new means of engaging with the citizens through a new mode to access basic citizen centric services by dialling a toll-free number over and above the existing physical and online modes. The scheme further has recruited two man-power agencies and a private call centre to manage the supply side management of this new service mechanism. While many governments have engaged private participation in the past to facilitate citizen centric services, the toll-free number to access government services through a ‘Mobile Sahayak’ is relatively new.
Impact & Results: Doorstep Delivery has thus far received More than 2 Million calls, processed about 4,30,000 service requests and successfully served 3,60,000 citizens since the inception of the project, including a major disruption to service delivery caused due to the pandemic. The project currently stands at an average serving 10,000 citizens per month.

Project/Initiative Name: Reform of seasonal employment in agriculture
Description:
The portal is a platform for employers who do business in agriculture, forestry, and fisheries sectors to electronically register their seasonal workers. It is directly linked to the services of the Tax Administration which enables automated generation of tax calculations which are then electronically forwarded to the employer. Seasonal workers can be registered through a mobile phone application which represents the first-ever mobile phone application of any Serbian Government Agency. Before January 2019, employers could only register their seasonal farm workers based on the Labour Law and in practice, over 95% of the approximately 80,000 seasonal workers engaged in the agricultural sector were employed without registration, working in the informal economy, without any labour-related rights.

The portal and application for e-registration of seasonal workers in agriculture are completely owned by Tax Administration of the Republic of Serbia. The newly created system is directly linked to the system of the Central Registry of Compulsory Social Insurance, which allows workers registered through this system to automatically obtain the right to pension insurance for each day of employment, as well as health insurance in case of injury at work. Further, the portal is directly linked to the services of the Tax Administration which enables automated generation of tax calculation, which are then electronically forwarded to the employer. Lastly, National Employment Service is connected to the portal which enables seasonal workers register to be created in accordance with the Law. 

In the three and a half years of the implementation of the system, a total of 72,300 seasonal workers who worked over 3,6 milion working days were registered and 9,6 million Euros was paid in the taxes and contributions. The portal and the application were accessed by 660 employers.
Novelty: The portal represents an innovative way for registration of seasonal workers in agriculture. It is very easy to use, represents one-stop-shop and takes several minutes to register worker(s). Before its creation, most of the seasonal employers in agriculture were not registered and about 95% of them worked in the grey zone without social, healthcare and labour rights. Since the start of 2019, the portal became available for general use and a few months later, in April 2019 it became available as a cell phone application. Application for seasonal employment is the first cell phone application of any Serbian government agency.
Impact & Results: The first years of the implementation of the reform were very successful. In 2019, the number of 28,407 seasonal workers were registered through the portal by their employers. In 2020, more than 32,000 seasonal workers were registered. The more successful year was 2021 with 38,000 seasonal workers. The total number of people registered until September 2022 has reached 70,000 seasonal workers. The number of workdays since 2019 is 3,600,000. The amount of taxes and contributions reached exceeds 9 million euros. It is important to mention that foreign citizens can be also registered for seasonal work in agriculture. More than 2,000 people from foreign countries were registered through the portal.

Description:
To accommodate the growing foreign talent pool in Vilnius and support the city’s economic development, we established a unique one-stop-shop service centre. Specialists representing various important state institutions gather in one place to provide services to foreign talent relocating to Vilnius. This is the first centre of its kind in Lithuania and comes as a great relief for foreigners while improving Lithuania’s image and increasing its attractiveness for foreign talents.

We started by conducting research to understand the needs of foreigners relocating to Vilnius and establish the pillars on which to construct IH Vilnius. The most important institutions were then invited to join the project – the Migration Department, Tax Inspectorate, Social Insurance Agency and Employment Service. Specialists from each of these institutions work on-site at IH Vilnius serving foreign clients.

In addition to providing foreigners with all the services they require in one visit, the specialists from different agencies can consult with each other and jointly identify the best solution to unusual situations. Continuous improvement work is carried out with partners using customer feedback to indicate processes that are not running smoothly and pinpoint emerging challenges. This helps identify systemic problems that can only be solved when institutions work together.

IH Vilnius focuses on foreigners who create high added value – qualified employees, start-ups, entrepreneurs, scientists. In addition to services and consultations, we also facilitate online and offline informational seminars, workshops, job fairs, networking events, Lithuanian language courses and a mentorship programme connecting foreigners to locals. All IH Vilnius services are free of charge and available to everyone relocating to Vilnius.

In the coming months we plan to launch a specialised “spouse programme” to help international spouses find employment. Another idea we are currently working on is an integration package for each foreigner moving to Lithuania. We are developing a product that will consist of informational material useful for starting a life in Vilnius, as well as some perks – discounts for touristic objects, free-of-charge SIM cards, and maybe even a free public transport card for their first days in Vilnius.
Novelty: International House Vilnius is the first joint service centre of its kind in Lithuania. Previously, talents relocating to Vilnius had to visit several public institutions to undertake the procedures for smooth relocation. Now, all they need to do is come to IH Vilnius and get all the information and services in one place.
Impact & Results:
We set the following KPIs for IH Vilnius:
• 1,000 clients served per month
• 1,500 participants in IH Vilnius seminars per quarter
• 4/5 client satisfaction rate
All these indicators were reached and exceeded within the first months of opening IH Vilnius.
• 14,000 foreigners were served in less than a year (on average, 1,500 per month)
• Over 2,000 persons participated in IH Vilnius online and offline events each quarter
• Over 100 people have taken free Lithuanian language lessons
• Over 400 pairs have been matched through our local/foreigner mentorship/buddy programme
• Average satisfaction with IH services: 4.8/5
• Average satisfaction with workshops and events organized by IH Vilnius: 4.7/5

Project/Initiative Name: Sci-Pop: Innovation at Gwacheon National Science Museum through Mobile Interactive Scientific Exhibits as its Response to COVID-19 and International Cooperation
Description:
Sci-Pop refers to box-shaped mobile exhibits that engage visitors to experience many scientific principles. Its bag-like figure makes it easy to be moved around and displayed anywhere, anytime. This has enabled the Museum to lease Sci-Pop articles to schools, local science museums and local governments for free; submit them to science festivals; and donate them to overseas science museums. The Museum plans to expand Sci-Pop into ODA’s science projects, as it can be produced at a low budget.

Sci-Pop exhibits were developed as trials in 2019. Though it began as a project to approach the disadvantaged group in science, it has served as an outreaching museum due to the spread of COVID-19 since 2020. It has been turned out to be a very effective tool for local science museums with strained budgets and those deprived of science experience. The Museum made these 80 items into mobile box shapes on its own, so they can be easily packed, carried and installed. Information panels are provided along with exhibits. For those who want to learn detailed scientific principles, the Museum created videos to elaborate on the pieces and opened them on YouTube. 

In 2021 alone, the Museum leased the mobile exhibits for free to five science museums in the Seoul capital area, and offered them education. Also, the Museum participated in five science festivals as an outreaching science museum to communicate with customers. By cooperating with local offices of education, the Museum has offered children and teenagers, who have limited access to science museums, opportunities to experience science and provided them with easy-to-understand explanations on the exhibits. The Museum has distributed Sci-Pop around the world and contributed to popularizing science by participating in Seoul International Book Fair and Bogota International Book Fair. It signed MOUs with two local governments, and created and installed exhibition articles for newly launched science museums run by local governments, forging cooperation in developing those museums. Now the Museum braces itself to advance into other countries around the world.
Novelty: Sci-Pop refers to mobile box-shaped exhibits that are easy to move, install and display; and is aimed to enable people of all ages to have fun and experience scientific principles applied in everyday life. The Museum receives feedback on the articles by continuously leasing them for free and bringing them to events. The Museum has built a system to reflect the feedback when creating new items. Sci-Pop has universal features and has gained popularity, proven by the invitation from an overseas international book fair and its donation to foreign science museums. The Museum plans to expand Sci-Pop as an ODA project to disseminate science contents.
Impact & Results: The Museum has developed 80 Sci-Pop exhibits that have been leased for free to local science museums, local offices of education and local governments, and used in over 50 events of those institutes. In 2022, Sci-Pop was invited to an international book fair, and its articles were donated to and are now displayed at overseas science museums. The Museum has uploaded over 70 YouTube contents to explain scientific principles of Sci-Pop exhibits. It signed MOUs with two local governments and one overseas science museum to distribute Sci-Pop and popularize science. This outcome was chosen as a success case of Executive Agency in 2021. Economically, the Museum invested about USD 200,000 in Sci-Pop, while it is estimated to have saved more than USD 1,000,000 by being leased to local science museums and educational facilities for free and by taking part in science festivals. Sci-Pop exhibits that have been disseminated to local science museums and schools so far are worth about USD 200,000.

Project/Initiative Name: The Civil Service Innovation Program for Implementation of the Stabilization and Association Agreement (Gov4SAA)
Description:
The Office of Prime Minister of the Government of Kosovo, together with GIZ in Kosovo and Innovation Centre Kosovo (ICK) have developed the Civil Service Innovation Program for Implementation of the Stabilization and Association Agreement (Gov4SAA). The program enables civil servants to develop their innovative products/services, in order to implement the necessary reforms of the SAA, shift to a startup mindset and explore new approaches to improve the services for citizens.

The program foresees two phases. The first phase which is currently being implemented, benefits the government officials from 5 government institutions (central and local level) with capacity building measures, networking, and exposure to start-up mindset. During this phase, these 5 teams are developing their prototypes of the products/services identified. The second phase foresees bringing to life these products/services designed by the civil servants and potential financing, benefiting government officials, citizens, job seekers, businesses, CSOs, educators, parents & kids, and tourists. Once finalized, the teams will pitch their projects in front of the jury, in a startup format. Up to 5 final projects will be financed by GIZ.

Main objectives of the program are: 1) Promote a culture of entrepreneurship in public institutions in the context of EU integration by encouraging innovative ideas and a problem-solving approach to SAA reforms; 2) Promote capacity-building of public institutions in implementation of SAA reforms through practice-oriented mentoring and coaching; 3)Promote cooperation between public institutions, private sector, CSOs and other stakeholders in implementation of SAA reforms through innovative products in support of policy implementation.
Impact & Results: Since the pilot program is currently being implemented, the main results and achievements include: 20,000 civil servants had the opportunity to participate in the open call for innovation in the Government; 8 teams consisting of 40 civil servants shortlisted and trained to pitch for their projects; 5 teams consisting of 20 civil servant are going through the incubation program and are being mentored by the best in country policy and digital experts; 35 % gender balance reached, out of 20 participants (from 5 teams).

Organisation: The State Agency for Public Service and Social Innovations under the President  of the Republic of Azerbaijan
Description:
The E-Gov Development Center has developed a new information exchange tool named ASAN Bridge in order to enable government organizations to transfer the necessary data to each other in more stable, secure and prompt ways. Through the assistance of this system, the key development challenge in the country was addressed by simply enhancing the safety and efficiency of the procedures and serving citizens better.

The system was established using the open source components and does not depend on any physical equipment. The tools for carrying out the project are specified below: (i) MS SQL, MongoDB, REDİS; (ii) Java (Spring Framework), Golang, JavaScript (Angular), Python; (iii) Message queues : RabbitMQ. The ASAN Bridge System operates on a fully-functional basis for the best interest of the government and citizens. 

As a consequence of successful operations, today, 54 government organizations are part of the ASAN Bridge System, while 6 public entities consented to be integrated into the system. Currently, the negotiations with 12 governmental structures proceed so as to ensure full incorporation of all government agencies into the project as well as more efficient service delivery for the population. 

It should also be emphasized that delivery of the updated system named “ASAN Bridge 2.0” is expected by the end of 2022. The updated version will function on a larger scale and procure an improved degree of security.
Novelty: The ASAN Bridge System allows government organizations to transfer the necessary data to each other in more stable, secure and prompt ways. It proved very effective, in the sense that it eliminated the complexities and slow procedures existent in the traditional service delivery. The system addressed the shortcomings of the traditional practices by hindering the misuse of the data and ensuring security.
Impact & Results:
ASAN Bridge System became the winner of the World Summit on the Information Society (WSIS) awards organized by International Telecommunication Union (ITU) in 2022

The results of the project:
- 54 government entities were integrated into the system;
- 426 root services are offered;
- Over 218 billion transactions were realized.

The overall impact of the project:
- During the decision-making process, public officials can use state database within their mandate,
- To perform business operations, companies can make use of the information in the state database within the mandate.

The Monitoring and Evaluation team employed following techniques to measure the results and impact of the project: interviews with the beneficiaries, evaluation surveys, and statistics related to the project results. [The results of this analysis were not shared with OPSI].

Project/Initiative Name: A data led approach to managing harms and supporting vulnerable users
Description:
AFSA regulates the bankruptcy system in Australia. The system is designed to support those in vulnerable situations due to circumstances beyond their control. However, there are bad actors who exploit the system by hiding assets/income. Using our risk-based regulatory approach, we have designed a system that uses an applicant's data and validates it against external data sets to detect potential misconduct. We aspire to build a data model to predict non-compliance at the system entry point.

We used the 'Double Diamond Design Thinking' principles to (a) explore the problem, (b) define it, (c) look at a range of potential solutions, and (d) select a solution which we believe will work best for us. The solution design has three elements: 
1. Randomized sampling of new cases: A statistically valid sample of new bankruptcy cases are selected for compliance assessments.
2. Harnessing Community Intelligence: We have begun to track all tip-offs and complaints against debtors and creditors to better understand community expectations and the drivers that cause erosion of trust in the system. 
3. Deep Diving into Harms Hypotheses: We are periodically doing deep dives into a sample of cases with unique characteristics to better understand the types of harms that manifest in the system and how they can be detected early so that preventative measures can be designed.

All three elements have generated a valuable new data asset which is yielding insights into the nature and magnitude of non-compliance and harms. This dataset is designed to enable statistically reliable estimates (SE ~ 10%, confidence level 5%), is subject to ongoing methodological review and refinement based on sound statistical principles (reducing and measuring uncertainty/defining risk) and engineered for continual improvement.

Preliminary work has commenced on leveraging the data from these activities to create a training dataset and build a machine learning model to auto detect harms and non-compliance at the point of a person's entry into the insolvency system. From a user perspective, it will allow applicants to be alerted to potential issues with the information in the application they are submitting and pinpoint areas they may need to review. This capability will be enabled by a continually learning data model. From a regulatory perspective, it will not only enable applicants to better comply with the regulatory framework, but also inform future policy and legislative changes to minimise harms.
Novelty: The program has generated a valuable new data asset which is yielding insights into the nature, magnitude, and prevalence of harms in the bankruptcy system. This dataset is designed to enable statistically reliable estimates and it demonstrates that digitisation of public services can deliver better value by leveraging data, technology, and human intellect to deliver solutions that are quick, seamless and user focused.
Impact & Results:
While it is early days, preliminary data suggests there is material non-compliance in 12% new cases. Around 37.5% of this non-compliant behaviour was subsequently assessed as unintentional user errors which provides us with valuable insights to improve the design of our services to reduce unintentional user errors. The remainder 62.5% was assessed as intentional/malicious non-compliance. These were typically bad actors seeking to hide assets and income from creditors by either not disclosing them or by transferring them out to friends or relatives.

All identified cases of material and malicious non-compliance were subject to regulatory actions which could include formal warnings, sanctions and/or referrals for criminal prosecution. This re-imagined approach to managing compliance has significantly refined our understanding of harms. It has enabled us to better appreciate the experiences of our vulnerable users, as well as examine the intentions and approaches of bad actors.

Project/Initiative Name: Presidential Employment Stimulus: Building a Society that Works
Description:
Since October 2020, as part of supporting economic recovery from the pandemic, South Africa's Presidential Employment Stimulus has created over a million jobs and livelihood opportunities, mainly for youth and women, across all skills levels and with high levels of spatial equity. It has 're-imagined' public employment (or public works) as an instrument for social innovation at scale, in relation to the forms of work and public value created, unlocking initiative in and beyond the state.
 
The Presidency provided strategic input, oversight and an 'authorising environment' for innovation undertaken by departments, who owned and implemented the programmes. A new envelope of funds approved programmes against criteria: scale, additionality, partnerships, quality of social outcomes, efficiency. The bar was set high. Participating in the PES meant a lot more work for participating departments. Hence it became a 'coalition of the willing' ready to go the extra mile to make a difference.

Some highlights of this program:
- The Department of Basic Education placed 596,109 young people as school assistants in over 22,000 schools (in 2 cohorts), reaching every corner of the country.
- The Social Employment Fund has been set up to support 'social employment' as part of the social economy, supported by the Department of Trade, Industry and Competition. Over 45,000 people currently work 16 hours a week, on food security, Early Childhood Development, combating gender-based violence, place-making, catchment management, community arts and more. A further 45,000 are part of Youth Service, on a similar model. 
- SA's biggest-ever stimulus to the creative sector provided work for over 32,000 people. They were invited to create work for themselves - by producing new creative work.
- Similar adaptations to traditional public employment models were applied to subsistence farmers - over 100,000 received production input vouchers to help them back to work after lockdown disruptions.
Novelty:
The Presidential Employment Stimulus 'reimagines' public employment on terms that enable the following:
-  the social value of labour is unlocked - even in a context in which it has no (or limited) market value
- the forms of work undertaken significantly expand traditional menus of 'public works', to focus on diverse forms of social value creation and work for the common good.
- Public employment is used as an instrument of community-driven development, unlocking local agency, building the commons and marshalling capacities, creativity and partnerships in the wider society to enable scale and impact.
Impact & Results: At present, over 1 million people have participated, with 15 national government departments reporting. 83% of participants are youth and 62% women - all subject to audit and verification.

Project/Initiative Name: National Public Health Observatory (NPHO) and Health Emergency Operations Centre (HEOC)
Organisation: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare (MoHFW)
Description:
India needed to enhance health system monitoring with integrated digital technologies, connected to various health datasets with in-depth insights at district level. The NPHO presents before the public health policy makers and experts of the country a bird’s eye view, with capabilities of digging deep into the health parameters of the 60+ Health IT Applications and 30+ Healthcare Programs, empowering them with holistic and advanced analytics to enable effective monitoring of the health system. The NPHO plays a pivotal role in monitoring of the health system by situational analysis and leveraging key indicators to understand the health system maturity and provide support in health emergency management.

This one of its kind set-up, leverages multiple health datasets which enable the development of a framework based on the various key indicators across each district focusing on healthcare infrastructure availability, healthcare workforce availability, healthcare service demand, & referral management, digital health adoption, healthcare financing, training & capacity, disease prevalence, public health program performance, healthcare coverage, availability of emergency infrastructure among others that enable understanding of health system preparedness and maturity score. 

Health System Preparedness Index provides scorecards to understand that system maturity could be developed from a district level and state level to understand overall preparedness and vulnerability to any potential health crisis. The maturity index and dashboards shall act as a decision support system and assess gaps in status to devise a robust roadmap or response strategy. This has been made possible with the core understanding of the health systems and policies during the Covid-19 pandemic and the learnings from the pandemic resulted in the creation of the NPHO which is a single source of truth and monitors the continuum of care.
Novelty:
The multiple series of database which has millions and billions of the tuples from the various health application will get monitored and filtered. It helps to minimize the data redundancy and departments loads. The NPHO at MoHFW will act as a single source of truth by bringing the 40+ Health IT application’s infrastructure, resources, disease surveillance, health programs and act as a guide for public health decisions catering to the need of the population by redressing inequalities in the quality healthcare services.

The integration with the NPHO will capture the micro-level data sets for the respective State and help in accessing the situation, studying the incidence, risk factors, and disease burden enabling the policymakers to adopt to a data-driven approach for an informed decision making.
Impact & Results:
The impact of this innovation has been measured with effective communication with the policymakers which has generated insightful information. For instance, in case of PMSSY Program, a better tracking of 22 AIIMS construction activity has been observed through live CCTV feeds which has in-turn increased the efficiency by 12% (compared to construction done in previous months) of the contractors both in terms of time saving and cost saving.

Today, the NPHO set-up is done at the Central Government level but in the future similar set-ups will be done at State and Specific Institution level which will provide deeper and more relevant insights from the ground-up.

Description:
The Open Digital Platform (ODP) is a key component in the digital infrastructure underpinning Singapore’s Smart Nation ambition, which seeks to harness technology to bring benefits from digital transformation in society, economy and government to how people and businesses live, work and play. The ODP takes an integrated masterplan approach from the ground up, optimising and synergising building & estate facilities management and operations onto a unified cyber-physical platform.

The ODP is designed to abstract building systems to allow for seamless development, integration and interoperability between these systems that leverage the platform, optimising and synergising building facilities management and operations onto a unified platform of digital smart city solutions. Further, being developed in a modular, reusable manner enables the formation of a Smart Estate Ecosystem of integrated smart city solutions – such as facilities, building and estate management systems, district cooling system, and pneumatic waste conveyancing system – and makes for rapid deployment nationwide.

The ODP was deployed in 2021 – and is currently operational – at the JTC Corporation headquarters. Beyond the targeted implementation at PDD, there are further plans for the ODP to eventually be implemented at other JTC estates.
Novelty: The ODP takes an integrated masterplan approach from the ground up, optimising and synergising building & estate facilities management and operations onto a unified cyber-physical platform. To ensure maximum interoperability with current systems, the ODP uses Geospatial Information Systems (GIS) and Building Information Model (BIM) technologies, common communication protocols and open data schemas. Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AIML) models optimise energy consumption, utilisation of in-building amenities and vertical transport services in ODP-enabled districts. Data collected by IoT sensors are placed in the estate are ingested by the ODP.
Impact & Results: ODP was developed in efforts to aid Singapore in achieving its pledge to cap its emissions by 2030. Through ODP implementations in smart districts and estates, it reduces the resources consumed in the estate by approximately 30% in energy and water consumption, and 50% in facilities management manpower compared to similar estates. It also increases the satisfaction with estate services by 80% and boost R&D development collaborations with more than 5 significant R&D projects, each >$100k using the Digital Twin of the ODP per year. With more collaboration efforts to develop smart solutions and services, ODP also opens up the generation of jobs and growth opportunities for the community and through these smart services, it can bring better living lifestyle and in turn create a greener & sustainable estate.

Project/Initiative Name: The Administrative City 2040 Carbon Neutral Policy in Korea
Organisation: National Agency for Administrative City Construction
Description:
The Paris Agreement sets out a global framework to avoid dangerous climate change by limiting global warming to below 2°C and pursuing efforts to limit it to 1.5°C. The Korean government also aims to strengthen countries’ ability to deal with the impacts of climate change and support them in its efforts through enacting related laws and establishing mid to long-term strategies. Accordingly, National Agency for Multifunctional Administrative City has set up a carbon neutrality vision 2040.

As the Administrative City would be completed by 2030, we tried to realize a carbon neutral city by dividing it into a carbon reducing stage (1st stage, ~30 years) focused on urban construction and a carbon neutral stage (2nd stage, ~40 years) focused on urban management. In the first stage, to reflect carbon reduction measures in the urban planning stage by 2030, the government plans to focus on supplying zero energy buildings, expanding renewable energy, and advancing eco-friendly transportation infrastructure by 2040.
Novelty: Specific promotion strategies in five sectors were presented to achieve the vision and goal of carbon neutrality. Various zero-energy-specific complexes, such as detached and multi-family houses, were created in the construction sector. The transportation sector decreased internal combustion locomotives by expanding infrastructure centered on eco-friendly vehicles and revitalizing public transportation. In addition, it plans to encourage power generation projects to expand renewable energy, monitor urban greenhouse gases, encourage power generation facilities, and come up with measures to encourage citizens' participation to improve their awareness of the climate crisis and promote a low carbon living culture.
Impact & Results:
A zero energy housing complex named the district 1-1, Lauren House, was completed in 2019. Two hundred thirty-eight of public rental houses in the district 6-3 are being constructed with zero energy level 3. As for the public building, the 119 Special Rescue Team building obtained the fourth grade of zero energy. In the district 6-2, a town-type zero energy complex that combines apartment houses, parks, and fuel cell sites will also be reflected, and detailed specialization plans will be established in the future.
The Administrative City focuses on expanding eco-friendly transportation systems such as pedestrian-centered transportation infrastructure and hydrogen vehicles under the public transportation-oriented plan. Five transfer parking lots and a total of 466km of bicycle-designated roads are being built near the BRT stops on the main transportation network to encourage public transportation and revitalize bicycle riding.

Project/Initiative Name: Smart Campus: a way to pave dreams
Description:
The 'Smart Campus' project was created to help direct students to their interest and prospective majors in college by communicating with successful people in their careers. The project expanded to disadvantaged rural areas and narrowed the educational gap between urban cities. It required inter-regional cooperation and coordination, from local schools to public institutions. This digital education system(portal) initiated a futuristic model that can progress continuously.

The 'Smart Campus' project began at the education department of a small municipality(Gangdong) in Seoul. The project was to help direct students to their interest, needs, and prospective majors in college by meeting and communicating with successful people in their careers. Face-to-face direct classes with such people were impossible due to COVID issues, so the whole project was based on a real-time online platform. The project required inter-regional cooperation and coordination; local schools and public institutions were brought together to exchange know-how and enhance the platform. This digital education system reduced local school's individual expenses and initiated a futuristic model that can progress continuously.

The project was launched on the portal system of Kangdong, a small municipality in Seoul. Students in this municipality had access to the source, but it was not limited to this group; the source was shared and spread out to disadvantaged rural areas in Korea. A total of 12 regions, 47 schools and 6,762 students had access to this project. In other words, 6,672 futures were brightly guided to their dreams.
Novelty: The initial setup of 'Smart Campus' was launched on an existing portal that was created by Kangdong municipality. Thanks to the already-setup-portal, the project itself was created under a low budget. The classes were open to students from cities to rural areas. Sharing and spreading the online contents(classes) was easily conducted by simply connecting and communicating with local schools and public institutions that needed the help. This was just the beginning. The platform has shown unlimited potential to grow with more participants and contents as it progressed.
Impact & Results:
The project was awarded with the following:
-'Outstanding Project' presented by the 'Korean Innovative Education Local Government'
-'Seoul Creative Award' in the Innovation Part presented by 'Seoul City'
The project was granted with $70,000 (100,000,000 Korean Won) by the 'Ministry of Public Administration and Security'. It became a benchmarking model for more than twenty local communities and school districts. They came to benchmark the process in detail.
The project is in its third year. This year we are on-going and looking forward to another thousands of students to happily navigate their way throughout the system.

Project/Initiative Name: Korea-Indonesia Joint Project to restore the burnt peatland in Jambi
Description:
Forest fire in peatland areas in 2015 in Indonesia released 884 million tons of carbon dioxide and made a severe impact on the ecosystem and surrounding people and became a local, national, and global issue. Korean government intended to contribute to the peatland restoration program as it has become a priority issue in Indonesia. The two governments are working together to improve the local livelihoods and reduce GHG emissions from peatland degradation, contributing to fighting climate change.

This project is executed by Korea-Indonesia Forest Cooperation Center (KIFC) in Peat Protection Forest Londerang (HLG Londerang) Jambi from 2019 until 2022 under the direct supervision of KFS and MoEF. As the institution that was jointly formed by KFS and MoEF in 2010, KIFC works together with both ministries, Jambi local government, and local people to build a multi-level collaboration, create multi-level ownership, restore and conserve the peatland, and deliver direct impacts from national up to site level. 

Furthermore, by working with multi-level stakeholders, this peatland restoration project has a strategic position to create synergy effect between the national and local agenda on the peatland issue. More specifically, this project has four main activities namely rewetting, revegetation, revitalization, and local capacity building such as building a small-scale peatland education center.

As for rewetting, this project is building 18 canal blocks and 20 wells. And as part of revegetation activities, it is in the process to restore the vegetation in areas of 200 ha, that were damaged by the forest fire in 2015 and 2019, with native species planted on peatland after re-wetting the dried peatland. In the initial phase, KIFC worked with the Research and Development Agency of MoEF to conduct the biophysics survey of HLG Londerang and provided detailed technical methods and guidance in revegetation activities. Through this collaboration, this project used the peat mounding method to plant seedlings in flooded peatland areas.
Novelty:
1. It links the ODA project to the recipient country’s central government policies.
To plan the project, these are the main concerns: Indonesia’s NDCs in unconditional 29% and in conditional 41% reduction, the ‘Long term strategy for low carbon and climate resilience (LTS-CCR) 2050’ and the mid-term development plan RPJMN 2020-2024, and FOLU Net Sink 2030.
2. It serves as an excellent opportunity to build a multi-level partnership and plays a strategic position as this project creates synergy effect between the national and local agenda on the peatland issue. Therefore, it is expected to deliver tangible results and direct impact across the industry, academia, and government.
3. This project is expected to set a model and be scaled up in peatland restoration ODA.
Impact & Results:
We have derived the following 3 major results from this project.
1. Additional carbon storage of 1,175 tons in the HLG Londerang peatland protected forest and securing biodiversity. After the restoration utilizing various vegetation, we could find habitats for large mammals such as Beruang Madu (Herlarctos malayanus).
2. The establishment of community businesses to increase income for residents while supporting 21 new projects in 10 villages. As a result, 250 new jobs were created for the vulnerable people and women in the region to manage fish farms and livestock and reprocess products, improving their livelihood.
3. Sustainable re-wetting peatland process through the construction of canal blocks with local communities has been created, enabling the resolution of various disputes related to the constructing and maintaining 18 canal blocks near to Londrang peatland protected forest. This has allowed us to jointly develop the Project Design Matrix with the Jambi National University.

Description:
The intervention seeks to expand and improve efficiency of physical rehabilitation services, ensuring high quality and well-targeted services are provided. It does this through implementing the Physical Rehabilitation Program (PRP). The intervention includes: three new Physical Rehabilitation Program centres in Mali (Mopti), Nigeria (Maiduguri) and the Democratic Republic of Congo (Kinshasa), as well as provision of training for local staff to deliver high quality physical rehabilitation services in these centres The intervention also seeks to pilot and rigorously assess pilot efficiency improvement measures across eight existing ICRC physical rehabilitation centres with the aim of improving efficiency and maintaining patient outcomes, while also operationalising the three new centres using improved operational protocols that are based on effective efficiency measures.

This five-year arrangement also includes a training component. Several European governments are also involved. The three orthopaedic centres are now under construction, and local staff are receiving ortho-prosthetics training.
Novelty: First impact bond / private-debt transaction with a focus on humanitarian action.
Impact & Results:
'- Date outcome contract signed (launch date): 2017
- Duration: 60 months
- Capital raised: total investment amount committed: $19,700,000
- Target population: Individuals with physical disabilities in living in Mali, Nigeria, and the Democratic Republic of Congo, who are victims of war, natural disasters, congenital impairments or disabling diseases such as polio.
- Size of cohort: 3600
- Outcome validation method: Historical comparison & validated administrative data
- Stage: Implementation

Project/Initiative Name: The Digital Competency Center, where all Koreans can enhance their digital competence
Organisation: Ministry of Science and ICT, National Information Society Agency
Description:
Since its inception in 2020 for the purpose of realizing a digitally inclusive society, the Digital Competency Center Project has been providing digital competency training for citizens to enjoy the benefits of digital technologies and services as their lives and socioeconomic activities are rapidly digitalized. Anybody can visit their local Digital Competency Center for a free, hands-on training program to foster skills for using mobile or digital devices in daily life.

Examples of training programs include: metaverse experience in public childcare services, software instructor training courses for women on career breaks, assistance in using local business vouchers and registering products on smart stores for small business owners, learning how to open online shopping sites and using job search sites for multicultural families.

We also collaborated with our partner institution to run a Digital Senior Internship, fostering digital competency for senior citizens and supporting them in finding jobs and social activities. We also developed a common guideline to continue providing training curricula safely even during the pandemic. We adhered to social distancing guidelines and introduced various types of classes such as online courses to prevent learning loss. Also, jointly with 17 metropolitan and provincial governments, we’ve established a system to manage the outcomes of our training program in real-time. We’ve also developed and distributed standardized digital education curricula and content.
Novelty:
1) The Digital Competency Center provides a comprehensive digital training program from basic, daily-life digital capacities to vocational training for every Korean in their vicinity.
2) The curricula are catered to each individual’s level, with basic, intermediate (daily life), and advanced courses, and to their various backgrounds, such as multi-cultural families, senior citizens, farmers, fishers, and small business owners.
3) We send Digital Competency Center Buses to marginalized regions across Korea to provide them with digital education opportunities.
4) We operate a help desk for residents to handle their issues with digital devices in real-time.
Impact & Results: Approximately 1.54 million people have taken digital competency courses from September 2020 to September 2022. We measured the improvement in our trainees’ digital competency to objectively assess the outcomes of our training program. The improvement in digital competency compares the digital competencies of our trainees before and after our training. We found that our trainees’ level of digital competency improved by 24.7% in 2020 and 28.8% in 2021.

Description:
During the Covid-19 pandemic, the Department of Work and Pensions’ Cyber Resilience Centre has detected attempts by organised criminals to defraud the benefits system on a huge scale and stopped fraudulent payments from being made.

The criminal attack against the UK benefit system during Covid-19 has been the biggest ever experienced. The Department for Work and Pensions’ Cyber Resilience Centre prevented £1.7bn of organised fraud against Universal Credit during the first nine months of the pandemic. It did so by innovating rapidly to enhance and its cybersecurity capabilities to detect and prevent organised fraud on a large scale and in real time.

The centre adopts five criteria for its activities: they will be lawful, proportionate to the threat faced by the public, reasonably necessary to counter that threat, effective in doing so and efficient. Amid the pandemic, the CRC’s skills became more useful as the significant increase in demand put stress on the department, which redeployed thousands of staff to assist with new claims and made changes to the process to help benefits reach those in need in good time.

Once the pandemic began, CRC began analysing the mass of data coming into the department, aiming to do it fast enough to detect organised fraud attempts in real time and prevent payments being made in the first place. The work had success rate of close to 100% in correctly identifying organised criminal cases. The value of fraud prevented was equivalent to funding the entire departmental security team for the next 50 years.
Novelty: Resilience and technological mechanisms applied to public finance (work pensions here) to prevent organized fraud to the system.
Impact & Results: Cyber Resilience Centre prevented £1.7bn of organised fraud against Universal Credit during the first nine months of the pandemic

Project/Initiative Name: Impact Bond to Address Child Labour in the Cocoa Value Chain
Description: The ILO has conducted field research to understand the root causes of child labour in the cocoa value chain and identify the most appropriate financial services to address them. The ILO's finance sector is moving from a cocoa-based and credit-driven approach to a more holistic and client-centric approach. In 2020, the social finance component of the ACCEL project in West Africa will also focus in Uganda and Malawi in the coffee and tea value chains.
Novelty: https://twitter.com/AfricaAccel/status/1577684893093580807
Impact & Results: Impact are not yet available.

Project/Initiative Name: Co-Gov toolkit: Transforming public organisations into co-creation hubs
Organisation: Faculty of Public Administration University of Ljubljana
Description:
The Co-Gov toolkit has been developed with the purpose of providing a holistic digital toolkit that supports and guides public organisations through the whole process of service renewal based on co-creation. More precisely, the Co-Gov (digital) toolkit is developed to support: (i) the assessment of the organisational readiness for co-creation (Co-Ready tool); (ii) the selection of public services suitable to be renewed through co-creation with service users (Co-Serve tool); (iii) the renewal of public services through co-creation with service users (Co-Renew tool); (iv) the assessment of the quality of the co-creation process in order to improve future co-creation initiatives (Co-Master tool).

Instead of being limited to a specific phase of the co-creation process and/or to a specific policy area, the Co-Gov toolkit claims wide applicability across public organisations and policy areas. It focuses on the service level and renewal of public services – as two overlooked aspects by the existing offer of tools. Even though it is a digital toolkit, the Co-Gov covers the renewal of both digital and ‘analogue’ services. The Co-Gov toolkit is developed as an evidence-based co-creation toolkit that builds on empirical data gathered in the context of the COGOV project. 

Three out of four tools ‒ Co-Ready, Co-Serve and Co-Master – are developed as decision support systems, embedding attributes defined on the basis of co-creation drivers and barriers from empirical research and in-depth content analysis of Web of Science (WoS).On this basis, Co-Ready, Co-Serve and Co-Master not only provide an informed assessment of the present state of play (regarding the organisational and service readiness for co-creation or the quality of the co-creation process) but also guidelines as to which aspects (and how) need to be improved or taken into consideration as potential challenges during co-creation.

In contrast to these tools, the Co-Renew tool is developed as a User Journey Model inspired by the Customer Journey concept with the purpose of providing a platform for public organisations to actively include service users in the process of service renewal. Its design builds on the conclusions of the review of the relevant literature on Customer Journey Mapping (CJM) and existing commercial CJM tools.
Novelty: The Co-Gov toolkit offers holistic digital support to public organisations interested in the renewal of their services with the help of co-creation with service users - it supports not only the very act of service renewal but also previous preparations, as well as the evaluation of its quality (through the prism of co-creation). Additionally, the originality of this toolkit is evident in the development and operationalisation of two new concepts: (1) organisational readiness for co-creation (embedded in the Co-Ready tool); and (2) readiness for renewal of public services through co-creation (embedded in the Co-Serve tool). Eventually, besides a channel for active engagement of service users, the Co-Renew tool offers an interactive and dynamic view of the data gathered.
