The robotization process of aid is already working for strategic sectors. In total, more than 300,000 aid apllications have been managed so far for nine projects.  
Specifically, these would be the following examples: 
•	Nearly 95,000 hours freed up for managers of aid to SMEs and artisans. 
•	More than 75 robots working at the same time on employment aid.  
•	More than 1,000,000 of documents have been processed with artificial intelligence. 
•	More than 30 requirements have been automated (SMEs, sales fall, bankrupt companies, average number of workers, identity, current payment of taxes and fees, registration in the public bankruptcy, registry sanctions for contracting with the General Administration, ...). 
The timely completion of the processing of these subsidies, with the pertinent verifications, allowed all beneficiaries to receive the aid.

Description:
Bank of Lithuania is implementing one of a kind state of the art enterprise data management solutions as part of a leading data management maturity programme. The mentioned solutions will ensure a unified IT architecture enabling centralized data management and automated real time data exchange and data availability involving regulated / supervised subjects as well as other responsible institutions. 

The Bank of Lithuania initiated an organisation wide Data Management Maturity programme (DAMAMA) with the aim of becoming a leading example of data management best practices locally as well as on the international level.  

The programme encompasses four key elements of data management value chain: 1) Data collection (DACO project) which relates to regulatory data collection activities as well as provision of collected data to responsible institutions, 2) Data platform for storage (DAFA project) which will ensure a unified data warehouse, 3) Data dissemination (DADI project) which will enable different stakeholders to seamlessly access data held by Bank of Lithuania depending on their needs / authorization rights, 4) Data governance (DAGO project) which helped bring about organizational change through preparation and implementation of a data management strategy and policy.

DAMAMA helps to achieve the goals of Data Management Strategy by ensuring: Consolidation of currently fragmented data storage across the whole organisation. One time entry of specific data which can then be reused multiple times for specific use cases. Capabilities to collect and disseminate data in an automated way where possible.
Novelty:
The selected data management IT solutions / architecture being implemented (Regnology Regulator, Open Data Hub on OpenShift with other custom components) have been selected based on Bank's needs specifically and when combined are one of a kind when it comes to enterprise data management hence requiring a competent inhouse team comprised of business analysts, data and IT architects and other professionals.  

The mentioned IT architecture has been tailor made to fully enable smooth and automated data value chain in the Bank of Lithuania reducing regulatory burden for market participants.
Impact & Results: Current results include increase data literacy among bank's employees, improved employer brand perception locally, increase data management maturity across the whole bank as well as increased change readiness and eagerness to implement state of the art tools for data collection, storage, analytics and dissemination.

Organisation: NSW Department of Planning and Environment (DPE)
Description:
The NSW Government has developed a whole of government spatial tool to improve planning and pre-development processes. The tool modernises the traditional approach to strategic planning, site assessment & land use evaluation and provides user friendly access to GIS capabilities where multiple agencies can work together on a project in NSW public sector.

The tool was created to make better use of land with consideration of social, economic, environmental and financial impacts. The tool was developed to reduce the number of related, yet different methodologies and tools used across Government to assess the impacts of different land uses and reduce due diligence timeframes and government spending on consultants. It also provides a sandbox for testing designs and applying and modelling planning outcomes.  

This spatial tool integrates over 40 land use typologies and 180 datasets into one single platform and provides advanced analytics and scenario planning capabilities to consider the impact of change on land use. The tool has 4 main functions (modules): 1) Data library, 2) Geographic-centric analysis, 3) Site search, 4) Scenario planning. 

Land iQ is intended to serve as the host platform for other appropriate digital systems in NSW government to reduce double-spend and maximise alignment. In this regard, the tool is planned to have stronger connection and integration with other major digital systems such as the NSW Spatial Digital Twin. Land iQ can be applied to any state or territory, or the private and non-profit sectors.
Novelty:
Land iQ offers advanced analytics and scenario planning capabilities that can be applied on a project-by-project basis. The tool can be used to identify sites with a set of criteria, undertake site layouts/feasibilities, make preliminary assessment of business cases, and assist strategic planning and development assessment by analysing the local context from street/suburb scale, to LGA and regional context to understand the existing economic, social, demographic, cultural and environmental context. 

Users of the tool experience benefits including reduced timeframes with faster/in-house due diligence, and budget improvements along with an enhanced adaptability of existing functions to support delivery of project needs.
Impact & Results:
The tool can help Government to save up to $15 million a year. There are numerous costs associated with current practice, including site identification, due diligence, cost planning, master planning, the time associated with the procurement of consultants etc. Land iQ helps to cut time, and therefore cost, from all these processes. 

Land iQ currently addresses land use projects across health, education, energy, infrastructure, housing, and transport with further scope to include measurable outcomes of these impacts. The tool also provides real solutions for those in vulnerable situations in a timely and efficiency manner.

Project/Initiative Name: Smart City Strategy / Gemeinsame Digital Berlin
Description:
„Gemeinsam Digital: Berlin“(Together Digital: Berlin) combines the digital and smart city strategies. We define smart as solving future challenges in creative, open, experimental and participatory ways. This strategy helps to establish new work approaches, agile methods, competence building, and a systematic knowledge transfer between the administration and its inhabitants. By aligning technologies, methods, and cultural processes, it supports existing strategies to reach their respective goals. 

In Berlin, the conditions for cross-departmental and cross-level work within the administration are significantly more complex than in other cities, as Berlin is both a state and a municipality. 

The process model of the strategy is framed by a "Values Compass," developed in a participatory process, and by the "Action Fields," that guides the implementation of individual measures. This results in the vision of a sustainable, community-oriented, resilient and cooperative city. It is intended to ensure that Berlin's digitization benefits everyone in the city, including strengthening Berlin as a business location , as well as ensuring that everyone can continue to participate and contribute to the strategy in the future.

"Together Digital: Berlin" strategy is a transformation program. Berlin's digital transformation can only succeed if all the stakeholders involved are willing to engage in a collective learning process. Consequently, the strategy itself is also designed as a learning strategy. Based on a comprehensive monitoring system, the strategy and its tools, as well as individual projects, are to be evaluated and adapted in regular cycles. It sets the framework for their implementation and is complemented by these specialized strategies
Novelty:
Together Digital: Berlin is innovative because: 
1. Interdepartmental and cross-sectoral work: The measures are aligned with local conditions and their solution approaches pay attention to larger cross-sectoral challenges. As a results, formats that support cross-departmental, cross-sectoral and cross-level work are used even before measures are launched, thus promoting the networking of policy fields.  
2. Implementation support: As part of the implementation and as an important element for the learning strategy, implementation teams receive methodological and technical support oriented toward agile approaches.  
3. Prototypical approach: The administration and other actors build and test prototypes, try out new approaches to learn quickly and, if necessary, define more precisely which specific services should be put out for tendering.
Impact & Results: The strategy will only be officially launched in November, so the overall impact cannot be described yet. The impact will be evaluated regularly. We expect that the projects and the resources provided by the strategy (such as a flexible framework regarding the planning and implementation phase) will strongly influence the collaborative spirit and increase of shared knowledge in the administration.

Project/Initiative Name: TAP portal -  development of legislation - from documents to data
Description:
The TAP portal is a unique e-government system at the European Union level, which ensures the development of digital government legislation, moving from documents to structured data. The TAP portal ensures the proceedings of the meetings of the Cabinet of Ministers and allows every citizen of Latvia to follow the progress of the Government's decision-making and express an opinion on the regulatory acts being developed by the Government. 

The TAP portal was created in order not to repeat the situation when the Government's decisions are made behind closed doors and to fully allow the public to follow the development of regulatory acts, as well as to ensure the continuity of the Government's work, regardless of the geopolitical situation and location. 

The objectives of the TAP portal are to: 1) make the work of state administration more efficient; 2) involve citizens in the public participation process; 3) enable digital governance regardless of location and time. 4) moving from documents to structured data in the development of regulatory acts, which will allow further use of artificial intelligence in the development of regulatory acts.

The drafting of legislation in the country has completely moved from paper to data. The number of manual steps is reduced. The information and amendments are generated automatically, the drafters of legal acts do not have to think about the design of legal acts. A unified co-creation environment has been created for the development of regulatory acts and opinions. The history of revisions and approvals of regulatory acts is easily reviewed. Society has the opportunity to follow and participate in the development, coordination and adoption of regulatory acts in real time.
Novelty:
TAP portal is an innovative becuase: 
1.The development of legal acts does not take place in the form of documents, but is done in the data itself. 
2.Amendments to the legal act are completely automatically generated from the consolidated version of the legal act (data). 
3.The work of ministries, institutions and government has been completely transformed in a digital environment. 
4.E-government - work is continuous, regardless of the users' location and time.  
5.The process of goverment takes place regardless of the geopolitical situation, as the e-government can work from anywhere in the world. 
6.The circulation of legal acts is provided by the system itself, thus reducing the work of clerks.
Impact & Results:
Economic benefits: reduction of technical activities, proceedings and employees in public administration. Annual reduction in staff resources of more than 600 human months.

Administrative benefits - Optimising the bureaucratic processes, reducing the time of development and decision-making of the TA by improving their quality. Transition to centralized, automated user administration using the Single Login module.

Description:
Engativa´s Town Hall developed “miengativapp”, it is a web-type application that was implemented to support process management. It was implemented because of the pandemic as a tool to manage the office remotely; it benefits around 300 professionals in the development of their work, creating agility, organization, and transparency in information handling; and it is a unique approach to public administration in the city at a local level, positioning this office as one of the best of Bogotá.

Miengativapp is a web-type application, developed with a low code tool called stripcase, that facilitates the processes management among different areas of Engativa´s town hall, the initiative for its creation arises from the need to have clearer, more precise, and real-time information for decision-making and better control of the flow of information in the data pipeline. 

Objectives of the app: 1) Obtain the necessary information for decision-making quickly and efficiently, 2) Avoid errors in the completion and information handling from different areas of Engativa´s town hall, 3) Optimize the internal processes of the areas involved in the application, 4) Be a reference at the local level in the management of processes and transparency of information. 

Additionally, it allows the different contractors of the projects to collect information from the beneficiaries of each project in order to have a database that can characterize the beneficiary population of the public investment made in order to establish their priorities and needs, and thus be able to have relevant data to improve future and current projects with direct impact in the local citizenship.
Novelty:
This technological development is a completely new and innovative concept, unique in its kind at a local public administration in Bogotá, since its creation it operates on the basis of efficiency and competitiveness, showing an advantage in the managed and handled of information to make decisions, responding to information needs of the different levels of the organization. 

This project has great benefits for all the users of our organization since it gives them control and order over their tasks, as well as helps to generate the necessary information content in activities that are repetitive but of great importance for Engativa´s town hall, such as requests for various types of information memorandums, data queries and reports among different areas.
Impact & Results:
Results: 
1)	Almost 100% decrease in typing errors, which avoids reprocessing and loss of time. 
2)	Interoperability of areas with unique and validated information. 
3)	Organization in the management of tasks, procedures, and requests. 
4)	Generation of reports in real-time. 
5)	Consolidation of information.

Organisation: Deutsche Gesellschaft für Internationale Zusammenarbeit (GIZ) GmbH
Description:
The techDetector is the first technology radar giving an overview of emerging technologies that have the potential to influence the area of sustainable development in the future.  It lists future technologies from all sectors of sustainable development and assesses their potential impact on sustainability and gender equality. The techDetector helps inform, sensitize and support foresight processes, enabling better decision-making by providing sound information on technological developments.

We at GIZ therefore asked ourselves, how can we get an orientation on future technologies relevant for our work as well as their potential impacts, when at the same time innovation cycles are shortening and the complexity of technology innovations is increasing tremendously. 

The techDetector lists emerging technologies which in the future can potentially disrupt the sectors GIZ is active in. We scouted technologies from all sectors like agriculture, governance or education, among others. The technologies are mapped in the technology radar format and assessed by a crowdsourced survey in order to determine the impact they might pose for gender equality or sustainability with its dimensions of ecological, social and economic sustainability. 

The techDetector also connects the technologies in the form of narrative story formats, where we move beyond the abstract description of technology applications and try to give context in order to inspire: Future Scenarios, Case Studies and Sci-Fi Chronicles help users think out of the box and trigger innovation. 

The techDetector also serves as a capacity development tool for "Futures Literacy" inside GIZ. Together with the internal corporate academy for international cooperation, we are currently designing a learning offer based on the techDetector in order to provide our colleagues with a robust set of methods to "use the future".
Novelty:
Technology Radars are being used by private sector companies to get an overview of future and emerging trends in their sectors. However, until very recently, all technology radars would focus solely on mapping technologies by their technology readiness as well as their potential economic turnaround. 

In order to adapt this format to the reality of GIZ as a leading service provider in ther area of sustainable development, the techDetector was developed: Adding criteria for the assessment of sustainability and gender equality, it is the first technology radar for sustainable development. Linking technologies of the future to the SDGs and using crowdsourcing to gather information on the potential impacts of technologies on the work of GIZ are also truly innovative and novel processes that have never been applied before.
Impact & Results: The techDetector since its launch has raised increasing interest among the GIZ colleagues as well as the international community. In 2021, we have conducted more than 10 internal workshops with diverse teams. The techDetector was one of the featured projects at the Paris Peace Forum 2021 and is part of the Toolkit on Digitalization of the German Federal Ministry for Economic Cooperation and Development. Every month, the website receives around 2000 unique visitors.

Project/Initiative Name: Blended Working in the Civil Service
Description:
The move to introduce a Blended Working Policy across the Irish Civil Service represents the most significant change to working arrangements in decades. Building on the proven success of remote working in response to the pandemic, this shift in traditional working arrangements represents a major change, enhances our positioning as an Employer of Choice and positions us to attract and retain a new generation of workers who prioritise flexibility, self-development, mobility and work-life balance. 

The Department of Enterprise, Trade and Employment published a research report ‘Remote Work In Ireland’ looking at the topic as part of the Future Jobs Ireland 2019 programme. However, it was the rapid introduction of working from home as part of the national response to reduce the impact of the Covid-19 virus that really heralded blended working as a viable alternative to traditional office-based working. This experience showed that blended working could work for the Irish Civil Service, offering increased flexibility to staff without compromising on high quality service delivery.  

The Framework was co-designed by the Civil Service Sector Group on a highly collaborative basis with representation from Civil Service organisations and in consultation with Trade Unions and Staff Associations. The Civil Service Blended Working Policy Framework was published by the Department of Public Expenditure and Reform in March 2022.
Novelty: The project aims to develop flexible and innovative ways of working while also seeking to: 1) Ensure the Civil Service (CS) continues to deliver high quality services to Government, the public and business, 2) Deliver on the Programme for Government commitment to move to 20% remote/home working, 3) Enable tailored Blended Working approaches that best meet the needs of individual organisations and their employees, within an overarching Framework.
Impact & Results: As of late September 2022, over 17,000 civil servants had used the NSSO application portal to apply for Blended Working this represents. 50% of the overall NSSO client case of 34,000. Some organisations that have adapted the Blended Working Framework are using alternative application methods and some yet to start accepting applications.

Project/Initiative Name: Ajman Self Enumeration + Integrated Administrative Data Census Project
Description:
Launched in 2020, the AJSC created a central registry to capture key statistical census data. 'Powering' this is the AJC Portal 2020, a sophisticated web-based application, combining a 'self-enumeration' feature, with administrative data integrated from various Government entities in Ajman. The 'self-enumeration' data component includes details on family and accommodation, populated by residents. This conceptual design enables the system to generate accurate census reports in real time.

The genesis of the project is linked to the ‘UAE Administrative Census 2020 Project’, announced in July 2019. The project spearheaded by the UAE’s Federal Competitiveness and Statistics Authority (FCSA), brought together all the Emirate-specific statistics centers (including the AJSC) along with other public service  entities across the UAE. The objective of the project was to foster collaborative efforts in developing a solution for leveraging key administrative data captured by relevant Government agencies, as a ‘feed’ for unified census reporting. This was towards enabling higher cost and time efficiencies in census data generation, following the United Nations directional recommendations to countries towards exploring synergies provided by administrative data collected by them. 

A core part of the design phase was the integration of external administrative data within the Portal and assessing relevant inter-linkages with the self-enumerated data component. As part of this process, an assessment framework (‘Statistical Maturity Index’) was designed to ascertain the quality and format of data maintained by the designated external entities – effectively, enhanced data quality meeting a certain threshold set by the Index, was set as a pre-requisite for initiating entity specific data integration efforts. 

Notably, various initiatives undertaken in Ajman post 2020, including the Ajman Attractiveness Survey (another flagship project of the AJSC), have significantly benefited from real time data made available via the AJC portal 2020 project – this is a significant deviation from the erstwhile dependence on the 2017 Ajman census data which arguably did not provide an accurate baseline, particularly given the external environmental changes that have occurred in the last three years.
Novelty:
The AJC Portal 2020 is a novel platform able to generate real time, statistical census data that powers robust planning outcomes. This is based on the combination of: 
•	The conceptual design of the system is premised on a self-enumerated data mechanism, integrated with administrative data, sourced from over 13 Ajman Government entities. 
•	In-built quality management protocols for determining ‘data completeness’ (based on captured records) and ‘data accuracy’ (based on cross verifying administrative data).
•	Self-management functionality enabling users to keep data records updated to reflect status changes in family and accommodation.
Impact & Results:
The AJC Portal 2020 has enabled the generation of accurate, census-level socio-economic data on a real time basis, which is a vital ‘feed’ to the public policy efforts of Government entities in Ajman.  

Availability of refreshed census data has enabled more accurate statistical ‘fine-tuning’ for forecasting purposes; a significant positive deviation from the use of the 2017 census baseline in prior policy planning cycles. The availability of periodic data readings within short- intervals enables 'agile' planning, ensuring an enhanced level of responsiveness to the community’s needs.  

The Project has enabled an innovative census approach associated with significant operational efficiencies vis-à-vis traditional models. As a reflection, the cost per record via this model is 97% lower than that in the traditional census.

Description:
InnoAgora is a dynamic digital platform powered that provides real networking opportunities and contributes to international business development. Specifically, InnoAgora is a matchmaking platform that enables innovative and extrovert companies, investors and business support structures, to enter into investment agreements and commercial partnerships. This is the first matchmaking platform for Greek enterprises that offers real-time opportunities for networking with international stakeholders.

 The way that it works is that each stakeholder creates a profile online and can then portray their competitive advantages. They can then either contact directly or being contacted by any interested member. When a Greek company creates their profile, they are instantly a member of EuroQuity, an ecosystem of more than 20.000 participants. Thus, Greek companies, investors and supporting mechanisms are benefited by having the opportunity to interact with all these members and through a very user-friendly platform, start the initial contact.
Novelty: InnoAgora is the first and unique matchmaking platform in Greece with gives the opportunity to enterprises to join for free and become a member of a universe consisting of more than 20.000 members. HDB acts as the facilitator which puts companies in touch with investors and partners, in their country and internationally and provides companies with value-added services provided by first-rate public and private organizations.
Impact & Results: The main objective and result from InnoAgora are the matchmaking intros achieved after our pitching sessions. So far, we have 23 such intros between enterprises and investors. Furthermore, after a survey conducted to the InnoAgora members after 6 months of using the platform, 70% have watched up to 4 InnoAgora webinars, 52,5% claim that the webinars have interesting content, 50% says that the navigation within InnoAgora is easy and 75% claims that it is highly probable to suggest InnoAgora to other entrepreneurs in order to support their extroversy and growth.

Project/Initiative Name: MEASURING AND CONTROLLING INPATIENT FALLS IN TERMS OF SAFETY AND CONTINUOUS QUALITY IMPROVEMENT (CQI) IN A HOSPITAL ENVIRONMENT
Description:
Inpatient Falls that occur in hospitalized patients are a frequent and serious threat to patients safety and considered to be among the leading safety-related adverse events reporting in general hospitals, especially for the elderly population, resulting in significant physical burden for the patients and family (increased injury, mortality rates reduced quality of life, etc) and financial burden for medical organizations (increased lenght of stay, increased medical care cost, litigation etc).

Measuring and controlling inpatients falls may be the solution to reduce the problem. The objectives of the innovation are: Firstly, to investigate the problem by measuring the frequency of falls in the hospital environment of the GHA "KAT" as well as the factors that contribute to their occurrence through a 6/month research program. The second phase of the innovative action will begin where the effectiveness of a new research physical therapy protocol will be investigated to prevent or further reduce the frequency of falls in hospitalized patients.
Novelty: Measurement effort to support improvements to tackle inpatient falls.
Impact & Results: We stand in the early stage of measurement, recording and investigation

Project/Initiative Name: Bicycle Barometer: a Citizen Science Platform for Improving Bicycle Safety for High School Students
Description:
Adolescents are often victim of traffic accidents in traffic between home and school. Cities need feedback from this group to optimize bicycle traffic safety and infrastructure, but it is difficult to reach and influence youngsters individually. Together with more than 70 Flemish high schools, UGent has developed the Bicycle Barometer, a citizen science ICT platform to collect information about bicycle behaviour and perceived road safety.

The Bicycle Barometer is a web platform where adolescents digitize their home-to-school route and assess the bicycle safety of their daily commute. It provides course material for three lessons: (1) adolescents indicate their route on a map, and evaluate the roads and intersections on their route by giving a score from 0 (very unsafe) tot 10 (very safe); (2) adolescents analyse the data near the school while learning GIS skills; (3) adolescents compare their analyses with mobility policy in their city and provide recommendations.

The project continues to:
- With minor modifications (translation and GIS cartography), make the solution available to schools and students across Europe
- Train participating schools and students to use the solution, while making them familiar with citizen science and GIS
- Collect objective and subjective data about bicycle safety (privacy law compliant)
- Facilitate an improvement dialogue between students and traffic authorities
- Promote cycling as a safe mobility mode for youngsters
- Disseminate the project results
Novelty:
Current solutions for measuring bicycle behaviour often use trackers or big data software, posing critical issues with regard to privacy and social inclusion for adolescents.
The Bicycle Barometer uses a unique combination of a teacher-supported citizen science methodology with a low-cost web platform.
This project engages high cities, schools, students and authorities into an educational traffic safety dialogue. It collects large amounts of data while guaranteeing low cost and high individual privacy

Project/Initiative Name: Pandemic Fighting System Software (Hayat Eve Sığar (HES) Application, HES Code)
Description:
Within the scope of the fight against the COVID-19 epidemic that broke out in Wuhan, Republic of China and spread to many countries, in order to effectively combat the new type of coronavirus outbreak described as a pandemic by the World Health Organization); the Hayat Eve Sığar mobile application has been developed by the General Directorate of Health Information Systems of the Republic of Türkiye Ministry of Health, in order to prevent the spread of the virus and minimize the contagion effect.

With the Hayat Eve Sığar (HES) application, which makes the most of the opportunities offered by information and technology during the fight against the pandemic period and controls and follows the whole process through health information systems, HES Code transactions, Safe Area, Vaccine Information, Rule Violation Notification, Regional Density and Risk Status Tracking on the map, Contact Status Tracking can be done.
Novelty: HES (Hayat Eve Sığar) Code, within the scope of Controlled Social Life, is a code that allows you to securely share whether you have any risk in terms of COVID-19 disease with institutions and individuals in transactions such as transportation or visits. Also, the app is the one of the most successful pandemic management applications has been created worldwide. It has been one of the most effective applications used in the pandemic period by benefiting from the developing technological opportunities in the fastest way.
Impact & Results:
The Hayat Eve Sığar application, which enables our citizens to securely share whether they are infected or in contact with COVID-19, with other individuals and institutions, has been put into operation. Hayat Eve Sığar (HES) application, which is one of the world's largest digital projects in this field, has been downloaded over 125 million times and nearly 222 million HES CODEs have been received. Nearly 16 billion HES CODE queries were made. Thanks to the HES CODE inquiry, 2.5 million risk warnings were received in intercity air travel and 6.8 million in urban transportation.
Until today;
• Number of users who downloaded the application: 125.914.124
• Total number of HEPP codes: 222,366.303
• Number of people who bought HES code: 98.056.586
• Number of HES codes inquired: 15.043.670.644
• Number of safe area QR codes: 89,217
• Number of safe area active QR codes: 80.399
• Number of users applying QR code: 78,091.

Description:
ESİM is an application developed for persons with hearing impairment to benefit from health services without communication barriers. Citizens can create records for emergency situations and request hospital appointments free of charge on Esim mobile applications from their smart devices. In addition, disabled citizens can receive instant support from expert medical personnel who know sign language by video call.

ESIM allows all disabled citizens to access the services in the application without paying any fee. Citizens can receive support 24/7 for all their health needs, including emergency situations, and hospital appointment requests. In addition, patient-doctor communication can also be provided through the application using sign language communication for the effective care of patients.
Novelty: Emergency call centers traditionally serve only with voice support over the phone. This system makes it almost impossible for people with hearing impairment to get services in emergency situations.
Impact & Results: The ESIM System has provided support to more than 13 thousand disabled citizens in more than 60 thousand cases since 2018. Thanks to the advantages provided by the system, more qualified health services have been provided in thousands of cases. Within the framework of the social state understanding in Turkey, access to health is seen as a fundamental right.

Project/Initiative Name: Renewable Energy and Energy Efficiency Project
Organisation: General Directorate of Prison and Detention Houses, Ministry of Justice
Description:
Prisons are an important expenditure unit in terms of the public service they carry out. It is considered that the establishment of solar power plants (GES) for the purpose of generating electricity in prisons will significantly contribute to the reduction of public expenditures for electricity, the most efficient use of our resources, the reduction of foreign dependency and the security of energy supply.

In order to meet some of the electricity need in the Sincan Prisons Campus, where our project is implemented as a pilot, the installation of a 3 megawatt solar power plant (GES) has been completed with the means of the 2020 budget. With the share allocated from the 2021 budget, an additional solar power plant with a power of 6.8 megawatts was installed to meet the entire electricity need of the same campus this time, and both facilities started production.
Novelty: For the first time in the history of Turkish Penal institutions, with the method of energy performance contracts; in addition to energy-saving solutions such as LED conversions in lighting, heating, cooling and meeting the need for hot water with heat pumps, it is planned to save energy costs by installing renewable energy systems, especially solar power plant (SPP) installation on suitable land or roof areas.
Impact & Results: Thanks to the developments made, during the period of June - September 2022, all electricity needs of the campus were met, and a total of 3,813,613 TRY revenue was obtained from the sale of surplus electricity, including VAT. When the annual consumption amount is compared with the production estimates, it is estimated that almost all of the electricity need of the campus (approximately 80 million TRY/year with current prices) will be met from these power plants.

Project/Initiative Name: Milan food waste hubs for reducing food waste by engaging different local actors
Description:
The Municipality of Milan together with actors of the private sector, academia and research centers launched 5 hubs with the aim of reducing food waste and innovating the ways of recovering food for the people in need. The hub network will also design and experiment a model of collection and redistribution of food surplus, based on local neighborhood networks.

Food Waste Hubs model allows to act on all types of food collected: fresh, cooked, dry, bread. Furthermore, it allows the mixing of recovered food, making it possible to donate a varied, nutritious and balanced food offer to people in need, alongside the direct contact between the supermarket and the non-profit organization.

The logistic model allows two daily paths for collecting food surplus:
Supermarkets – Morning: In the morning the organizations in charge of the management of the Hub, collect fresh, dry and packaged unsold foodstuff, from supermarkets directly from the points of sale belonging to the network, using the insulated van. The recovered food is stored at the Hub and the food packages are prepared selecting the goods recovered.
Company canteens – Afternoon: In the afternoon the collection of cooked unserved food, packaged or bulk fruit and bread surplus from company canteens, is directly delivered to people in need through the non profit organization connected with the Hub. Where possible, it is carried out at the same time as collection from supermarkets.
Novelty:
The hub innovates in integrating the logistics to "save" food that otherwise would be thrown away or would go rotten due to surplus. Under the system, each hub is equipped with:

- a refrigeration unit for the management of fresh food
- shelves for dry packaged foods
- a small office for the management of archives and logistic procedures.

The use of a small refrigerated van, provided by logistic operator Number1 Logistics Group, allows the daily recovery of food surplus from supermarkets and the storage in the hubs for the redistribution to the non profit organizations. 

Moreover, another vehicle is provided for the recovery of food surplus and its redistribution from company canteens directly to nonprofit organizations.
Impact & Results:
Results from the pilot of 2019
- 11 supermarket and food-chains involved
- 3 hubs running 
- 77 tones of eatable food recovered
- equivalent to >154,000 food rations

Project/Initiative Name: Failing forward - a framework for fostering innovation in the public sector
Organisation: CPI - Washington, DC; Kansas City, MO; Little Rock, AR; King County, WA; Seattle, WA; and Dallas, TX
Description:
Working with six local governments, they first investigate why it is so difficult for those in local government to learn from failures. Second, they consider what department leaders can do to build the conditions in which they and their teams can fail forward. 

The process is multi-step: 
- First, a public servant (or several) must identify that a failure has occurred.
- Second, they discuss this failure with peers or supervisors, and next, they work to understand why the failure occurred.
- Finally, they take action to address the failure. 

The project revealed four primary opportunities to promote failing forward in government:
- Mindset & beliefs
- Human relationships
- Systems & processes
- External ecosystems

Departments participated in a three-step process in which they identified the top barriers that prevent them from failing forward, brainstormed ideas to overcome those barriers, and ultimately developed two to three concrete action plans to implement the highest priority solutions. Each of them was tailored to the departments' needs through pre-work surveys and interviews with department leadership and staff. Data was anonymized.
Novelty: It brings ‘failure’ out of the shadows and to inspire local governments to create cultures of innovation that embrace failing while, at the same time, move forward. Anonymizes the participation so people feel free to open up and share their experiences.
Impact & Results: The innovation has already identified four key areas of action to intervene with solutions and agile methods to co-create and pilot solutions.

Project/Initiative Name: The DC COVID-19 Response Collaborative to help Local Education Agencies share knowledge and resources about testing and responding to positive cases in schools
Organisation: District of Columbia’s Office of the State Superintendent of Education - CPI and Rockefeller Foundation
Description:
In Washington, D.C., the Collaborative equipped participants with the information they needed to minimize in-school transmissions and respond to positive cases through policies around contact tracing, exclusion, dismissal, and return to school. It aims to: 
- Increase knowledge among participants about best practices in COVID-19 testing and responses to positive cases in the school setting;
- Inform LEAs’ testing and positive case response programs, practices, and policies;
- Strengthen peer relationships and increase information sharing across LEAs; and
- Create a sustainable framework for this model replicable by other school systems.

Key to the success of the Collaborative is its composition as a peer learning network. The Collaborative’s structure can be replicated and expanded to help school staff in communities across the country learn emerging strategies on a range of pandemic response topics.

The toolkit is a step-by-step guide to building a peer learning network capable of responding to the complex challenges related to COVID-19 in schools. It includes best practices and resources for creating peer learning networks.
Novelty: The toolkit provides users the means to determine priorities and levels of relevance according to each activity to help users decide how to most effectively allocate their time for their own learning network goals. Furthermore, it strictly focuses on schools which may strengthen connections between schools when addressing public health challenges.

Project/Initiative Name: The Living Standards Framework for addressing the different dimensions of wellbeing
Description:
The LSF is a flexible framework that prompts our thinking about policy impacts across the different dimensions of wellbeing, as well as the long-term and distributional issues and implications of policy. It supports the Treasury by providing a framework to understand the drivers of wellbeing and to consider the broader impacts of policy advice in a systematic and evidenced way.

The LSF includes three levels:
- Our Individual and Collective Wellbeing
- Our Institutions and Governance
- The Wealth of Aotearoa New Zealand

The framework also includes analytical prompts that are key lenses used to analyse wellbeing across the three levels of the framework. The analytical prompts are: distribution, resilience, productivity and sustainability.
Novelty:
Incorporates three different frameworks: 
- He Ara Waiora for a mātauranga Māori perspective on wellbeing
- Fonofale for a Pacific perspective on wellbeing, and
- The Children’s Commissioner’s Wellbeing Wheel for a perspective on children’s wellbeing.

Helps to understand the interdependencies and trade-offs across the different dimensions of wellbeing is consistent with robust economics and policy analysis.
Impact & Results: Possible to visualize on the online dashboard: https://lsfdashboard.treasury.govt.nz/wellbeing/

Project/Initiative Name: Full Fact - a team of independent fact checkers that address misinformation that hurts democracy
Description:
Full Fact fact checks information of public interest and with the potential of bias the public debate and democracy. It also cluster claims according to certain topics of interest (i.e. energy bills or Brexit). Most of the targeted information provides from politicians, authorities and people's representatives. They provide fact checkers with an automated fact checker software and a set of scalable, robust, automated fact checking tools to be used on/offline.

They collect a range of data from leading news sites and social media platforms that may contain claims to fact check. Data is turn into text and pass through a word-enriching process to finally identify claims, label and cluster them using a BERT-style model. Additionally, the model detects semantically similar words and phrases through a deduplicating process using Google big query to power this service. Once the patterns and language checking are out, and as a last step, it compares the extracted information with the corresponding data via the UK Office For National Statistics API. 

Once the claim has been fact checked it is published together with some specific markup (claim review) that describes content on a range of topics in domain specific terms, so it has larger impact.
Novelty:
the whole fact checking process and the incorporation of cutting edge data process based on AI and algorithmic transparency to fact check. 
Open source datasets and software.

Project/Initiative Name: The Mayor's Office of Social Equity and Innovation (OSEI)
Description:
OSEI was established to develop and implement the policies, programs, regulations, and initiatives of the city and county of Denver related to race, social equity, and social justice. They incorporate innovative practices to drive transformative change with a commitment to equitable outcomes. As a basis of its approach, OSEI defined equity as "when race and other social identities can no longer be used to predict life outcomes". 

The approach is sustained in the Mayor's Equity Platform which requires that all city agencies submit Equity Plans for feedback, support and future training. The support includes: 
- Equipping residents with new skills via virtual workshops, trainings and events
- Reducing barries to career pathways for residents from under-resourced communities, POC and workers
- Build relationships with industry and communities to create long lasting, intersectional paths for creating green and better jobs.
- Connect residents to immediate employment opportunities during virtual job fairs. 

On a recent adjustment, the Equity Platform would work on a $6 billion in public projects for the next decade oriented to reduce inequities and favor local communities and small businesses.
Novelty: Incorporation of an "equity filter" through the application of Equity Plans of every city agency.

Project/Initiative Name: Kingston's Buddy System and the Internet Oasis
Description:
The City of Kingston had many systemic and systematic barriers for newcomers so the main challenge was dismantling them. The city identified that systemic barriers are community-wide and based in procedures and processes that they were able to turn down. 
Through the training in innovation for public servants they managed to refine an initial digital concierge proposal into a two-way solution approach:
1. The Buddy System: an in-person concierge service to welcome newcomers and connect them with both social and employment opportunities. This program compensates for current gaps in existing service offerings across the Kingston community. It also solves the need for social connection, providing an interim solution while newcomers develop a community and network of their own.
2. The Internet Oasis: a program that offers affordable high-speed internet access, recycled technology hardware, and training. This addresses the primary challenge of the digital concierge approach: access. Similar to The Buddy System, this program bridges gaps between community program offerings. In addition, it will help to catch individuals who were falling through the cracks due to technology and capacity discrepancies between current programs.

The City is still working through the implementation strategy phase for both programs and are exploring funding supports that will help inform deployment timelines.
Novelty: #NAME?

Project/Initiative Name: BeamPro - Drone Delivered Medications and Drone Patrol in Alexandra Hospital
Description:
During the highest peak of the pandemic, Alexandra Hospital trialed touch-free options with patients by using drones to deliver medicines and to "talk/interact" with patients in the COVID-19 isolation area. 

The ‘BeamPro’ robot is programmed to chat with patients under isolation. The robot can converse in four different languages with a relatively small response delay. In addition to this task, the ‘BeamPro’ robot makes it possible for the doctor to continuously inspect the patient in isolation and deliver the basic needs without a dedicated staff. 

A second robot, LionsBot, is being deployed for sweeping, mopping, vacuuming and scrubbing at the hospital.
Novelty: #NAME?

Project/Initiative Name: Giessdenkiez for fighting the urban heat island
Description:
The open source application (web and App), developed by CityLAB Berlin of the Technology Foundation Berlin, locates around 750,000 Berlin city trees on an interactive map. Berliners can use the application to find out about the type and age of the trees, read off the current "water level" and adopt, water and mark trees. The platform already has over 2,070 users. 

Gieß den Kiez displays almost all of Berlin's street and plant trees with information such as their water requirements, age and species, and invites all citizens to participate in watering endangered trees. Citizens can find out about the water needs of trees in their neighborhood, mark when they watered and how much, and "adopt" trees. The project is based entirely on open source code and uses open data from the city administration (tree cadastre), the German Weather Service (daily precipitation) and Open Street Map (pumps). 

The city identified that the trees are particularly vulnerable due to pollution and rain water shortfall. Moreover, in recent years, more and more trees had to be cut down and their lifespan is decreasing.
Novelty: #NAME?

Description:
The Latrobe Valley created the Health Innovation Zone as recommended by the Hazelwood Mine Fire Inquiry. It gives voice to community aspirations in the planning and delivering of better health and wellbeing outcomes, and where a process of co-design with individuals and organisations is actively encouraged.

The LHIZ provides a focal point for coordination and integration of health services and supports a range of health-related projects (i.e. Early Detection and Screening including Tobacco Initiative)
Novelty: Incorporates what the Latrobe Valley community identify as important to their health and wellbeing together with healthcare providers (public, mostly)

Project/Initiative Name: Auto Arborist - a multiview urban tree classification dataset
Description:
The dataset is a fine-grained multiview dataset that contains over 2 million trees belonging to over 300 genus-level categories in 23 cities across the US and Canada. Most of the data is pulled from public tree censuses and merged these records with Google Street View and overhead RGB imagery. This is built to foster the development of robust methods for large-scale urban forest monitoring. 

It is a a multiview urban tree classification dataset that, at ~2.6 million trees and >320 genera. The project cover multiple cities so it helps to analyze in detail how forest models can generalize with respect to geographic distribution shifts, crucial to building systems that scale. 

The curation process hast four steps: 1) obtaining online data from tree censuses, 2) using geolocation for each tree, an RGB aerial image is taken within 2-10 meters around it, 3) images are filtered to maximize visibility of tree of interest and assure user privacy, 4) finally the system only keeps images where the tree position was sufficiently close to the street level camera.
Novelty:
Measuring the size, health, and species distribution of trees in cities over time — allows researchers and policymakers to 1) quantify ecosystem services, including air quality improvement, carbon sequestration, and benefits to public health; 2) track damage from extreme weather events, and 3) target planting to improve robustness to climate change, disease and infestation. 

Urban forest monitoring is an ideal testbed for working on several computer vision challenges (domain generalization, fine-grained categorization, long-tail learning, multiview vision)

The dataset is taxonomically diverse, with >300 different genera represented.

Project/Initiative Name: Responsible Data 4 Children to help users to identify the systems generating data about children
Description:
RD4C is an initiative with the aim of supporting organisations and practitioners in ensuring that the interest of children is put at the centre of any work involving data for and about them.

The initiative has aimed to be field-oriented, driven by the needs of both children and practitioners across sectors and contexts. It does so by ensuring that actors from the data responsibility sphere are informed and engaged on the RD4C work. The work is intended to address practical considerations across the data lifecycle, including routine data collection and one-off data collections; and compliments work on related topics being addressed by the development community such as guidance on specific data systems and technologies, technical standardization, and digital engagement strategies.

They use agile approaches to create the toolkits. Four call the attention in particular: 1) RD4C Data Ecosystem Mapping Tool, 2) RD4C Decision Provenance Mapping, 3) Ethical Assessment – Data for Children Collaborative with UNICEF, 4) RD4C Opportunity and Risk Diagnostic.

RD4C initiative has started translating its resources into different languages.
Novelty: It provides a toolkit and several associated resources to assess the work involving data of children

Project/Initiative Name: TrashBlitz to collect data on trash to foster environmental stewardship
Description:
The platform and app TrashBlitz collects data on trash and contribute to a robust dataset that can be used to further campaigns, strengthen legislation, and foster environmental stewardship across the U.S 63 national parks. It uses a research platform to tag trash and upload a photo during cleanups, and it automatically geotags the location and tallies the number of items, materials, and brands found. This data contributes to Break Free From Plastic’s Global Brand Audits to identify and track plastic pollution trends.

With state of the art data collection software, they are able to identify how much plastic is on streets and in rivers. The data will show how much of each type of plastic there is as well as the most prevalent brands. 

Park visitors register for free and can specify the types of trash that they’ve spotted, such as if the discarded item was used for food packaging.
Novelty:
AI-based software for trash identification and categorization 
Data creation about how much of each type of plastic there is as well as the most prevalent brands in national parks and recreation areas
Impact & Results:
316,666 pieces of trash picked up
Most picked up items: light stick, miscellaneous fragment, beverage bottles, bottle caps and rings, medical waste

Most picked up brands: Nestle, marlboro, Filly Folly, Peanut Free, Unknown

Project/Initiative Name: The Violence & Impacts Early-Warning System (VIEWS) to use Wikipedia for conflict forecasting
Description:
The Violence Early-Warning System (ViEWS) is a publicly available, data-driven forecasting system. It generates monthly predictions of the number of fatalities in impending state-based conflict 1-36 months ahead, as well as probabilistic assessments of the likelihood of three different types of political violence – for each country and 55×55 km location in Africa and the Middle East. 

This is a research that brings together three distinct but interrelated research projects: the political Violence Early-Warning System (ViEWS), and the interdisciplinary conflict impacts projects Societies at Risk and ANTICIPATE.

It uses Wikipedia country page views to capture tension on what individuals actually read. Page changes capture controversy between opposing political views. Scientific topics such as global warming (politically controversial) are edited more often than continental drift (less controversial). It applies the same logic to countries and its politically controversial topics.

There was a noticeable increase in predictive power of up to 62% for the true forecast into the future. The model provides among the most revealing insights, disclosing patterns for predicting early signals of tension escalation and long-term trends like those recently seen in Egypt, Nigeria, and Cameroon.
Novelty:
The model reveals how Wikipedia's ability to capture the salience and controversy of a topic makes it a valuable new data source for conflict forecasting.

Evaluates findings with out-of-sample predictions using held-out, previously unseen data, and true forecasts into the future.
Impact & Results:
This innovation brought to publish papers on prestigious academic journals. This scientific breakthrough has generated significant interest in international fora, leading to a number of collaborations with governments, IGO agencies, and other research institutes, allowing us to test the applicability our research in different contexts and develop tailor-made solutions to maximise the usefulness of our research to a larger audience.
Our forecasts are now used by fellow researchers, governments, IGOs and NGOs alike, and we continue to closely engage with our key user groups to iteratively improve our forecasting system and provide a high-quality public good as we transition to also predicting impacts of armed conflict.

Project/Initiative Name: GDP Prototype to track real time inequality
Organisation: Center for Equitable Growth at UC Berkeley
Description:
The novel methodology and the web platform provide the first timely statistics on how economic growth is distributed across groups in the United States. 

The methodology to estimate the distribution of income in real time combines the information contained in high-frequency public data sources—including monthly household and employment surveys, quarterly censuses of employment and wages, and monthly and quarterly national accounts statistics—in a unified framework.

The result of this combination is a set of harmonized monthly micro-files in which an observation is a synthetic adult (obtained by statistically matching public micro-data) and variables include income, wealth, and their components.

When new growth numbers come out each quarter, it shows how each income and wealth group benefits. This allows us to estimate economic growth by income groups, race, and gender consistent with quarterly releases of macroeconomic growth, and to track the distributional impacts of government policies during and in the aftermath of recessions in real time.
Novelty:
Estimate economic growth by income groups, race, and gender consistent with quarterly releases of macroeconomic growth.

Track the distributional impacts of government policies during and in the aftermath of recessions in real time
Impact & Results: https://www.nber.org/papers/w30229

Project/Initiative Name: Red Cross Collective Crisis Intelligence Tools
Description:
This collective Crisis Intelligence method combine the collective intelligence of local community actors (e.g. volunteers in local branches) with a wide range of additional data sources, artificial intelligence and predictive analytics to support crisis management and reduce the impacts of humanitarian crises.

The two collective crisis intelligence tool prototypes developed were:
- NFRI-Predict: a tool that predicts which non-food aid items (NFRI) are most needed by different types of households in different regions of Nepal after a crisis.
- Report and Respond: a French language SMS-based tool that allows Red Cross volunteers in Cameroon to check the accuracy of COVID-19 rumours or misinformation they hear from the community while they’re in the field, and receive real-time guidance on appropriate responses.

Both tools were developed using NESTA participatory AI methods, which aimed to address some of the risks associated with humanitarian AI by involving local communities in the design, development and evaluation of the new tools.
Impact & Results:
Technical report Nepal: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1NS7SY4nSKi9EDYYLMW-NlWLbfrhgpcdQT6cSfOkCZP0/edit 

Technical report Cameroon: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1bmUgtA_wqglByHV3JuTzt5sF1DP1JF-zQp8NMJTHxdE/edit

Project/Initiative Name: GuardianScore to help police administrations to understand how their police officers treat community members
Description:
Guardian Score is a survey solution designed to help police administrations use real-time data analytics and insights to understand how their police officers treat community members. Moreover, it seeks to help police departments improve legitimacy and gather meaningful performance metrics for police evaluations and quality of "customer service". 

Community members provide star ratings (1-5 stars) about several key behavioral traits and we take the average which becomes the “Guardian Score.” This score is also evaluated on a team and department level.

It works on a three-step basis. First, police officer gives an interaction card equipped with a QR code to a community member. Second, the community member fills out anonymous survey to rate their police interaction based on officer's ability to explain "why", as well as other metrics. Third, through the dashboard app supervisors review service scores and create/compare metrics.
Novelty:
It defines itself as "an Uber-like scoring system for police officers" 
Seeks to create new metrics to improve the work of police departments.
Impact & Results:
- >150 police officers being evaluated
- >10,000 community members with access to score
- >5,000 surveys submitted

Project/Initiative Name: Zero Fatality Corridor to reduce road crash fatalities
Description:
The Zero Fatality Corridor (ZFC) model looks at conditions and realities specific to low- and middle-income countries and makes scientifically-backed suggestions on the most cost-effective solutions for road crash and fatality prevention. The model is adjustable and scalable for contexts where complex settings and low resources are the major deterrents to employing the traditional road safety guidance emerging from developed nations.

The initiative is conceived in this context, with a vision to build a replicable and scalable model to reduce road crash deaths in India. The Government of Maharashtra together with ZFC aim to reduce road crash deaths on the Yashwantrao Chavan Mumbai-Pune Expressway to from an annual average of 135 to 0 (zero) by the year 2021 - transforming it into India's first Zero Fatality Corridor.

Some of the practices that are a part of the ZFC solution include optimized placement of ambulances at high-fatality locations, the utilization of drones to identify parked vehicles to preemptively prevent rear-end collisions, and road engineering solutions unique to the realities of countries like India.
Impact & Results:
- a 38 percent reduction in deaths on the “Yamuna 111km Expressway” in Uttar Pradesh, India’s most populous state,
- brought down road crash deaths on this stretch from 268 in 2018 to 79 in 2020, a 54% reduction in deaths.

Project/Initiative Name: The Economic Value Atlas for aligning investment and public values
Organisation: Kansas City, Minneapolis-Saint Paul City, Portland City, Brookings Consulting
Description:
The EVA is a regional engagement, value-setting, and measurement process culminating in an interactive regional map that indexes neighborhood-level, value-based performance metrics. The overall framework helps practitioners delve into geographic disparities in how the region is living up to its values—opening the door to more equitable, place-based decisionmaking for business, infrastructure, and land use purposes. 

City members shared lessons around community engagement and values alignment, tested new methods to measure progress, and considered various policy reforms. For example, Portland's EVA helped create an Equitable Development Index, which informed a future light rail investment and a region-wide ballot initiative: https://evatool.oregonmetro.gov/
Novelty: Open access and multivariable platform to gather information from a wide set of common activities directly from the stakeholders.

Project/Initiative Name: Barcelona Superblock - Digital Twin Smart City Initiative
Organisation: City of Barcelona - Barcelona Supercomputer Center
Description:
Barcelo is making use of its Supercomputer (MareNostrum) to improve urban planning by creating/designing a digital twin of the city. This is a data-based clone of Barcelona so the public administration has the opportunity to test potential city planning proposals as part of the pilot project. 

Furthermore, Barcelona may design, test, and create urban policies digitally first by relying on this twin model as part of its updated smart city development. The main advantage is the capacity to anticipate impacts and avoid negative turn outs. 

A good example of the outputs of the city is the Barcelona Superblock concept: https://ajuntament.barcelona.cat/superilles/en/
Novelty: Build a twin city concept by using data - a synthetic city for trialing policy making

Project/Initiative Name: Drones Coordination Cell for Humanitarian Assistance
Description:
WFP Drones initiative offers a relatively cheap way of obtaining the data needed for decision making on a humanitarian crisis setting. WFP DEEP (Digital Engine for Emergency Photo-analysis) is a solution that reduces the analysis time of affected areas from weeks to hours. 

DEEP is based on open-source applications so the code is visible to programmers to inspect, change or enhance. It was field-tested in Mozambique to quickly quantify the number of houses that were partially or completely destroyed during a climate-related crisis. They are equipped with high-resolution imaginary and mobile sensor stations. These information is enhanced with AI to extract data.
Novelty: Cutting edge crisis response using drone photography of affected areas

Organisation: Flying Labs, WeRobotics, Pfizer, Dominican Republic Ministry of Health
Description:
The project sought to pilot affordable, locally-repairable cargo drones that are operated locally and (ideally) by healthcare professionals to reliably and autonomously deliver medicines on demand. Moreover, to enable clinicians and logisticians in the country’s health care system to operate UAV delivery systems without requiring expert or vendor- maintained operations, and to understand how such systems might be operated routinely in the future

It tackles the biggest barrier for healthcare delivery in remote areas, which is the cost of local transportation and to avoid on-ground conditions that may prevent samples, medicines and the people to arrive safe and sound. The pilot also wants to create a local robotics capacity and build up an ecosystem to create new jobs through robotics technology and data products. 

The key innovation was embedding an autonomous delivery model within the country’s existing health care system, operated and supported by local technicians and healthcare personnel as part of their everyday activities.A total of 101 autonomous flights were carried out to two separate health facilities, traveling a total of 994 kilometers. Of the 51 outbound flights, 40 carried medicines totaling 21.25 kilos.
Novelty:
'- locally-produced and operated drones to supply healthcare provision 
- reduce transportation costs (time, money, physical resources)
- generate robotics and data skills

Organisation: N3C - National Center for Advancing Translational Sciences
Description:
N3C has built a unique public-private partnership with >300 participating organizations to overcome privacy concerns and data silos to make >13 million patient records interoperable and to address a particular challenge related to a certain disease or outbreak. 

Through a series of dashboards, these data have helped in myriad ways: they led to identification of the clinical characteristics of acute COVID-19 for risk prediction, assisted in providing clinical care for immunocompromised adults, revealed how COVID infection affects children, and documented that vaccines appear to reduce the risk of developing long COVID.

N3C has created the largest national, publicly available patient-driven dataset in US history. The public health data collected by N3C aims to enrich the landscape of interoperable data systems that can guide public policy in an era of rapid environmental change, sophisticated biological threats, and to enable the economy of biotechnology.
Novelty:
Biggest publicly available patient-driven dataset in the US 
Use of public private partnerships to overcome privacy concerns and data silos.

Description:
The EU-funded MICS project is developing a platform of tools to measure costs and benefits of citizen science. The impacts of citizen science are being measured on the following domains: (1) Society: impact on society and individuals as well as collective values and understanding; (2) Economy: implications for entrepreneurial activity, and economic benefits derived from data, e.g. for the public good; (3) Environment: impact on the bio-chemical-physical environment, e.g. on the quality or quantity of specific natural resources or ecosystems; (4) Science: impact on the scientific process as well as research more broadly, and the scientific system; (5) Governance: impact on the processes and institutions through which decisions are made.

MICS uses a novel impact-assessment metrics and instruments that measure costs and benefits of citizen science, with particular attention in the domains of society; democracy; the economy; NBS science, and citizen scientists. This will test the applicability of the MICS impact-assessment tools in regions with differing opportunities and constraints for NBSs, and with different levels of citizen science uptake.

This is part of a policy priority of the European Research Area to improve the interaction between the research system and society at large. It aims to engage citizens in the design and implementation of research and innovation policies to improve the quality of research outcomes.

Project/Initiative Name: Amsterdam's Algorithmic Transparency in Automated parking control and Reporting issues in public space
Description:
Amsterdam's has introduced AI, machine learning and algorithmic transparency systems/concepts as part of their public order efforts. In both cases the system use an algorithm that recognizes certain keywords and determines which category it begins to and to which department within the municipality it should be delivered for case examination. 

It follows a strict protocol of data processing and data protection. First, the text the report is broken down into single words and trained to recognize its weight using TF-IDF (term frequency-inverse document frequency). Additionally it uses a logistic regression to evaluate the combination of words used to determine which category is most likely to fit so they can allocate it to the right office. Second, the dataset generated would be part of a trining set of >300,000 reports to help training and corrections. Third, with human supervision any past mistake or additional training of the algorithm can be automated manually in the future. 

The algorithm is language-based and mostly applicable to Dutch and English. All reports that are assigned by the system to a specific category with <40% certainty are forwarded to the Action Service Center so an employee will assess it and re-routed.
Novelty: Incorporation of AI, machine learning and algorithmic transparency as part of its complaint policies and public order initiatives.
Other relevant URL(s) - eg reports, pres:
Automated parking control: https://algoritmeregister.amsterdam.nl/en/automated-parking-control/ 

Reporting issues in public space: https://algoritmeregister.amsterdam.nl/en/reporting-issues-in-public-space/ 

Reporting environment platform: https://meldingen.amsterdam.nl/incident/beschrijf 

https://www.wsj.com/articles/cities-take-lead-setting-rules-around-how-ai-is-used-11649448031

Project/Initiative Name: #DataforUkraine to report on the incidence of important events in Ukraine
Description:
The project aims to gather data on civilian resistance (CR), human rights abuses (HRA), displacement of people (IDP) and humanitarian support/needs (HS) across Ukraine, with the intention of providing timely information for private citizens, NGOs, INGOs and policymakers responding to the Russian invasion and war. 

The system uses hourly data from the Twitter API to report on the incidence of important events and relies on several hundred key accounts to identify Twitter communities of interest. It classifies millions of individual tweets according to the aforesaid categories to provide hourly information on the incidence of each event type over the last three days. 

The data are drawn from an initial collection of more than 400 Twitter accounts (and their followers) covering a broad range of profiles and political positions. Tweets are automatically searched for more than 600 keywords in Ukrainian and Russian. Keywords were initially derived iteratively from social science theory and translated in to Ukrainian and Russian by native speakers. Through a period of consistent multi-team member verification and careful study of the living language actually used in Tweets about the phenomena of concern, we expanded the terms to include these ‘real world’ formulations.
Novelty: The project is the first attempt to collect and distribute near real time information on humanitarian needs, human rights violations, displaced people and collective action in the context of war. It does so innovatively using hourly data from the Twitter API. The project presents the data within a time frame that makes it potentially actionable on the ground, while at the same time preserving a quantitative and deep qualitative archive for use by researchers later. In addition, the ability of the innovation to geo-locate events at a low level of aggregation makes the data more useful both in real time and in later analysis.
Impact & Results: In an initial assessment, the machine learning approach helped identify major events of human rights abuses roughly 3hrs before media were able to report on them. Use of the data by academic and other communities is still in its infancy, but it is expected to be a major area of impact going forward. To date, the impact has been hard to measure systematically.

Project/Initiative Name: Cities4Cities - a matching platform for Ukrainian and other European cities
Organisation: Council of Europe - Association of Ukrainian Cities - Deutscher Städtetag
Description:
The platform is a free online exchange tool that allows local authorities in Ukraine and in the rest of Europe to share their needs and offers related to local infrastructure and get in direct contact to receive practical help. 

The platform's main idea is to "matchmake" the demands and needs of Ukrainian cities with the capacity, know-how and supply of other European cities. The aim is to enable fasted and more efficient support in the reconstruction process of Ukrainian cities. 

According to the Council of Europe, this is a result-oriented solidarity model at the local level. A public authority or public officer creates an account to access to a bank of supply-demand-oriented platform. There it is possible to highlight needs and identify potential support.
Novelty: Concept of sisters cities taken to the actionable level of providing different means of collaboration and support.

Description:
This toolkit is designed to help companies develop trustworthy artificial intelligence for children and youth. It was co-created with a team of youth, technologists, academics and business leaders to help companies develop trustworthy artificial intelligence (AI) for children and youth and to help parents, guardians, children and youth responsibly buy and safely use AI products.

The checklist for C-suite executives and guidelines for product teams contain actionable frameworks and real-world guidance to help your company design innovative and responsible AI for children and youth. By using these guidelines, they are expected to lead as a trusted company. 

The AI labelling system is designed to be included in all AI products on their physical packaging and online accessible through a QR code. Like nutritional information on food packaging, the labelling system is designed to concisely tell consumers – including parents and guardians, as well as children and youth – how it works and the options available to the users.
Novelty: First AI toolkit for private sector and companies to assess the impact of AI on children and youth

Project/Initiative Name: Gender Data Lab to highlight data about gender discrimination and bias
Organisation: Digital Fems - The Data Place - Gender Data Lab
Description:
The project aims to reflect on the contributions of women in technological environments, and put in place mechanisms that help increase the visibility of women in these environments. The platform built the open data environment to advise on data modeling and use issues in relation to gender bias and women's perceptions. 

The project also supports organizations and public institutions to visibility female technologists through soft skills support and generating inclusive data collection initiatives (i.e. applied to justice, technology sector or Human Resources).
Novelty: Support to female technologist profiles through soft skills building and data collaboration for inclusive environments.

Organisation: Americas Cohort of the Collab Challenges
Description:
The political misogynistic discourse monitor is a web application and API that detects hate speech against women in Spanish and Portuguese. This project is part of a global initiative that brings together media organizations, the academia and Google News Initiative to explore innovative solutions to improve journalism via the use of AI technologies. 

The web application is a simple platform that requests two things: 1) access key - a word or set of words in Portuguese or Spanish language, and 2) include tweets to analyze. The search engine runs it and displays results about misogynistic attacks from people and/or politicians on the specific domain of interest. 

The platform is on itself a tool to help identifying attacks against women among a large volumen of data on Twitter, so it highlights content that can be analyzed by a human moderator afterward.
Novelty: web-base platform running in Spanish and Portuguese (usually such platforms are available in English only)

Project/Initiative Name: The AIJO Project to leverage the power of AI to mitigate newsroom bias
Description:
The project aims to leverage the power of AI to understand, identify and mitigate newsroom biases in relation to gender. It seeks to uncover the binary gender representations in various news and publications and the overall media.
