It has three main components: 1) understand - how biases shape news, 2) identify - how can AI helps uncovering biases, and 3) mitigate - what to do with the AI insights?

On its initial experiment/pilot, the project used an AI and machine learning-based image and language identification to analyze >28,000 images to show the average ratio of women representation in contrast to those of men. 

The machine learning model deployed by the AIJO team went through 28,000 pictures sourced from the different media outlets involved to measure the number of images representing female and male characters. The results were predictable: men are significantly more represented in the media than women. When the model identifies the gender for a given face in an image, it also outputs a confidence score value. This allows to manually review the images with lower confidence scores to achieve higher accuracy, with the least amount of effort. After the human review, the average ratio of women was 27.2% and 72.8% for men.

This tool generate a csv file you can use to analyze the gender diversity in your content. Additionally, there is a GUI tool for fixing gender detection by human review.
Novelty: AI Gender bias mitigation project

Project/Initiative Name: Medical Matchmaking to discover alike treatments and share your medical journey
Description:
The app and web platform uses health data and machine learning to match people who are alike on a holistic wellbeing level. The AI's matchmaking takes into account considerations such as co-morbilities, lifestyle factors, age, and gender to create patient similarity networks. Patients are put into into contact with anonymized communities so they can share or receive relevant and personalized insights that help them to better manage their conditions. 

The innovation sees that regulatory changes that make possible for everyone to gain instant electronic access to the personal health records are valuable to create health profiles and direct connections with networks that may also include health providers. 

The score of a user/patient and the score of others becomes the basis of the ability to the AI system to match precisely with another person and a network.
Novelty: Medtech oriented to matchmake patients with similar conditions through an algorithm to share their journeys and learn from each other's medical conditions.

Project/Initiative Name: Madrid te Acompaña - Madrid is with You
Description:
The initiative is a digitally-based social project of Madrid City Council. It brings together elderly Madridians (>65 years old) and volunteers to support/help them in various tasks such as accompany them to the doctor, go for a walk or carrying out administrative procedures. It seeks to trigger intergenerational solidarity and closer neighborhood relationships. 

It is a public-private service and allows a platform for an initial digital interaction, thanks to the provision of two mobile apps - one for elderly citizens and the other for volunteers. This is elderly people who request for company on a specific domain, and volunteers who accompany them. It is only necessary to guarantee that they are elderly people registered in the city of Madrid and volunteers are recognised and trained by the Volunteering Department of the Madrid City Council.

The service is provided completely digitally and City Council is able to manage and monitor the main indicators about it and the citizen engagement thanks to a specifically designed scorecard.
Novelty:
Digital platform to connect on a demand-basis the needs of elderly Madridians and volunteers to support them with their needs. 
This completely digital way of putting elderly people and volunteers in contact with each other allows, at any time and in a very easy way, for an elderly person and a volunteer who do not know each other to reach an agreement for an accompaniment service without the need for third parties to intervene.
Seniors can rest assured that only volunteers certified as "Volunteers for Madrid" can access the system.
The mobile applications use cloud computing infrastructure services, guaranteeing their availability at all times.
Impact & Results:
Given that the Madrid te Acompaña service is provided entirely digitally through its apps, the City Council is able to manage and monitor the main indicators on the service and citizen participation thanks to a specifically designed scorecard. 
Madrid Te Acompaña came into operation in December 2021. In the first 10 months of operation, more than 4,300 elderly people and 750 volunteers had already registered, and more than 2,800 accompaniment services had been carried out, managed exclusively by the new apps. 
In terms of services requested, rides represent 73% of the total number of requests and medical appointments 17%, ahead of other services such as shopping, leisure, pets, hospital, formalities, etc.

Project/Initiative Name: The Monash Net Zero Precinct - an interdisciplinary approach to decarbonising cities
Organisation: Monash Sustainable Development Institute - Monash University
Description:
Monash's Net Zero Precincts Project seeks to help creating cities that use net zero energy and to help them in transitioning to it. This is developed by approximating the precinct as an optimal scale for urban transition in order to provide tailored support to communities. 

The project brings together two key areas of scholarship for the first time — transition management and design anthropology — to pioneer a new approach to transitioning cities to net zero. It experiments in energy systems, mobility and buildings within the precinct of Monash University. With the experiences they can derive learnings to the different stakeholders, while at the same time, acquiring significant evidence/data to accelerate urban transitions. 

Net Zero Precincts is part of Monash’s Net Zero Initiative — a $135m program that is transforming Monash University’s four Australian campuses to become net zero in terms of carbon emissions by 2030. It includes a range of measures including energy efficiency upgrades, high-performing building facades, campus electrification, a 100% renewable electricity microgrid and 1 MWh of battery storage. 

The approach has four main axis: 1) understand the drivers that frame the precinct community's experiences and visions, 2) co-create and envision collective and shared pathways to net zero, 3) design and test three living labs aligned to the shared visions, 4) develop a methodological approach to net zero transitions.
Novelty: Co-creative initiative that integrates design, anthropology and technology to develop and pilot net zero practices within precincts.

Project/Initiative Name: CrisisReady for equitable and transparent crisis planning and response (CMDN)
Description:
The CMDN’s focus is on the use of digital human mobility data to provide insights into the impact of various catastrophic events such as fires, floods, or pandemic. The platform shares daily updates from analysis using aggregated population mobility data from social media platforms and telecom companies, as well as publicly available data from local public health partners.

The platform is an example of crowdsourced science and collaboration between academic partners, technology companies and response agencies around the world to embed data-driven decision-making for better local disaster planning.

Through a data platform + a network of experts/volunteers the solutions are co-developed, as well as data repositories and tools. The web platform displays maps and reports in the form of population density and movement maps, interactive maps and data reports on the state of the art during crisis happenings - all of them free to download. The key outcomes from these outputs include a global community of practice of researchers and responders that advance the application of these analysis in public health response and to improve forecasting decision-making at the local level.
Novelty: Use of data mobility from social media to approximate the "human impact" in terms of mobility and behavior during catastrophic events.

Project/Initiative Name: Pollinator Highway Augmented Reality Application - Avalinn AR
Description:
Avalinn AR is Estonia's first app combining urban planning and augmented reality. It introduces Putukaväila's vision for the future in North Tallinn and it is planned to be used in other areas of the city and to support urban development projects as well (Augmented Urban). 

The purpose of the App is to enable citizens to experience the landscape architecture solution along the Pollinator Highway via AR. This is relevant as approximately 40% of the citizens of Tallinn live within 30 minutes walking distance. 

Once the App is downloaded, it can be used to scan the murals on the end walls of the garages at the Pollinator Highway. There are a total of 4 wall paintings that people can scan and benefit from the experience and information: "Species Richness", "Sustainable Mobility", "Reuse of Garages" and "Leisure Activities". Through the App people have access to experience the future of the project intervention.
Novelty: First AR application to support urban development and the co-creation of urban interventions (Augmented Urban).

Organisation: National Network to End Domestic Violence
Description:
The toolkit is intended to help judges and judicial officers respond to the technological needs of their courts and cases involving the misuse of technology. These resources include tools and information about various technology platforms, how technology is misused in the context of domestic violence, understanding and admitting technology evidence, judicial ethics in technology, and leadership opportunities involving technology in the courts. 

The tools follows the good practices and examples used in the Cyberviolence Court Training Initiative. The resources include a guide on current online platforms and technology terms, a guide about primary functions of popular tech platforms, unique quizzes to test technology savviness, and more. 

The innovation believes that digital evidence is nowadays an essential part of many court cases, thus the toolkit aims to gather resources to help judges and judicial officers better understand the benefits and challenges of digital evidence and how to navigate it.
Novelty: Toolkit to help judges and judicial officers respond to the technological needs of their courts and cases involving the misuse of technology.

Description:
The Sensor Register Map is a digital platform that shows all the sensors that are present in the public spaces of Amsterdam. On that map, it is possible to see what data the sensors process. In addition, special stickers are placed on sensors highlighting the type of personal information that it/they collect. With a URL embedded, once a citizen passes by a sensor it will pop out information about its purpose, what data it collects and where such information can be accessed from. 

The aim of the city is to place stickers on all sensors, not only on those ones processing personal data. In that sense, sensors installed all over Amsterdam will be labeled and of easy access to the citizens. To meet this objective, this website, the Sensorenregister, was designed and the Slimmeapparten website was dropped.

The AI Register is a window into the artificial intelligence systems used by the City of Helsinki. Through the register, citizens can get acquainted with the quick overviews of the city's artificial intelligence systems or examine their more detailed information based on specifics interests and search criteria. People can also give feedback and thus participate in building human-centered AI in Helsinki.

It is a standardised, searchable and archivable way to document the decisions and assumptions that were made in the process of developing, implementing, managing and ultimately dismantling an algorithm. With this, transparency, and when applicable, explainability, can be given for public debate, independent auditors, and individuals citizens.

This innovation is part of the city's initiative to improve the accessibility and experience of the city’s services, and in these, artificial intelligence can help: for example, the city’s chatbot experiments are a step towards making the services accessible from anywhere and anytime.
Novelty: The platform gathers information of all AI-based systems currently running in Helsinki and that are oriented to the citizens.

Project/Initiative Name: Do No Harm Guide: Applying Equity Awareness in Data Visualization
Description:
The guide's aim is to unlock the full potential of research data by applying an equitable lens to every step of the data/evidence transformation process. This guide is not a one-size-fits-all tool, rather it is constantly being co-created and improved with data scientists and researchers. 

Data visualization often reduces people to points on a map or bars on a chart, so framing and context that uplift the people and communities behind the data are necessary for data communicators to empathetically present their work. These steps should be taken in tandem with existing standards of rigorous research, and researchers should seek to work with communities rather than simply on behalf of them. By starting with empathy, sophisticated research methods, and community buy-in, data analysts can produce quality research that provides background context and points toward effective solutions and recommendations.

The main considerations of the guide are three-fold: 
- Use people-first language. Data labels and framing should start with the people behind the data, not their characteristics.
- Order labels and responses purposefully. Often, surveys and other data collection methods will order responses in ways that reflect historical biases. Rather than using orders that reinforce “white” and “male” categories as norms, consider ordering labels by sample size or magnitude of results.
- Carefully consider colors, icons, and shapes. In visualizations, images and colors can help readers connect with the data, but researchers should be mindful of their capacity to exacerbate stereotypes.
Novelty: The guide as an initial standard to frame and context the data so it uplifts the people and communities in evaluation/measurement.

Project/Initiative Name: NYC Bill to audit bias of AI hiring tech
Description:
This is a local Law to amend the administrative code of the city of New York, in relation to automated employment decision tools. Moreover, it will require companies that sell AI technologies to obtain audits assessing the potential of those products to discriminate against job candidates. 

The bill would require that a bias audit be conducted on an automated employment decision tool prior to the use of said tool. The bill would also require that candidates or employees that reside in the city be notified about the use of such tools in the assessment or evaluation for hire or promotion, as well as, be notified about the job qualifications and characteristics that will be used by the automated employment decision tool. 

The legislation calls on companies using automated decision tools for recruitment not only to tell job candidates when they're being used, but to tell them what information the technology used to evaluate their suitability for a job.
Novelty: Innovative Bill to tackle bias in hiring AI-solutions and to hold companies more accountable.

Project/Initiative Name: nativeDATA - A Data Sharing Resource for Native Peoples and Organizations
Description:
NativeDATA is a free online resource that offers practical guidance for Tribes and Native-serving organizations on obtaining and sharing health data. The platform offers guidance on the process of obtaining and sharing data, as well as tips for those seeking to collaborate with Tribes in data-matters. It also connects the people and organizations to the Tribal and Urban Indian Organizations and Tribal Epidemiology Centers. 

The focus of the platform is to keep tribal communities aware of what entails to share their data, what should be a good practice around it and about the formal agreements behind such practice. Moreover, that data can always be retrieved. In that sense, leaders can make informed data-health decisions to uplift their communities.
Novelty: First guidance on data sharing and consent for tribal communities.

Project/Initiative Name: Census Mapper - helping  journalists get straight to finding Census stories
Organisation: Pitch Interactive - Census 2020 Data Co-Op - Google News Initiative
Description:
The Census mapper shares embeddable maps that displays data at the national level, as well as for states, counties and census tracts to support journalism efforts processing, analyzing and visualizing data to pump out stories. The platform helps mapping and charting in a comprehensive way so that it help readers and policymakers make sense of the demographic shifts or any demographic related piece of evidence. 

The Open Project includes 2010 and 2020 Block level files for each state, a data dictionary for raw block level files, a data dictionary for select data files, and a README on the overall project. Also a census resources file has been added, including an R tutorial. As another component of the Co-op’s toolkit, Census Reporter built a tool to help journalists quickly get the redistricting data aggregated to neighborhoods and other geographies which aren’t directly included in the Census Bureau’s release. 

Anyone can submit a link to publicly available shapefiles, and, in short order, Census Reporter will produce CSV data tables and map-ready GeoJSON files which can be used for analysis and visualization.
Novelty: One of the most innovative aspects of Census Mapper is the ability to use the interface to select specific demographics and locations and then embed this specific information through an embed feature on the site. This gives the intended audience, local news organizations, the ability to quickly find stories without having to learn web programming or any technical skillsets. They can specify the dimensions for their own blog or website template and Census Mapper will generate the code needed to drop into place.
Impact & Results: As of September, 2022, Census Mapper has been live for almost a year and has seen many news organizations utilizing this tool.

Project/Initiative Name: The Delphi Experiment - modeling people’s moral judgments on a variety of everyday situations
Organisation: The Allen Institute for Artificial Intelligence
Description:
Delphi is a research prototype designed to model people’s moral judgments on a variety of everyday situations. The demo shows the abilities and limitations of state-of-the-art AI models today.   Our innovation – known as “Delphi” – is the world’s first attempt to apply modern deep learning methods to build an AI system that makes human-like moral judgments. 

There are AI systems that screen resumes and help to drive cars.  Yet these systems are rarely programmed with morality in mind despite the fact that the decisions of the systems often have direct moral implications for stakeholders. Thus, it is becoming ever more critical to find additional mechanisms to align AI systems to human values and norms. 

Delphi is an experimental framework based on deep neural networks trained directly to reason about descriptive ethical judgments, e.g., "helping a friend" is generally good, while "helping a friend spread fake news" is not. Delphi demonstrates strong generalization capabilities in the face of novel ethical situations, while off-the-shelf neural network models exhibit markedly poor judgment including unjust biases, confirming the need for explicitly teaching machines moral sense. We created Delphi by exposing an LLM to the Commonsense Norm Bank, curated by us.  Commonsense Norm Bank is a collection of 1.7 million crowdsourced instances of ethical judgments of everyday situations. We hope that it will act as a moral textbook for teaching machines about morality and social norms beyond the system we created.  

Delphi predictions matched human-like judgment 92.8% of the time, performing much better than the state-of-the art language model (called GPT-3)  which only makes correct predictions 60.2% of the time. This lack of moral sense in standard LLMs highlights the need for explicitly teaching AI systems with moral textbooks.
Novelty:
Our innovation, Delphi, represents the first time that modern deep-learning approaches have been applied to the problem of predicting human moral judgment by training a large language model on a corpus of ethical judgments.  Other models have been trained on large-scale datasets, but none of them are able to predict human-like moral judgments as well as Delphi. 

Telling right from wrong of simple actions like “killing” and “stealing” is easy for most large language models. However, moral judgments are defeasible with the availability of additional context. For example, “killing” is wrong. But doing so in self-defense, may become defensible. State-of-the-art AI systems struggle to adapt to changing contexts. One of the most powerful language models, called “GPT-3”, shows a lack of social understanding. For example, “skipping work when you are sick”, is “not good” according to GPT-3.
Impact & Results:
Training: Delphi is trained on the Commonsense Norm Bank, a collection of 1.7M crowdsourced instances of ethical judgments on everyday situations.  

Evaluation: When evaluated on the test situations, Delphi predicts the correct judgment 92.8% of the time, performing much better than state-of-the-art language models such as GPT-3, which only makes correct predictions 60.2% of the time.  

Key Result: This lack of moral sense in state-of-the-art neural language models shows that merely scaling up language models does not endow them with human ethical norms.  
Outlook: Delphi is a first step towards investigating the promises and limits of current methods for teaching machines everyday moral sense. The public demo of Delphi system has received an unexpectedly high volume of public engagement, with over four million queries to date.

Description:
The Equity Atlas and Matrix is an interactive tool that highlights the demographics, disparities and some infrastructure distribution within the city of San Antonio to make data-informed decisions and to address disparities across a variety of indicators that affect communities differently. Furthermore, this is a tool to inform the work of the city and guide the investments to achieve equity goals. 

The aim of the Atlas and Matrix is that all the city Departments ask for technical assistance from the Office of Equity before using these maps and census tract scores to determine how best to apply this tool to your work. This tool are of use together with other equity-related ones. 

Using two demographic variables; race and income, we have designed a simplified Equity Matrix that focuses on breaking points above and below the citywide averages for those demographic variables. More points are assigned to a census block that has a higher than citywide average concentration of people of color and/or people below the average for median family income. The Equity Matrix is demonstrated in the maps as the “Overall Rank” tab.
Novelty: Indicator-based map to address equity goals in the city and inform Departments to better allocate public resources.

Description:
The UN Biodiversity Lab will enable access to state-of-the-art spatial data and dynamic indicators that will generate brand-new insights into conservation and sustainable development. The lab will provide decision-makers with access to over 400 spatial data layers across biodiversity, climate change, and development – helping them to identify new opportunities to act in the best interest of both people and planet

The UNBL 2.0 responds to a known global gap in the types of spatial data and tools, providing an invaluable resource to nations around the world to take transformative action. Users can now access over 400 of the world’s best available global spatial data layers; create secure workspaces to incorporate national data alongside global data; use curated data collections to generate insight for action; and more.

 Without specialized tools or training, decision-makers can leverage the power of spatial data to support priority-setting and the implementation of nature-based solutions. Dynamic metrics and indicators on the state of our planet are also available.
Novelty: Open access to state-of-the-art spatial data and dynamic indicators that will generate brand-new insights into conservation and sustainable development.

Project/Initiative Name: Master on Artificial Intelligence for Public Services (AI4Gov)
Description:
This world-class master’s program prepares future digital transformation leaders for AI in the public sector. Participants learn throug a multidisciplinary approach and systems thinking design how to plan and implement AI in the delivery of public services, so as to improve the provision, effectiveness and value of these public services as well as the public sector’s capacity by means of an adequate management, knowledge and development of AI-related projects and services, focusing on AI ethics. 
 
The AI4GOV master and related project of ecosystem building  aims at training “functional specialists” in applied Artificial Intelligence use cases within the public sector, with an integral curriculum that teaches both the technical, service design and policy aspects of AI, with the ultinate ambition to establish a Center of Excellence with the goal of giving life to a community of practice around the concept of AI4GOV. 

The overall expected outcome culminates in the education of a new generation of AI  specialists with skills for understanding and apply AI into public services. A functional specialist in AI and public services will not only understand the foundations and state of the art of AI technologies, but also the full cycle for the development and delivery of public services that make use of AI technologies, including design, development, delivery and evaluation. 

The core objectives of the master program is to develop skills in the following areas: (i) the management of AI-based public services, (ii) the adoption of AI in public administration’s operations, and (iii) the governance of AI in the public sector and for the delivery of public services to society. Furthermore, an important feature of the programme is the project-based educational approach, which is implemented through the following learning objectives: 1) Understand the potential of data, 2) Understand what AI can do, 3) Propose practical AI projects, 4) Be able to commission/buy AI-based systems, 5) Make informed judgements on AI. 

The master programme is one of the pillar of a broader project which aims not only at designing, piloting and delivery a world-class international master rooted in blended learning and project-based work, but also to develop an ecosystem on AI in public services across the EU (and beyond) and to establish an EU Centre of Excellence on AI for the public sector recognized worldwide.
Novelty:
First Master program that integrates AI and service design applied to the public sector. It has a twofold innovation potential:
- On the one side, the educational programme is innovative as it aims at promoting an interdisciplinary approach, from the side of the participants that are professionals (executive and policy/decision makers) from public and private sector, with diverse background. Students in fact  come from various disciplines (not only Computer or Data Science) aiming to become functional specialists for the implementation of AI-based services in public institutions, and creating as such a dynamic community of practice through peer-learning, knowledge exchange and GovTech Contests and Hackatons. 
- On the other side, the programme itself is a truly international partnership of recognised universities, industry, National and  International Organisations to partner in building an ecosystem and a Centre of Excellence on AI4GOV initiated by the EU, involving many organisations worldwide, from the OECD to the UN and The World Bank.
Impact & Results:
The first edition of the master was launched in October 2021, with a Pilot Edition of 40 carefully selected high-level participants as alumni, very strong gender balance (50/50) and a highly diverse educational and professional background, with two spearheading profiles, social sciences and engineering and technology. 

The pilot edition of the AI4Gov's master has been highlighted by the JRC as an EU-wide excellence in its field in its latest report, "Al Watch - Road to the Adoption of Artificial Intelligence by the Public Sector" recommending AI4Gov master as a spearheading action to create an EU-wide ecosystem for AI capacity-building in the public sector. 

AI4Gov enjoys a strong social media presence, with over 300,000 people reached through its facebook site, more than 5.000 profile visits in Linkedin and a curated list of followers in Twitter and it has been invited to present its innovative design in many high-level national, EU-wide and international research and policy forums.

Description:
The Environment Agency has a responsibility to protect communities from flood and coastal risks. In the past, the agency has struggled to scale their public engagement and reach their diverse audiences, while also retaining a local relevance. Hello Lamp Post was brought on to provide an interactive, live 24/7 conversational channel to educate and inform the public on flood safety, in high-risk locations around the South West of England and Newcastle.

Hello Lamp Post has ‘brought to life’ flood gates, tidal defences and other infrastructure across the country. By making these flood objects and spaces interactive, the Environment Agency is able to educate residents on how the defences work, gather insights and invite residents to sign up for flood alerts/ prepare for flooding - all by scanning a QR code and interacting via their mobile phone. Residents can engage directly in a two-way text message conversation, which is then anonymously shared back with the Environment Agency.

The approach is simple: 1) signs next to objects catch people's attention, 2) to start a conversation with a Bus Stop the user texts "Hello Bus Stop #123" on SMS, WhatsApp or Facebook Messenger, 3) citizens share their views and learn about key topics in their city, 4) all street furniture has a physical reference code already assigned to it. Citizens are encouraged to awaken any other object.

Hello Lamp Post’s have been designed for forward thinking urban planners, governments and construction companies. It aims to bring cities and their citizens closer together. Hello Lamp Post has been deployed in over 25 cities around the world such as Vancouver, Singapore, London, Malmo, Summerside, Belfast, Mesa and Sydney. The signs invite people to playfully interact with their built environment (i.e. lighting poles or phone cabins), whilst collecting data and insights to better inform the development and planning of cities of the future.
Novelty: City infrastructure "furniture" labelled so that citizens can interact with them, provide direct feedback and get informed. Hello Lamp Post is the first-ever on street engagement platform that can facilitate active, two way conversations between people and objects. Using artificial intelligence.
Impact & Results:
Engage with new and existing partners to compliment the Environment Agency’s messaging and to support their place based approach:
- Engaging with other organisations like Newcastle University, Newcastle City Council and South West Water
- Increased awareness for behaviour change campaigns:
Net new data relating to the Environment Agency’s objectives, with aim to include more sentiment/ opinion questions moving forward
- Cost saving across engagement - equivalent reach to 40 ‘traditional’ events (so far)
- Carbon saving - 4.93 tonnes (and rising)
- Outputs informing future comms/campaigns
- The current traction is: 1,848/year User Messages

Project/Initiative Name: Metaculus - forecasting technology platform for predictions
Description:
Metaculus is a forecasting technology platform that optimally aggregates quantitative predictions of future events. Based on crowdsourcing big amounts of feedback, they aggregate people's predictions of the probability of future events from a specific question. The main motivation is that the aggregation of the predictions of many forecasters is more accurate than that of (almost) any individual - this is collective intelligence. 

Collective intelligence can help solve complex problems. The method uses simulations, machine learning, and data science approaches to both traditional and complex systems for modeling, testing, understanding, and determining the right interventions in the real world.

Metaculus main goal is to build tools for collaborative systems thinking, fueled by an open and accountable ethos and a collaborative forecasting community. So far there are thousands of questions (i.e., biology, housing economics, war in Ukraine) grouped under four main categories: 1) feeding humanity, 2) healthy communities, 3) flourishing futures, 4) trustworthy news.
Novelty: Quantitative aggregation of collective intelligence predictions.

Description:
This is a data-based and design redefinition of what an atlas can be. Transforming enormous data sets into rich maps and cutting-edge vizualisations, the Atlas uncover patterns to reflect from the past and highlighting today's complexity. For example, the publication explores happiness and anxiety levels around the globe; it traces the undersea cables and cell towers that connect communications worldwide; it examines impacts of geopolitics.

The authors explain how information from different sources, places and time scales can be brought together through maps and graphics to enable us to make sense of the world, exploring patterns and concepts and their related opportunities and threats. The text provides ideas to support your thinking, but then it is over to you. What can you see? What can you learn? What will you do with this information?
Novelty: Graphics from a new book show causes and consequences that are hard to detect with the naked eye.

Project/Initiative Name: Crowdsorsa - collecting infrastructure data on your bike
Description:
Crowdsorsa is a mobile App that collects data on the infrastructure sector (public infrastructure, roads, energy and water infrastructure) by using gamification approaches. Through the App Inventories and condition surveys of any kind of physical assets can now be carried out in only a matter of hours. Moreover, citizens can earn money by participating in the data collection missions co-organized by Crowdsorsa and its contracting partners (i.e. the city of Helsinki). 

The game is played by collecting and placing virtual objects on the map while taking gps-tagged photos or videos with your smartphone. Supplied with AI and asset management analysis, it is expected that participants (i.e. citizens) go around their cities or around a specific pre-determined area, map them according to a set of rules, take pictures/photos and upload them to then be evaluated by the AI and asset management platform.

It follows an 8-step approach: 
1.  Target/goal definition (could be a City or a private institution)
2. Crowdsorsa creates a mission in a mobile game format
3. Crowdsorsa take care of marketing and user acquisition
4. The mission starts, and users begin collecting data
5. Crowdsorsa quality checks the uploaded photos/videos
6. The users get paid for all approved uploads
7. The data is analyzed by artificial intelligence
8. The main stakeholders benefits from high-quality crowdsourced data

With the Crowdsorsa mobile game, the citizens earn money and compete against each other by taking gpstagged photos of specific assets around the city. The photo's data can be analyzed either manually or by artificial intelligence, based on a pre-determined criteria. The results are presented to  on an intuitive map interface or exported to the system of the stakeholders choice.
Novelty:
Crowdsorsa is innovative because:
1) The innovation engages people in a completely new manner through gamification. Similar applications are based on points, but this is the only application that rewards the participants by paying money.
2) It has been proven to attract strong participation and reduce the time and cost of data collection dramatically compared to traditional methods. The innovation has so far been used in around 50 cycle lane and car lane condition surveys in Finland, Sweden, UK, and Estonia.
3) The main client for the innovation is the public sector and it is demonstrably a powerful tool to facilitate the coordination and incentivizing of people's participation in looking after their own built environment. This is proven by the fact that up to 7,000 people have registered for the application so far and the most popular surveys have attracted hundreds of participants.
Impact & Results:
The method has been used to survey over 7,000 km of roads and cycle lanes in 50 projects. The survey time to cover this amount has been altogether roughly 10 days, which is revolutionary compared to what the road and cycle lane condition surveys typically last. In addition the method was used to create an inventory of manhole covers in just 24 hours in the third largest city in Finland, which is a task that would have typically taken years.
The results have been directly observable by the participants as well as the people in charge of Crowdsorsa's project management. There has been approximately 100 stories in the media regarding the innovation in Finland, Sweden, and UK.
In the future Crowdsorsa is expected to expand to further use cases and make a big positive impact in many different sectors in addition to the infrastructure sector.

Organisation: Deliberative Democracy Lab and Crowdsourced Democracy Team (Standford University)
Description:
The Online Deliberation Platform is a video discussion platform designed to facilitate a structured and equitable conversation with better opportunity for participants to speak up. 

The platform is designed based on the Deliberative Polling methodology to massively scale deliberation to allow unlimited number of participants to deliberate in small groups together simultaneously. Among its many features is an automated moderator that allows participants to form speaking queues, discuss in small groups with timed agendas, and allow for equitable participation. 

The platform is easy to use without any downloads, includes abuse prevention, and real-time analytics. The platform has been used in several languages and for national Deliberative Polling events in Chile, Canada, and the United States. One of its key features is the incorporation of real-time analytics, including live transcription, speaking time distribution, and audio recording. 

Because of its dynamics, the platform enables more equitable participation from all participants, and will nudge participants that have not spoken much. When the system detects toxic behavior, or when a participant is reported, the automated moderator asks the group to validate this and takes action accordingly.
Novelty: A video discussion platform designed to facilitate and scale deliberation under a structured and equitable conversation digital format. It is unique because it is an AI-assisted platform that provides high quality and consistent deliberative experiences to participants at scale.
Impact & Results:
The platform is under active development, and has recently been successfully deployed in Canada, Chile, Hong Kong, Japan, and at Stanford with 15 parallel groups each. It has supported deliberations with up to 1250 unique participants. So far, it has hosted over 1450 hours of group discussion with 7000 unique participants. 

The Deliberative Polling projects have seen decrease in political and affective polarization, as evidenced in the recently published American Political Science Review article, from Fishkin et al. There is also forthcoming papers targeting 18-29 age cohort, with noting moderation even among like-minded participants. Participants after deliberation have desires for more engagement and sustained participation, and request to stay in contact with groups and exchange of social media handles and emails.

Description:
The S.E.T. project is the continuation and upgrade of the Transition Experiments Open Online Expert Tool (TEOOET), which was developed under the City Liveability by Redesign (CLEAR) EIT Urban Mobility project, offering a free, open-access and worldwide street experiments collection. 
​
The S.E.T. platform offers a collection of Resources for implementing and supporting street experiments based on novel approaches evidence and technology-based. As well as a special section of COVID-19 devoted to the best practices of street experiments that offered solutions and strategies for cities to respond to the current pandemic and a SET Guidelines Kit that provides insights and considerations on creating impactful street experiments with long-term effects.

It aims to act to accelerate positive change on mobility to make urban spaces more liveable.
Novelty: The S.E.T. platform offers a collection of Resources for implementing and supporting street experiments based on novel approaches evidence and technology-based.

Project/Initiative Name: The AI and Digital Transformation Competencies for Civil Servants framework
Description:
The framework aims to strengthen the abilities of civil servants to effectively use digital tools, develop and implement digital transformation projects, and address complex governance challenges. All with a particular focus on emerging technologies and AI. 

Under three core concepts of: 
1. Promoting trustworthy, inclusive and human rights-centric implementation of AI technology.
2. Enhancing the capabilities of civil servants to engage in national digital transformations
3. Building a knowledge platform for future capacity-strengthening efforts by the Broadband Commission, including localized initiatives

The initiative seeks to challenge the cultural and organizational, as well as the data and infrastructure barriers currently present in public sector institutions. Moreover, with a clear focus on upgrading the human resource capacity, it provides AI and digital transformation-related competencies to bridge the current gaps of knowledge.
Novelty: Standardized framework for enabling AI and digital transformation competencies for civil servants.

Project/Initiative Name: The Software Alliance Framework to Build Trust in AI
Description:
The Framework is a playbook organizations can use to enhance trust in their AI systems through risk management processes that promote fairness, transparency, and accountability. It is a first-of-its-kind framework for AI bias risk management to perform impact assessments to identify and mitigate risks of bias that may emerge throughout an AI system’s lifecycle.

The framework targets, specifically, AI systems that systematically and unjustifiably yield less favorable, unfair, or harmful outcomes to members of specific demographic groups. The framework addresses: 1) bias and fairness as contextual events worth to evaluate iteratively, 2) efforts to mitigate bias as trade-offs in a context-appropriate manner, 3) post-deployment bias - even if the system has been thoroughly evaluated prior to deployment, it may produce biased results if it is misused or deployed in a setting in which the demographic distribution differs from the composition of its training and testing data.
Novelty: First-of-its-kind framework for AI bias risk management to perform impact assessments to identify and mitigate risks of bias that may emerge throughout an AI system’s lifecycle.
Impact & Results: They are currently gathering user cases to call for a potential federal legislation that incorporates such topics.

Project/Initiative Name: PLACE - Hyperlocal Mapping for Positive Change
Description:
PLACE solves for inefficiencies of modern-day mapping by creating a trusted intermediary between the public and private good providers through a membership model that creates a data club for good. They deliver hyperlocal and detailed optical imagery of the earth’s surface. 

The non-profit aims that mapping images and data would become an integral part of modern digital ecosystems, thus critical to unlocking economic, social and environmental opportunities and sustainability. Under its umbrella, they make the provision of such evidence free of cost for cities. It collects and stewards two types of ultra-high resolution optical imagery data utilizing open-source, open component high precision aerial mapping drones and commercial street camera systems. They collect:
- 5cm ground sample distance (GSD) resolution, 24-bit RGB, geo-referenced optical aerial imagery (Geo Tiff) positionally accurate to +/- 20 cm (absolute)
- High resolution, 24-bit RGB, geo-referenced street imagery acquired using mobile mapping systems that produce 360-degree street images (Geo Tiff)

They operate the solution under five principles: 1) governing data as a commons - this is that imagery data is non-excludable and non-rivalrous asset, so is publicly available, 2) avoiding a "tragedy of the commons" to ensure data sustainability, 3) tackling data asymmetries and re-distribution of responsibilities - stakeholders and users have asymmetry of access to information, 4) independence and strong engagement - building a board of trustees to make sure the terms in which evidence is provided and utilized, 5) inclusion and protection - preventing surveillance or bias against the community and the most vulnerables.
Novelty: The platform provides a new Governance approach for accessing ultra-high resolution optical imagery mapping data.
Impact & Results:
Big Tech and technology companies are part of its board of trustees (Microsoft, Mozilla, Omidyar Network). They have mapped heat islands across the global south. 
It furthers develop the concept of "the internet of places" to support smart cities initiatives.

Project/Initiative Name: Participatory Technology Assessment - ECAST pTA method
Organisation: Arizona State University - Consortium for Science Policy & Outcomes
Description:
Participatory technology assessment (pTA) is a tool for bringing public perspectives to bear on critical science and technology decisions. It refers to a class of methods to integrate new voices into science policy discussions. This class of methods encompasses various forms of public deliberation including citizens’ assemblies, citizens’ juries, and consensus conferences. 

ASU Consortium for Science, Policy & Outcomes brings together academic research, informal science education, citizen science programs, and non-partisan policy analysis to engage citizens. The current ECAST pTA method includes three participatory phases: 1) Problem Framing; 2) ECAST Citizen Deliberation; and 3) Results and Integration. Proving adaptable and replicable, the method has generated outputs for decision-making on a variety of science and technology issues and at governance scales ranging from the local to the national and international. ECAST's distributed network model has also promoted independence, continuity, and sustainability through changing sociopolitical contexts.
Novelty: This is a method to support democratic science policy decision-making in different technical, social, and political contexts.
Impact & Results:
School for the Future of Innovation in Society: https://sfis.asu.edu/ 
President's Biden Administration is planning to create a government entity tasked with supporting federal government deliberations through pTA. This entity can be a laboratory for bringing inclusivity, deliberation, and transparency into decisionmaking.

Project/Initiative Name: Establishment of the office of consumer data protection and imposes a tax on data controllers and data processors
Description:
The Bill establishes the office of consumer data protection for the purpose of properly safeguarding personal data; imposes a tax on data controllers and data processors required to register with such office. Examples of this free labor include filling out search engine requests, sharing social media updates, filling out CAPTCHA requests and visiting different websites.

Specifically, the bill aims to enact a 2% tax on annual receipts earned off New York residents’ data. This tax and other rules and regulations aimed at safeguarding citizens’ data will be enforced by a newly created Office of Consumer Data Protection outlined in the bill.

The office would require all data controllers and processors to register annually in order to meet state compliance requirements. Failure to do so, the bill states, would result in fines. As for the tax, all funds will be put toward improving education and closing the digital divide.

The revenue from the tax will be put towards digital literacy, workforce redevelopment, STEAM education (science, technology, engineering, arts and mathematics), K-12 education, workforce reskilling and retraining.
Novelty: This is the first to propose a data privacy bill that would implement a tax on big tech companies that benefit from the sale of New Yorkers’ consumer data.

Project/Initiative Name: Locus Charter - Improve standards of practice related to the use of location data
Description:
The Locus Charter is a proposed set of common international principles to support ethical and responsible practice when using location data. It specifically proposes that wider, shared understanding of risks and solutions relating to uses of location data can improve standards of practice, and help protect individuals and the public interest.

Under the understanding that risks and solutions relating to the uses of location data can improve standards of practice, and help protect individuals and the public interest, the charter expect to benefit communities by using geospatial technologies. In that sense, common international principles could help users of geospatial data make more informed decisions, and provide the basis for communication with people affected by those decisions.
Novelty: Targeted data ethics principles and guidelines can improve understanding of rights, obligations and responsibilities, and support better decisions. This is a step ahead on geospatial ethics and imagery (self) regulation.
Impact & Results: The Charter is written for individuals and organizations who use location data or have responsibility for activities that create, collect, analyze and store location data.

Project/Initiative Name: AHI KAA Rangers - an environmental science and indigenous knowledge game
Description:
Ahi Kaa Rangers is a mobile app game that combines environmental science and indigenous knowledge. As an Ahi Kaa Ranger a plater needs to take care of a living world, just like the Kaitaiki (Guardian) of Aotearoa (New Zealand) care for Papatūānuku (mother earth]).
​
A player cares for the living world by earning resources in the game. This can be done by moving and breeding creatures in the world or by helping other players. But to really get ahead and earn bonuses, a player should complete a real-life citizen science mission. By playing the Ahi Kaa Rangers game and completing citizen science missions, each player is contributing to real world climate change research that is taking place across different parts of the world. 

There are three missions (worlds): 1) healthy monarchs, 2) marine meter squared, 3) rate NZ's packaging that will provide data about the decision making, rationale and ideas from the players to solve the aforesaid mitions.
Novelty: ARA's unique approach to bringing Indigenous history to life, through gamified storytelling enables people to connect with historical places and learn the rich (often forgotten) cultural stories of a region.
Impact & Results: Gamification, crowdsourced citizens participation and storytelling as part of a game to gather evidence that will help solving complex problems.

Description:
Sonar Global (now ISIDORe) aims to build a sustainable international social sciences network to engage the active participation of the social sciences in preparedness and response to infectious threats, including COVID-19 and Ebola. This responds to the demand to develop and coordinate social science expertise to maximize its public health impact.

The project aims to identify and process lasting responses to the needs of populations that are most vulnerable to global health hazards. In that sense, the main goal is to create a critical mass of social scientists with whom they can share experiences, tools, models and curricula. 

The platform offers transnational access to social science services in epidemiology ranging from readiness and preparedness, to qualitative surveillance and ex-post evaluations. They also foster closer collaborations with experts from other disciplines.
Novelty:
- Mapped social sciences research for the Ebola response in DRC and neighboring countries. 
- City of Munich support to implement COVID-19 response
Impact & Results:
- >4,3 million in funds to run the pilot phase
- >15 partners involved

Description:
BanTheSacn (Decode Surveillance NYC) is an initiative of Amnesty International that maps/identifies street intersections where CCTV cameras are located to categorize them and provide feedback as of facial recognition/surveillance is not only likely but is happening. The pilot aims to target the risk of wrongful arrest and police violence by misuse of facial recognition technologies. 

The innovation identified >22 thousand public and private cameras across traffic intersections in NYC with the support of >7 thousand digital volunteers who analyzed every intersection using different digital tools (i.e. google maps, special AI-based softwares or taking pictures with their mobile phones). The pilot targeted in particular socioeconomically deprived areas across NY boroughs and four locations where Black Lives Matter (BLM) protests happened. The data analyzed was classified using AI, geography and 3d-based models.

The platform displays a map of NYC where users/citizens are required to insert two address points (starting and end address). It will display how many surveillance private/public cameras the person runs into along a single journey and, therefore, are at risk of stop-and-frisk. By doing this they also evaluate the likelihood of become a target and, therefore, being subject of constant surveillance.
Novelty:
Crowdsourced and technology based initiative to map private/public surveillance CCTV cameras across street intersections in NYC to evaluate the likelihood of stop-and-frisk of a single route. 
Addresses the risk of facial surveillance and profiling.
Impact & Results:
- Analysis of the data shows that New Yorkers living in areas at greater risk of racist stop-and-frisk policing are likely to be more exposed to invasive facial recognition technology.
- In the Bronx, Brooklyn and Queens, the research also showed that the higher the proportion of non-white residents, the higher the concentration of facial recognition compatible CCTV cameras.
- When New Yorkers marched for racial justice during the Black Lives Matter protests of mid-2020, they risked high levels of exposure to facial recognition. A protester walking a sample route from W4 St/Wash Sq Subway station to and from Washington Square Park is, for example, surveilled for approximately 100% of their journey by pan, tilt and zoom cameras operated by the NYPD.

Description:
NeuroFlow's platform streamlines the collection of behavioral health insights at scale and empowers care teams with the decision support they need to avoid costly and dangerous outcomes. The platform creates a scalable feedback loop of integrated care-steps that keeps patients monitored and practitioners informed. Using applied AI tactics, the technology aims to identify the most at-risk mental health users based on red flags in data.

The innovation describes its integrated behavioral health system in three steps:
- Population health: Remotely gather critical behavioral health insights through frequent between-visit digital screening at scale, and offer app-based resources available 24/7.
- Triage: AI-driven clinical decision support empowers care teams with informed next step for each individual, at scale Intuitive care team tools make it easy to prioritize caseloads and monitor individuals’ progress within existing workflows. 
- Open APIs & seamless integrations: to connect to the existing ecosystem through open APIs and SSO so NeuroFlow fits easily into current workflows.
Novelty: Connect the patient to care providers and specialists, empowering them to remotely coordinate and deliver effective mental health care at scale.
Impact & Results:
34% reduction in ED utilization for a leading health system
72% of collaborative care patients experienced clinically significant depression improvement in 6 months
90% of users would recommend NeuroFlow to others

Description:
The 2022 AI Settlement Generation Challenge is the first of its kind as part of the Minecraft's contest since 2018. The task for participants is to build an artificial intelligence that can generate realistic towns or villages in previously unseen locations. Though the techniques of use are "just for fun", competitors could use real-world city planners techniques. 

The challenge also aims to stretch the abilities of the machines in use by writing an algorithm that can create a settlement for a given, unknown Minecraft map. The algorithm has to be adaptive towards the map, creates a settlement that satisfies a range of functional requirements - but also looks good and evokes an interesting narrative. In this way, it can rival the state of the art of what humans can produce in real life.
Novelty: Incorporation of AI as part of the challenge and the concept of "unseen locations" that "rival the state of the art" of what humans can produce in real life.
Impact & Results: Participants can not only use algorithms but incorporate real-life constructions techniques/concepts, which has potential for advancing in the infrastructure and cities development.

Description:
ISeeChange is a platform to share experiences and collect data to investigate how people's perceptions about the environment affect communities and how much they can change in relation to it. The initiative aims to offer communities a way to build and celebrate relationships with nature, their cities, the people who study and manage them, among others. They build the link between these relationships. 

It is a collaborative platform that collects information about the users feelings and perceptions about the weather or any other nature and climate-related circumstance. The software works in a three-fold way: 1) users download the app and watch the weather & climate nearby, 2) use the app to tell how it's affecting themselves and/or the community, 3) the software creates a community record. These results are then processed and the information delivered to the people and local authorities to start co-creating solutions to change a situation of vulnerability or uncertainty, among others.
Novelty: Explorative software about the feelings and the relationship of people with their environment. This is a crowdsourced initiative to understand such relationships and improve policymaking and people's behaviors towards the environment.
Impact & Results: Example of New Orleans: Collectively, the stories on ISeeChange in 2021 also helped contribute to community solutions. In New Orleans, flooding posts across 29 flood events helped reallocate $4.8M to low income neighborhoods in Gentilly.

Description:
EIR is a project focused on two specific rural areas located in distant points of the globe, but united to be part of this ongoing process of territorial transformation. The region of Aomori, in Japan, and the area of ​​Fortore beneventano, in Southern Italy, thus become places of experimentation through the languages ​​of contemporary art, sound practices, new technologies, to open up the space for critical reflection on changes in rurality on a local and global scale. 

EIR aims specifically at investigating invisible forces that operate in impacting and transforming ecosystems and communities, such as energy: earth sounds, earth signals, human and non-human corporal energies. In that sense, listening to practices, technocultures become critical and analytical devices with respect to this transformation process that offers unexpected relief to rural areas.

This project includes a series of cultural actions on both territories: artistic micro-residences, workshops, meetings, publications, also experimenting hybrid formats between virtual and in presence practices, implementing an emergency strategy due to the limitations caused by the pandemic
Novelty: Combination of art, sound practices, new technologies, to open up the space for critical reflection on changes in rurality on a local and global scale.

Description:
The Federal Agency for Disruptive Innovation SPRIND is an agency that creates spaces for innovators, where they can take risks and think radically different. It is a landmark experiment for Germany. Through it, Germany will provide support for individuals and organisations with original ideas. Using the concept of innovation sandbox, SPRIND is looking for answers to the social, ecological and economic challenges of our time.

The agency's goal is to create new disruptive innovations from Germany. That means products, services and systems that make all the lives noticeably and sustainably better. It creates spaces in which people can take risks and think radically differently. The support model is comprehensive. SPRIND finance, help put together teams and link them with the right networks from science, business and politics. They only support innovators and innovations that are based on basic humanistic values. They allocate funds of between 4-15 million EUR per year at a share of 500,000 to 3 million EUR per winning team.
Novelty: SPRIND is a non-traditional funding agency that uses concepts of startups and innovation sandboxes to identify entrepreneurs, incubate ideas and escalate them.
Impact & Results: In 2021, SPRIND received 375 applications. About 7% of the projects were found to have potential for disruptive innovation. After further analysis about 3.5% of the projects were pursued further.

Description:
Bias Buccaneers is the first non-profit algorithmic bias bounty organization. We organize competitions to engage a broad global community in identifying and fixing ethical problems in the algorithms all companies use. Pioneered by Twitter and continued by Stanford HAI and Anthropic (among others), Bounties allow a democratized and crowdsourced method of identifying and mitigating algorithmic biases, using the wisdom of many to address a near-infinite array of problems.

Bug bounties are a standard practice in cybersecurity that has yet to find footing in the algorithmic bias community. Bias Buccaneers is the first nonprofit intended to create ongoing bounties, collaborate with technology companies, and pave the way for transparent and reproducible evaluations of Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems. The initiative aspires that bias bounties should be a part of any gold-star algorithmic ethics program. 

Its model follows a two-step approach: 
- First: Create engaging, and transparent methods of evaluating and addressing algorithmic bias. They operationalize these methods through bug bounties focusing on specific datasets, algorithms, and applications. 
- Second: Create standards that are crowd-tested and approved, and therefore, useful, making it easy and fun for AI engineers and data scientists to adopt and use algorithmic bias, fairness, and explainability standards. 

The long term vision is to create worldwide expertise, standards, and verifiable talent in a nascent, but rapidly growing, field. Armed with the guidance and frameworks developed by the partner AVID, the "bias hackers" (or "Crew") will be well-equipped to tackle future challenges in this area -- be it as part of the broader AI ethics community or as leaders and contributors in organizational efforts.
Novelty: The initiative allows a democratized and crowdsourced method of identifying and mitigating algorithmic biases, using the wisdom of many to address a near-infinite array of problems.
Impact & Results: Bias Buccaneers launched their first Bias Bounty at CAMLIS, a global AI Security conference, on October 20th, 2022. It will conclude one month later. To date, the company received great enthusiasm from corporations, but no empirical results can be found yet.

Description:
Global Plastic Watch (GPW) is a digital platform that maps the world’s plastic pollution in near real-time using a unique combination of satellite imagery and artificial intelligence. Using freely available data from the European Space Agency, GPW detects plastic waste sites on land and monitors them over time.

GPW can identify waste sites to enable site clean-ups and better enforcement of laws against dumping, provide risk indicators for existing plastic waste sites such as proximity to water or communities, highlight priority areas for investment in waste and recycling infrastructure, and demonstrate visible progress towards waste management targets. GPW detects the locations of plastic waste dumpsites and aggregation sites on land. GPW can also monitor and report on the trends in the size of the plastic waste sites.

This is a tool that governments, funding agencies, intergovernmental organisations, NGOs, scientific researchers and the general public can use to make evidence-based decisions on how to manage and mitigate the world's plastic pollution problem.
Novelty: Digital platform that maps the world’s plastic pollution in near real-time using a unique combination of satellite imagery and artificial intelligence.
Impact & Results: It has mapped wasteland sites in >40 countries (i.e. they have mapped 23 sites in Peru, and 71 in the USA - mainly in the South East coast and gulf area.

Project/Initiative Name: Government measure for a Municipal Algorithms and Data Strategy for an Ethical Promotion of AI
Description:
The measure was approved on 26 June 2020, expressing support for an ethical and trustworthy municipal technical model. This technological humanist-based model aims to facilitate scientific advances, preserve Barcelona's technological leadership and guarantee new digital rights for citizens and their participation in the monitoring of the use that is made of technologies in the public sector. 

This municipal algorithm and data strategy for an ethical promotion of AI represents the first stage to developing the cornerstones for applying emerging technologies, especially AI, in Barcelona City Council, helping to safeguard citizens’ rights in the face of the automation of the decisions that affect them.

Combining principles of good data management and AI ought to enable institutions to reach citizens in a more personalised, direct way, based on more accurate knowledge. This is why the Administration will be ready to provide public services that are more sensitive towards social diversity and, at the same time, more capable of guaranteeing their universality. It is in this context that the city of Barcelona proposes to develop an AI and emerging technologies approach under three main essentials:
- Maintaining and increasing democratic monitoring of AI by public institutions and citizens.
- Ensuring through transparency and auditability that the algorithmic models and the databases they are applied from follow human-rights and public-interest criteria.
- Clarifying the liability regime for any harm or loss that may arise from the creation and use of AI-based solutions not just by the Administration but also by companies and developers.
Novelty: Seconding UK and the Netherlands, Barcelona is the first city developing a comprehensive citizens-centered regulatory framework that targets AI and emerging technologies.
Impact & Results: Not possible to identify impacts at the moment.

Organisation: Global Parnetship for Sustainable Development Data
Description:
The Data Values Manifiesto is part of a global network using data to achieve the Sustainable Development Goals demanding a fair data future. They aim to challenge power structures in data to ensure that the benefits from its collection and use are accesible for everyone. The #DataValues Manifesto calls for change in how we design, collect, fund, manage, and use data.

The Manifiesto seeks to unlock data for a better, greener, safer future which, at the same time, targets investments where they matter most and help to navigate the complex risks and choices to achieve the SDGs. The network of coders and policymakers represent >60 countries worldwide, as well as civil society organizations. 

The initiative is sustained on five main pillars: 
1) Support people to share how they are represented in data. 
2) Invest in public participation for accountability. 
3) Democratize data skills for greater equality. 
4) Create cultures of transparency, data sharing, and use. 
5) Fund open and responsive data systems so that all people share in te benefits of data.
Novelty: Manifiesto to support SDG achievement through data and to attract data-driven investments where they matter the most.
Impact & Results: https://www.data4sdgs.org/index.php/about/our-impact

Description:
The Million Neighborhoods initiative is a project that displays a series of maps of a selected group of cities from the global south that are so densely populated that the movement of people and the construction of vital infrastructure becomes quite difficult. 

This map highlights neighborhoods with limited access to streets. Red areas show buildings that are very hard to access directly from the street, while blue areas display buildings with better street access. The maps are  crowdsourced from OpenStreetMap and make it possible to create new models of urban planning that are people-centric, built from local knowledge and enhanced with technology. In the hands of communities and local governments this can become a powerful resource to support decision-making and action.

Streets connect each home or place of work to basic services. Without street access there is often no sanitation or clean water. There are also no addresses or routes for emergency responders, trash collectors, and buses. Around the world, over a million neighborhoods lack adequate access to such services. The initiative mapped them, block by block to display the relationship between connectivity and the access to certain services and infrastructure.
Novelty: To create the Million Neighborhoods Map, researches applied algorithms to an open-source GIS databasewhich is unique because it is available free to anyone and allows volunteers and local residents to contribute data about the location of throughways, buildings and structures — even in informal settlements, which often lack consistent, publicly available data: https://github.com/mansueto-institute/prclz
Impact & Results: The goal is to provide municipal leaders and community residents with a tool to help inform and prioritize infrastructure projects in under-serviced neighborhoods, including informal urban settlements that are sometimes known as “slums.”

Description:
Update Germany (UpdateDeutschland) is all about Open Social Innovation - a methodology that was tested with the support of the German government at the #WirVsVirus Hackathon and its implementation programme. Out of around 1,500 solutions, 150 were supported in further development, piloting and broad implementation. The initiative helped in the Corona crisis and are already having a sustainable impact today. Building on this, UpdateDeutschland is creating a nationwide future laboratory together with citizens and partners from all federal levels: Hundreds of solutions are being tested in parallel and implemented with the help of cooperations. UpdateDeutschland is an experiment in which the German Government learns from mistakes.

The platform questions the following: Whether loneliness, lack of digitalisation or social inequality: in the Corona pandemic, each individual experienced the major challenges of our society in everyday life. How do we manage to emerge stronger from the crisis instead of falling back into the old normality? 

The initiative aims to gather a plethora of diverse participants, from citizens and mayors, administrators and civil society, activists and founders, digital developers and analogue tinkerers, decision-makers from the federal, state and local governments, young and old. The goal is to identify the problems and then find new solutions or further develop existing approaches. Through rapid testing of good ideas the German government will support their scalability and incorporation as part of the solution portfolio of the public sector.
Novelty: First government's Open Social Innovation Lab/platform to take over and upscale the solutions provided in the #WirVSVirus hackathon. In addition it will co-create and pilot solutions for a post-pandemic Germany.
Impact & Results: Not yet available.

Organisation: Massachussets Institute of Technology - Sensible City Lab
Description:
ProxiMATE provides evidence that the loss of workplace proximity during the lockdown prevented MIT researchers from building new cross-group connections that stimulate exchange. Based on Mark's Granovetter's (1973) theory of weak/strong ties to transmit novel information in networks, the project studied the email exchange network at MIT before and after the university implemented a mandatory lockdown due to COVID-19

The platform analyzed the communication habits of 2834 faculty and postdoctoral researchers across over 100 university units to understand how the formation of weak ties is facilitated by physical proximity. It estimated that university researchers lost more than 4800 weak ties due to remote work. The most significant decline in weak ties is observed among people with offices located close to one another – ties between distant parts of the social system that enable the flow of novel information. Furthermore, the study found that the re-introduction of partial co-location through a hybrid work mode led to a partial regeneration of weak ties. 

The results indicate that the likelihood of establishing a connection is a function of proximity.
Novelty: Pilot to prove the theory of weak/strong ties that allow and prevents people from building connections that stimulate knowledge and overall human exchange.
Impact & Results: Results indicate that employees who are not colocated are less likely to form ties, weakening the spread of information in the workplace. Such findings could contribute to a better understanding of the spatio-temporal dynamics of human communication networks, and help organizations that are moving towards the implementation of hybrid work policies evaluate the minimum amount of in-person interaction necessary for a productive work environment.

Organisation: United States Department of Health & Human Services - Coforma
Description:
The HHS Health+ combined program uses human-centered design to co-create patient-centered solutions with patients impacted by the most pressing healthcare challenges. The goal is to improve U.S. government programs to better support and serve those affected. This Health+ model works best when applied to complex, multi-systemic, multi-disciplinary challenges like long COVID in collaboration with patients, caregivers, practitioners, and diverse partner communities.

The Health+ Long COVID work aims to improve healthcare provision and government services using human-centered innovation, while simultaneously advancing interagency efforts to address the longer-term effects of COVID-⁠19 including Long COVID. The Health+ process involves those affected by Long COVID working side-by-side with HHS.

The cycle culminates in a “Healthathon,” a health-focused innovation sprint with hackathon roots to rapidly prototype and deliver solutions with the community. Healthathons bring together a diverse community of problem solvers including those affected by Long COVID, whether current or prior patients, caregivers, healthcare personnel, policy experts, technologists, designers, and others to collaborate within specific focus areas. The goal is to transform insights from patients, so their understanding drives the development of new tools, action, and delivery with real-world impact that supports the Long COVID community.
Novelty: The HHS Health+ combined program uses human-centered design to co-create patient-centered solutions with patients impacted by the most pressing healthcare challenges such as long COVID.
Impact & Results: The Health+ Long COVID Report will be publicly available on this page in late 2022. It will include Health+ Long COVID archetypes, journey maps, and opportunity areas for action. This report will help government to focus attention and interventions in areas that matter the most to the community, as articulated by patients and those with first-hand, lived experience with Long COVID.

Project/Initiative Name: Local Indicators of Climate Change Impacts
Description:
The LICCI project aims to bring insights from local knowledge systems to climate research by 1) providing data on local perceptions of climate change impacts on climatic (e.g. rainfall patterns change), physical (e.g., shrinking glaciers), biological (e.g., phenological changes), and socioeconomic systems (e.g., crop failure due to rainfall patterns change), and 2) testing hypotheses on the global spatial, socioeconomic and demographic distribution of local climate change impacts indicators.

Through cutting-edge scientific methods, the initiative strives to deepen the understandings of perceived climate change impacts, and endeavor to bring indigenous  knowledge into policy-making processes and influence international climate change negotiations. For such purpose, they have deployed >40 partners and collaborators on the field around the world to explore local indicators of climate change impacts by indigenous people and local communities. Furthermore, they are building a web-based platform to receive crowdsourced feedback on the indicators.

There is evidence that climate change influences many physical and biological systems. Much of this evidence comes from natural sciences and mostly relies on large-scale records of weather and climate and the use of modelling techniques to describe climate change in data deficient regions. However, these data is too coarse, for which local climate change impacts, particularly in socioeconomic systems, remain poorly understood. Social scientists argue that this is so, not only because global models are too coarse to capture the specificities of local climate change impacts, but also because climate change impacts largely depend on people´s local conditions, which can not be captured by global models.

As world coverage of weather stations leaves large areas of the world with poor, incomplete, or unreliable weather records. A review of the literature on local indicators of climate change impacts suggest that this information is also spatially biased.
