Novelty: First of its kind indicators of climate change impacts derived from local and indigenous communities.
Impact & Results:
Map of site impacts: https://opentek.eu/licci 
- The inventory of local indicators of climate change impacts collected through this project will improve localized responses of the physical and biological systems to climate change.
- Data on perceptions of climate change impacts will improve the understanding of the perceived impacts of climate change on local livelihoods. This will guide policies attempting to mitigate its immediate impacts.
- The involvement of local communities and lay citizens in data collection holds the potential to increase local agency for adaptation.

Description:
The Narrative Observatory aims to solve some of the technical, definitional, and practical challenges bedeviling narrative and cultural strategy. The Narrative Observatory provides narrative and cultural strategists tools, along with industry-grade data infrastructure, to understand audiences relative to their place in culture; to identify, measure, and track narratives within audiences over long time scales and across media platforms and devices; and to surface audience-specific story opportunities and threats.

With funding from Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, the Narrative Observatory’s first iteration has focused on conducting narrative analysis and content testing on narratives of poverty and economic mobility in the U.S. They are analyzing the media behaviors of over 50,000 people and  the content they interact with, across platforms and devices. The platform uses this process as a way to explore the interaction of values-based audiences and the persistent narratives that condition culture.
Novelty: A platform to solve some of the technical, definitional, and practical challenges bedeviling narrative and cultural strategy.
Impact & Results: Documentation about the initiative and other works can be found here: https://medium.com/harmony-labs

Description:
DDP is a project and organization designed to educate and mobilize consumers to collectively exercise their legal data rights. The project seeks to recover the money that big tech companies made off from citizens data (currently Californians). In that sense, DDP is a movement to stand against the unregulated use of people's data. 

The initiative is based on the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) and the California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA), which give consumers a set of data rights: the right to know what data is being collected about them, the right to opt out of the sale or sharing of that data, and the right to demand deletion of their data. These laws also hold corporations accountable when they violate data rights. Moreover, it also restricts the sale of personal information of minors.

Through a Data Right Settlements Tool, it asks every person to sign a written authorization so that DDP act as an authorized agent to exercise  legal rights under the CCPA -- only as it concerns to data rights. DDP will harness that combined power to Opt Out of the sale and sharing of your data and to demand deletion of your data.
Novelty: Collective initiative to recover the money that big tech companies make off from citizens data (currently Californians).
Impact & Results: Several claims can be visualized on the website. For example: Equifax to pay between $380 - $505 million. Facebook paid out $650 million.

Description:
The Disinformation Tracker is an interactive map to track and analyse disinformation laws, policies and patterns of enforcement across Sub-Saharan Africa. The map offers a birds-eye view of trends in state responses to disinformation across the region, as well as in-depth analysis of the state of play in individual countries, using a bespoke framework to assess whether laws, policies and other state responses are human rights-respecting. 

At the time of launch, it covered 31 countries (see below for the full list), with an aim to expand this in the coming months. All data, analysis and insight on the map has been generated by groups and actors based in Africa. 

Lexota explores how laws and government actions against disinformation impact freedom of expression across Sub-Saharan African with this interactive tool. It provides detailed analysis on laws and government actions on disinformation across Sub-Saharan Africa, to inform and strengthen the work of human rights defenders, researchers and policymakers.
Novelty: A map to track and analyse disinformation laws, policies and patterns of enforcement across Sub-Saharan Africa
Impact & Results:
Identified 2 disinformation-specific laws, 85 general speech laws, 4 proposed laws, 69 law enforcement and 12 government pressure. 
https://lexota.org/comparator/?type=action

Description:
The Food Systems Dashboard combines data from multiple sources to give users a complete view of food systems. Users can compare components of food systems across countries and regions. They can also identify and prioritize ways to sustainably improve diets and nutrition in their food systems.

The Dashboard contains over 150 indicators that measure components, drivers, and outcomes of food systems at the country level. Data can be viewed on a map as well as on bar, line, and other chart types. Country Profiles allow users to view a curated set of 41 indicators for each country to gain in-depth insights into the challenges and opportunities facing countries and their food systems. 

In recent years, the public health and nutrition communities have used dashboards to track the progress of health goals and interventions, including the Sustainable Development Goals. To the initiative's knowledge, this is the first dashboard that collects country-level data across all components of the food system.

The dashboard can be used by anyone interested or involved in the food system:
- Policymakers at the country, regional, and global levels
- National statistical agency workers
- Policy analysts in government ministries
- United Nations and non-governmental organization development practitioners
- Civil society workers
- Business leaders and entrepreneurs
- Researchers, academics, and students
Novelty: First dashboard that collects country-level data across all components of the food system.
Impact & Results: Visible through the map's use: https://www.foodsystemsdashboard.org/indicators/percent-of-the-population-who-cannot-afford-a-healthy-diet-at-52-percent-of-income-co-hd-headcount/map

Organisation: Grenoble Alpes University Hospital Center, Bibliovid, Marseille University Hospital Center
Description:
The project Bibliovid was born out the observation that, when confronted to a serious global crisis, like during the COVID-19 pandemics, it was difficult for health professionals to keep in touch with the latest development and knowledge, results and recommendations on how to manage the situation. Moreover, it was particularly complicated to quickly distinct between valid and doubtful information, high- and low-quality data, as these get thrown around into the media maelstrom.

The project goal is to develop tools that could assist domain experts in scientific monitoring task. It possesses two instances: the first one on monitoring and analyzing the most recent literature on COVID-19 through its Bibliographic and Translation Watch Unit, and the second one aimed at providing support for health students and practitioners during the COVID-19 health crisis, through the display of a dashboard that consolidates all the knowledge categorized by strength of evidence: https://bibliovid.org/en. It has classified >600 scientific and evidence-based documents.
Novelty: Tool to assist domain experts in scientific monitoring tasks.
Impact & Results: The project breached the French boundaries, reaching health professionals in many countries with teams from Belgium and Canada joining-in to help with the development and dissemination of this tool.

Description:
The precarious lives mapper is a platform that documents the processes and mechanisms that generate housing precarity in today’s Beirut. The platform seeks to make visible patterns of deprivation, overcrowding, unaffordability, displacement, eviction, and foreclosure that characterize the housing conditions of Beirut’s social majorities. Through data collection, analysis, and visualization, it places these narratives at the disposal of activists, researchers, journalists, and city-dwellers in order to emphasize the urgency of debating, denouncing, and resisting the devastating impacts of neoliberal urban policies and real-estate speculation in today’s cities.  

The platform first introduces the layers of citywide conditions that contribute to urban precarity. Having provided context, this initial analysis is followed by several case studies which zoom in to particular buildings or clusters of buildings to examine the consequences of these forces on the ground. This research relies on intersecting two methodological approaches to the study of urban precarity: 
- The first approach reads urban precarity systematically at the scale of the city by a set of historical or contemporary disruptive interventions in the urban fabric such as –for example- the illegal occupation of land, the temporary establishment of a refugee camp. 
- The second methodological approach relies on the study-up method of understanding the city where investigating cases of precarity through direct fieldwork allowed to unravel the urban processes acting to destabilize the urban fabric, and consequently its resident
Novelty: This is a city-scale reading of urban precarity by utilizing novel technology and data approaches to identify geographic locations where processes of urban precarity have converged due to historical or ongoing development trends.
Impact & Results: The platform has mapped precarious neighborhoods (refugee camps, urban informal settlements, developments of permanent refugee camps, as well as buildings demolished or at risk of such, areas where roads or streets are planned, and buildings affected by the Beirut Port Blast: https://storymaps.arcgis.com/stories/cccb8c8863c945f6a57340dace891808

Description:
The Supply Chain Climate Performance tool seeks to engage suppliers with climate change targets by visualizing their supply chain strategies and set the debate around increased data definitions that are currently missing in the scoping and mapping of value chains. The main goal is to bring private sector suppliers on board to meet their business targets while, at the same time, meet the climate performance by creating a digital twin based on evidence from the industry/sector. 

The initiative states that the food industry is overlooking the majority of its emissions. In that sense, they provide with a novel architecture for the entire supply chain: 
- A seat for increased data quality:The seat for increased data definition is preset in the digital twin.
- Traceable to the deep end: Track the climate performance of your suppliers, their suppliers, their suppliers' suppliers all the way to the farm and every step of the way. 
- Harmonized data at every stage through a CarbonCloud network. 

The initiative supports too several other pilots such as the climate solutions for branding.
Novelty: Digital twin of sector and industry supply chains to incorporate improvements that meet both budget targets and climate performance.

Description:
Codex Virtualis is an artistic research framework oriented towards the generation of an evolving taxonomic collection of hybrid bacterial-AI organisms. It proposes a symbolic formulation of a style transfer machine learning environment as a host, in which to merge bacterial/archaea time-lapse microscopy footage along with multidimensional cellular automata models as endosymbionts, all under the orchestration of an autonomous generative non-adversarial network architecture.

The initiative aims to encounter novel algorithmically-driven aesthetic representations. Moreover, it stands on the idea of cooperation to expand on the concept of intelligence by including machine and non-human agents into its configuration. And intends to articulate new schemes for the social imaginary to picture life outside the planet, and to better appreciate life on earth.

The innovation is planning to create an image database by combining the collected visual data from bacterial colony growth with visual data and mathematical models of unicellular life forms, to pre-train a generative non-adversarial network, through style transfer interpolated from the biotic database and the self-organizing autonomous patterns into a continuous cellular automaton. It seeks to activate a self-generative system, Artificial Intelligence, and algorithmic approximation for generating virtual organism models based on morphology and rule modeling
Novelty: An artistic initiative that explores the taxonomy of hybrid bacterial-AI organisms. It plans to create an image database of bacterial colonies with visual data and mathematical models to evidence the patterns of cellular automation.
Impact & Results: Further explores the intersection of morphology, rule modeling and AI to understand cellular configuration from an artistic point of view.

Organisation: Urban Next, Consejo Superior de los Colegios de Arquitectos de España (CSCAE)
Description:
The Affordable Housing Atlas aims to look into the issue of housing access on a global scale. The approach to the issue is supported by data provided by multiple sources, making it possible to describe the planet on a global scale using the criteria of the Affordable Housing Activation (AHA) scientific committee, which has defined different barriers contributing to housing inaccessibility.

The tool allows a city-by-city analysis of how urbanisation has expanded over the last 50 years, using a map showing all the metropolises on the planet at a resolution of 250 metres. The format in which the data is displayed allows to consult and explore relationships between different indicators since 1976, classified into the 6 barriers that impede access to housing. 

To achieve this result, the innovation integrated data from the Global Humanitarian Settlement Layer (GHSL), which details more than 16,000 metropolitan regions around the world. Indicators can be searched either by source, by the barrier to which they belong, by relevance or by keyword. More than 4,000 indicators from more than 200 countries are available. Most of this new data is provided by GHSL, both data from the metropolitan region scale and data available for the city scale or lower. The tool also incorporates data from the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), which describes both cities and countries, offering a consistent, global view.
Novelty: The atlas is a tool that has been created at a very particular moment in time, when, on the one hand, previous reference tools have become obsolete, and on the other hand, new data has emerged that can describe urban settlements on a planetary scale, allowing for a unique and novel approach.
Impact & Results: The tool looks into the issue of housing access on a global scale. The approach to the issue makes it possible to describe the planet on a global scale using the criteria of the Affordable Housing Activation (AHA) scientific committee, which has defined different barriers contributing to housing inaccessibility.

Description:
Anti-pilot is a method to be able to test for a limited time what happens if we stop or shut something down - before we do it definitively. It's called making an anti-pilot. Stopping abruptly with a way of working or an activity can be perceived as something fraught with risk and frightening. Hence the importance of using this tool to remove and stop doing things - consciously - that don't work so well or that are no longer needed. 

The anti-pilot process: 
Step 1:  Make a list of things that don't work or create value.
Identify and make a list of elements, components, processes, meetings or roles that from a user and employee perspective do not create value, are an obstacle, a time-stealer, do not work or are appreciated or questions/problems that keep recurring.
Step 2: Reflect on consequences.
Briefly reason about what could happen if you removed a part/process/role? What effects and consequences can it have: For the user? For employees? For other actors? For the business in general?
Step 3:  Make an anti-pilot.
Test now - for a limited period of time and on a small scale in the business - to remove or stop using the one or more components/moments that you have selected. Do a quick current situation analysis before you do the test, to be able to compare with when the test ends.
Step 4: Document the anti-pilot.
Documenting the anti-pilot: What happened? Is there something we can stop doing permanently/do less of to make room for something else that works better, saves time or improves the user experience? Do a quick follow-up after the test to see how the test was experienced and what the result was.
Novelty: First public sector tool (Anti-pilot) that describes a method to be able to test for a limited time what happens if we stop or shut something down - before we do it definitively.
Impact & Results:
Two executed Anti-pilots:
1) No more manual reporting of driving statistics: By thinking anti-pilot, the local statistics office found that they were requesting manual reporting for driving statistics when in fact they already had all the data they needed from other automatically generated sources. In an anti-pilot, they have therefore tried to remove the manual reporting.

2) No more applying for bus tickets on paper: The Administration for Children and Education has identified the application for a bus pass for high school students as an unnecessarily complicated process. In an anti-pilot, one therefore tries to simply remove the application procedure. The anti-pilot means that the student does not have to apply on paper. The test is carried out before the start of the autumn semester in 2022 and is evaluated in September.

Description:
This is an initiative based on 300 participants, leaders in museums, galleries, performing arts, music, funding organizations, curators and producers from every continent. It examines emerging forces, drivers of change and the possible impacts that may reshape the cultural sector in the coming decade. The project was created as a collaboration among futurists, sector experts and stakeholders.

The project emerged as a way to see past the fog of global pandemic, to better understand how existing and emerging pressures, innovations and models of culture might unfold -- to understand different ways in which creators, institutions and audiences might come together as a results of these shifting forces. It explores 9 high-level drivers of change happening in arts and culture globally with one driver sitting above all others -- climate change. 

Changeist analysed the causes and impacts mapped in the responses, their relationships and outcomes, and developed three inductive scenarios from these inputs. The scenario narratives represent the dominant views of the group as a whole.
Novelty: Transdisciplinary future scenario-making that examines emerging forces, drivers of change and the possible impacts that may reshape the cultural sector within the coming decade.
Impact & Results: The outcomes displayed are three inductive scenarios. The scenario narratives represent the dominant views of the group as a whole.

Description:
The art project takes the form of a fully-functional cell tower (GSM base station) in pink color, hence Pink Cell Tower. It intervenes upon the electromagnetic capture and aims to represent an alternative to state-monitored mobile infrastructure. The tower is solar-powered and open source to demonstrate that infrastructure for calls and texts can not only be truly free, but publicly owned and operated with no dependence on corporations. 

The innovation also serves a website with instructions to replicate it (from BladeRF SDR to mastpole design & antennae), and a downloadable ISO complete with source code to flash onto a Raspberry Pi. Provided on the website is a bootable ISO image for the Raspbery Pi 4 (tested on Model B) and for use solely with the BladeRF Micro A4 and suitable GSM antennae. 

This will allow users to replicate the functionality seen with Pink Cell Tower in the Berlin deployment. (It is a large download and the webserver has been set to throttle so as to allow for the rest of this and other sites hosted on such infrastructure to function). The ISO is two partitions, one the bootable part, the other the actual OS. The decompressed OS partition is ~12G, 4.6G of which is used, with 6G to play with (logs, other data you wish to serve, etc).
Novelty: The tower is solar-powered and open source to demonstrate that infrastructure for calls and texts can not only be truly free, but publicly owned and operated with no dependence on corporations.
Impact & Results: Potential for publicly owned and open-sourced operated infrastructure system for calls and texts.

Description:
The loss of a single species of pollinator can mean the end of a plant species. Given all the many ways that plants and their pollinators co-exist, creating public and private gardens that maximise pollinator diversity is not only vital, it’s also a complex challenge.

The initiative is devised as a living artwork, built using a unique AI that combines algorithms and horticulture – Pollinator Pathmaker. The tool was created to "design gardens with empathy" for other species while considering the specifics of a garden, such as sunlight, soil type, etc. 

To encode a human emotion like empathy into an algorithm, it means designing planting that supports as many pollinator species as possible. The Pollinator Pathmaker algorithm follows a set of rules using the inputs – pollinator species and the plants they forage from – to calculate the planting design. It then selects and arranges plants to suit the different preferences of their visitors. The platform states that is an AI-based artwork, not with scientific purposes.
Novelty: AI-based and climate-positive artwork that invites people to "empathetically" build a garden that support pollinator species (both plants and insects) to create awareness and fight the loss of the species.
Impact & Results: Not available.

Description:
Trees As Infrastructure is an eco-fintech that integrates open-source technologies to enable the modeling, monitoring, and financing of urban Nature-based Solutions. Our mission is to catalyze investment through data-rich models that value Nature. As a business, mobilizing private capital beyond greenwashing requires us to: (1) grow the supply of high-quality projects preserving natural assets; 2) develop the trusted, efficient and adequate technology to match demand and supply and; (3) offer services for ongoing reporting and monitoring requirements of natural assets performance.

Currently, we are undergoing a pilot with Glasgow City, FloodRe, ScottishWater, Nationwide, and other partners in developing a strategic portfolio of uNbS. TreesAI has formed a workgroup of committed regional stakeholders in exploring, mapping, and investing in uNbS in Glasgow region. As a business, Trees as Infrastructure integrates innovative open-source technologies to enable the modeling, monitoring, and financing of Nature-based Solutions. The platform's official launch will happen in November 2022.  

We will offer users a series of subscription packages that will allow them to: (1) Model their project's ecosystem services impact; (2) Map their climate-related risks via cloud-GIS and remote sensing, and; (3) Invest in NbS portfolios to address climate risks with proof of impact reporting. Our Go-To-Market strategy begins with onboarding municipalities into the platform, supporting on aggregating minimum data requirements (such as weather data and tree inventory) to access the capacity of our impact models. 

In a city-by-city acquisition strategy, we envision regional asset managers coming to our platform as we unlock data, intelligence, and analysis on natural assets in their geography. At this stage, our focus is on offering tailored features and microservices for cities (as main asset owners) to benefit from our tech offers, while in parallel we work to embed market demands related to reporting and monitoring from disclosures and standards, such as TNFD and EU taxonomy. 

Trees As Infrastructure (TreesAI) is a cloud-based platform, built to address declining tree stocks, and support the expansion of urban forests. The goal is to establish trees as assets with multi-dimensional values. Moreover, TreesAI provides the impact assessment and investment tools to fund, manage and maintain portfolios of nature-based solutions.
Novelty:
Trees As Infrastructure is unique in that: 
(1) We offer services across the chain of mapping, modeling and investing in NbS. Currently, these solutions are offered in fragmented and in siloes, burdening NbS project developers in accessing finance and impact intelligence. 
(2) We are experts in urban Nature-based Solutions. Few competitors have acquired critical knowledge on this NbS typology and we believe focusing on this scientific niche can unlock immense value. 
(3) We use our open-source platform to build portfolios and not project-by-project investing. Investors often state their difficulty in finding scalable NbS solutions, asset managers often spend a lot of time sourcing deals with local organizations which increases transaction costs immensely. We provide portfolio-level data, intelligence and investment opportunities for users to reduce deal-sourcing resources and provide access to a new NbS asset class.
Impact & Results:
The uNbS portfolio in Glasgow will generate: 
(1) sequester 812,000kg of CO2e,  
(2) total improvement on stormwater retention 1.1 million liters per m2, 
(3) total avoided stormwater runoff of 295,000 liter per m2, 
(4) annual improvement of PM10% (air pollutant) of 4.9% 
It will be composed by:
(1) 5000+ new trees 
(2) 15ha+ of new woodland 
(3) 30ha+ of trees preserved 
(4) SUDs construction in vulnerable areas. 

This portfolio will generate benefits to insurance companies, water utilities, Glasgow city council, Glasgow residents, high street shops, and many other regional stakeholders as the modeled impact will generate public co-benefits. We used a Risk-Based Vulnerability Assessment to map climate risks: using a mixture of open, proprietary and confidential data, this tool enables the customizable sharing of city-wide and location-specific data across stakeholders. Also with our internally built Green Urban Scenarios that provide ecosystem services valuation via agent-based modelling. 

See impacts on the Glasgow portfolio factsheet: https://treesai.org/glasgow-nbs-portfolio 
And on the impact modeling section: https://drive.google.com/file/d/127XlBUlQ-9_llwJawalifxWoqbBxG8ha/view

Description:
Constitutin is a civic education project that helps to understand how different constitutions around the world address various issues. Constitutin was built by a group of academics (scientists, engineers, and lawyers) at the Collective Learning Centre of the University of Toulouse. The tool does not pretend to describe all possible constitutions, but rather to provide a basic overview of the themes and structures commonly found in constitutions.

The aim of Constitutin is to promote instances of informed public deliberation. Each of the games on the platform can be completed individually, or collectively, using them as a starting point to discuss different issues. Users can use Constitutin at home as a family activity, at school as an educational activity, or use it to learn about constitutions personally.

More than 20 games allow you to learn and participate in specific subjects, such as the right to life or the territorial organisation of the state. Each game starts with examples taken from different constitutions around the world. They educate about how the constitutions address these issues and what the most common exceptions are. For example, the game on the right to life shows how different constitutions address the three most common exceptions to this right: the death penalty, abortion, and euthanasia.
Novelty: Constitutin is a civic education project that helps to understand how different constitutions around the world address various issues.
Impact & Results: The data collected in Constitutin was presented in a report as anonymous and aggregated information to the Chilean Constituent Convention.

Description:
The Relational Landscapes is an initiative to explore the numerous examples of ecological, social, economic, and cultural relationships between South America and Central Europe, highlighting the erasure of indigenous epistemologies and knowledge practices that characterize these processes and the many hidden technologies developed to transport resources between these regions. 

They aim to make visible and narrate the diversity of relationships with communities that seem distant but are connected by shared histories, matter, atmosphere, people, non-human beings, and technologies. 

For the process, the initiative recorded all the relevant information on a Miro Board and gradually created an information backlog. They built a sociogram to map all found actors and interactions from an indigenous and an industrial perspective. Then the interface was built with the shape of a tree, the Mahogany. It is conceptually divided in three phases: The colonial view, the indegenious view and the pluriversal view. Stories that can be assigned predominantly to a colonial view are located in the trees crown, while predominantly indigenous views are located in the roots, to emphasize the interconnectedness and dependancy of the colonial development on its indegenious origin. The pluriversal view is the final phase to reach. It displays connections between the nodes of the stories through links. To emphasize the transition to the pluriverse, the single mahogany tree from the first phase is embedded in a whole forest, that contains diverse species. This phase also offers an option for the user to add a story to the pluriverse of mahogany, so that it can grow bigger. As being part of the relational landscape project website, the pluriversal phase directly connects to its front page, showing the connection of mahogany to other species and matters from the amazon and Brandenburg.

The development was conducted through a method called granular synthesis. The sounds recordings were divided into small prices of about 1-100 ms duration. These small parts are called grains. Several grains can be layered on top of each other and play with different speeds, phases, volumes and frequencies, among other parameters. The platform worked with satellite imagery, to found the interactions with the landscapes of South America mainly in digital space. The result is an augmented reality liana, that can grow anywhere in the world, by just scanning a QR-code with a phone. Once it's placed, users can walk around it and find a lot of information, articles about and experiences in South America but also Europe, collected on little tokens that are placed in different parts of the plant.
Novelty: an innitiative to explore the numerous examples that highlight the erasure of indigenous epistemologies and knowledge practices that characterize interaction processes and the many hidden technologies developed to transport resources between these regions.

Description:
The Allen Coral Atlas is a complete global habitat map of the world’s tropical, shallow coral reefs. By combining satellite imagery, advanced analytics, and object-based analysis, the Atlas' global collaboration has resulted in maps that show the marine ecosystem's benthic and geomorphic data. Reefs between the latitudinal lines 30º N and 30º S were mapped and turned into downloadable data available on the Atlas website. 

The journey of a habitat map begins as satellite imagery from Planet Labs, Inc. This imagery, along with water depth calculations from the Australian State University, is sent to the Remote Sensing Research Center (RSRC) at the University of Queensland. There, scientists leverage machine learning and advanced analyses to craft and refine benthic and geomorphic habitat maps from satellite imagery.

To create high-quality maps, the initiative relies on global collaboration. Over 450 datasets and existing local reef maps have been provided by research and conservation teams. These data support the Atlas maps at various stages - from training the algorithms to accurately recognize and classify a reef area to assessing the quality of the Atlas habitat maps. Benthic (the composition of the ocean floor) and geomorphic (the seascape or structure of the ocean floor) data provide useful information about the reef ecosystem.

Currently, officials from 14 countries are engaged with Atlas team members, working on 48 new marine planning projects using the Atlas maps as their foundational data set.
Novelty: First and complete global habitat map of the world’s tropical, shallow coral reefs.
Impact & Results: - Over 450 datasets of coral reef maps between the latitudinal lines 30º N and 30º S.

Organisation: Bonn University, Institut für Wissenschaft und Ethik
Description:
The Data Pollution & Power (DPP) Initiative examines how power dynamics and interests in AI data determine the handling and distribution of data in data ecosystems and considers actions and governance approaches that are intrinsically interrelated in systems of power and interests. Furthermore, it delves into the dynamics that shape the data pollution of AI across the UN Sustainable Development Goals. 

The initiative examines the data of AI as a human and natural resource in data eco-systems of power and consider actions and governance approaches that are intrinsically interrelated in systems of power and interests.This is a nascent environmental data pollution movement. 

It frames data pollution in the context of powers and interests exploring eight domains in which data pollution has the greatest impact: Nature, Science & Innovation, Democracy, Human Rights, Infrastructure, Decision-Making, Global Opportunities, and Time. The main objective is to ensure that data pollution of AI in particular is included in the global sustainable development agenda.
Novelty: It examines how power dynamics and interests in AI data determine the handling and distribution of data in data ecosystems and display impacts across the UN SDGs.

Description:
The Digital Experience and Client Data group at Service Canada created this virtual lab where anyone can see, test, and provide feedback on new projects in development. Applying directly through the web platform, though, is not possible yet. 

The virtual space is created to explore works in progress and test experimental ideas with the users' team and other teams working in the Government of Canada. On the website it is possible: 
- Try out current projects
- Give anonymous feedback on projects when trying them out
- Sign up to get notified and participate in upcoming research sessions with a single or multiple teams. 

The initiative makes it possible too to give anonymous feedback without signing up to be a participant. If a government official seeks to get more involved, a participant form is available to be invited to research sessions.
Novelty: Virtual Lab as an space/platform to test and provide feedback on new projects in development across the different agencies within the Canadian Government.
Impact & Results: The platform is still on its beta form so not possible to display impacts.

Description:
AI localism is a growing and open repository of projects applying AI localism principles across the world. AI localism refers to the actions taken by local decision-makers to address the use of AI within a city or community. AI Localism affords policymakers a better understanding of the tradeoffs involved. By calibrating algorithms and AI policies for local conditions, policymakers have a better chance of creating positive feedback loops that will result in greater effectiveness and accountability.

The initiative focuses on 1) mapping the current state of AI Localism around the world. Providing rigorous insight in an emerging phenomenon. 2) Analyzes what works and what doesn't and why? Developing an evidence-base of AI Localism. 3) Guide local leaders towards responsible AI implementation and governance innovation.

Through the AI localism repository the initiative aims to identify/map projects at the local level that use and apply AI for public sector solutions so that they can extract AI localism principles from experiences around the world. So far they have identified and "case studied" more than 100 initiatives.
Novelty: A growing and open repository of projects applying AI localism principles across the world.
Impact & Results:
- Together with the NYU Center for Responsible AI, The GovLab will develop an interactive repository and a set of training modules of Responsible AI approaches at the local level.
- Global mapping of AI-localism in the context of COVID-19 as to inform and guide local leaders and city officials towards best practices.

Organisation: Tribunal administrativo de Faltas de la Municipalidad de Córdoba
Description:
This program, carried out by the Administrative Court of Misdemeanors of Cordoba, changes the sanctioning paradigm into one that incorporates the preventive function of the state, achieving a more normative recognition and compliance with the "Código de Convivencia" (Living-Together Code) of the City of Cordoba. Created with a 2020 ordinance, Living-Together Promoters ("Promotores de Convivencia"), are part of a four-part program.

The program involves:
1. The creation of these public servants called Living-Together Promoters. Among the main functions are: prevention, education, and control of the rules of the "Código de Convivencia", applying personal and team skills such as active listening, mediation, service vocation, and companionship.
2. The design and decision-making based on behavioral and data sciences
3. The use of technological tools in work operations: supporting contacts with citizens, improving the analysis of their problems, supporting the implementation of solutions. 
4. The adaptation of the Living-Together Promoters to the social context of the Pandemic and Post-Pandemic.

Following the management paradigm-shift, it is hoped that in the future, residents will no longer have to receive a warning or commit an infraction, but that the educational and preventive measures will help them to change their habits and behavior as responsible citizens.
Novelty:
Since its origins this project, because of its varied activities, has provided novelties. We can mention:
In 2020, controls were carried out on health protocols (related to Covid-19)
In 2021, 2022:
- Control of street vending on public roads and at social economy fairs in different neighborhoods
- The "Responsible Driving" campaign was launched to encourage responsible driving, with alcohol testing
- A preventive operation is being carried out at public shows
- Actions of preventing the insecurity in the streets.
- Preventive control of noise nuisance in downtown areas and residential neighborhoods
- The Living-Together School ("Escuela de Convivencia") was created to promote the Living-Together Code
- Participated as "overseers" in the democratic processes of the neighborhood centers
- Traffic and environmental infractions are monitored with cameras.
Impact & Results:
With the current paradigm shift in public management, now the problems have to be detected jointly by all, both the residents and the state itself, which would jointly control and supervise the implementation of the Living-Together Code. The state is no longer only there to collect and sanction, but to educate, prevent, manage and evaluate the best solutions.
To this end, measuring data and quantifying our actions in the past two years allowed the municipality to be more efficient in the implementation and application of behavioral and data sciences in the public sphere, to also be able to evaluate and observe the degree of compliance with the rules by the citizen, from this more persuasive and non-coercive approach.

Reports were published periodically in the press and social networks, showing how the preventive actions of the "Tribunal de Faltas and Promotores de Convivencia", had a positive impact that caused a decrease in the number of infractions committed. For instance, more than 80% of the environmental offenses detected in vacant lots and pavements were repaired by the offenders without the need for a sanction or fine. Many campaigns are being carried out, such as responsible driving campaign aimed at reducing those infractions that most threaten road safety, such as the use of mobile phones in the car and crossing red lights.
Other relevant URL(s) - eg reports, pres:
Official source: https://tribunaldefaltas.cordoba.gov.ar/promotores-de-convivencia/
Other sources from the project team can be found here: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1qGdt0cgI52oTXySAsjqf-G4oJkXTmixz

Project/Initiative Name: São Paulo Metro - AI procurement in a Box
Description:
The Metro also applied Brazil's first-ever Algorithmic Impact Assessment to evaluate the risks of using AI and help formulate effective mitigation strategies. The solution involved the adoption of a pre-commercial procurement procedure to allow the development of a new service, product, or innovative process when technological risk is involved. 

The Center came up with an algorithmic impact assessment model to fit Brazilian laws and regulations modeled after Canada's Algorithmic Impact Assessment, which incorporated the main risks with the use of AI into the Metro’s request for proposal. The objective was to purchase an AI-powered predictive maintenance system for online real-time monitoring of rail tracks throughout the subway network. High-definition cameras on trains and sensors will enable the use of deep learning and automated image processing to support decisions from Metro’s engineering team.

The guidelines were tested on a concrete procurement process. Due to the high traffic volume the subway system deals with daily, many maintenance interruptions arise throughout the day, delaying service for approximately 1,840 minutes of interference between 2016 and 2020. To increase efficiencies, the Metro is preparing to buy a real-time online predictive modelling tool that can identify segments of rail that need maintenance and minimize service interruptions to benefit both passengers and the subway system.
Novelty: An algorithmic impact assessment model to fit Brazilian laws and regulations modeled after Canada's Algorithmic Impact Assessment, which incorporated the main risks with the use of AI into the Metro’s request for proposal.

Organisation: Ministry for Technological Innovation and Digital Transition
Description:
The initiative is aimed at increasing citizens' digital skills and competences, promoting the use of digital public services to promote the full enjoyment of citizenship rights. The protocol of the Digital Civil Service falls within the aims of the Digital Republic program to counter the digital divide among civil servants. 

The Protocol allowed a base of 1000 volunteer operators to be employed with the role of "digital facilitators" to train young people to then retrain public servants and their communities, as well as guide and provide support with the use of technologies, in particular those related to the provision of public services. 

The initiative is working with the civil service and the private sector to upskill citizens. Italian mobile telephone company TIM, for example, has pledged to equip >1 million Italians with digital training courses by the end of 2020. As the country recovers from the pandemic, accelerating digital innovation in both the public and private sector will help it rebuild stronger for future crises.
Novelty: First protocol and initiative to create Digital Facilitators to train the public service and communities in digital and new technologies.
Impact & Results:
Axis 1: Higher Education and Training - 52 Actions 
Axis 2: Active Labour Force - 28 Actions
Axis 3: ICT specialised skills - 7 Actions
Axis 4: Citizens - 24 Actions

Description:
UK Government is investing in innovative public policy design expertise. Public policy design has been gathering momentum over the past 10 years, since when policy design established a foothold in government with the birth of Policy Lab. But 2022 was a landmark year for public policy design. For the first time, the UK’s government is sponsoring major networks that champion design as key for making policy and services that drive outstanding public value.

Over the past 2-years, the UK’s public policy design community has seen exponential growth in membership. It now includes public sector design practitioners from over 60 organisations inside UK central, local and devolved government, plus hundreds of university researchers and practitioners employed outside of government. The current spike in interest has been seeded by two important investments by the UK Government. First, central government’s Policy Profession, who support over 30,000 policymakers in the UK, invested in sponsorship of the specialist community of practice for policy design. The second key investment has been made by the UK Government-funded Arts and Humanities Research Council. They have invested in the new universities-led Design and Policy Network

Most of the UK’s big departments of state now have some policy design capability, and many universities now teach and research public policy design. Growth and demand are expected to continue as officials and researchers around the world strive to find better ways to lever more value from public investments within increasingly complex environments. The UK’s public policy design community has ambitious plans over the coming year including to teach design fundamentals to all government officials involved in making policy and services, formalise policy design as a career path in government, and create a new cadre of public design leader champions.
Novelty: This significant investment in innovative policy design expertise is unique in the UK and probably globally. The UK was at the forefront of investing in transactional service design through initiatives like Government Digital Service. It is now taking an influential new role in promoting next generation strategic policy design inside government but also engages academics in relevant fields, specifically university researchers in political science and studies of design, to strengthen the network’s conceptual basis, support practice development and help build its evidence base.
Impact & Results:
The impact of this innovation is demonstrated by the growth of the policy design community, that now includes public sector design practitioners from over 60 organisations inside UK central, local and devolved government, plus hundreds of university researchers and practitioners employed outside of government. The latest investments in policy design demonstrate the existence of an iceberg of interest in public policy design. Most of the UK’s big departments of state now have some policy design capability, and many universities now teach and research public policy design. Growth and demand are expected to continue as officials and researchers around the world strive to find better ways to lever more value from public investments within increasingly complex environments.
The direct beneficiaries are UK government officials and university researchers, but the downstream future benefit extends to businesses working with government such as suppliers and partners, all UK citizens and businesses whose activities are affected by public policies or use public services, as well as students on undergraduate, post-graduate and short courses.

Description:
The UK Home Office diagnosed that conventional public sector ways of working are not well-suited to seize opportunities from advances in science or the exponential growth in technology. Government actors are responsible for crucial public services, but often they are not able to identify the emerging opportunities or threats that constantly arise from the changing science, technology and social environment. Decisions have been generally time-consuming and risk-averse, organisational culture has preferred conformity over creativity, and opportunities to significantly improve outcomes for the public have been missed.

This is what pushed Home Office to seed-fund a self-sustaining start-up within the ministerial department: ACE. Its initial mission was to solve challenges and address opportunities in public security and safety, as a result of advancing science and technology, at the pace demanded by a fast-changing world. ACE offers fluid teams drawn from public, private, academic, and not-for-profit organisations and problem-solving and rapid solution delivery. With mission-oriented approach, ACE quickly builds a shared understanding and informed evidence base, then provides a possible solution.

ACE founded a purposeful, problem-solving community of collaborating private, not-for-profit and academic organisations, known as Vivace; now in 2022 with more than 350 members (80% of which are SMEs and academia). ACE draws on this growing community to explore problems and produce solutions. ACE uses a powerful funding model, which instils a customer culture, with a clear focus on their missions and results. ACE has served diverse public missions: offender risk in the justice system following terrorist attacks; health forecasting from wastewater-based epidemiology at the height of the UK’s COVID pandemic response; children protected from sexual exploitation and abuse (CSEA); disruption, interdiction and prosecution of illegal drug transport by criminal gangs exploiting children and vulnerable adults; cross-sector commitment to verification of children online as an element of the UK’s “Online Harms’ policy; joint maritime situational awareness and coordination to combat border risks and respond to migrant crossing; and many other mission-critical needs.
Novelty:
By behaving as if a business rather than a public sector organisation, ACE inspired the creativity of individuals by conceiving novel public sector ways of working in its leadership, commercial management and procurement, funding, delivery and security, combining these with collaboration spaces and technology. ACE is funded by its public sector customers, which is also novel in UK government. This means ACE accepts paid “commissions” to solve a “problem” for a “customer” organisation. ACE can only succeed by delivering innovative value to the customer, not by relying on established process. It also means the cost of ACE is shared fairly across the public sector.
ACE has developed its own products which magnify its values. For example, ACE’s key innovation value “make it real”: ACE’s Impact Lab is a key engagement mechanism to provide private companies and academic institutions with access to real data and public sector front-line specialists (for example from police investigations).
Impact & Results:
Since 2017, demand for ACE has grown strongly. Through 250+ commissions for 50+ public bodies, ACE has now completed £120M of delivery, with savings of £13M and secured co-investment from the private sector of £5M. SMEs and academia account for 75% of that work, by value. 
In the last years, ACE has grown organically. The demand for commissions has grown 20% per year (this is forecast to maintain), in FY 21/22 receiving orders for work valued in total > £40M
Other relevant URL(s) - eg reports, pres:
Main official source: https://ace.blog.gov.uk 
Other resources:
- http://aceannualreview2022.co.uk/ 
- https://vimeo.com/ace2021/aceintro (pitch)
- https://vimeo.com/ace2021/aceimpactlab-rasso 
- https://vimeo.com/ace2021/wbevideo

Project/Initiative Name: Innovation in Public Services (Innovación en Servicios Públicos)
Organisation: Agencia Nacional de Investigación e Innovación (ANII)
Description:
The innovation in public services programme implemented by ANII and IDB Lab seeks to help improve public services used by people, taking advantage of the incorporation of new knowledge and technologies, based on open innovation mechanisms and digital tools. 

To achieve this objective, this programme addresses three lines of action: 1) To promote the demand for innovation by public entities responsible for providing services to the population, 2) To make innovators aware of the business opportunities that can come from the co-development of solutions together with those who provide these services, and 3) To reduce the financial risk associated with innovation, both for service providers and solution providers.

Access are open to ministries, directorates, secretariats, intendancies, autonomous entities, decentralised bodies that provide services to the population and meet the requirements requested for each tool, focusing on sectors initially prioritised by the Government. The online platform provides: 1) consultancies for public innovation, 2) information of internal competitions for public innovation, 3) public innovation toolkits, 4) information and guidance about the most recent public challenges.
Novelty: First government platform to connect GovTech Startups to the public sector ecosystem, and to support public sector institutions in incorporating innovation in their policymaking.
Impact & Results: Impacts not available.

Description:
Ratsense is an integrated infrared sensor-based system backed by data analytics, on-demand, as-a-service. By embracing Internet of Things (IoT) and cloud computing, the system uses infrared sensors as “electronic eyes” that monitor, detect and capture real-time data on rodent movements 24/7. That data is then analysed to track, trend and map rodent movements and behaviours, providing city and private building managers with insights and location-based information on rodent infestations.

Intelligence from data analytics identifies movement pathways, breeding and harbourages of rodents, boosting the efficacy of traps and glue boards. It also allows for M&E rectifications and improvements of infrastructure to be made to ensure future rodent entry and intrusion is minimised. The system also sets a benchmark for the level of rodent activity in the building and identifies rodent sources and points of entry, supported by ongoing rectification works.

The sensors are built upon well established infrared technology. They have a lens which allows for a conical field of perspective, which enables a wide radius for each sensor to help determine direction. Typically, pest control operations are “very reactive”, but with a clear understanding of what exactly what is happening in the unseen spaces of their building” in real time, teams can then focus pest control efforts on where the rodents are active, saving on time and resources.
Novelty: An integrated infrared sensor-based system backed by data analytics, on-demand, as-a-service and IoT to monitor, detect and capture real-time data on rodent movements for decision-making about pest control in the city and private buildings.
Impact & Results: No impacts available.

Project/Initiative Name: Delivering a personalised citizen experience using Rules as Code as a shared utility
Organisation: Salsa Digital Pty Ltd and Department of Finance
Description:
The Australian Department of Finance sponsored a Proof of Concept (PoC) that looked at how Rules as Code (RaC) might be provided as a shared utility that can be used to deliver simpler, personalised digital user journeys for citizens. Salsa Digital delivered and showcased the PoC and related artefacts. This innovation demonstrated that RaC provides an easy-to-reuse and legislative based rules utility that can provide user journeys that transcend government and jurisdictional boundaries, offering a truly citizen-centric or ‘simple pathways’ where people can easily find out about rules and entitlements relevant to their specific needs.

RaC is the process of taking legislation and regulations and turning them into machine-readable code. While RaC and RaC technologies have been available for many years, the vision of providing RaC as a shared, centralised service or utility, hosted and maintained on a common technology platform has not been widely tested. The Proof of Concept helped demonstrate that RaC can foster greater government transparency, openness and accountability in that rules mapping, statements and code can all be made public, allowing the interpretation of the rules to be challenged and verified. RaC can also facilitate greater accessibility and more equitable distribution of benefits in that citizens can access the rules through any number of different user interfaces and devices.

Salsa Digital worked on ‘My COVID Vaccination Status’, the chosen subject for the PoC, drawing from publicly available COVID vaccination rules available from Australian State and Federal health websites. A simulated health website was then developed and it provided simple user journeys that helped answer questions such as "Am I up to date with my COVID vaccinations?" and "Do I have to be vaccinated for my job?". 

The PoCs, including the Rules as Code PoC, are being assessed by the GovCMS community, the responsible for the Government website, to prioritise new capabilities they could leverage to improve and personalise government services for citizens. The normalisation of Rules as Code in service delivery would provide immediate benefits for policy makers and thus enable a rapid pathway to agile, iterative and test driven policy development. To date, Department of Finance has made no decisions on any future role that RaC may play in the GovCMS program.
Novelty: While RaC technologies have been available for many years, the vision of providing RaC as a shared, centralised service or utility, hosted and maintained on a common, Open Source (Drupal and OpenFisca) technology platform has not been widely tested. The Proof of Concept helped demonstrate how a shared, API based RaC platform may work in practice. It helped identify the challenges and provided innovative pathways.
Impact & Results: The Proof of Concept provided a reference example of how written versions of reasonably complex regulation, in this case COVID vaccination rules, can be codified and successfully deployed in a rules engine. A Whitepaper was delivered in September 2022 by Salsa Digital to provide insights for Whole-of-Gov programs wanting to implement RaC systems and processes. Government response is waited.

Description:
The initiative's goal is to raise the overall translation and language standards in Singapore and has a broad base of volunteers to join as "Citizen Translators (CT)". This project is a first-of-its-kind in Singapore which aims to harness the expertise of the translation community to crowdsource public feedback for Government communication materials. The NTC aims to engage CTs in meaningful and enriching programs such as translation-related sharing sessions and seminars.

The main goal is to further strengthen MCI’s in-house translation capabilities and partnerships, which includes working with a pool of experienced translators attuned to Singapore’s social and cultural norms, to help ensure translations produced suit the local context

CTs are expected to support the vetting and proofreading of Government communication materials as well as identifying translation errors, if any. They have access to the functionalities on the SG Translate Together (SGTT) web portal and help post-edit translations generated by the platform's Neural Machine Translation engine – SG Translate. These edited translations will then be used to train SG Translate to raise its accuracy.
Novelty: A first-of-its-kind crowdsourced translation community to provide public feedback for Government communication materials.
Impact & Results: The platform was just launched on June 2022 and impacts are not showcased yet.

Project/Initiative Name: Reference model for innovation management system
Organisation: Ministry of the Interior and Kingdom Relations
Novelty: This innovation sets up a comprehensive set of content (models, information, insights), tools (online, canvas, step-by-step schemes), workshops, training and communities supporting building all governmental organizations own innovation management system. The focus on a systematic way of working is new, the integrated approach is new, and last but not least the online Innovation Maturity Scan is unique for governments (and can easily be adjusted for local use as well).
Impact & Results:
After the first year a study on the direct and indirect impact was conducted. The results are very positive and promising:
- 8% has created more structure on innovation activities
- 16% has shared insights with the top-management and 24% with colleagues
- 23% has expanded the ecosystem for the organisation
- 17% has further explored ways to innovate due to the training

The team expects to train around 50 professionals per year with an impact on around 20 - 25 new organisations as a result. Due to the nature of the cooperation with TIM we already save 50% on innovation training expenses. Content is now co-owned and therefore open for sharing amongst governments and other institutes. The use of the Innovation Maturity Scan is free of charge and open for all organisations and citizens (available in Dutch and English).

Project/Initiative Name: BIFIDEX - Official Business and Financial Analytics for the single market in the Western Balkans
Description:
One of the key challenges all governments faced was how to enable transparent and safe business operation in the Common Regional Market based on accurate and reliable data for businesses, bridging different business legislations, practices, languages and currencies. To achieve this ambitious goal the governments of 5 countries in the Western Balkans decided to create a single digital platform that would establish real-time interoperability between official registry data sources, unify all legal and financial data about 1.5M+ companies, their financials and connected entities, registered in various national registries, make searches and complex business and financial analytics in all national languages and English, as well as national currencies and Euros, create a core digital infrastructure that is extendable for future government-to-business and business-to-business services, and use public cloud services, while seamlessly integrating with the national digital infrastructures, respecting data sovereignty and privacy.

Official business registries of the countries, mandated by their respective governments, contracted consultancy and development company REAKTIV to develop BIFIDEX as a single regional digital platform to support business. BIFIDEX offers its services on a free and commercial basis, with Public-Private Partnership revenue-share model between REAKTIV and the official business registries. On BIFIDEX it is now possible to find: full company profiles, with complete legal and financial data, real-time tracking of changes in the registered data of the companies and their involved officials, financial KPI’s and company comparisons within individual economies and across borders, industry analytics by various financial criteria of choice, connected companies and persons across borders and their ownership percentage, company roles and appointments across borders, company insolvency information from the blocked bank accounts and duration of blockades, court sanctions to companies, their owners and officials, and national statistics of foreign ownership.
Novelty: BIFIDEX offers a novel service which was much needed in the region. Further, the innovation operates from a public cloud infrastructure, with complete depersonalized data, protecting data sovereignty and depersonalization of private information residing in national database infrastructure, while offering rich searching experience with full GDPR compliance.
Impact & Results:
As a novel service in the region of Western Balkans BIFIDEX has significantly facilitated cross-border trade, eased access to company information and facilitated investment opportunities across the Western Balkans region. Its major impact is demonstrated by the commitment of all participating countries to further extend BIFIDEX as one of the core digital platforms to enable the single market in the region. As the single go-to source for official business and financial information in the region BIFIDEX currently has statutory and financial data on over 1.5 million companies and persons in the Western Balkans. More than 63.000 users have visited BIFIDEX and more than 1.100 have registered to use its services.

The project won the prestigious 2022 OECD / SIGMA / ReSPA Western Balkans public administration award (https://www.respaweb.eu/0/news/495/congratulations-to-the-winners-and-special-recognitions-of-this-years-public-administration-awards-contest)

Project/Initiative Name: Bridging the Barriers Between Government and Academia
Organisation: Office of Innovation -- City of Austin, TX
Description:
Since 2020, the City of Austin (COA) and the University of Texas (UT) have collaborated on over twenty diverse research projects under the legal and administrative framework of a five-year, ten million dollar master interlocal agreement (ILA). Among a very few of its kind in the USA, this ILA is an "innovation enabling innovation" that bridges the barriers between two large, extremely complex organizations and fast-tracks the launch of research and innovation projects by four to five times.

In 2019, the COA contacted UT to explore opportunities to collaborate with the university’s Good Systems program. Good Systems supports teams of interdisciplinary researchers that take on real-world problems and propose solutions that are informed by computer science, social science, and humanities perspectives, with a strong focus on the ethical implications of artificial intelligence technologies on society. To address the fact that there was no easy way for teams to meet, the initial solution involved curating design-thinking workshops for targeted groups to come together. The collaborative workshops had a twofold purpose: 1) identify potential real-world projects that had a need for research; and 2) identify research teams that were interested in or aligned with the research needs. This partnering prototype introduced a viable, repeatable framework for city-university collaboration.

In August 2020, the City Council unanimously approved a five-year, ten-million-dollar master interlocal agreement (ILA) between the city of Austin and UT. ILAs serve as a means of increasing cooperation among government entities for the performance of governmental functions. This ILA provides pre-negotiated terms and conditions (including data security and intellectual property protection), removes legal and administrative slowdowns, and introduces pre-approved authorization so that departments aren’t required to seek Council approval for every project. This master ILA and new set of processes can now get a project from idea to team formation, scope creation, signed agreement, and project kick-off in as little as two months. Before the ILA, it would take a similar project four to five times the time and effort.

The partnership was piloted with nine AI projects funded by UT in 2019-2020. The program was then formalized in August 2020 with the City Council’s unanimous approval of the ILA. The first project under the ILA commenced in March 2020. Since then, twenty other projects with eleven unique City departments have entered the portfolio. Near the end of the second year, the city obligated over three million dollars dedicated to research within this project.
Novelty: This innovation can overcome the complexity of two organizations that employ tens of thousands of people and are comprised of dozens upon dozens of unique organizational entities, each with their own culture, bureaucratic hierarchy, business needs, and way of operating. This ILA and accompanying support framework provide a single, consistent way for any City employee or UT researcher to propose ideas for research projects in any field and get the assistance needed to make those ideas a reality. There appears to be no other mechanism available of this size and scope to provide this level of flexible, yet resilient partnering capability between Cities and their universities in the USA.
Impact & Results: Each project has its own success metrics by which it is measured and must complete a project close-out report. The program surveys end-users, key stakeholders, and members of our Audit and Finance committee twice a year. Four of our twenty-one projects completed on time, on budget, and realized their intended outcomes. Another eight are progressing so well that the teams have added scope, money, and/or time and amended their original agreements. On average, the ILA enables projects to go from idea to implementation four to five times faster. The overall program was so successful after its first year, that City Council unanimously approved an increase of $2.5 million dollars to the pre-approved spending limit. City departments have used the insights and recommendations from their research to create new ways to use machine learning to analyze data, improve their community-servicing programs, update strategic plans, and successfully advocate Council for additional funding and support.

Organisation: Corona foundation and extituto de politica abierta
Description:
Council to the street is an innovative strategy to strengthen democracy through citizen participation. It is a strategy that brings the city's institutions out to listen and respond to citizens in public sessions. Prior to the public session, a joint work session is held with community leaders to identify and prioritize their needs. This information is shared with the City Council and the Mayor's Office. A public space is selected in the sector of the city where the community leaders involved live. In the public session, the leaders receive an institutional response and generate commitments that benefit the citizens. 

Citizens receive a methodological accompaniment that seeks to strengthen their competencies in Open Government, methodologies and the right to citizen participation and political incidence. Methodologies of dialogue tables, co-creation, KAIROS methodology and others are used. The methodology is adapted based on the needs and particularities of the community and the lessons learned in the implementation.

Street Council has been held seven times in different sectors of Buenaventura (urban and rural). It was held four times in 2021 and three times in 2022. The City Council is expected to adopt this practice and institutionalize it through a municipal agreement.
Novelty: Compared to other types of strategies for dialogue between government and institutions, the Street Council is innovative because: (i) it allows citizens to be trained and be part of the process and not only participate, (ii) it has adaptive methodology, as the project owners build with citizens and institutions the work route to ensure effective participation and legitimacy in the process, (iii) the requests and commitments made in the space for participation are compiled in a systematization document for citizen monitoring.
Impact & Results: Councils were held four times in 2021 and three times in 2022. It is under permanent evaluation by the implementation team, the methodological team, allies, the city council and citizens. This allowed improving the methodology from one iteration to another in order to achieve greater participation and impact.

Project/Initiative Name: Blueprint for an AI Bill of Right
Description:
The Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights is a guide for a society that uses technologies in ways that reinforce USA core values. This is a handbook to incorporate these protections into policy and practice, including detailed steps toward actualizing these principles in the technological design process. These principles help provide guidance whenever automated systems can meaningfully impact the public’s rights, opportunities, or access to critical needs.

The Bill's Blueprint is sustained under five main pillars:
1) Safe and effective systems
2) Algorithmic discrimination protections
3) Data privacy
4) Notice and explanation
5) Human alternatives, consideration and fallback

This framework uses a two-part test to determine what systems are in scope. This framework applies to (1) automated systems that (2) have the potential to meaningfully impact the American public’s rights, opportunities, or access to critical resources or services. These Rights, opportunities, and access to critical resources of services should be enjoyed equally and be fully protected, regardless of the changing role that automated systems may play in people's lives. 

Moreover, the framework describes protections that should be applied with respect to all automated systems that have the potential to meaningfully impact individuals’ or communities’ exercise of: 1) Civil rights, civil liberties, and privacy, 2) Equal opportunities, 3)Access to critical resources or services.
Novelty: A handbook to incorporate these protections into policy and practice, including detailed steps toward actualizing these principles in the technological design process.
Impact & Results: This is a Bill Blueprint, therefore no impacts available.

Description:
The Smart Forests Atlas is a living archive and virtual field-site exploring how digital technologies are transforming forests. The Atlas is a research platform, which investigates the social-political impacts of digital technologies that monitor and govern forest environments. It considers how forests and technologies are co-constituted. Rather than advocate for smartness, the study questions how and why forests are becoming technologically optimised to address environmental change.

The Atlas platform includes open data from the Smart Forests project, and provides tools for researchers, stakeholders and publics to gather, explore, analyse, annotate, reflect on and reimagine smart forest knowledges and technologies. It seeks instead to open multiple trajectories and connections across plural knowledges and open data co-emergent with smart forests.

The site draws on and extends practices of "digital gardening." Rather than presenting finished ideas, the Smart Forests Atlas is a space for cultivating ideas publicly and sharing multimedia content. It will grow visibly over time and interweave different perspectives, thereby generating possibilities for critical reflection, practical relations, and epistemic justice.

Different parts of the site are connected through a continually changing heat map of tags. By bringing together emerging digital technologies that sense, respond to, and reconstitute forests, the Atlas offers tools for attending to the complex socio-political impacts of smart forest technologies and how they reconfigure human, nonhuman, and environmental relationships.
Novelty: A living archive and virtual field-site exploring the social-political impacts of digital technologies that monitor and govern forest environments.
Impact & Results: On the map it is possible to find the living archive of technology-and-nature related interactions from different regions: https://atlas.smartforests.net/en/map/

Description:
Beta.Gouv is an incubation program that helps public administrations build useful, simple, easy to use digital services that meet citizens need. The program is fueled to improve from the inside by training and teaming up with intrapreneur public officials. Every 6 months the program conduces stock takes of the impact of the product or solution to finally reach the State Startup phase. 

This is a four-stage process:
- A civil servant investigates a pain point affecting citizens dealing with government.
- A team of two to four builds a product in under six months using modern technologies.
- The team scales the service out by meeting the needs of a broader user population.
- A public sector body assumes ownership of the product and continuously improves it.

Additionally, each team receives support from the "Brigade", a multidisciplinary team of digitally expert public officials (entrepreneurship, digital development, design, and impact) to quickly launch interdepartmental digital public services with high potential for social, environmental or economic impact. The main areas of action are: 1) Crisis management and emergency situation, 2) Priority policy for the five-year period, 3) Interdepartmental digital Commons.
Novelty: A Government incubation program that helps public administrations build useful, simple, easy to use digital services that meet citizens need, which is accompanied by expert intrapreneurs.
Impact & Results:
'- a methodological approach inspired by the lean startup
- a manifiesto proposing a new way of designing digital public services
- 16 incubators within the state
- >800 people with different professions (business expert, development, UX, deployment, coaching...)
- 150 product teams dedicated to improving the lives of citizens and/or public action

Project/Initiative Name: Climate Mapping for Resilience and Adaptation
Description:
Climate Mapping for Resilience and Adaptation (CMRA) integrates information from across the USA federal government to help people consider their local exposure to climate-related hazards. People working in community organizations or for local, Tribal, state, or Federal governments can use the site to help them develop equitable climate resilience plans to protect people, property, and infrastructure. 

The platform allows to view real-time statistics and maps documenting where people, property, and infrastructure may be exposed to hazards. By clicking any hazard to display its associated map, or by clicking areas of interest on any map for more information. The tool provides information on past, present, and future climate conditions. 
 
CMRA is designed to work in a complementary the US Climate Resilience Toolkit. It integrates decision-relevant information from across the U.S. Federal government, including:
- Climate maps and data — both historical observations and future projections; 
- Non-climate data — including building codes and economic justice and social vulnerability information; and 
- Federal grant funding opportunities.
Novelty: A map that integrates information from across the USA federal government to help people consider their local exposure to climate-related hazards.
Impact & Results: Research shows that every $1 invested in proactive climate hazard mitigation can save $6-$12 in post-disaster recovery costs. Thus, CMRA also helps communities identify potential federal funding opportunities that can be used to plan and implement climate resilience plans.

Description:
The Data Commons is the vision of data governance that advances commons-based approaches: those that are based not on private control but collective stewardship and public interest.
