The approach seeks to address power imbalances between data subjects and entities that hold and use the data. It aims at maximizing societal value of data, instead of simply maximizing the volume and intensity of data use.

The initiative's goal is to support the development of policies that support this vision. The creation of the Data Commons will be enabled by policies that support data use in the public interest, so that society-wide and not just private benefits are obtained. This includes the need for governance models where users’ privacy and data protection are safeguarded while data can be used, and generate value. In that sense, its work focuses on policies that fall under the current European Strategy for Data, and they advocate for the public interest provisions in policy files like the Digital Governance Act and the Data Act. 

Data commons present a way out of the trilemma: how do we balance the protection of fundamental rights (both individual and collective), generation of economic value and the public interest in access and use of data?:
- First, it presents key issues and approaches that provide a theoretical underpinning for data commons designs.
- Second, it proposes a set of design principles for building data commons.
- Third, identify areas and needs to develop alternative data governance logics. And public policies will play a key role in ensuring that these alternatives are developed, and then made sustainable.
Novelty: An initiative for the creation of the Data Commons will be enabled by policies that support data use in the public interest and to advance towards a data governance model based on commons-based approaches
Impact & Results: The White Paper: https://openfuture.eu/publication/ai_commons-white-paper/

Project/Initiative Name: Citibeats - Ethical AI for Social Understanding
Description:
We live in an infodemic context. A lot of data doesn't mean good data. This brings to question the information sources and the data itself. Decision-makers don’t understand citizens’ needs and act based on uninformed decisions, and citizens feel neglected. This generates a systemic lack of trust.
Citibeats' ethical AI makes helps governments interpret the huge quantity of data exchanged every day by citizens in real time, understand their needs, and make faster and better-informed decisions. 
Citibeats is a  ethical AI software platform that searches and analyzes large amounts of text provided by citizens - we structure this data using Machine Learning and identify social trends, leading indicators, and insights for governments, and organizations.
Novelty:
Instead of being purely focused on single keywords, our research method is culturally focused, language-agnostic, easily adaptable, and monitors complex narratives evolving over time around a specific topic and that involves other related subtopics and multiple opinions expressed by users. 
Citibeats’ AI analyzes large amounts of unstructured text data and other types of files where text can be extracted to identify in real time, social trends, people’s opinions, and concerns. All this information - collected and centralized in the dashboard of our platform - represents precious actionable insights for governments, multilateral organizations, and private companies to act upon and improve citizen's lives. Reacting to leading indicators helps our clients act faster, with a social and economic positive impact.
Impact & Results:
Citibeats' Social Understanding technology has been used by international organizations like WHO, World Bank, IDB Group, UNESCO, and UNDP to interpret citizens' needs and help local, regional, and national governments act timely to satisfy people's demands (up to 90 days before traditional survey methods and the media).
Our AI platform has been implemented globally being able to collect real-time data from +70 countries, thanks to its agnostic set-up.
We measured the results of our technology based on the voices collected (over 1 billion) and on the more than $1 billion in social risk mitigation decisions that we informed (both in the private and public sectors).
In particular, thanks to our collaboration with IDB, in October 2021, the Green Climate Fund approved financing for the Amazon Project of 279M to foster and support the cultural and economic growth of the region.
As a result of this, we were recognized as a 2022 Technology Pioneer by the World Economic Forum.

Description:
The new hiring process at the Danish Design Center is fully human-centered and ideated to remove power from the recruitment process. The recruitment process is based on the institutions human values as an organization rather than on the classic, narrow fit for the job. This is a hiring process by design. 

The recruitment process has three phases: 
1. The first dialogue to find mutual match: A personal dialogue and examination of whether they have shared ambitions; what they are passionate about in professionally and privately, and in which settings they are most comfortable.
2. Development session co-work and get to know each other: Based on a real, specific work task at DDC, they got to solve a current problem in an equal collaboration environment. 
3. A broader look at the organization: In the last phase, they met again with the candidate and a new circle of colleagues who would not be the applicant's primary colleagues. 

The three stages guarantee to know the person rather than the candidate, and to investigate how a mutually valuable collaboration could arise. This last point in replacement of a strict interview format that meant not using resumes or applications. It was all about dialogue, exchanging ambitions, personal dreams, and ideas about job satisfaction. 

Under the slogan of "Recruitment as an exercise of the future", DDC did not simulate any case presentation but sought an immediate understanding of how they could work together and how a potential new colleague thinks and wants to approach working in teams in practice.
Novelty: A novel recruitment process based on the institutions' human values as an organization rather than on the classic, narrow fit for the job. This is a hiring process by design.
Impact & Results: First cohort of people were hired under the aforesaid method.

Project/Initiative Name: Mind the Class - Reversing Youth Mental Health Outcomes
Description:
Mind the Class, a mental health prevention organization, is partnering with school systems to reduce the risks and reverse the rates of mental and behavioral health disorders by creating a community ecosystem. This year-long project collaboratively designs a preventive implementation plan using proven wellbeing research and data-driven solutions in collaboration with our University of Warsaw partners and through local public and private partners support sustainable development.

In close examination of the research through a thematic analysis, the team found the areas of wellbeing that support good mental and physical health were related to 5 areas. These were broken down into 60 key factors including 30 internal and 30 external variables. By categorizing the supportive practices, the team could identify path around the barriers, thereby eliminating the barriers to achievement. This is the basis of their model. In this way, the team designed an evidence-based public health capacity building approach to preventive mental health best-practices.

Min the Class provides strategic and operational consultation, professional development and community partnership development for 30 weeks. The team measures wellbeing needs defined from its evidence-based framework, population factors, and risk factors through public health data. The team provides training to a trainer who coordinates a task force (including youth, school leaders, key community members, parents, and educators) for planning and scaffolding implementation. Local public and private partnerships are created to secure resources and connect technology partners to fill need gaps and build capacity in teams for self-sustaining programs. This provides teams with a preventive implementation plan and a replicable model with a supportive ecosystem within their communities.

System-wide prevention creates a supportive environment, reduces risk of mental health problems, reduce counseling referrals and behavioral incidents, is more cost effective, and creates a calmer more supportive culture and climate. When we architect proactively to improve wellbeing, people feel better equipped to manage uncertainty and change through capacity building, empowerment and connection, improving achievement and retention.
Novelty:
The Mind the Class Program is Innovative as it:
1. proactively addresses the core mental health needs of youth,
2. systemically builds capacity in school communities for sustainable preventive solutions to a global problem allowing for a wider impact more quickly,
3. integrates cross-disciplinary approaches and resources to increase awareness, access, equity and address the complexity of wellbeing and shared burden of responsibility.
Impact & Results: In the first Pilot, life-cycle modeling and evaluation planning was conducted with the support of University of Warsaw researchers as consultants. Use of the model and approach was recorded, task force members were surveyed using a needs assessment and a wellbeing questionnaire was used with a sample of students. The benefits reached 1200 students in 12 schools. Psychological safety teams and a peace club was created, specialized trainings were conducted on high risk factors and community partnerships were made. The evaluation plan will scale with our phase 2 cohort impacting 36 schools with pre and post assessments with local public risk factor data stored on our software solution to initiate predictive preventive solution insights.

Organisation: ENAP - National School of Public Administration
Description:
BORA (acronym for "Online Relationship Database for Evaluators", in Brazilian Portuguese) is an innovative online application developed by ENAP that connects research professionals and policy managers around the country. The app makes it possible to link the needs of Brazilian public administration for high-quality policy analysis and the research of well-targeted projects by specialists based on a quick, transparent and free solution. With BORA there is no need for human intermediation, no space for privileged information, no bureaucracy. Using open source programming language Flutter and Firebase database, BORA is a people-oriented project. 

The main feature developed by our team is "bora" (Brazilian Portuguese for "let's go") is a button pressed when you are willing to work on a project or with a professional. Whenever there is mutual interest on a profile/project, both ends' contact details are displayed and they are able to discuss a partnership. The app also has the option of showing interest, which represents a like button on a project or a professional but without the intention of generating a partnership right away. No contacts are displayed in that case but the feature creates a separated list of interests, allowing to increase and enhance professional network for future collaborations.

BORA was first presented during a public session at 2022 ENAP Innovation Week on August 10th. A group of users among researchers and policy managers have made the final feedback round, which was a neat success. During the last month, our teams worked on implementing the last details, debugging some features and making sure both Android and Mac apps were ready to see their final version. It was officially launched on September 28th in both Google Play and Apple Store.
Novelty:
When we analyze traditional recruitment tools bureaucracy is the number one obstacle for both candidates and recruiters. Lots of required information, steps to follow, without even knowing there is a real match between the job offer and applicants. With BORA obstacles are removed and simple, quick, direct profiles increase the chance of finding the one project or professional made for you.

This unique technology project inside the Brazilian public administration shows that the State is able to serve public needs in innovative ways. Through UX/UI design, data science and development teams and tools, altogether, high-quality public policy research is made easy and transparent.
Impact & Results: The first tests were successful. Now the app is online and more evidence of its impact will be available.

Description:
The Disinformation Register lists prominent pieces of disinformation the AEC has discovered regarding the electoral process. It also provides details of actions the AEC has taken in response. The AEC claims that "they are not the arbiter of truth regarding political communication and do not seek to censor debate in any way. However, when it comes to the election process they conduct, they’re the experts and they’re active in overseeing Australia’s democracy"

Each piece of disinformation the AEC has discovered is presented in the register with information about the platform it was spread on, the timing, the factual information regarding the matter and AEC actions taken to correct the record. The register will be updated regularly in the lead-up to, and during, the 2022 federal election period.
Novelty:
The use of keywords to monitor conversations outside of AEC accounts.
They will occasionally jump in to those conversations to provide facts about how electoral processes work.
Impact & Results: This campaign attracted more than 63 million social media impressions during the previous federal election and 100,000 click throughs to website material. The campaign will be expanded for the 2022 federal election.

Project/Initiative Name: Climate Atlas - Indigenous Knowledges and Climate Change
Description:
The Climate Atlas of Canada is an interactive tool for citizens, researchers, businesses, and community and political leaders to learn about climate change in Canada. It combines climate science, mapping and storytelling to bring the global issue of climate change closer to home, and is designed to inspire local, regional, and national action and solutions.

The Atlas explains what climate change is, how it affects Canada and what these changes mean in our communities. Various aspects of climate change can be explored using maps, graphs and climate data for provinces, local regions and cities across the country. Plain-language description and analysis make climate science understandable and meaningful. 

The data behind the Atlas now includes twice as many climate models, and we’ve made a lot of other changes to how things look and feel. Our goal is to continually improve the atlas’ usability and functionality to better support public education and applied decision-making.

The unique approach illustrates how Western or Eurocentric climate change science and Indigenous expertise can complement one another. It's the embodiment of a concept sometimes called two-eyed seeing, which Hetxw'ms Gyetxw describes.
Novelty: A comprehensive map that uses Pacific Climate Impact Consortium's PCIC statistically downscaled data derived from 24 CMIP5 global climate models and link them with indigenous/community knowledge.
Impact & Results: The map: https://climateatlas.ca/map/canada/plus30_2030_85#

Description:
In response to the Covid-19 pandemic and the ensuing economic crisis, governments worldwide have mobilised an unprecedented amount of fiscal support aimed at stabilising and rebuilding their economies – now around USD 18.2 trillion.

The Tracker assesses national recovery plans against levels of 2021-2023 government spending specified in the IEA Special Report on Sustainable Recovery (2020) that are consistent with levels required to stay on track with a Net Zero Emission Trajectory by 2050.

The IEA Sustainable Recovery Tracker measures global recovery plans against this target level of spending by:
- Monitoring energy-related policies and government spending on clean energy measures by country and by sector in the wake of the pandemic
- Evaluating the actual impact on total public and private recovery spending on clean energy measures.

The Tracker relies on new, extensive policy analysis conducted by the IEA, including new modelling to estimate how much government spending mobilises private sector participation by region and measure type.
Novelty: A data and information tracker that includes more than 1000 clean energy-related policies following the pandemic induced economic crisis and government spending programmes by country and by sector through the Policies and Measures (PAMS) database.
Impact & Results: Information about impacts here: https://www.iea.org/reports/sustainable-recovery-tracker/tracking-sustainable-recoveries

Project/Initiative Name: Teaching Public Service in the Digital Age
Organisation: Teaching Public Service in the Digital Age
Description:
Governments need staff equipped with key digital capabilities alongside traditional skills of policy, economics and ethics. The majority of post-secondary programs producing future public servants lack training on digital era skills and competencies. To solve this problem, we lead a train-the-trainer model to share open educational materials, including a syllabus on digital era public administration skills. We are nurturing a global community of faculty who teach these materials.

Our goal is to train and support 250 professors and educators to use this syllabus, who will then go on to train at least 10,000 learners across the US and around the world over the next two years.
Novelty:
Our train-the-trainer model is the first of its kind to tackle a root cause of digital incapacity in today’s governments: those responsible for teaching current and future public servants are often ill-equipped to provide relevant digital-era public sector competencies to their students. By upskilling these educators and providing them with novel open educational resources they can use in their classrooms, we greatly scale our ability to modernize the competencies of today’s governments.

Our innovation follows the principles of the open educational resource approach that fits squarely within the open government movement: We volunteer to make otherwise localized materials publicly available and explain step-by-step how to use them.
Impact & Results: We have run three train-the-trainer sessions so far with international participants. We asked each of them whether their schools have officially adopted their courses into the standard curriculums of their public affairs programs and ran review sessions with selected faculty. We have successfully enabled professors in eighteen universities in over a dozen countries to teach new, critical digital era skills to public servants, and to students who are progressing to careers in public service.

Project/Initiative Name: NYC Automated Decision Systems Task Force
Impact & Results: The ADS Task Force published a report in November 2019. It should be noted that AI Now published a shadow report the next month to identify what went wrong with the ADS Task Force project.
Other relevant URL(s) - eg reports, pres:
Automated Decision Systems Task Force (nyc.gov) 
2019 Report: https://www1.nyc.gov/assets/adstaskforce/downloads/pdf/ADS-Report-11192019.pdf 
AI Now shadow report: ads-shadowreport-2019.pdf (ainowinstitute.org)

Description:
Algorithm Audit is a nonprofit organization that builds and shares knowledge about ethical algorithms. Its main activity is to form independent audit commissions that give ethical advice on real-life algorithmic methods as used in the private and/or public sector. On top of that, Algorithm Audit facilitates a platform for multidisciplinary experts to engage in (inter)national automated decision-making policy development.

Algorithm Audit worked on an algorithmic risk assessment tool used by the municipality of Rotterdam that was judged biased by the Rotterdam Court of Auditor. Further, it is working on a model that uses the type of SIM card as a predictor variable to detect payment fraud.

Algorithm Audit also developed a bias scan tool, an unsupervised algorithm that identifies bias in AI systems. It identifies potentially discriminated groups of similar users that are affected by the output of a binary classification algorithm. The bias scan tool serves as a starting point to assess potentially discriminatory AI classifiers with the help of subject-matter expertise. The features of identified clusters are examined on critical links with protected grounds, and whether the measured bias is legitimate. 

Recently, Algorithm Audit's application for the EU AI Fund has been accepted, hence it will receive a substantial sum to expand its work. Further, the NGO is engaging with the Dutch Ministry of Internal Affairs in experts' sessions to share ideas on institutionalising the prospective algorithmic watchdog.
Novelty: It is a non-governmental actor that is able to affect public sector algorithms with its activity. The method is novel and the subject it deals with is at the forefront of the interplay between new technologies and society.
Impact & Results: Algorithm Audit published two reports and is working on more.

Description: The Spanish Government has approved a specific budget line for the creation of a Supervisory Agency of Artificial Intelligence (AESIA), which will be the first of its kind in the European Union. In fact, if deadlines are met, the Spanish AI Agency will be established in 2022 and fully operational in 2023. The Spanish AI Agency will be responsible for the development, supervision, and monitoring of projects within the framework of the National AI Strategy, as well as the projects promoted by the European Union.
Novelty: The Spanish Supervisory Agency of AI would be the first of this kind.
Impact & Results: Not available yet as the agency is not in place yet.

Description: Canada’s Directive on Automated Decision, issued by the Treasury Board Secretariat, requires that agencies using or considering any algorithm that may yield automated decisions to complete an Algorithmic Impact Assessment in the form of a questionnaire that calculates a risk score the prescribes what actions must be taken. Further, the Directive imposes transparency requirements such as notice before decision, explanation after decision, and access to components. Moreover, it established quality assurance and that applicable recourse options must be available to clients.
Novelty: First regulation of this kind, which establishes a novel way of implementing algorithms in the public sector.
Impact & Results: The Directive is effective and in place. It ensures that the implementation of algorithms in the public sector is for the good of citizens.

Project/Initiative Name: Etalab's Guiding Principles on Algorithmic Accountability
Description:
Etalab works extensively on algorithms in the public sector and on keeping them accountable. It devised 6 guidelines principles for keeping algorithms accountable. These are:
1. acknowledgement: agecies should mention when an algorithm is used
2. general explanation: agencies should provide a clear and accessible explanation of how algorithm work
3. local (individual) explanation: agencies ought to provide a personalised explanation of a specific result or decision 
4. justification: agencies should justify why an algorithm is used and reasons for choosing one particular algorithm
5. publication: agencies should publish the source code and documentation as well as note whether the algorithm was developed by a third party or not
6. allow for contestation: agencies should provide ways of discussing algorithmic processes and appeal them
Novelty: The work of Etalab is innovative because it is oe of the few cases where a specific institution was created to deal with digital government and its work on algorithmic accountability is a rare example of public sector regulation on this matter.
Impact & Results: Etalab's work in the field of algorithmic governance had a huge impact on French public sector.

Project/Initiative Name: Government marketplaces for government procurement teams
Description:
The City of Seattle spends over $720 million every year. With the surge of COVID-19, small businesses were left in a highly vulnerable position, forcing many to shut down. This is especially true for women and minority-owned small businesses (WMBEs). We worked with the City to launch its e-commerce government marketplace, providing a platform for government buyers to seamlessly find local WMBEs, access their products and services, obtain quotes, and check out with a few clicks.

Before launching the Portal, government buyers at the City of Seattle had to navigate through three different sources of information before finding WMBE vendors.  We found that searching on the system for WMBEs was very cumbersome and certain labels were not accurate. 

By leveraging these 3 fragmented sources of information, we: 
1) Updated and curated vendor data using AI
2) Transformed the City's portals into actionable catalogs of goods and services; 
3) Enabled a checkout similar to those of online commercial marketplaces; 
4) Directed the purchasing process towards a more diverse, equitable, and inclusive one; and 
5) Optimized for efficiency and compliance.
Novelty: Bridging this digital gap and enabling government agencies to make convenient and compliant purchases under their bidding thresholds at the speed of e-commerce, while reaching their socioeconomic goals, is our unique objective, and biggest difference.
Impact & Results: This marketplace  is simplifying government purchasing by digitizing products from their current blanket contracts (which are in PDF format and are not actionable), bringing them to an actual transactional platform. Buyers have also been able find highly-relevant open market vendors and compare prices in real time. Our key metrics to date are as follows: 1) 6,720 products onboarded; 2) 20+ blanket contracts digitized; and 3) 59 open market businesses onboarded.

Description:
The hard-fought gains of democratization have come under attack in many countries. To reverse this trend, policymakers and civil society need new tools to navigate sophisticated forms of democratic erosion. Machine Learning for Peace (MLP) combines recent advances in machine learning with massive webscraping to produce high-frequency data and forecasts predicting where democratic backsliding will occur and the specific forms it will take. We equip pro-democracy forces with advanced warning to guide more strategic responses.

Since 2021, MLP has been disseminating the forecasts on a quarterly basis to policymakers at USAID and practitioners within the INSPIRES consortium to inform their decision-making around program implementation and development. In January 2022, MLP made this information publicly available for the first time by launching our website and interactive data dashboards at the Open Government Partnership Global Summit.
Novelty: First, MLP dramatically accelerates the provision of high-quality data to practitioners and policymakers who need to take timely, evidence-based action to counter attacks on human rights and democracy. Second, MLP collects data from local sources of news and information in more than 20 languages. Third, MLP goes beyond merely tracking important historical events by using big data to produce accessible forecasts about future events that can be used by organizations focused on human rights and democracy-promotion to anticipate and plan for changing conditions.
Impact & Results: MLP has rapidly scaled our efforts across 40 countries, with funding secured to expand to another 12 countries over the next 12 months. While the impact of MLP tools on decision-making is hard to quantify, in its last quarterly survey of 70 activists living in 19 countries, more than 80% of respondents indicated that they were likely use MLP forecasts to inform programming decisions and communicate with stakeholders about the future.

Description:
Since the COVID-19 pandemic, more people commute by bicycle instead of using crowded public transport. Accidents on bicycle paths are also on the rise. Bicycle paths are mainly along rivers, and there are few buildings or facilities on the bicycle path, which makes it difficult to determine the location of such accidents and difficult to report emergencies. 

To solve this problem, we have developed an "SOS One-Stop System" that accurately identifies the current location on the bicycle path by installing a basic number plate that displays the name of the bicycle path and the location number.

Also, a QR code image is inserted, and scanning it opens the "SOS One-Stop System." In this system, the exact current location is automatically displayed on a mobile device, so that a rescue request can be made via phone call or text message. Since it makes it possible to request a rescue by text message, it can be used easily by the socially disadvantaged who cannot speak to rescuers or who have difficulty communicating.
Novelty: New way to determine locations and immediately reach out for assistance.
Impact & Results: These plates are installed in 282 locations in both directions at 50m to 100m intervals in 143 places, including street lights and safety signposts, for easy identification by bicycle path users.

Organisation: Department for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy
Description:
The rate of innovation often exceeds the speed at which regulatory systems can adapt, blurring lines between sectors and cutting across transitional regulatory and geographical boundaries. The RPF aims to keep the UK at the forefront of regulatory thinking and experimentation. It sponsors projects, led by regulators, aiming to help create a UK regulatory environment that encourages business innovation and investment. It is market-led and uses real-world innovation settings to deliver.

The RPF recognises the important role regulators play in enabling an innovation friendly environment and encouraging innovative regulatory thinking and experimentation. It was a way of helping encourage innovation without risk. It provides grants to market-led projects by regulators and local authorities which help to develop a UK regulatory environment that encourages business innovation and investment. The emphasis is on projects that are ambitious, collaborative, innovative and reflect a research, learning and experimentation approach to regulation. They also address regulatory challenges associated with market innovation, emerging technologies or new/disruptive business models.
Novelty: The RPF is the first market-led fund whereby regulators and local authorities identify key issues within their areas, to remove barriers to innovation for business and help businesses get innovative products and services to market. This involves real-world delivery, cutting-edge research, testing of concepts, the opportunities within the Fund are endless. Other funds have targeted businesses however, the RPF specifically targets the areas where regulatory systems can be seen as a barrier to innovation, giving businesses more opportunities and a direct route to work with Government on improving the business environment and public policy outcomes, helping foster a pro-innovation culture and overcome risk-adversity.
Impact & Results:
The RPF has led to the establishment of Innovation Hubs and advisory services for business and innovators, technology driven solutions and data tools, frameworks and new regimes (some world-first), research to inform/improve regulatory approaches and new guidance or standards for business innovation.

The RPF is independently reviewed combining elements of impact and process evaluation, these include qualitative interviews, case studies and analysis of management information and project reports. Within this evaluation the RPF was seen to directly increase collaboration between regulators and sector stakeholders, helping increase confidence in regulation and the role of regulators in encouraging innovation.

Participants have also shared lessons learned from their projects which will impact the development of future innovations, thereby influencing wider regulatory practice. Given the success of projects many have been able to secure funding from core budgets to continue their RPF work.

Project/Initiative Name: PAZOS (Peace and Opportunities) Strategy for the Social Prevention of Violence.
Description:
PAZOS is an alternative to dealing with the historically high rate of juvenile homicides with law enforcement in the community of Palmira. It was developed when the city peaked among the most dangerous cities in the country and the world. This is an articulated and focused intervention, oriented towards the dual functionality of public spending and the generation of opportunities (societal second chances).

This is a social innovation initiative of the local public administration as an alternative to confronting with public force, which stems from the territorial control between criminal organizations and gangs. It consists in the deployment of five (5) components that seek to respond to the specific needs of the territories and from citizen participation for the implementation of the following components:
1) Interruption: Actions that contain or dissuade violent behaviour in the prioritised territories by Coexistence Managers. 
2) Intervention: Provision of psychosocial support, soft skills, training and economic support conditional on adherence to the process, aimed at access to the labour market for young people in vulnerable conditions in the face of violence, reducing gaps in the opportunity to generate income in a legal manner. 
3) Prevention: Implement social policy programmes in the targeted territories, seeking to reduce risks through the occupation of the free time of young people and children, the peaceful resolution of conflicts and the reduction of risks and damages in relation to the consumption of psychoactive substances. 
4) Safe Environments: Improve the conditions of community infrastructure in micro-focused locations, promoting the appropriation of spaces that have historically been affected by violence and crime. 
5) Access to Justice: Implement different actions to bring justice closer to citizens and show the community benefits of restorative justice measures.
Novelty:
It is an alternative but comprehensive response to the multidimensional and multi-causal phenomenon of homicidal violence victimisation and escalating community conflict in the municipality of Palmira. PAZOS uses tools such as sport and art (street cinema, urban art), psychosocial support and income generation and integrates them into the municipal security policy. 

It is a comprehensive strategy because it complements crime prevention with surveillance, the articulation of public, private and community efforts, data information analysis, technology, and community management.
Impact & Results:
- In 2022 Palmira has the lowest number of homicides in 17 years. Disruption. More than 600 conflict mediations in the clusters. 
- Intervention: 290 young people with psychosocial support and entry to an income-generating route. 62 young people enrolled in diploma courses and courses to strengthen their employability skills.
- Prevention: 120 young people in the Urban Graphics programme. 6 courses of the Municipal School of Art with 65 young people impacted. 400 young people impacted with sports tools.
- Safe environments: 23 days of recovery and beautification of spaces in prioritised areas. 58 points were intervened with the change of lighting. Implementation of a 3-kilometre healthy cycle route.
- Access to justice: 23 non-repetition agreements with post-convicts. Socialisation of the House of Freedom with 90 inmates. 30 agreements for the non-repetition of violence with adolescents in the System of Criminal Responsibility for Adolescents.

Description:
The IDT is a multi-stakeholder dialogue methodology whose objective is to generate spaces for collaborative construction to create a public policy proposal for a local development challenge. 
The Yumbo IDT worked on a challenge prioritised by citizens. 

The IDT in Yumbo emerges as a methodology that helps to solve the lack of citizen participation and the legitimacy gap of public decisions regarding a development challenge in the municipality for two reasons: 1) it qualifies citizen proposals through the public policy prototype built in a collaborative manner, and 2) it generates spaces for advocacy with decision makers so that these solutions are incorporated. 

Through the principles of design thinking, creative problem solving (CPS) and agilism, the IDT develops three phases to produce a public policy proposal document that is used to advocate with decision-makers.
Novelty: The Yumbo RTD is an innovative proposal due to its multi-stakeholder character, as it brings together: (i) private sector and foundations, (ii) institutions, represented by the Mayor's Office and the Government of Valle, (iii) civil society, including young people and social and community organisations, and (iv) thematic experts.
Impact & Results:
'- A multi-stakeholder dialogue generated with 43 leaders (social, private and public), who constructed 5 problems, 5 causes, 5 challenges, 69 draft solutions and 15 prototypes of solutions, synthesized into a single public policy proposal focused on the prevention of violence among young people. 
- The prototype is synthesised into two structural axes, 5 components and 50 actions. The first axis focuses on the social prevention of violence, and the generation of opportunities for young people; the second axis focuses on the territorial management of security. 
- Citizens presented the draft public policy to decision-makers; Mayor of Yumbo, Secretariats of Social Welfare, Education, Government and Security, the Office of Economic Development and the Council of Government. 
- The Mayor's Office of Yumbo expressed its willingness to articulate the public policy proposal as a fundamental axis of the public youth policy and development plans.

Project/Initiative Name: Network collaboration for digital services for Ukraine refugees
Description:
The Ukraine war resultedin a commitment to work across sectors to improve digital services to refugees. A focus on end user needs and an unbureaucratic organizational model have produced tangible results such as: 
-	better update of address information in national register
-	electronic identification - access to digital services

Twelve agencies worked with obstacles and bottlenecks in the refugee's user journey, with the objective of good transitions between services. Refugees from Ukraine now receive, among other things, an electronic ID, they are easier to reach through more updated addresses in the national register, and they receive faster processing decision on residence by a robot at UDI (the immigration administration). A cross-sector and inter-agency network collaboration in the field of digitalization contributed to this. The network collaboration co-existed with traditional bureaucracy and governance structures
Novelty: Interagency collaboration is known to be challenging. We established an informal network collaboration that was dialogue-based with regular weekly digital meetings. It was open to anyone who wanted and found a benefit in participating. We started with seven agencies, and at most thirteen agencies participated.
Impact & Results: The agencies that participated state that the collaboration contributed to greater interest from more people in their organizations for the totality of the user journey. There is a greater understanding that services must connect and that improvement of user-experience requires collaboration and trust. The agencies also report good use of better insight into the processes in other agencies, regularly being updated and being able to communicate internally in respective agencies. The agencies got a much broader contact surface towards other agencies and lower threshold for contacting and collaborating with other agencies.

Project/Initiative Name: Transforming Public Healthcare and Education Using AI-powered Mixed Reality Technology
Organisation: Hospital Center "Dr Dragisa Misovic" / Faculty of Medicine, University of Belgrade
Description:
Public healthcare in Serbia is transformed through innovative use of mixed reality technology powered by Artificial Intelligence, that has increased efficiency and quality of healthcare, minimized risks and efforts, and optimizes procedures. Through remote collaboration, doctors have the same insight into patient’s condition without the need of physical presence, enabling joint real time inputs and medical interventions with experts from anywhere, as well as remote education for medical students.

The COVID pandemic amplified the problems for medical professionals, such as chronic stress and exhaustion at work, with an increasing incidence of burnout. The need for reduction of the staff’s exposure in the red zone led to most demanding patients in the intensive care being rarely treated by the most experienced physicians.

With the support from the Office of the Prime Minister and through partnership with Microsoft, University Hospital Center piloted Hololens2, mixed reality device that revolutionized healthcare collaboration and improved efficiency and quality of healthcare, as well as working conditions for doctors. Rather than the entire medical team, only one doctor enters with the mixed reality headset and views all relevant medical documentation and images as 3D holograms, while other doctors monitor from outside providing input and advice, while having the same experience and information as the doctor in the red zone.

Aside from international collaboration, mixed reality can play a key role in enabling the highest level of healthcare even in the most remote areas. In this way, a small number of experts from different fields can be hired to solve numerous problems, without the need for their immediate presence. This saves time and money, and raises the quality of the service since top medical teams can remotely treat patients whenever they are located.
Novelty: #NAME?
Impact & Results:
Excellent results during the pandemic traced the path for future scenarios of surgeons that are now able to share real-time best practices during the course of the intervention with their peers. As one groundbreaking application of virtual hologram technology, the doctors in Serbia performed a unique intervention on the spine in collaboration with doctors from Germany. 

Medical education is another sector where application of mixed reality achieved excellent results. During the pandemic, students of medicine had limited access to regular practical work activities (visiting the patients, diagnostics, medical councils). By using mixed reality, students now have a chance to see patients in real time and follow diagnostic procedures, while located at safe place, avoiding COVID exposure.

Organisation: Department for Equal Opportunities - Prime Minister’s Office
Description:
Within the implementation of the National Recovery and Resilience Plan (NRRP). the Italian government has introduced a national “Gender Equality Certification System” that aims at guiding and incentivizing companies to adopt policies aiming at reducing the gender gap in all the most critical areas, such as opportunities for growth in the company, equal pay for equal work and maternity protection, and also supporting a paradigm shift developing from the business world.

By decree of April 29, 2022, the guidelines for the adoption of specific KPIs related to gender equality policies were enforced in the National legislation.
Impact & Results: Too early for concreate results, but has significant potential.

Organisation: Office of the High Commissioner for Diaspora Affairs
Description:
Armenia, however, is a unique   case   among   different   diaspora communities   in   that   there   are   more Armenians   currently   living   outside   of Armenia than within its borders. The Armenian Government is committed to ensuring sufficient conditions for Armenians to assemble in their historical homeland. 

To this end,  the iGorts program seeks to encourage professional repatriation and to promote the inclusion back into the Armenian collective of those who have been separated from the Armenian environment

“iGorts” is an unprecedented program that invites Diaspora Armenian professionals to work in Armenia's state institutions. Within the framework of the program, Diaspora Armenian professionals are placed in state agencies in need of their expertise and have the opportunity to lend their experience and knowledge toward improving and developing the state institution and its policies and programs.

Following an application and interview process, every year 50 Diaspora professionals are placed in state agencies across different sectors in need of their expertise. 

The objectives of the Program are: 
1) Professional repatriation 
2) Improvement and development of Armenian public institutions. 
3) Promote inclusivity of diaspora Armenians within the public sector
4) Encourage the exchange and implementation of international best practices within the governmental sphere.
Novelty:
This Program is innovative as:
1)It offers diaspora to come and have an active and effective impact on the development of the country in terms of policy making and strategy bringing innovative ideas to relevant spheres and initiating new projects. 
2)It promotes repatriation and facilitates the integration process of the repatriants.
3)It created a new platform for government institutions for collaboration.
Impact & Results:
Over  60% of the participants stayed in Armenia following the end of the program. Most of them continued working in the  same institutions they were placed at the beginning of the program, some of them were appointed high officials within the Government (Deputy Minister of Economy, Head of  the Tourism Committee, Director of the National Centre for Innovation and Entrepreneurship etc.).

We conducted a quantitative survey among the iGorts participants and 80% of them expressed overall satisfaction with the results and about 75% to 80 % of the state institutions assed the program positively.

Project/Initiative Name: Exploration Projects - Fomentation and development of disruptive and unconventional research in Chile
Organisation: National Agency of research and Development
Description:
The National Agency of Research and Development has piloted a new program, called ‘Exploration Projects’, that allows funding of disruptive, unconventional, and high novelty science and research in all areas of knowledge. The program goals are to promote this type of science, propose the exploration of new and unconventional perspectives, methodologies, theories, and technologies, with the potential for significant reward to its discipline, field of knowledge, as well as society.

This program funds a duo of researchers, in all areas of knowledge such as Arts, Social Sciences, Humanities, Life Sciences, Engineers, Exact Sciences, and Inter and Transdisciplines. The pilot was designed to promote new paths in the researcher's career and ways to develop science, not only based on the conventional result, as an article, but also patents, policy briefs, outreach, public engagement, and advisory in public and private decision-making.

During the 2022 pilot contest, 250 proposals were received from twenty-three research institutes or universities in the different research areas, covering twelve of the sixteen regions of Chile. The grant, that 30 applications will award, consists of $270 million Chilean pesos (approximately $300.000 US dollars) for 24 to 36 months and will allow the researcher duo to finance R&D personnel, equipment, and operational and administrative costs.
Novelty:
The Exploration Projects pilot is an innovation in the Chilean science ecosystem because:
1. It’s the first funding program for unconventional, disruptive, and high novelty science. 
2. The program was co-designed in near collaboration with the scientific community and strategic actors in the ecosystem.
3. Introduced a new paradigm of doing science in contrast to existing programs, considering a duo of researchers, the high failure possibility, focus on complex problems, and new evaluation factors 
4. Innovation in the advisory and follow-up program, monitoring the execution of the proposals.
Impact & Results:
As of the date of submission in 2022, this project is pilot stage. This pilot contest received 250 proposals: 107 in Life Sciences, 59 in Engineers and Exact Sciences, 46 in Inter and Transdisciplines, and 38 in Arts, Social Sciences, Humanities. Of these, 224 have been admissible to continue, that represents 90% of total.
Currently, a technical admission is being held and soon will start the panel review.

Organisation: Aragon Open Government Laboratory (LAAAB) - Department of Citizenship of the Government of Aragon
Description:
Easy Government invites people with disabilities to redesign legal and administrative texts, such as laws, subsidy bases or forms, using easy reading. This is to address the language used by the public administration to communicate with citizens, which is often too technical and not adapted to the needs of different groups. 

This is a project based on accessibility to information, working on administrative texts, plans, laws or forms. More specifically, it consists of rewriting complex texts using the easy reading methodology, which are tested in validation workshops with people with functional diversity.

Texts are adapted, both in language and in the format in which they are presented, trying to make a translation or conversion to a more summarised, simple and visual complementary version with the aim of facilitating their understanding to a wider sector of the population that, otherwise, may have difficulties and access barriers. In this activity, 50 people with disabilities have participated in 4 workshops, where a total of 326 ideas related to the fields of governance and participation, equal opportunities, accessibility, education, employment, health, social services and legal protection have been collected.
Novelty: With Easy Government, people with functional diversity, such as intellectual disabilities or mental health illnesses, have been able to integrate for the first time in citizen participation workshops that address laws and other public policies and services. It generates safe spaces of participation and empowerment for these people. In addition to accessing and discussing adapted content, they can express their visions, concerns and expectations.
Impact & Results:
- Almost 10 workshops have been held with the participation of more than 100 people with disabilities. Attendance at these workshops has had an impact on the lives of each of these people, but the impact goes much further. 
- The fourth most visited page of the Transparency Portal during 2020 was 'Easy Government' and one of the documents with the highest number of downloads in 2020 was the "Coronavirus Questionnaire" in easy reading, downloaded 51270 times.

Description:
The regulation of public procurement is a major barrier, but there are more: the difficult access and identification of the public purchaser, the lack of spaces for mutual knowledge, the different rhythms of the administration and that of entrepreneurs, or the difficult access to space for piloting and experimentation, are just some of the barriers that limit technology transfer and innovation in the public sphere. 

In response to all these challenges, the first govtech lab in Latin America, Govtechlab Madrid, was created in 2020. The aim is to bring innovation generated by startups and scaleups to public administrations and thus promote a new generation of digital public services. We promote the transfer of technology and innovation to public administrations by creating public innovation ecosystems where entrepreneurs, public institutions, corporations and universities collaborate to create a new generation of digital public services that respond to the new expectations and needs of citizens.

We have worked on four key components: 1. govtech challenge lab: We have worked on 5 challenges per year. 2. Govtech Academy: Training activities that complement the practical experience of the participants. 3. Innovative ecosystem: The Madrid govtech ecosystem is articulated. 4. Open work: (i) application of clear communication to all communications. The work of this laboratory has inspired the continuity of a larger laboratory in the Community of Madrid, the creation and design of the Govtech LATAM of the Inter-American Development Bank.
Novelty: The laboratory has been incorporated into international networks with which it has maintained a close relationship: Civictech Alliance, Govtech Hub, and international organisations working in this area (CAF, IDB, OECD, Segib, which have provided inspiration and feedback. Training has been provided for both public employees and startups. A pilot open innovation platform specialised in govtech has been created, with more than 50 startups registered. The project has been designed to work openly, leaving and sharing all the materials and processes for the development of the programme independently, guaranteeing sustainability, which has allowed the Community of Madrid.
Impact & Results: We have worked with 24 municipalities, and trained 60 public workers. We have received 170 applications from startups, 40 have participated in bootcamps. We have generated a network of more than 50 collaborating initiatives allowing us to generate a network of more than 500 govtech startups. We have held 10 national and international events and organised panels at South Summit, in Spain and Brazil, DigitALL of the European Commission and a workshop with Apolitical. More than 137 startups, 186 representatives of public institutions and 37 large companies have been involved in our activities. Commercial collaborations have been generated between participating startups, and 70% of the startups that have piloted are in talks with the municipality to scale up the collaboration initiated in the programme. The Community of Madrid has tendered for a larger Govtech lab inspired by GovtechLab Madrid.

Description:
The "Digital Sherpas" in Las Rozas guide businesses in digitalization and the multichannel and gamified platform "Las Rozas Fans" helps them connect to the world in an attractive and easy way. 

To achieve an effective digital transformation, we think of the process in two phases: one, digitize and two, connect. It is not worth a recipe the same for everyone. There are two important innovations: personalize and accompany in this process. To digitize, we created the figure of the Digital Sherpas who, like the Sherpas do in the mountains, accompany and personalize this process for free and above all working on cultural change. In digital transformation, the easiest thing is technology. The Digital Sherpas are consultants specialized in digitalization of commerce and SMEs and who applied a methodology designed by Las Rozas Innova.

Once digitized it is necessary to help commerce to be connected, but valuing the uniqueness and value of the local, as well as the people who lead these businesses. To do this, to the participants in the Sherpas we provide free access to a multichannel SaaS platform, gamified called "Las Rozas Fans" and that will allow a high dynamization of commerce, very easy to use and attractive both for commerce, and for the most important thing of Las Rozas Fans: the final customer. It is intended that this platform, which is not a "market place", does not seek to compete with search engines or large shopping platforms, is very attractive and with an important focus on gamification, can connect people on the one hand with the place where they live and, on the other, provide a local business with a tool of high dynamization that, on its own, it would be very difficult to access.

In 2022 and 2023 the Sherpas Program is launched again to try to reach 50% of the city's businesses and in 2024, the maximum possible. This program has been created taking as a reference the lessons learned from the pilot program and thus achieve more effective in the digital transformation of the participants. The proven methodology consists of four phases: listening, analyzing, proposing and acting. Listen to understand the business and uniqueness well. Analyze business information (market, product, etc.). Propose a P@Di (Individualized Digital Action Plan) to launch and personalize each business and where above all free or reduced cost tools and software are used.
Novelty:
Digitalized and Connected addresses digital transformation by focusing on the people who must carry it out. Hence the figure of the Digital Sherpa since like the "Sherpas" in the mountains, the "Digital Sherpas" in Las Rozas guide businesses in digitalization, they do it in a personalized way, listening, analyzing and proposing actions that are adapted to the reality of a business and the people who manage it. 
It is proposed to create Fans of the products and services of our city. The multichannel and gamified platform "Las Rozas Fans" helps them connect to the world in an attractive and easy way for everyone.
Impact & Results: The Sherpas Digitales 2021 pilot project evaluated its quality as soon as it ended with very satisfactory results (4.7/5). A year later, participants provided their assessment of both the program and content, the economic impact, as well as their needs afterwards. Most of them have stated that the program has significantly helped them to transform digitally, improve sales and win customers and have been very interested in the creation of a Digital Sherpas Community.

Organisation: Secretariat of Innovation and Digital Transformation, in conjunction with the Secretariat of Urban Development of the Government of the City of Buenos Aires.
Description:
Ciudad 3D is an open digital platform that makes it possible to visualise on a 3D map the areas where it is possible to build in each neighbourhood of the city and their conditions according to current regulations. The platform is open to residents and professionals working in the construction industry, and functions as an interdisciplinary work tool to simplify procedures, provide predictability and transparency, and streamline the tasks of the sectors that use it.

In Ciudad 3D you can access information on the plans, land use and land use of each plot, with access to options to apply filters in the visualisations, so that whoever enters can access detailed information to find out what can be built and in what volume.
Previously, building procedures had to go through a long process that entailed a lot of difficulties for professionals, investors and even more so for private individuals who needed to build. It was time-consuming because it was manual and the interpretation of the urban code was entirely in the hands of the person who carried out the procedure in an analogue way. 

Ciudad 3D not only simplified access to this information but also presents it in a visual and user-friendly format, making it more understandable in just a few clicks. The platform started with high-demand sectors of the City and, as the months went by, a greater number of areas were incorporated, as well as new tools, for example, more variables, indicators and functionalities, so that any resident of the City has the possibility of accessing the information he or she needs in a simple and intuitive digital platform.
Novelty:
3D City was developed in open source so that, by making the source code available, other institutions can study, modify and improve its design. It is based on the "Digital Twin" concept, which compiles the urban plan of a city virtually and in 3D. With this technology, algorithms interpret the Urban Code and predictively model the building potential of each lot. 
Ciudad 3D is developed in two parts: one concerning data ingestion, in which the information is processed with geographic algorithms and integrated with complementary information. The second part corresponds to the visualisation of this data in 2D and 3D, together with all the necessary information of a parcel. For this, information is consumed from an API and visualised in a front end made with React and Leaflet. At the same time, it allows visualisations in layers and the possibility of using different filters by height, areas and mixtures of use.
Impact & Results:
The results we have observed by implementing the platform so far are as follows:
- Today, more than 13000 users use the platform. This represents a 218% increase in monthly consultations compared to Plano Abierto, the predecessor of Ciudad 3D. 
- Optimisation of resources, by reducing the approval time for construction projects, and reducing the time it takes to process files with government bodies.
- Savings in terms of money and working hours.
- Reduction of 88% in requests for planning certificates (January 2021 vs. September 2022) and 63% in help desk consultations (September 2021 vs. September 2022).

Organisation: Executive Secretariat of the National Anti-Corruption System
Description:
The Anti-Corruption Digital Marketplace (MDA) is the first space developed by a public institution in the history of Mexico, in which public institutions can share free-to-use digital tools in order to generate savings and promote innovation and cooperation among those that need software and other digital resources to achieve common goals. The MDA contains more than 22 tools, some of which have been developed by other public institutions and some of which have been developed by other public institutions.

The National Digital Platform (PDN) is an intelligence tool that aims to remove information barriers so that public data is comparable, accessible and usable in order to combat any act of corruption. It is composed of IT elements through which various systems, subsystems and strategic data sets in the fight against corruption are integrated and connected. 

The PDN must contain information of a national nature, i.e. from the three branches of government (executive, legislative and judicial), from the three levels of government (federal, state and municipal), including the Autonomous Constitutional Bodies, which is why it is an ambitious and complex project.

The idea of creating the Anti-Corruption Digital Marketplace (MDA) arose from the need for public institutions, as set out in the General Law of the National Anti-Corruption System (LGSNA), to comply with the obligation to incorporate data into the PDN.
Novelty:
The MDA is the first space created by a government institution, totally public, to share technological tools that facilitate the connection and encourage the transfer of knowledge, as well as generate great savings for the treasury, as it avoids the repeated use of large amounts of resources (human, material and economic) in the development of applications.
MDA promotes a new way of doing government, where knowledge is not hidden but transmitted, there is no excessive bureaucracy, as no formalities are required to use the tools and resources are used efficiently and economically, eliminating technical and budgetary barriers.
Impact & Results: The tools available in the MDA generate savings of between 1 and 2 million pesos for public institutions, cutting the time required to obtain information for publication in the PDN for at least 5 entities that used the complete solutions put on the market, in addition to the standardisation and use of the capture systems by municipal and state comptrollers, due to their free use, there is no way of knowing exactly how many times the tools have been used.

Description:
In an increasingly digital world, Participa.gov represents our vision for civic participation in political decision-making processes. Participa.gov is the Portuguese Public Administration's centralized, cross-cutting platform for facilitating participatory processes at all levels of government. It employs cutting-edge technology to ensure that citizens can actively participate in policy decision-making via secure and transparent mechanisms that foster trust.

Participa.GOV is a one-stop-shop platform where citizens can present their proposals and decide through their votes on all relevant initiatives for their lives – From the national budget to a GovTech contest or their children’s school initiatives, everything is possible at Participa.gov. Simply put, public entities launch a challenge, based on a set of rules predefined at the back office, and citizens present their proposals and then decide through their voting.
 
When developing participa.gov the team took into account two major factors:
1. The need to be customizable, because each entity has its own idiosyncrasies and target audiences and because each initiative is different.
2. The need to be safe, secure, and transparent because we know that the biggest challenge of any participatory system is related to trust factors.
The Participa.gov platform was therefore built using highly secure eID mechanisms and blockchain technology, ensuring security, auditing, and transparency in the process from start to finish. Secure digital identity mechanisms add an extra layer of security and, in the case of the Portuguese eID ecosystem, it allows the participants' scope to be defined based on the attributes available in the Portuguese digital identity: age, address, professional attributes, and so on.
Novelty:
The innovative nature of this project derives from a conjunction of features, most notably three:
- The possibility to centralize all digital public participatory processes in a single platform
- The use of blockchain technology, which despite currently trending is still not widespread in public sector projects.
- The use of the national eID ecosystem not only adds another layer of security but also allows for greater personalization in the type of initiatives proposed. Indeed, any attribute present in the citizen's digital ID can be used to define the scope of the initiative
Impact & Results: It is not yet possible to provide results outcomes and impacts since the project is at its early stages and few initiatives have been submitted. However, the major outcomes/impacts are predicted as (i) a significant cost reduction for the public administration in the mid/long term in the use of participatory platforms and (ii) higher social impact, as demographic trends and technological advancements are changing the way citizens communicate and engage with decision-makers. According to these trends, face-to-face forms of participation are progressively being replaced by online modes of civic engagement. This innovation embraces this trend.

Project/Initiative Name: Madrid Mobility 360 (planificador avanzado multimodal para realizar desplazamientos urbanos)
Organisation: Ayuntamiento de Madrid - Dirección General de la Oficina Digital
Description:
Madrid Mobility 360 is a mobile application that allows citizens moving around the city to calculate the most efficient route between all modes of transport in the capital, both public and carsharing or bicycles. It includes a powerful route planner, which can estimate journey times now and on future days, and provides the capacity on a particular bus. The app allows payment for various transport methods and reports the CO2 emissions generated.

Madrid Mobility 360 offers a unique and fully functional MaaS (Mobility as a Service) solution, including booking, unlocking and payments and an innovative solution beyond the powerful MPlanner, Madrid Mobility 360's route planner, which is a world first in this type of tool, providing the user with information on bus occupancy. 

Madrid Mobility 360 brings together different options from Madrid's mobility ecosystem and allows the user to identify on a single map the wide range of mobility solutions available in the city. The application provides information on the main public transport operators, such as EMT city buses, metro, light rail, suburban train and intercity buses. It also includes the many existing shared mobility options, such as BiciMAD electric bikesharing (station-based) or BiciMAD Go (free-floating), scooters, cars and motorbikes. 

Thanks to its two main components: MRoute (the route planner) and MPass (the payment platform), Madrid Mobility 360 has been designed to respond to the needs of the mobility market by reducing complexity and offering a fully digital experience to citizens. Incentivising the use of the solution and maximising citizen engagement.
Novelty: One of the innovations of Madrid Mobility 360 is to provide information on bus occupancy in the travel segment of the specific route calculated. The occupancy, which is displayed in 25% jumps, is not merely descriptive. In case the planned route includes a high occupancy section, the tool offers the user two alternative options: a second route in the same time slot with a lower occupancy, and the same route at a later time with a lower occupancy. This functionality allows users to plan their journeys taking into account this type of service data, and to have the necessary information to modify their demand for the service, either temporally or spatially. Thus, a user waiting at the bus stop will have the information to decide whether to take the next bus or, on the contrary, to wait.
Impact & Results:
The system provides dashboards with the main indicators of its use. 
Beyond the figures on the impact of this service, almost 24k downloads and more than 10k users in 2021, Madrid is a leading model in public transport, and an attractive scenario for the implementation of new mobility solutions. High-capacity public transport continues to be the backbone of urban mobility, while other alternatives are emerging to complement it and provide users with multiple possibilities, building a wide, varied and multimodal offer.

Project/Initiative Name: Characterisation of the nation's wastelands with Artificial Intelligence and Satellite Imagery
Organisation: Innovation Centre of the Organisation of Iberoamerican States
Description:
Through the intensive use of new technologies and innovative methodologies, the problem of characterising uncultivated areas was solved for the benefit of local farmers.
New methods in the application of national standards made it possible for the first time to consolidate the information that will give them title to the land.

Using the design thinking methodology, the characterisation of a 200,000 hectare polygon was structured using Artificial Intelligence with satellite images, social mapping workshops with digital screens in the territory, the development of online monitoring platforms and the design of socialisation tools. Already 134,499 hectares in 3 municipalities have been characterised, benefiting nearly 8,000 people with information on 1,900 properties.

This innovation will make it possible to transfer the characterisation methodology to the rest of the country for 48 million hectares of uncultivated land in the nation and other countries with similar conditions. Social Innovation, Innovation in public management and Technological Innovation in articulation of the quadruple helix model: state, academia, business and society.
Novelty:
This is the first time in Colombia that geo-cadastral information has been accessed in one of the most difficult to access areas in the country, mixing information from public records, territorial information, satellite images, 4RI technologies and design thinking methodologies.
The application of the analysis of secondary sources such as satellite images and with the application of declarative and collaborative methods, it was possible to identify around 1900 properties in 8 months; a similar area, with traditional methods of surveying the physical information of the properties, could take up to four years. In this sense, research related to the application of information analysis through artificial intelligence and other technologies opens up the possibility of having alternatives that promote the application of technical procedures through the use of indirect methods.
Impact & Results:
The innovation process has allowed the characterisation of more than 130,000 hectares, 1900 properties, in 3 municipalities, benefiting more than 8,000 people. 
For monitoring and measurement, an online monitoring platform was designed for all project components. The measurement methodologies are subject to the institutional procedures of the partner entities.

Organisation:
The State Agency for Public Service
  and Social Innovations under the President 
  of the Republic of Azerbaijan
Description:
NCII is a comprehensive capacity building platform developed by the State Agency for developing innovators in the public sector. NCII means measuring the organization's innovation ability and success, and taking measures to build an innovation culture within the organization. With the creation of NCII, there is direct support for finding solutions to the difficulties and problems faced by state organizations in the implementation of their daily activities with more innovative approaches.

For the first time in the country, the Corporate Innovation Index methodology was developed in order to measure the innovation intelligence quotient of the employees working in government organizations, and to evaluate the creative and innovation-oriented activities.

The implementation of the Corporate Innovation Index will create the following opportunities:
- Evaluation of innovation skills and performance of the organization
- Determining the degree of application of efficiency mechanisms in the work of employees
- Identification of factors supporting innovation
- Studying the rate of adding creativity to the routine work of structural units
- Comparing groups in the organization
- Preparation of the innovation action plan and monitoring over time

Innovation KPIs for time, product, finance, management and other categories are determined and the pace of development in the field is measured over a certain period.
After the evaluation is completed, a national corporate innovation index score is produced for the organization. Based on the score, an innovation index program is prepared for the organization. The program includes increasing the knowledge and skills of government employees, forming an innovation environment in the organization, and writing an innovation strategy.
Novelty: New type of innovation measurement tied directly to actions.
Impact & Results: Not clearly detailed in submission.

Organisation: Subred Integrada de Servicios de Salud Norte E.S.E.
Description:
Repetitive tasks for a civil servant take between 3 and 6 hours a day, generate delays in administrative processes, increase human error and minimise people's professional capacity. By innovating the automation of these tasks, using free Robotic Process Automation (RPA) software, response times, costs and quality of service within the District institutions and to the citizen are improved.

By automating these tasks, using free Robotic Process Automation (RPA) software, the response times, costs and quality of service within the institutions and to the citizen are improved. Challenges Solved by the Robot Incubator:
- How to automate repetitive tasks with few resources
- How to robotize repetitive tasks in a humanized way without making people afraid of losing their jobs
- How to disseminate the proposed robot solution without technical knowledge barriers

How does the Robot Incubator work?
A District Entity proposes the repetitive task it wants to solve.
The Incubator, together with the Entity, evaluates the feasibility of a solution with robots, either by adapting an existing one or creating a new one. Training and/or support in the adaptation / creation of the robot.
Sharing the new robot with the community.
Novelty: #NAME?
Impact & Results:
So far, the key metrics of the Incubator are the savings in resources of the Entities where the robots have been implemented, which in the case of the North sub-network has been the savings, only in licensing, of $ 200 million per year, the savings in time of the robotised process that went from three (3) weeks to less than one (1) week and its execution is now performed by one (1) person when previously it required the transfer of twenty (20) people from other areas.
As RPA solutions are implemented from the Robot Incubator, the metrics to measure its achievements will be the number of District Entities benefited, the number of robots implemented, the savings in man hours, process times and licensing costs related to the implemented repetitive tasks.

Organisation: Digital and Population Data Services Agency
Description:
The key challenge has been to develop measurement tools that collect unified data from digital services from all levels of public sector to enhance user experience and build customer intimacy between the end-users and public administration. The objective has been to smoothen and straighten digital processes in public digital services. With the Suomi.fi Quality Tools an organisation can evaluate, monitor and compare the quality and use of its services within and between organisations. The tools include a Self-assessment, Customer Feedback and Utilisation Rate Measurement tool and are free of charge. The tools were developed primarily for public sector organisations to help them develop customer-oriented digital services and improve knowledge-based management. On a national level the tools provide data on the state of digitalisation.

Using the service is simple and customer oriented. Once the organisation has access rights to the Suomi.fi Finnish Service Catalogue (PTV) and the service(s) and service channel(s) are described in the catalogue, the Quality Tools can be activated and implemented. The tools include a Self-assessment, Customer Feedback and Utilisation Rate Measurement tool. The Self-assessment tool includes a national set of criteria that organisations can use to evaluate their digital services internally. With the Customer Feedback Tool service providers can collect feedback from users right after use of the service and it includes a star rating and open feedback form. The Utilisation Rate Measurement Tool monitors the utilization rate of services and the transition of the usage between different service channels.

In the future, the aim is to have most or even all public sector organisations, but also private and third sector organisations, to be using the service, as they discover its benefits. Then Soumi.fi will provide an accurate and real-time overview of the state of digitalisation and quality of digital services on a national level. Organisations will have better tools and means to develop their services. Citizens will have smoother and more customer-oriented digital services offered to them. As a scalable solution, the opportunities to expand the deployment of the Quality Tools also to other countries, are realistic.
Novelty: Suomi.fi Quality Tools is a unique, universal and never seen before service for public sector organisations to evaluate their digital services. The service’s user interface gives access to citizens, administrations and media to view, compare and benchmark the collected data transparently.
